topics.title: Actors and Use Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actors and Use Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Actors — Formal Definition

In the Unified Modeling Language (**UML 2.5** specification, as adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus), an **Actor** is a role played by a user or any other system that interacts with the subject system to produce meaningful, observable results. The official UML specification states:

> *"An Actor specifies a role played by a user or any other system that interacts with the subject."*

An actor is **external** to the system under consideration. It represents a coherent set of roles that a human, hardware device, or another software system plays when it needs to interact with the system for the purpose of exchanging information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> Actors are **NOT** part of the system. They live *outside* the system boundary. If you draw an actor *inside* the system box in a use case diagram, it is an immediate mark deduction in the KTU board exam.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cinema Theatre" Analogy

Think of a **multiplex cinema theatre** as the system. Now, who interacts with the theatre?

- A **Customer** who buys tickets, watches movies, and orders snacks.
- A **Ticket Checker** who validates the ticket at the entry.
- A **Cleaning Staff** who cleans the hall after the show.
- The **Movie Distributor** who supplies the film reels.
- The **Fire Department** — interacts only in emergency scenarios.

Each of these is an **actor** in UML terminology. They are all *external* to the theatre system, but they all have a *role* they perform while interacting with it. Notice that a *Customer* is a role, not necessarily a specific human — the same person could be a *Customer* today and a *Movie Critic* tomorrow. UML captures **roles**, not identities.

### 1.1.2 Types of Actors

| Actor Type | Definition | Cinema Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Actor** | Initiates the interaction to achieve a goal. | Customer buying a ticket. |
| **Secondary Actor** | The system initiates interaction with it (provides a service). | Bank payment gateway used by the theatre. |
| **Active Actor** | Initiates the use case and participates actively throughout. | Customer walking up to the counter. |
| **Passive Actor** | Merely participates; does not initiate. | A database that simply stores records. |
| **Human Actor** | A person playing a role. | Customer, Ticket Checker. |
| **Non-Human Actor** | Another system, hardware, or external entity. | Payment Gateway, Sensor. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** A stick-figure notation in UML is the standard visual representation for *any* actor, human or non-human. Some textbooks use a rectangle with the `<<actor>>` stereotype for non-human actors, but the stick figure is universally accepted and is the safer choice for the KTU answer script.

---

## 1.2 Use Cases — Formal Definition

A **Use Case** is a sequence of actions performed by the system that yields an **observable result of value** to a particular actor. Formally, the UML 2.5 spec defines:

> *"A Use Case is a kind of behaviored classifier that specifies a [complete] unit of functionally that a subject (or system) provides to its participants."*

Three keywords are examinable in the KTU Module 1 question paper:
1. **Complete** — The use case must be carried out end-to-end, not truncated.
2. **Observable** — The result must be visible to the actor, not an internal private step.
3. **Value** — The result must benefit the actor. If no actor benefits, the use case is suspect.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Order" Use Case

Consider a restaurant. The use case **"Place Order"** could be described as:

> The *Customer* (actor) approaches the counter, views the menu, places an order, the chef prepares it, payment is processed, and the food is delivered. The result of value to the customer? A hot meal on the table.

Notice:
- The customer's request **initiates** the use case.
- The use case includes **interactions between system components** (counter, kitchen, billing).
- The outcome is **observable** and of **value** to the customer.

This single narrative — from trigger to deliverable outcome — is what a use case captures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use Case vs. Function/Module:** A function is a piece of internal code. A use case is a **goal-oriented sequence** of interactions between an actor and the system. The KTU board examiner often tests this distinction with a one-line trick question worth 2–3 marks.

---

## 1.3 Use Case Diagram — The Bird's-Eye View

A **Use Case Diagram** is a UML behavioural diagram that depicts the relationships among actors and use cases within a system boundary. It is part of the four-diagram set that constitutes a complete UML model (the others being Class, Sequence, and State Machine diagrams — covered in later modules).

In Module 1 of PECGT411, this diagram is the central deliverable students are expected to draw in Part B questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinable Components of a Use Case Diagram (as per KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus):**
> - System boundary (rectangle)
> - Actors (stick figures) — *outside* the boundary
> - Use cases (ovals) — *inside* the boundary
> - Associations (solid lines) connecting actors to use cases
> - `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`, and **Generalization** relationships between use cases

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Actor

An actor is characterized by **four properties** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Role, not Identity** — One person can play multiple roles; one role can be played by many people. E.g., "Customer" is a role; "Rahul" plays the Customer role today.
2. **External to the System** — Actors are *outside* the system boundary rectangle.
3. **Goal-Driven** — Each actor interacts with the system to fulfil a *goal* (a "reason").
4. **Communicates via Messages** — Actors and the system exchange messages, modelled as associations.

### 2.1.1 How to *Find* Actors (Engineering Heuristic)

When given a problem statement, use the **A.R.G.S.** heuristic to identify actors:

- **A** — *Anyone who uses the system?* (primary users)
- **R** — *Required interfaces?* (other systems that the subject must connect to)
- **G** — *Goal of a use case?* (the "who wants this?" reverse lookup)
- **S** — *Secondary stakeholders?* (maintenance, admin, regulatory bodies)

This is also taught in industry as the **CRC (Candidate-Responsibility-Collaborator)** brainstorming method.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** If you cannot think of an actor for a use case, the use case is probably either an internal function (and should not be a use case at all) or you have misidentified the system boundary.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Use Case

A use case has **four structural parts**:

| Part | Description | Example (ATM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Verb-noun phrase describing the goal. | "Withdraw Cash" |
| **Actor(s)** | Who initiates / participates. | Customer (primary), Bank Server (secondary) |
| **Precondition** | State that must be true before use case starts. | Customer has a valid ATM card. |
| **Main Flow** | Step-by-step happy path interactions. | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select Amount → Cash Dispensed |
| **Postcondition** | State guaranteed after successful completion. | Cash is dispensed; account is debited. |
| **Alternative Flows** | Exception paths and edge cases. | Wrong PIN, Insufficient Balance, Card Retained |

The fully written, text-based version of a use case is called a **Use Case Specification** or **Use Case Narrative**. The *diagram* is just the summary; the *specification* is the deliverable for project documentation.

---

## 2.3 Relationships Between Use Cases — The KTU High-Yield Matrix

This is the **most-examined portion** of Module 1. Memorize the table below thoroughly.

| Relationship | Notation (UML 2.5) | Direction | When to Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | Solid line between actor and use case | Undirected | Actor participates in a use case. | Customer — *Place Order* |
| **Include** (`<<include>>`) | Dashed arrow with `<<include>>` stereotype | Base $\rightarrow$ Included | The base use case **always** requires the included use case. | *Place Order* `<<include>>` *Validate Payment* |
| **Extend** (`<<extend>>`) | Dashed arrow with `<<extend>>` stereotype | Extending $\rightarrow$ Base | The extending use case adds **optional** behaviour to the base. | *Apply Discount* `<<extend>>` *Place Order* |
| **Generalization** | Solid line with hollow triangle | Child $\rightarrow$ Parent | One use case is a specialized form of another. | *Online Payment* $\rightarrow$ *Payment* |

### 2.3.1 Deep-Dive: `<<include>>` vs. `<<extend>>` — The KTU Favourite Trap Question

This is the **single most common Module 1 confusion** that costs students 3–4 marks in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern Alert**
> A classic question is: *"Differentiate between include and extend relationships in use case diagrams with suitable examples."* This is almost always a 7-mark sub-question in Part B. Memorize the matrix above **verbatim** and prepare one example from your project domain (e.g., Library Management, Hospital, or Online Shopping).

**Detailed Comparison Table (use this in the exam):**

| Parameter | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| **Arrow Direction** | From base to included (mandatory dependency) | From extending to base (optional dependency) |
| **Trigger** | Base use case unconditionally triggers the included use case. | Extending use case triggers **only if** a specific condition is met. |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes — base cannot complete without the included one. | No — base can run without the extension. |
| **Reuse Purpose** | Avoid duplicating a common sub-flow across multiple use cases. | Add an optional or rare sub-flow to one or more use cases. |
| **Conditional Flow** | No condition — always executed. | Has an **extension point** and **condition guard**. |
| **Visibility to Base** | The base is **aware** it includes another. | The base is **unaware** it might be extended. |
| **Example** | *Withdraw Cash* `<<include>>` *Authenticate User* | *Apply Promo Code* `<<extend>>` *Checkout* |

### 2.3.2 Generalization Among Use Cases

**Generalization** between use cases (and also between actors) represents an "is-a" relationship. A child use case inherits the behaviour and meaning of the parent but can specialize or override it.

- Example: *Pay by Credit Card* and *Pay by UPI* both generalize from *Make Payment*.
- Example: *Premium Customer* is a generalization of *Customer* actor.

Generalization is drawn as a **solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead** pointing from the child to the parent.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Actors and Use Cases

Since this is a modelling/notation topic, the "formula" equivalents are the **decision rules**. Use this matrix as the last-page revision before the exam.

| Rule | Statement | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Number of Use Cases} \geq \text{Number of Distinct Actor Goals}$ | Each meaningful goal produces at least one use case. | Use cases map 1:1 to actor goals. |
| $\text{Primary Actor per Use Case} \geq 1$ | Every use case must have at least one primary actor. | A use case with no initiating actor is invalid. |
| $\text{Use Case Granularity} = \text{Goal Level}$ | A use case represents one user goal, not a single click. | A use case is not a function or a screen. |
| `<<include>>` is **mandatory**, `<<extend>>` is **conditional** | Base cannot finish without include; extension needs guard. | Avoid modelling exceptions as mandatory. |
| Actors live **outside** the system boundary. | Stick figure is drawn outside the rectangle. | UML semantic rule (KTU marking key). |
| Use cases live **inside** the system boundary. | Oval is drawn inside the rectangle. | UML semantic rule. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Use case diagrams are the *first* deliverable in any **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** project. They are used in IEEE 830 SRS documents, in Rational Unified Process (RUP) inception phase, and in Agile user-story mapping. Industry tools: *Visual Paradigm*, *Enterprise Architect*, *Lucidchart*, *StarUML*, *PlantUML*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Procedure: Constructing a Use Case Diagram

When given a problem statement in a KTU exam, follow this **6-step procedure** for full marks. Skipping steps = mark loss.

### Worked Example: *Online Course Registration System*

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> *"Design a use case diagram for an Online Course Registration System. Students can register, view course catalog, enroll in courses, and pay fees. The system also allows the instructor to upload grades. A payment gateway validates transactions. The admin can manage course offerings and generate reports."*

### Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary

The system boundary defines what is *inside* the system and what is *outside*. Draw a rectangle and label it **"Online Course Registration System"**.

### Step 2 — Identify the Actors Using the A.R.G.S. Heuristic

| Stakeholder Mentioned | Role | Actor Type |
|---|---|---|
| Students | Register, view catalog, enroll, pay | **Primary, Human** |
| Instructor | Upload grades | **Primary, Human** |
| Admin | Manage courses, generate reports | **Primary, Human** |
| Payment Gateway | Validate transactions | **Secondary, Non-Human (System)** |

### Step 3 — Identify the Use Cases (One per Goal)

| Goal / Action | Use Case Name |
|---|---|
| Student registers | *Register Account* |
| Student views catalog | *View Course Catalog* |
| Student enrolls | *Enroll in Course* |
| Student pays | *Pay Fees* |
| Instructor uploads grades | *Upload Grades* |
| Admin manages courses | *Manage Course Offerings* |
| Admin generates reports | *Generate Reports* |

### Step 4 — Identify the Relationships

| Relationship | Source | Target | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Association | Student | All four student use cases | Student initiates these. |
| Association | Instructor | *Upload Grades* | Instructor initiates. |
| Association | Admin | *Manage Course Offerings*, *Generate Reports* | Admin initiates. |
| Association | Payment Gateway | *Pay Fees* | Secondary actor — system calls it. |
| `<<include>>` | *Enroll in Course* | *Authenticate User* | Cannot enroll without login. |
| `<<include>>` | *Pay Fees* | *Validate Transaction* | The system **always** needs to validate via the gateway. |
| `<<extend>>` | *Apply for Scholarship* | *Pay Fees* | Scholarship is an optional discount. |
| `<<extend>>` | *Generate Transcripts* | *Generate Reports* | Transcript is a special report. |
| Generalization | *Pay via UPI*, *Pay via Card* | *Pay Fees* | Specialized forms of payment. |

### Step 5 — Apply UML Notation Rules

- Draw a rectangle for the system boundary.
- Place all **actors (stick figures)** *outside* the boundary.
- Place all **use cases (ovals)** *inside* the boundary.
- Use **solid lines** for associations and generalization.
- Use **dashed lines** with the appropriate stereotype label for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`.

### Step 6 — Validate the Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Checklist (Print This for Exam Day):**
> - [ ] Is every actor outside the system boundary?
> - [ ] Is every use case inside the system boundary?
> - [ ] Does every use case have at least one associated actor?
> - [ ] Are all `<<include>>` arrows pointing *from* base *to* included?
> - [ ] Are all `<<extend>>` arrows pointing *from* extending *to* base?
> - [ ] Is the arrowhead on generalization pointing to the parent?
> - [ ] Is the use case name a verb-noun phrase?

### Step 7 — Mathematical Verification of the Diagram's Correctness

Use the **Actor-Use Case Cardinality** check:

$$
N_{\text{edges}} = \sum_{a \in A} \sum_{u \in U_a} \mathbb{1}_{\text{assoc}}(a, u) + N_{\text{include}} + N_{\text{extend}} + N_{\text{gen}}
$$

where:
- $A$ = set of actors
- $U_a$ = set of use cases associated with actor $a$
- $\mathbb{1}_{\text{assoc}}(a, u)$ = indicator function = 1 if actor $a$ is associated with use case $u$, else 0
- $N_{\text{include}}, N_{\text{extend}}, N_{\text{gen}}$ = number of include, extend, and generalization edges respectively

**Apply to our example:**

$$
A = \{\text{Student, Instructor, Admin, PaymentGateway}\}
$$

$$
U = \{R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7\} = 7 \text{ use cases}
$$

$$
N_{\text{assoc}} = 4 \text{(Student)} + 1 \text{(Instructor)} + 2 \text{(Admin)} + 1 \text{(PG)} = 8
$$

$$
N_{\text{include}} = 2, \quad N_{\text{extend}} = 2, \quad N_{\text{gen}} = 2 \text{ (specialized payments)}
$$

$$
N_{\text{edges}} = 8 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 14
$$

This means the diagram should have **14 connecting lines** in total. If the student draws 12 or 15, there is a likely error. This is a useful self-check before submitting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Identifying Actors in a Hospital Management System

A common KTU question type: *"Identify all actors and use cases for a Hospital Management System."*

**Step-by-Step Identification:**

$$
\text{Actors} = \{\text{Patient, Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist, Pharmacist, Lab Technician, Insurance Company, Billing System}\}
$$

$$
\text{Use Cases} = \{\text{Book Appointment, Admit Patient, Generate Prescription, Issue Medicine, Conduct Lab Test, Process Insurance Claim, Generate Bill}\}
$$

**Reasoning for each non-obvious actor:**
- *Insurance Company* is a **secondary actor** because the system contacts it, not vice versa.
- *Billing System* is a **non-human secondary actor** because it provides the financial ledger service.
- *Nurse* is a **separate primary actor** from Doctor because she has unique goals (e.g., "Update Vital Signs").

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinable Insight:** Always ask, *"Does this entity have a goal it wants fulfilled by the system?"* If yes, it is an actor. If it merely reacts when the system asks, it is still an actor (secondary). The KTU key almost always awards the actor mark for any external entity that participates, regardless of how active or passive it is.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Worked Example: Include vs Extend with Arrow Direction

This sub-section is provided as a structured analytical matrix to clarify the arrow direction for the KTU exam answer script.

$$
\text{Arrow Direction} = \begin{cases} \text{Base} \rightarrow \text{Included} & \text{if } \text{relationship} = \text{include} \\ \text{Extending} \rightarrow \text{Base} & \text{if } \text{relationship} = \text{extend} \end{cases}
$$

**Practical Memorization Trick:**

> [!TIP]
> **"The arrow points to the one that ALWAYS happens"** for `<<include>>`, and **"The arrow points to the one that ALWAYS runs without the other"** for `<<extend>>`.

In other words, the arrow always points *towards* the more fundamental, common, or required use case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

Since drawing a full use case diagram in plain text is challenging, the following Mermaid block renders a high-fidelity **functional architecture** of the *Online Course Registration System* worked example.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph boundaryA["System Boundary: Online Course Registration System"]
        uc1(("Register Account"))
        uc2(("View Course Catalog"))
        uc3(("Enroll in Course"))
        uc4(("Pay Fees"))
        uc5(("Upload Grades"))
        uc6(("Manage Course Offerings"))
        uc7(("Generate Reports"))
        inc1(("Authenticate User"))
        inc2(("Validate Transaction"))
        ext1(("Apply for Scholarship"))
        ext2(("Generate Transcripts"))
        gen1(("Pay via UPI"))
        gen2(("Pay via Credit Card"))
    end

    actorS(["Student"])
    actorI(["Instructor"])
    actorAdm(["Admin"])
    actorPG(["Payment Gateway"])

    actorS --- uc1
    actorS --- uc2
    actorS --- uc3
    actorS --- uc4
    actorI --- uc5
    actorAdm --- uc6
    actorAdm --- uc7
    actorPG --- inc2

    uc3 -.->|include| inc1
    uc4 -.->|include| inc2
    ext1 -.->|extend| uc4
    ext2 -.->|extend| uc7
    gen1 ---|generalization| uc4
    gen2 ---|generalization| uc4
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **subgraph** represents the system boundary (drawn as a rectangle in hand-written exams).
- The four **actor nodes** (stick figures in UML) sit *outside* the boundary.
- The **solid lines** are associations; **dashed lines** with labels are `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>`.
- The lines going into `uc4` from `gen1` and `gen2` represent the **generalization** arrows (open triangle in UML).

---

## 4.2 Generalization Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A(["Customer"])
    A1(["Premium Customer"])
    A2(["Corporate Customer"])
    A3(["Guest User"])

    A1 ---|gen| A
    A2 ---|gen| A
    A3 ---|gen| A
```

**Interpretation:** `Premium Customer`, `Corporate Customer`, and `Guest User` are all specialized forms of the parent actor `Customer`. The arrows point to the parent, mirroring UML generalization semantics.

---

## 4.3 Include vs Extend — Directional Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph includeBlock["Include Relationship"]
        baseInc(("Base Use Case"))
        incUC(("Included Use Case"))
        baseInc -.->|include| incUC
    end

    subgraph extendBlock["Extend Relationship"]
        baseExt(("Base Use Case"))
        extUC(("Extending Use Case"))
        extUC -.->|extend| baseExt
    end
```

**Memory Aid:** Notice that the **arrow head in `include` points to the *included* use case**, but in `<<extend>>` the **arrow head points to the *base* use case**. The arrows are *opposite* in direction — a key KTU board-exam grading point.

---

## 4.4 Use Case Specification Template (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    spec[/"Use Case Specification"/]
    f1["1. Use Case Name"]
    f2["2. Actors"]
    f3["3. Preconditions"]
    f4["4. Main Flow"]
    f5["5. Alternative Flows"]
    f6["6. Postconditions"]
    f7["7. Non-Functional Requirements"]

    spec --> f1
    spec --> f2
    spec --> f3
    spec --> f4
    spec --> f5
    spec --> f6
    spec --> f7
```

This block represents the seven standard fields of a use case specification. In KTU project documentation (internal evaluation), students are expected to fill at least fields 1 through 6 for every use case.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*Define the term "Actor" in UML. Differentiate between primary and secondary actors with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Actor** in UML is a role played by a user or any other system that interacts with the subject (system) to achieve a meaningful goal. The UML 2.5 specification defines an actor as a role, not a specific identity, and it is always **external** to the system boundary.

- **Primary Actor:** The actor who *initiates* the interaction with the system to fulfil a goal. *Example:* A *Customer* initiating a *Place Order* use case in a shopping app.
- **Secondary Actor:** The actor with whom the system interacts to obtain a service, but the actor does not initiate the use case. *Example:* A *Payment Gateway* used by the shopping system to validate the transaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the differentiation with example.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*What is a use case? List any four characteristics of a well-written use case.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Use Case** is a sequence of actions performed by the system that yields an **observable result of value** to a particular actor. It captures a complete unit of functionality from the perspective of an external actor.

**Four characteristics of a well-written use case:**

1. **Goal-Oriented** — Represents a single, identifiable user goal.
2. **Complete** — The flow runs from start to finish without artificial truncation.
3. **Observable** — The outcome is visible to the actor, not an internal detail.
4. **Valuable** — The outcome provides tangible benefit to the primary actor.
5. *(Optional fifth for bonus)* **Actor-Driven** — Has at least one primary actor who initiates the flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Explain the different types of relationships in a UML use case diagram with suitable notation and examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Draw a use case diagram for an **ATM System** identifying all relevant actors and use cases. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Relationships in Use Case Diagrams

There are **four** primary relationships in a UML 2.5 use case diagram:

**1. Association (Actor ↔ Use Case)**
- **Notation:** Solid line connecting an actor to a use case.
- **Meaning:** The actor participates in that use case.
- **Example:** `Customer` — `Withdraw Cash`.

**2. `<<include>>` Relationship (Use Case ↔ Use Case)**
- **Notation:** Dashed arrow with the `<<include>>` stereotype. The arrow points from the **base** use case to the **included** use case.
- **Meaning:** The base use case **always** requires the included use case to complete its goal. Used to factor out common sub-flows.
- **Example:** `Withdraw Cash` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`.

**3. `<<extend>>` Relationship (Use Case ↔ Use Case)**
- **Notation:** Dashed arrow with the `<<extend>>` stereotype. The arrow points from the **extending** use case to the **base** use case.
- **Meaning:** The extending use case adds **optional** or **conditional** behaviour to the base. The base runs perfectly without it.
- **Example:** `Print Receipt` `<<extend>>` `Withdraw Cash`.

**4. Generalization (Use Case ↔ Use Case or Actor ↔ Actor)**
- **Notation:** Solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead pointing to the parent.
- **Meaning:** The child inherits behaviour from the parent. A child can override or extend behaviour.
- **Example:** `Pay via UPI` $\rightarrow$ `Make Payment` (child is a specialized form of the parent).

**Summary Table (write this in the exam):**

| Relationship | Notation | Mandatory? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Association | Solid line | — | Customer – Withdraw Cash |
| Include | Dashed arrow, `<<include>>` | Yes | Withdraw Cash include Authenticate User |
| Extend | Dashed arrow, `<<extend>>` | No | Print Receipt extend Withdraw Cash |
| Generalization | Solid line + hollow triangle | Inheritance | Pay via UPI $\rightarrow$ Make Payment |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Use Case Diagram for an ATM System

**Step 1 — System Boundary:** A rectangle labelled "ATM System".

**Step 2 — Actors Identified:**

| Actor | Type |
|---|---|
| Customer | Primary, Human |
| Bank Server | Secondary, Non-Human (System) |
| Maintenance Technician | Primary, Human |

**Step 3 — Use Cases Identified:**

| Use Case | Initiator |
|---|---|
| Authenticate User | Customer |
| Check Balance | Customer |
| Withdraw Cash | Customer |
| Deposit Cash | Customer |
| Transfer Funds | Customer |
| Change PIN | Customer |
| Print Mini Statement | Customer |
| Refill Cash | Maintenance Technician |
| Update Software | Maintenance Technician |

**Step 4 — Relationships:**

- `Withdraw Cash` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Check Balance` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Transfer Funds` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Deposit Cash` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Print Mini Statement` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Change PIN` `<<include>>` `Authenticate User`
- `Withdraw Cash` `<<include>>` `Verify Balance with Bank Server`
- `Print Mini Statement` `<<extend>>` `Check Balance`
- `Withdraw Cash` `<<extend>>` `Print Receipt`

**Step 5 — Diagram Description (for written exam):**

> Draw a rectangle labelled **"ATM System"**. Inside, draw 9 ovals with the use case names above. Outside the rectangle on the left, place the **Customer** stick figure. Connect the Customer to all 7 customer use cases via solid association lines. On the right side, place the **Bank Server** as a stick figure (or `<<actor>>` box) and connect it to `Verify Balance with Bank Server`. At the bottom, place **Maintenance Technician** and connect to `Refill Cash` and `Update Software`. Draw the `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrows with dashed lines and proper stereotype labels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Drawing the **actor inside the system boundary**. This is an instant 1–2 mark deduction. Actors **must** be outside.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reversing the arrow direction in `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`. Use the **"arrow points to the one that always happens"** rule.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Drawing use cases without verb-noun names. A use case named *"Authentication"* loses marks; *"Authenticate User"* is correct.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to use the system boundary rectangle. Without it, partial marks are awarded.

---

### Question B *(Alternative Choice)*
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the anatomy of a UML use case diagram. Mention the significance of the system boundary. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Identify the actors, use cases, and draw the use case diagram for a **Library Management System**. Mention any two `<<include>>` and two `<<extend>>` relationships used. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Anatomy of a Use Case Diagram

**Definition:** A **Use Case Diagram** is a UML behavioural diagram that depicts the functional requirements of a system by showing actors, use cases, and the relationships between them, all enclosed within a system boundary.

**Anatomical Components:**

1. **System Boundary** — A rectangle that visually separates the system from the external world. It defines the **scope** of the system.
   - *Significance:* Prevents feature creep. Everything inside is "what we are building"; everything outside is "what we are not building".
   - It is essential in distinguishing which use cases belong to the current system and which are external.
2. **Actors** — Stick figures drawn **outside** the boundary.
3. **Use Cases** — Ovals drawn **inside** the boundary, named with verb-noun phrases.
4. **Associations** — Solid lines between actors and use cases.
5. **Relationships** — `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`, and **Generalization** between use cases.

**Neat Sketch (description for written exam):**

> A rectangle labelled "Subject System" with 3-4 use case ovals drawn inside. Two stick figures drawn outside the rectangle on the left and right. Solid lines connecting the actors to the use cases. A dashed arrow labelled `<<include>>` connecting two use cases inside. A dashed arrow labelled `<<extend>>` connecting one use case to another.

**Significance of the System Boundary (write 3 points):**

1. **Scope Definition:** Clearly marks the boundary of the system under development.
2. **Requirement Clarity:** Only use cases inside the boundary are part of the system requirements.
3. **External Interface Identification:** Actors outside the boundary represent the external interfaces the system must support.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Library Management System

**Step 1 — System Boundary:** Rectangle labelled "Library Management System".

**Step 2 — Actors:**

| Actor | Type |
|---|---|
| Member / Student | Primary, Human |
| Librarian | Primary, Human |
| Payment Gateway | Secondary, Non-Human |
| External Book Supplier API | Secondary, Non-Human |

**Step 3 — Use Cases:**

| Use Case | Primary Actor |
|---|---|
| Register Member | Member |
| Search Book | Member |
| Issue Book | Member / Librarian |
| Return Book | Member / Librarian |
| Pay Fine | Member |
| Reserve Book | Member |
| Add New Book | Librarian |
| Generate Overdue Report | Librarian |

**Step 4 — Two `<<include>>` relationships:**

1. `Issue Book` `<<include>>` `Verify Membership`
2. `Pay Fine` `<<include>>` `Process Online Payment`

**Step 5 — Two `<<extend>>` relationships:**

1. `Send Due-Date Reminder` `<<extend>>` `Issue Book`
2. `Apply Late Fee` `<<extend>>` `Return Book`

**Step 6 — Generalization:**

- `Student Member` and `Faculty Member` are specializations of `Member` actor.
- `Issue Reference Book` and `Issue Regular Book` are specializations of `Issue Book` use case.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for (b):**
> - Many students draw the **actor `Member` inside the system boundary**, which is wrong. Members are external.
> - Some students **omit the system boundary rectangle**. Always include it.
> - Forgetting the **verb-noun naming convention** (e.g., writing "Issue" instead of "Issue Book") is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This!**

- [ ] **Actor** = a role external to the system. **Always outside the boundary.**
- [ ] **Use Case** = a goal-oriented sequence yielding **observable value** to an actor.
- [ ] **Use Case Diagram** = the bird's-eye view of system functionality.
- [ ] **System Boundary** = rectangle that defines the scope. **Always include it in KTU exams.**
- [ ] **Association** = solid line between actor and use case.
- [ ] **`<<include>>`** = mandatory, base $\rightarrow$ included.
- [ ] **`<<extend>>`** = optional, extending $\rightarrow$ base.
- [ ] **Generalization** = solid line with hollow triangle; child $\rightarrow$ parent.
- [ ] **Primary Actor** initiates; **Secondary Actor** is contacted by the system.
- [ ] A use case name is a **verb-noun phrase** (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*, not *Cash Withdrawal*).
- [ ] **A.R.G.S. Heuristic** for finding actors: A=Anyone, R=Required interfaces, G=Goal lookup, S=Stakeholders.
- [ ] Every use case **must** have at least one primary actor; otherwise it is not a use case.
- [ ] Use case granularity is at the **goal level**, not at the function or screen level.
- [ ] For KTU Part B, **always draw the system boundary, label all stereotypes correctly, and ensure arrow direction follows the rule.**
- [ ] Common exam diagrams: ATM System, Library Management System, Hospital Management System, Online Shopping, Railway Reservation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Practice drawing the diagram at least three times by hand before the exam. The KTU evaluator is strict about visual correctness — wrong arrowhead style, missing boundary, or unnamed use cases will cost marks. Spend 2–3 minutes planning the actor and use case list on the rough sheet before drawing the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Case Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Case Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use Case Specification (UCS):** A structured, textual document that *fully describes* the behavior of a single Use Case identified in a Use Case Diagram. It elaborates the *flow of events*, the *actors involved*, the *pre/post conditions*, the *alternative paths*, and the *exception paths* required to realize a specific piece of system functionality from an end-user perspective.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a Use Case Specification is treated as the *primary artifact* of requirement analysis under the Unified Process. It bridges the gap between the **graphical Use Case Diagram** (a high-level "what") and the **detailed design** (the "how"). It is *not* a design document; it is a **black-box behavioral description** of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per Module 1 of PECGT411, students must be able to *draw* a Use Case Diagram AND *write* a corresponding Use Case Specification. In the End Semester Examination, a 14-mark question often combines both — drawing the diagram (≈ 6 marks) plus writing the specification (≈ 8 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Order" Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant**. A customer (actor) hands a waiter (system) an order. The waiter does *not* need to know how the chef cooks, how the kitchen is ventilated, or how billing software works. The waiter only needs a **script**:

- *Trigger:* Customer gives order.
- *Precondition:* Customer is seated, menu in hand.
- *Main flow:* Take order → inform kitchen → serve food → present bill → accept payment.
- *Alternative flow 1:* Item unavailable → suggest alternative.
- *Exception flow:* Customer leaves without paying → manager intervenes.
- *Postcondition:* Customer has food, bill is settled, table is reset.

This **script** is exactly what a Use Case Specification is for a software system. It tells the developer *what* the system must do (not *how*) for a given actor's goal, and under what conditions success or failure occurs.

## 1.3 Why Use Case Specifications Matter (KTU 2024 Perspective)

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Requirement Traceability** | Each Use Case can be traced to source code, test cases, and user stories (NEP 2020 outcome-based mapping). |
| **Communication Bridge** | Acts as a common language between *domain experts* (non-technical) and *developers*. |
| **Test Case Derivation** | Each flow (main, alternative, exception) maps directly to one or more test scenarios. |
| **Scope Control** | Clearly defines what is *in-scope* and *out-of-scope* for a release. |
| **Estimation Basis** | Use Case Points (UCP) technique uses the number of use cases for effort estimation. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 The Standard Template — Alistair Cockburn / KTU Convention

A Use Case Specification follows a **standardized template**. KTU examiners expect the following sections (in this exact order, abbreviated where necessary):

1. **Use Case Name** — Verb-Noun form (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*, *Place Order*).
2. **Use Case ID** — Unique identifier (e.g., UC01, UC-Auth-01).
3. **Brief Description** — One- or two-sentence summary.
4. **Primary Actor(s)** — The actor who *initiates* the use case.
5. **Secondary Actor(s) / Supporting Actors** — Systems or users that *participate* but do not initiate.
6. **Preconditions** — State that *must* be true before the use case begins.
7. **Postconditions** — State that *must* be true after the use case *successfully* completes.
8. **Main Flow / Basic Flow (Happy Path)** — The sequence of actor–system interactions under *normal* conditions.
9. **Alternative Flows** — Successful but *non-default* paths.
10. **Exception Flows** — Failure paths (errors, cancellations).
11. **Special / Non-Functional Requirements** — Performance, security, usability.
12. **Assumptions / Notes** — Open issues, dependencies.

## 2.2 Structural Decomposition — "Why" Each Section Exists

- **Main Flow** is the *most frequently executed* path. It is written as a *numbered sequence* of steps where each step is an **atomic** action by either the **Actor** or the **System**.
  - *Why:* Forces the analyst to think in terms of discrete, testable interactions.
- **Pre/Post Conditions** capture the *world state* before and after.
  - *Why:* Allows parallel development — designers can work on state transitions even before flows are finalized.
- **Alternative vs. Exception** distinction is critical.
  - *Alternative flow* = The use case still *achieves its goal* (e.g., user pays by card instead of cash).
  - *Exception flow* = The use case goal is *not* achieved (e.g., network failure during payment).
- **Special Requirements** capture *quality attributes* (NEP 2020 emphasizes this under cross-cutting concerns).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use Case Specification — Field Reference Sheet (Memorize for KTU ESE)**

| # | Section | Notation Convention | Mandatory? (KTU) | Common Mistake |
|:--:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Use Case Name | Verb–Noun (imperative) | ✅ | Using nouns only (e.g., "ATM Machine" ❌) |
| 2 | Use Case ID | UCxx or domain-prefix | ✅ | Reusing IDs across diagrams |
| 3 | Brief Description | 1–2 sentences | ✅ | Writing a paragraph (too verbose) |
| 4 | Primary Actor | Singular, named role | ✅ | Confusing actor with system role |
| 5 | Preconditions | State of system | ✅ | Describing actor's intent instead of system state |
| 6 | Postconditions | State of system *after success* | ✅ | Writing post-conditions for failed paths |
| 7 | Main Flow | Numbered, Actor/System tagged | ✅ | Mixing actor and system actions in one step |
| 8 | Alternative Flow | Refs main-flow step number | Optional | Duplicating the main flow |
| 9 | Exception Flow | Refs main-flow step number | ✅ | Listing happy paths as exceptions |
| 10 | Special Req. | NFRs (perf, security) | Optional | Omitted entirely |
| 11 | Assumptions | Open issues, deps | Optional | Confused with preconditions |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, a Use Case Specification is often maintained in a *requirements management tool* (e.g., IBM DOORS, Jama Connect, Azure DevOps). For each use case:
- **Test teams** write 1 test case per main-flow step and 1 per alternative/exception flow.
- **UI/UX teams** map each step to a screen wireframe.
- **Estimation teams** apply the **Use Case Points (UCP)** method:

$$UCP = (UUCW + UAW) \times TCF \times ECF$$

where $UUCW$ = Unadjusted Use Case Weight, $UAW$ = Unadjusted Actor Weight, $TCF$ = Technical Complexity Factor, $ECF$ = Environmental Complexity Factor. This formula is **KTU-relevant** and often appears in Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 The 6-Step Process for Writing a Use Case Specification (KTU Exam Recipe)

We will use the classic **ATM Withdrawal** example (the most frequently asked KTU scenario).

### **Step 1 — Identify the Goal**
The *goal* of a use case is the *observable value* the actor receives.
For ATM: *Withdraw cash successfully.*

### **Step 2 — Name the Use Case**
Use a **verb-noun** form. Use active voice.

> **UC01: Withdraw Cash**

### **Step 3 — Identify Actors**
- *Primary Actor:* **Customer** (initiates the use case).
- *Supporting Actor:* **Bank System** (validates PIN, dispenses money).

### **Step 4 — Define Pre/Post Conditions**

| Type | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Precondition** | Customer has a valid ATM card and is authenticated. |
| **Postcondition (Success)** | Cash is dispensed, account is debited, card is returned. |
| **Postcondition (Failure)** | Account state is unchanged; card is returned. |

### **Step 5 — Write the Main Flow (Happy Path)**

Each step must be either an **Actor action** or a **System response** — *never both in the same step*.

| Step | Actor | Action / System Response |
|:--:|---|---|
| 1 | Customer | Inserts ATM card into the card reader. |
| 2 | System | Reads the card and prompts for PIN. |
| 3 | Customer | Enters PIN. |
| 4 | System | Validates PIN with Bank System. |
| 5 | System | Displays account menu (Withdraw, Balance, Deposit). |
| 6 | Customer | Selects *Withdraw* and enters amount. |
| 7 | System | Verifies sufficient balance and daily withdrawal limit. |
| 8 | System | Debits the account via the Bank System. |
| 9 | System | Dispenses the requested cash. |
| 10 | System | Prints a transaction receipt. |
| 11 | System | Ejects the card. |
| 12 | Customer | Takes card and cash. |
| 13 | System | Returns to the welcome screen. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Insight:** Writing the main flow correctly is worth **≈ 4 to 5 marks** in a 14-mark question. Use a clear *two-column format* OR use explicit "Actor:" / "System:" prefixes in a single column. Both are accepted.

### **Step 6 — Write Alternative and Exception Flows**

#### **Alternative Flows (A-series — Goal still achieved)**

| ID | Trigger Step | Flow Description |
|---|---|---|
| **A1** | Step 6 | Customer chooses to check balance *before* withdrawing. |
| **A2** | Step 6 | Customer withdraws from a *savings* account instead of a *current* account. |
| **A3** | Step 4 | Customer has *multiple accounts*; System prompts for account selection. |

#### **Exception Flows (E-series — Goal NOT achieved)**

| ID | Trigger Step | Flow Description |
|---|---|---|
| **E1** | Step 3 | Customer enters **wrong PIN** 3 times → System **retains the card**, displays message, and terminates the use case. |
| **E2** | Step 7 | **Insufficient balance** → System displays "Insufficient Funds" and returns to step 5 (account menu). |
| **E3** | Step 7 | **Daily withdrawal limit exceeded** → System displays limit message and returns to step 5. |
| **E4** | Step 9 | **Cash dispenser empty** → System rolls back the debit, displays apology, and ejects the card. |
| **E5** | Step 11 | **Card not taken within 15 seconds** → System **retains the card** for security. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students often *omit exception flows* and only write the happy path. Exception flows carry **2 to 3 marks** on their own in the KTU marking scheme. Always include at least **2 exception flows** and **1 alternative flow** in your specification.

## 3.2 Worked Example — A Complete Specification Document

Below is the *full* Use Case Specification for **Withdraw Cash** in a form directly acceptable in KTU answer scripts.

---

### **Use Case Specification: UC01 — Withdraw Cash**

**1. Use Case ID:** UC01
**2. Use Case Name:** Withdraw Cash
**3. Brief Description:** Allows an authenticated ATM customer to withdraw a specified amount of cash from a linked bank account. The System validates the request, debits the account, and dispenses cash.
**4. Primary Actor:** Customer
**5. Supporting Actor(s):** Bank Authorization System
**6. Preconditions:**
   - P1. The Customer holds a valid, unexpired ATM card.
   - P2. The ATM is online and connected to the Bank System.
   - P3. The Customer's account is active and not blocked.

**7. Postconditions:**
   - PS1 (Success). Cash is dispensed, account balance is reduced, and a receipt is printed.
   - PF1 (Failure). The account balance is unchanged, and the card is returned to the Customer.

**8. Main Flow (Basic Flow):**

| Step | Actor / System | Action |
|:--:|---|---|
| 1 | Customer | Inserts the ATM card into the card reader. |
| 2 | System | Reads the card magnetic stripe / chip and requests PIN. |
| 3 | Customer | Enters the 4-digit PIN. |
| 4 | System | Authenticates the PIN via the Bank System. |
| 5 | System | Displays the account-type menu (Savings / Current). |
| 6 | Customer | Selects account type and chooses *Withdraw*. |
| 7 | Customer | Enters the withdrawal amount. |
| 8 | System | Validates the amount against available balance and daily limit. |
| 9 | System | Sends a debit instruction to the Bank System. |
| 10 | System | Confirms debit and dispenses the cash. |
| 11 | System | Prints the transaction receipt. |
| 12 | System | Ejects the card. |
| 13 | Customer | Removes the card and the cash. |

**9. Alternative Flows:**

- **A1 — Multiple Accounts (at step 5):** If the Customer has more than one account, the System displays the list of accounts. The Customer selects one, and the flow continues at step 6.
- **A2 — Balance Enquiry Before Withdrawal (at step 6):** If the Customer chooses *Balance Enquiry* first, the System displays the balance, prints a balance slip, and returns to step 5. The Customer may then select *Withdraw*.
- **A3 — Fast Cash Option (at step 6):** If the Customer chooses the *Fast Cash* shortcut, the System presents preset amounts (e.g., 500, 1000, 2000, 5000). The flow resumes at step 8 with the selected amount.

**10. Exception Flows:**

- **E1 — Invalid PIN (at step 3):** If the Customer enters a wrong PIN, the System displays "Invalid PIN" and allows a retry. After **3 consecutive failures**, the System **retains the card**, prints a hotline number, and terminates the use case.
- **E2 — Insufficient Funds (at step 8):** If the requested amount exceeds the available balance, the System displays "Insufficient Funds" and returns to step 5.
- **E3 — Daily Limit Exceeded (at step 8):** If the requested amount exceeds the remaining daily limit, the System displays the remaining limit and returns to step 7.
- **E4 — Cash Dispenser Fault (at step 10):** If the cash dispenser fails or has insufficient notes, the System **rolls back the debit transaction**, displays an apology, and ejects the card.
- **E5 — Card Not Collected (at step 12):** If the Customer does not remove the card within **15 seconds**, the System **retains the card** for security, beeps, and terminates the use case.

**11. Special Requirements:**

- **SR1 (Performance):** The complete transaction must finish within **30 seconds** under normal load.
- **SR2 (Security):** All PIN entries must be masked and encrypted end-to-end.
- **SR3 (Usability):** All user-facing messages must support English and the regional language (e.g., Malayalam).
- **SR4 (Availability):** The ATM service must be available 99.9% of the month, excluding scheduled maintenance.

**12. Assumptions / Notes:**

- **AN1:** Network latency between ATM and Bank System is assumed to be under 2 seconds.
- **AN2:** The Bank System handles concurrent transactions with ACID guarantees.

---

This single document — when reproduced in the KTU exam — typically fetches **full marks (14/14)** if the diagram (6 marks) is also drawn correctly. For the diagram, you would draw a *Use Case Diagram* showing the *Customer* and *Bank System* as actors, the oval *Withdraw Cash*, and the *include* / *extend* relationships (e.g., *Validate PIN* ⇐≪include≫ *Withdraw Cash*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Visualizing the Use Case Specification as a Hierarchical Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **internal block architecture** of a Use Case Specification — how each section of the specification document is structured and what each block contains. This is a *functional architecture flow*, not a UML use case diagram itself.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UCS Root Block: Use Case Specification UC01] --> B[Identification Block]
    A --> C[Actor Block]
    A --> D[State Block]
    A --> E[Flow Block]
    A --> F[Quality Block]
    A --> G[Meta Block]

    B --> B1[Use Case ID]
    B --> B2[Use Case Name]
    B --> B3[Brief Description]

    C --> C1[Primary Actor]
    C --> C2[Supporting Actor]

    D --> D1[Preconditions]
    D --> D2[Postconditions Success]
    D --> D3[Postconditions Failure]

    E --> E1[Main Flow Numbered Steps]
    E --> E2[Alternative Flows A1 A2 A3]
    E --> E3[Exception Flows E1 E2 E3 E4 E5]

    F --> F1[Performance NFR]
    F --> F2[Security NFR]
    F --> F3[Usability NFR]
    F --> F4[Availability NFR]

    G --> G1[Assumptions]
    G --> G2[Open Issues]
    G --> G3[Traceability Links to Test Cases]

    E1 -.-> H[Atomic Step: Actor Action OR System Response]
    E1 -.-> I[Sequential Numbering 1 to N]
    E2 -.-> J[References Main Flow Step Number]
    E3 -.-> K[References Main Flow Step Number]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — How the Use Case Specification Drives Downstream Artifacts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UCS[Use Case Specification] --> TS[Test Specification]
    UCS --> UI[UI Wireframes]
    UCS --> DS[Design Sequence Diagrams]
    UCS --> EST[Effort Estimation UCP]

    TS --> TS1[1 Test Case per Main Flow Step]
    TS --> TS2[1 Test Case per Alternative Flow]
    TS --> TS3[1 Test Case per Exception Flow]

    EST --> EST1[Count Actors UAW]
    EST --> EST2[Count Use Cases UUCW]
    EST --> EST3[Apply TCF and ECF]
```

## 4.3 Relationship Map — How a Use Case Specification Fits in the UML 2.5 Artifact Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RR[Requirements Document] --> UCD[Use Case Diagram]
    UCD --> UCS1[Use Case Spec UC01]
    UCD --> UCS2[Use Case Spec UC02]
    UCD --> UCS3[Use Case Spec UC03]

    UCS1 --> CL1[Class Diagram]
    UCS1 --> SD1[Sequence Diagram]
    UCS1 --> AC1[Activity Diagram]
    UCS1 --> SM1[State Machine Diagram]

    UCS2 --> CL2[Class Diagram]
    UCS2 --> SD2[Sequence Diagram]

    UCS3 --> CL3[Class Diagram]
    UCS3 --> SD3[Sequence Diagram]
    UCS3 --> AC3[Activity Diagram]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each Use Case Specification is the *parent* of one or more *design diagrams* (Class, Sequence, Activity, State Machine). In KTU Module 2 and beyond, you will learn to derive these from the Use Case Specification. This traceability is a **key outcome** under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Use Case Specification. List any FOUR sections that a Use Case Specification must contain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Use Case Specification is a structured textual document that fully describes the behavior of a single use case. It captures the actor(s), pre/post conditions, main flow, alternative flows, and exception flows required to achieve a specific user goal.

> **Any FOUR sections (4 × 0.375 ≈ 1.5 marks):**
> 1. Use Case Name and ID
> 2. Primary and Supporting Actors
> 3. Preconditions and Postconditions
> 4. Main Flow (Basic Flow)
> 5. Alternative Flows
> 6. Exception Flows
> 7. Special / Non-Functional Requirements

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
**Differentiate between an *Alternative Flow* and an *Exception Flow* in a Use Case Specification. Give one example of each from an ATM system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Alternative Flow | Exception Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal Achieved?** | Yes — the use case *still* succeeds | No — the use case *fails* to achieve its goal |
| **Trigger** | A different but valid user choice or system state | An error, fault, or invalid input |
| **Example (ATM)** | Customer uses *Fast Cash* shortcut to withdraw a preset amount (UC01-A3) | Customer enters wrong PIN 3 times → card is retained (UC01-E1) |

*(Award 1.5 marks for the differentiation table and 1.5 marks for the correct examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

> **(a)** Draw a **Use Case Diagram** for an **Online Book Shopping System**. The system should support the following functionalities:
> - Customer browses the catalogue, adds items to a cart, and places an order.
> - Customer makes payment (via Credit Card, UPI, or Net Banking).
> - System validates the payment with the Bank.
> - Customer can also *cancel* an order *only if* it is still in *Pending* state.
> - Admin manages the book inventory and order status.
> - The *Place Order* use case *includes* the *Make Payment* use case, and the *Cancel Order* use case *extends* the *Place Order* use case under a specific condition.
>
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Write a complete **Use Case Specification** for the *Place Order* use case in the same system. Include preconditions, postconditions, main flow, at least TWO alternative flows, and at least THREE exception flows.
>
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Use Case Diagram (7 marks)**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Actors
    Cust((Customer))
    Adm((Admin))
    Bank((Bank System))

    %% System boundary
    subgraph SYS [Online Book Shopping System]
        direction TB
        UC1((Browse Catalogue))
        UC2((Add to Cart))
        UC3((Place Order))
        UC4((Make Payment))
        UC5((Cancel Order))
        UC6((Manage Inventory))
        UC7((Update Order Status))
    end

    %% Primary associations
    Cust --- UC1
    Cust --- UC2
    Cust --- UC3
    Cust --- UC5

    Adm --- UC6
    Adm --- UC7

    %% Include and Extend
    UC3 -.->|≪include≫| UC4
    UC5 -.->|≪extend≫| UC3

    %% Supporting actor
    UC4 --- Bank
```

**Mark Allocation (KTU Examiner's Scheme):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|:---:|
| Correct identification of *actors* (Customer, Admin, Bank System) | 1.5 |
| Correct identification of *use cases* (7 ovals) | 2.0 |
| Correct *associations* between actors and use cases | 1.0 |
| Correct *include* relationship (Place Order ⇐≪include≫ Make Payment) | 1.5 |
| Correct *extend* relationship (Cancel Order ⇐≪extend≫ Place Order) | 1.0 |
| **Total** | **7.0** |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Use Case Specification: Place Order (7 marks)**

**1. Use Case ID:** UC03
**2. Use Case Name:** Place Order
**3. Brief Description:** Allows a Customer to place an order for one or more books in the cart. The System calculates the total, processes payment via the Bank, and confirms the order.
**4. Primary Actor:** Customer
**5. Supporting Actor(s):** Bank Payment Gateway (for payment validation)
**6. Preconditions:**
   - P1. The Customer is logged in.
   - P2. The cart contains at least one book with stock > 0.
   - P3. The Customer has selected a shipping address.
**7. Postconditions:**
   - PS1 (Success). The order is confirmed, inventory is reduced, and a confirmation email is sent.
   - PF1 (Failure). The cart contents and inventory are unchanged.

**8. Main Flow:**

| Step | Actor / System | Action |
|:--:|---|---|
| 1 | Customer | Clicks *Checkout* on the cart page. |
| 2 | System | Displays the order summary, shipping address, and total amount. |
| 3 | Customer | Confirms the order and selects a payment method. |
| 4 | System | Includes *Make Payment* (UC04). |
| 5 | System | Receives payment confirmation from the Bank. |
| 6 | System | Creates the order with status = *Confirmed*. |
| 7 | System | Reduces the inventory count for each book. |
| 8 | System | Sends an order confirmation email to the Customer. |
| 9 | System | Empties the cart and displays the order tracking page. |

**9. Alternative Flows:**

- **A1 — Apply Coupon (at step 3):** The Customer may enter a valid coupon code. The System validates the coupon, recalculates the total, and returns to step 2.
- **A2 — Save Address (at step 2):** If the Customer adds a *new* shipping address, the System saves it to the profile and continues at step 2 with the updated address.
- **A3 — Cash on Delivery (at step 3):** If the Customer selects *COD*, the System skips payment and continues at step 6 with status = *Pending Payment*. The goal of the use case is still achieved (order placed).

**10. Exception Flows:**

- **E1 — Empty Cart (at step 1):** If the cart is empty, the System displays "Your cart is empty" and terminates the use case.
- **E2 — Payment Failure (at step 5):** If the Bank rejects the payment (insufficient funds, gateway timeout, or invalid card), the System displays the failure reason, retains the cart, and returns to step 3.
- **E3 — Out of Stock (at step 7):** If any book is out of stock during inventory reduction, the System rolls back the order, displays "Item unavailable", and returns to step 1.
- **E4 — Session Timeout (at step 3):** If the Customer is inactive for 15 minutes, the System logs them out, saves the cart, and terminates the use case.

**11. Special Requirements:**
   - **SR1:** Payment must be processed within 10 seconds.
   - **SR2:** All payment data must comply with **PCI-DSS** standards.
   - **SR3:** Order confirmation email must be delivered within 2 minutes.

**12. Assumptions:**
   - **AN1:** The Bank Payment Gateway is assumed to be available 99.5% of the time.
   - **AN2:** Inventory is updated atomically to prevent overselling.

**Mark Allocation (KTU Examiner's Scheme):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|:---:|
| Correct *identification block* (ID, name, description) | 1.0 |
| Correct *actors* | 0.5 |
| *Preconditions* and *postconditions* | 1.0 |
| *Main flow* with clear Actor/System separation | 2.0 |
| At least *2 alternative flows* | 1.0 |
| At least *3 exception flows* | 1.0 |
| Special requirements / assumptions | 0.5 |
| **Total** | **7.0** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Draw a **Use Case Diagram** for a **Hospital Management System (HMS)**. The system must support:
> - Patient registers and books an appointment with a Doctor.
> - Doctor views the patient's medical history and updates the prescription.
> - Receptionist generates the bill.
> - The *Generate Bill* use case *includes* the *View Treatment Details* use case.
> - The *Cancel Appointment* use case *extends* the *Book Appointment* use case *only if* the appointment is more than 24 hours away.
>
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Write the complete **Use Case Specification** for *Book Appointment*. Include preconditions, postconditions, main flow, two alternative flows, and three exception flows.
>
> **(7 marks)**

*(For brevity, the *book appointment* specification for the Hospital Management System follows the SAME template as in Q-A(b). The examiner expects: Preconditions — patient is registered, doctor is available; Main Flow — login → select doctor → select slot → confirm → receive confirmation; Alternative Flows — reschedule, choose teleconsultation; Exception Flows — slot unavailable, doctor on leave, payment failure for paid consultations.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Use Case Specification Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing Actor and System actions in the same numbered step** (e.g., "System prompts user and user enters data" in step 3). *Always split: step 3a = System prompts; step 3b = User enters.*
> 2. **Writing preconditions that are actually assumptions** (e.g., "Internet is available" is an *assumption*, not a precondition; "Patient is logged in" *is* a precondition).
> 3. **Confusing postconditions** — Writing postconditions for failed paths as if they were successful. Use separate *PS1 (Success)* and *PF1 (Failure)* labels.
> 4. **Omitting the Use Case Diagram** in a combined question. A 14-mark question with "draw + write" expects both — drawing only fetches ~6 marks, writing only fetches ~8 marks.
> 5. **Using nouns instead of verb-noun for the use case name** (e.g., "Appointment" ❌ vs. *"Book Appointment"* ✅). The use case *name* must be a *goal*, not a *thing*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ A **Use Case Specification** is a *text document*, not a diagram. It elaborates a *single* use case from the Use Case Diagram.
- ✅ Mandatory sections: **ID, Name, Brief Description, Primary Actor, Preconditions, Postconditions, Main Flow, Alternative Flows, Exception Flows.**
- ✅ Use the **Verb–Noun** convention for naming (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*, *Place Order*, *Book Appointment*).
- ✅ Each step in the main flow is **atomic** — performed by *either* the Actor *or* the System, never both.
- ✅ **Preconditions** describe the system state *before* the use case starts; **Postconditions** describe the state *after success* AND *after failure* (use separate labels).
- ✅ **Alternative Flow = Goal achieved** (e.g., user pays by UPI instead of card).
- ✅ **Exception Flow = Goal NOT achieved** (e.g., payment gateway timeout → order rolled back).
- ✅ **≪include≫** is used when one use case *always* needs another (mandatory reuse). **≪extend≫** is used when a use case *optionally* adds behavior under a specific condition.
- ✅ The Use Case Specification is the *parent* of *Class*, *Sequence*, *Activity*, and *State Machine* diagrams in later modules.
- ✅ For KTU's 14-mark combined questions, allocate approximately **6 marks for the diagram and 8 marks for the specification** (split between flows, pre/post conditions, and alternative/exception flows).
- ✅ Always include **at least 2 alternative flows and 3 exception flows** to fetch full marks in the specification section.
- ✅ Special Requirements capture **NFRs** (performance, security, usability, availability) — NEP 2020 emphasizes these.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Use Case Specification Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PECGT411, and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actor Generalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actor Generalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Unified Modeling Language (UML), **Actor Generalization** is a directed relationship between two actors (a generalization set) in which one actor (the **child** or **specialized actor**) inherits the roles, permissions, identity, and use-case associations of another actor (the **parent** or **generalized actor**). 

It is a direct application of the Generalization relationship (a foundational UML classifier-level relationship) applied specifically to actors participating in a **Use Case Diagram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:**
> *Actor Generalization arranges actors in a hierarchy where the child actor inherits the use-case associations and behavioural semantics of its parent actor. The child may participate in every use case the parent participates in, and may additionally participate in specialized use cases unique to itself.*

### 1.2 UML Notation Standard

The UML 2.5 specification mandates the following visual notation:

- A **solid directed line**
- Terminating in a **hollow (open) triangular arrowhead**
- The arrowhead points **from the child actor toward the parent actor**
- The line contains **no labels** (labels are optional and rarely used in KTU answers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identification Rule:** The triangle arrowhead ALWAYS points toward the **more general (parent) actor**. If you remember this single rule, you can never draw the relationship backwards in your KTU answer scripts.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Job Role Hierarchy in a Hospital:**

Imagine a hospital organization chart. Every employee has an **ID card** that allows them to enter the building. Doctors have an additional ID card that allows them to enter the **Operation Theatre**. Senior Surgeons have yet another card that allows them to enter the **Cardiac Surgery Suite**.

Here:
- **Employee** = Generalized (parent) actor — can access common zones.
- **Doctor** = Specialized (child) actor — inherits Employee's access PLUS Doctor-specific zones.
- **Senior Surgeon** = Further specialized (grandchild) actor — inherits everything above PLUS Cardiac Surgery access.

You do not rewrite the access list from scratch for each role. The child automatically **inherits** all permissions of the parent. Actor Generalization works the exact same way — the child actor automatically participates in every use case of the parent.

**Analogy 2 — The Mobile Banking App:**

A **User** can log in and check balance. A **Registered User** inherits login and balance-check, but can additionally transfer funds. A **Premium User** inherits everything Registered User can do, plus request priority customer support.

**Registered User → User** and **Premium User → Registered User** are both Actor Generalization relationships.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** *"Children inherit the parent's powers, and the arrow points TO the parent (who gives the powers)."*

### 1.4 Why Actor Generalization Matters — The Engineering Motivation

| Engineering Concern | How Actor Generalization Solves It |
|---|---|
| Redundancy in use-case diagrams | Eliminates duplicate association lines from child to parent use cases |
| Maintainability | Changes to the parent's use cases automatically reflect on children |
| Semantic clarity | Expresses real-world role hierarchies (e.g., Manager IS-A Employee) |
| Polymorphism support | The system treats the child actor anywhere the parent actor is expected |
| Real-world modelling | Mirrors actual organizational/inheritance structures in the domain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules of Actor Generalization (Bullet Logic)

The following are the binding rules every KTU examiner expects you to know and apply:

1. **Directionality Rule:** The hollow triangle arrowhead points FROM child TO parent. Never reverse this.
2. **Inheritance of Associations:** A child actor automatically participates in **every** use case the parent participates in, through the transitive inheritance property of UML generalization.
3. **Selective Overriding Allowed:** A child actor may have its own additional associations to other use cases that the parent does not participate in. This represents role-specific behaviour.
4. **No Cycle Rule:** The generalization hierarchy must be a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. You cannot create circular inheritance (A inherits B, B inherits A) among actors.
5. **Multiple Inheritance Permitted:** A single actor may have **multiple parents** (multiple generalizations), inheriting from all of them. This is called multiple inheritance.
6. **Inheritance of Identity:** The child actor is-a kind-of the parent actor (IS-A relationship). A `PremiumCustomer` IS-A `Customer`. This is the substitution principle — anywhere `Customer` is required, `PremiumCustomer` may be substituted.
7. **Notation Specificity:** Only actors may participate as endpoints of an actor generalization. You cannot draw a generalization line directly from a use case to an actor (that would be association or include/extend).
8. **Independence of Use Case Generalization:** Actor generalization and Use Case generalization are **independent** constructs in UML. They use similar visual notation but model entirely different concepts.

## 2.2 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every definitional, syntactic, and semantic rule required to answer any KTU question on Actor Generalization. Memorise this table before the examination.

| **Element / Property** | **Specification in UML 2.5** | **Notes for KTU Answers** |
|---|---|---|
| Relationship Name | Generalization (applied to actors) | Sometimes called "Actor Inheritance" in textbooks |
| Visual Notation | Solid line + hollow triangular arrowhead | Arrow points to the parent |
| Direction | Child → Parent | Always this direction |
| UML Stereotype (optional) | `<<actor>>` on parent node | Rarely required in KTU answers |
| Multiplicity | Implicit 1 (each actor is one entity) | No multiplicity shown on actor generalization |
| Inherited Associations | All parent use-case associations | Implicit, not redrawn |
| Allowed Arity of Parents | One or more (multiple inheritance) | Use multiple lines fanning into the parent |
| Cyclic Hierarchies | Prohibited | Cycles are UML-invalid |
| Diagram Type | Used Case Diagram only | Cannot appear in Class or Sequence Diagrams as actor gen. |
| Distinct From | Use Case Generalization | Different semantics; do NOT confuse in answers |
| Substitution Principle (LSP) | Child may replace parent anywhere | Refer to Liskov Substitution Principle if asked |
| Visibility Modifiers | Not applicable to actor generalization | Do not put `+` or `-` on actor generalization arrows |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Shorthand for Drawing in Exam:**
> In answer scripts you may simply write *"a solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead pointing from the child to the parent actor."* This textual description earns full marks when a hand-drawn diagram is impossible.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Actor Generalization is widely deployed in the following practical engineering contexts:

- **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems:** Modelling hierarchical user roles — `Employee → Manager → SeniorManager`.
- **E-Commerce platforms:** Modelling customer tiers — `Customer → RegisteredCustomer → PremiumCustomer`.
- **Healthcare information systems:** Modelling staff permissions — `Staff → Doctor → Surgeon`.
- **Academic portals (relevant to KTU itself):** Modelling user types — `User → Student → PostgraduateStudent`.
- **Banking software:** Modelling account holders — `AccountHolder → JointAccountHolder → PrimaryAccountHolder`.

In each case, the modelling choice reduces the diagram's complexity, prevents redundant associations, and accurately reflects the underlying business-rule hierarchy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure to Identify and Model Actor Generalization

The following is the exhaustive, KTU-recommended methodology for identifying whether an Actor Generalization relationship exists and for drawing it correctly.

### Step 1 — Enumerate All Actors in the System

List every external entity (human, hardware device, or external software system) that interacts with the system under design. Each becomes a candidate actor.

### Step 2 — Identify Roles and Capabilities

For each actor, write down:
- The use cases it directly participates in (its association list).
- The real-world role it represents (e.g., customer, administrator).

### Step 3 — Detect Role Subsumption (IS-A Test)

For every pair of actors (A, B), apply the following logical test:

$$
\text{ActorGeneralization}(B, A) \iff B \text{ IS-A } A \text{ AND } \text{UseCases}(A) \subseteq \text{UseCases}(B)
$$

Where:
- $B$ is the candidate child (specialized) actor.
- $A$ is the candidate parent (generalized) actor.
- $\text{UseCases}(X)$ denotes the set of use cases actor $X$ is associated with.

**Translation in plain English:** "Actor B should inherit from Actor A if and only if B is a kind of A, and every use case A can do, B can also do (possibly more)."

### Step 4 — Draw the Relationship

- Draw a solid line.
- Attach a hollow triangular arrowhead at the parent end.
- The line emerges from the child actor.

### Step 5 — Eliminate Redundant Associations

Once the generalization is in place, **remove** any redundant direct association lines between the child and use cases already associated with the parent. The inheritance takes care of them implicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important for KTU Valuation:** Examiners actively look for whether you have drawn redundant associations. If you draw both the inheritance AND direct association lines to the same use case, you will be marked down for redundancy.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Online Shopping System

**Given System:** An e-commerce portal with the following actors and use cases.

**Actors Identified:**
1. `Guest` — visits the site, browses, adds items to cart, checks out.
2. `RegisteredCustomer` — does everything Guest can do, plus views order history, manages wishlist, writes reviews.
3. `PremiumCustomer` — does everything RegisteredCustomer can do, plus accesses exclusive deals, uses priority support chat.

**Use Cases Identified:**
- UC1: Browse Products
- UC2: Add to Cart
- UC3: Checkout
- UC4: View Order History
- UC5: Manage Wishlist
- UC6: Write Product Review
- UC7: Access Exclusive Deals
- UC8: Priority Support Chat

### Step 1 — Build the Association Matrix

The matrix below (with the pipe symbol replaced by `\vert` per protocol) expresses actor-to-use-case participation:

| Actor | UC1 Browse | UC2 Add to Cart | UC3 Checkout | UC4 View History | UC5 Wishlist | UC6 Review | UC7 Exclusive Deals | UC8 Priority Chat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guest | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| RegisteredCustomer | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| PremiumCustomer | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

### Step 2 — Apply the IS-A Test

**Test 1: Does Guest IS-A RegisteredCustomer?** No — Guest is NOT a kind of RegisteredCustomer; they are distinct categories. **No generalization.**

**Test 2: Does RegisteredCustomer IS-A Guest?** Conceptually, a registered customer is a customer who has logged in, but they are NOT the same as a Guest in business terms. **In this domain, no generalization between them** (though in some systems you might model it the other way).

> **Reformulation (more natural for e-commerce):** All three actors are kinds of `Customer`.

**Test 3: Does RegisteredCustomer IS-A Customer?** Yes. Every use case Customer can do, RegisteredCustomer can also do (browse, cart, checkout). **Generalization exists.**

**Test 4: Does PremiumCustomer IS-A RegisteredCustomer?** Yes. Every use case RegisteredCustomer can do, PremiumCustomer can also do. **Generalization exists.**

### Step 3 — Final Hierarchy

$$
\text{PremiumCustomer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RegisteredCustomer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Customer}
$$

### Step 4 — Refined Use-Case Association Matrix (after applying inheritance)

After drawing the two generalization arrows, the diagram only needs to show the **differential** use-case associations explicitly. The inherited ones are implicit.

| Actor | Explicitly Associated Use Cases | Implicitly Inherited From |
|---|---|---|
| Customer | Browse, Add to Cart, Checkout | (none — this is the root) |
| RegisteredCustomer | View Order History, Manage Wishlist, Write Review | Browse, Add to Cart, Checkout (from Customer) |
| PremiumCustomer | Access Exclusive Deals, Priority Support Chat | All 6 use cases from RegisteredCustomer → Customer |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In the KTU answer sheet, you may either (a) draw the inheritance arrows AND omit the redundant associations to parent use cases, or (b) draw all associations explicitly without inheritance arrows for clarity. Either earns full marks, but option (a) is considered the more sophisticated UML approach.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use vs Not Use Actor Generalization

| Scenario | Use Actor Generalization? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Two actors share most use cases but one has additional roles | **Yes** | Reduces redundancy, models IS-A relationship |
| Two actors are completely unrelated | **No** | No IS-A relationship exists |
| Two actors share some use cases but neither is a kind of the other | **No** | Use simple associations instead; do not force inheritance |
| Multiple actors share one common use case (e.g., Login) | **Optional** | A common parent actor can be created to absorb the shared association |
| A child actor has all parent use cases PLUS additional ones | **Yes** | Perfect generalization scenario |
| Both actors have entirely distinct use cases | **No** | No semantic overlap to inherit |

## 3.4 Multiple Inheritance — Advanced Concept

A single actor may inherit from **multiple parents**. The notation is multiple solid lines, each with a hollow triangular arrowhead, fanning out from the child to each parent.

**Example:** In a university system, a `TeachingAssistant` actor is both a `Student` and a `FacultyMember`. Therefore:

$$
\text{TeachingAssistant} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student}
$$
$$
