\text{TeachingAssistant} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{FacultyMember}
$$

The `TeachingAssistant` inherits all use cases from BOTH parents — viewing grades (Student) AND submitting grades (FacultyMember).

> [!WARNING]
> **Constraint:** UML permits multiple inheritance among actors, but **diamond inheritance** (where two parents share a common grandparent with conflicting semantics) is allowed only if no semantic conflict arises. KTU questions typically avoid this complexity but be aware of it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Diagram Showing Actor Generalization Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram reproduces a UML Use Case Diagram demonstrating the Actor Generalization relationships derived in Section 3.2.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    direction TB

    subgraph hierarchy["ACTOR GENERALIZATION HIERARCHY"]
        direction LR
        customerNode["PARENT ACTOR  CUSTOMER"]:::parentActor
        regCustNode["CHILD ACTOR  REGISTERED CUSTOMER"]:::childActor
        premCustNode["GRANDCHILD ACTOR  PREMIUM CUSTOMER"]:::grandActor
    end

    customerNode -- "UML hollow triangle arrow" --> regCustNode
    regCustNode -- "UML hollow triangle arrow" --> premCustNode

    subgraph system["SYSTEM BOUNDARY  ONLINE SHOPPING SYSTEM"]
        uc1(["UC1 BROWSE PRODUCTS"]):::useCase
        uc2(["UC2 ADD TO CART"]):::useCase
        uc3(["UC3 CHECKOUT"]):::useCase
        uc4(["UC4 VIEW ORDER HISTORY"]):::useCase
        uc5(["UC5 MANAGE WISHLIST"]):::useCase
        uc6(["UC6 WRITE REVIEW"]):::useCase
        uc7(["UC7 ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DEALS"]):::useCase
        uc8(["UC8 PRIORITY SUPPORT CHAT"]):::useCase
    end

    customerNode --- uc1
    customerNode --- uc2
    customerNode --- uc3

    regCustNode --- uc4
    regCustNode --- uc5
    regCustNode --- uc6

    premCustNode --- uc7
    premCustNode --- uc8

    classDef parentActor fill:#FFD9A8,stroke:#B85C00,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
    classDef childActor fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef grandActor fill:#D4F4D4,stroke:#2E8B2E,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef useCase fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Reading Guide for the Diagram Above

1. The **solid lines with text "UML hollow triangle arrow"** between the actors represent the actor generalization arrows (the hollow triangle cannot be drawn in Mermaid but the relationship is labelled).
2. The arrow direction is **from the more specialized (lower) actor to the more generalized (upper) actor**.
3. The plain lines between actors and use cases inside the system boundary are **association lines** (not generalization).
4. The diagram shows only the **differential associations** — each actor is linked to use cases specific to its own tier, while inheriting the upper-tier use cases automatically.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multiple Inheritance Scenario

The following diagram models a **University Information System** where the `TeachingAssistant` actor inherits from two distinct parents (`Student` and `FacultyMember`), demonstrating multiple inheritance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph parents["PARENT ACTORS"]
        parentA["STUDENT"]:::parentActor
        parentB["FACULTY MEMBER"]:::parentActor
    end

    subgraph child["CHILD ACTOR  MULTIPLE INHERITANCE"]
        childNode["TEACHING ASSISTANT"]:::childActor
    end

    parentA -- "hollow triangle arrow" --> childNode
    parentB -- "hollow triangle arrow" --> childNode

    subgraph uniSystem["UNIVERSITY INFORMATION SYSTEM"]
        ucA(["VIEW OWN GRADES"]):::useCase
        ucB(["REGISTER FOR COURSES"]):::useCase
        ucC(["SUBMIT STUDENT GRADES"]):::useCase
        ucD(["UPLOAD COURSE MATERIAL"]):::useCase
        ucE(["CONDUCT TUTORIAL SESSION"]):::useCase
    end

    parentA --- ucA
    parentA --- ucB
    parentB --- ucC
    parentB --- ucD
    childNode --- ucE

    classDef parentActor fill:#FFD9A8,stroke:#B85C00,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
    classDef childActor fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef useCase fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Architecture Insight

`TeachingAssistant` is a hybrid role. The diagram conveys that:
- By being a `Student`, it can view its own grades and register for courses.
- By being a `FacultyMember`, it can submit student grades and upload course material.
- As a `TeachingAssistant` specifically, it has its own unique use case — conducting tutorial sessions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Hint:** When drawing by hand in an exam, use a ruler to draw the inheritance lines and draw the hollow triangle by leaving the arrowhead unfilled (unlike the solid black arrowhead used for association arrows). This visual distinction is what examiners look for.

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Actor Generalization vs Use Case Generalization

A common KTU question asks students to distinguish between these two. The schematic below contrasts them visually.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["SCENARIO A  ACTOR GENERALIZATION"]
        direction LR
        ag_parent["PARENT ACTOR  CUSTOMER"]:::parentActor
        ag_child["CHILD ACTOR  PREMIUM CUSTOMER"]:::childActor
        ag_parent -- "hollow triangle  child to parent" --> ag_child
    end

    subgraph B["SCENARIO B  USE CASE GENERALIZATION"]
        direction LR
        ug_parent(["PARENT USE CASE  PROCESS PAYMENT"]):::parentUseCase
        ug_child1(["CHILD USE CASE  CREDIT CARD PAYMENT"]):::childUseCase
        ug_child2(["CHILD USE CASE  UPI PAYMENT"]):::childUseCase
        ug_parent -- "hollow triangle  child to parent" --> ug_child1
        ug_parent -- "hollow triangle  child to parent" --> ug_child2
    end

    classDef parentActor fill:#FFD9A8,stroke:#B85C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef childActor fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef parentUseCase fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef childUseCase fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Key Difference Visible in the Schematic

| Aspect | Actor Generalization | Use Case Generalization |
|---|---|---|
| Endpoints | Two actors | Two use cases |
| Endpoints labelled as | Stick figures or rectangles with `<<actor>>` | Ellipses |
| Semantic Meaning | Child actor IS-A parent actor | Child use case IS-A kind-of parent use case |
| What is inherited | Use-case associations | Behavioural steps of the parent use case |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define actor generalization. How is it represented in a UML use case diagram? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Actor generalization is a UML relationship between two actors in which the child actor (specialized actor) inherits the use-case associations and identity of the parent actor (generalized actor). It represents an "IS-A" relationship — the child actor is a kind-of the parent actor. In a UML use case diagram, it is represented by a **solid directed line terminating in a hollow (open) triangular arrowhead**, with the arrowhead pointing **from the child actor toward the parent actor**. Through this relationship, the child actor automatically participates in every use case the parent actor participates in, without those associations needing to be redrawn.

*(Word count: ~95 words. Within the 3-mark answer length.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any two benefits of using actor generalization in a use case diagram. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Two key benefits of using actor generalization are:

1. **Reduction of Redundancy:** It eliminates the need to draw multiple identical association lines from the child actor to the use cases already associated with the parent actor, since the child inherits them automatically.

2. **Improved Maintainability and Semantic Clarity:** Actor generalization accurately models real-world role hierarchies (e.g., `PremiumCustomer` IS-A `Customer`). When the parent actor's use-case associations are modified, all child actors automatically reflect the change, reducing maintenance effort and making the diagram semantically faithful to the domain.

*(Additional acceptable benefits: support for polymorphism/Liskov Substitution Principle, cleaner diagrams, easier extension via further specialization.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Consider an **Online Banking System** with the following actors: `Customer`, `PremiumCustomer`, `JointAccountHolder`, and `Administrator`. The use cases include: `View Balance`, `Transfer Funds`, `Apply for Loan`, `Request Priority Support`, `Manage User Accounts`, and `View Audit Logs`.

Draw a complete use case diagram showing all appropriate actor generalization relationships, and justify each generalization. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the IS-A Test to Each Pair:**

- *Is `PremiumCustomer` IS-A `Customer`?* **Yes.** A premium customer is still fundamentally a customer. So `PremiumCustomer → Customer` (Generalization).
- *Is `JointAccountHolder` IS-A `Customer`?* **Yes.* JointAccountHolders are still customers of the bank. So `JointAccountHolder → Customer` (Generalization).
- *Is `Administrator` IS-A `Customer`?* **No.** An administrator manages the system but is not necessarily a customer. **No generalization.**

**Step 2 — Assign Use Cases to Actors:**

- `Customer`: View Balance, Transfer Funds
- `PremiumCustomer` (specialized): Apply for Loan, Request Priority Support *(inherits View Balance and Transfer Funds from Customer)*
- `JointAccountHolder` (specialized): Apply for Loan *(inherits View Balance and Transfer Funds from Customer)*
- `Administrator`: Manage User Accounts, View Audit Logs

**Step 3 — Draw the Diagram (textual description for hand-drawn answers):**

Place `Customer` at the top of the actor hierarchy. Below it, place `PremiumCustomer` and `JointAccountHolder` side-by-side, each connected to `Customer` by a solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead pointing upward. Place `Administrator` separately (no generalization). Inside the system boundary, place the six use cases as ellipses. Connect `Customer` to View Balance and Transfer Funds. Connect `PremiumCustomer` to Apply for Loan and Request Priority Support. Connect `JointAccountHolder` to Apply for Loan. Connect `Administrator` to Manage User Accounts and View Audit Logs.

**Justification:**

The two generalizations are valid because:
- `PremiumCustomer` represents a specialized tier of customer with additional privileges, satisfying the IS-A test.
- `JointAccountHolder` represents a multi-user variant of a customer, also satisfying the IS-A test.

`(7 Marks — 3 for correct diagram, 2 for IS-A test application, 2 for justification)`

---

**Q: (b)** Differentiate between **actor generalization** and **use case generalization**. State the UML notation for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Basis of Comparison | Actor Generalization | Use Case Generalization |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Relationship between two actors where child inherits parent's use-case associations | Relationship between two use cases where child inherits behavioural steps of parent |
| Endpoints | Two actors | Two use cases |
| UML Notation | Solid line + hollow triangle arrowhead, pointing from child actor to parent actor | Solid line + hollow triangle arrowhead, pointing from child use case to parent use case |
| Inherits | Use-case associations (which use cases the actor participates in) | Behavioural sequence / structure of the parent use case |
| Semantic Meaning | "Child actor IS-A parent actor" | "Child use case IS-A kind-of parent use case" |
| Purpose | Models role hierarchies (e.g., Manager IS-A Employee) | Models behavioural specializations (e.g., CreditCardPayment IS-A Payment) |
| Example | `PremiumCustomer → Customer` | `CreditCardPayment → MakePayment` |

**Key Point:** Although the visual notation (solid line + hollow triangle) is identical for both relationships, the diagram context (which element is at the endpoints) determines whether it represents actor generalization or use case generalization.

`(7 Marks — 4 for tabulated differentiation, 2 for notation statement, 1 for the key point)`

---

### Question B — Set 2 (Alternative to Question A, 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** For a **University Library Management System**, the actors identified are: `Student`, `ResearchScholar`, `Faculty`, `LibraryStaff`, and `ExternalVisitor`. The use cases include: `Search Catalog`, `Borrow Book`, `Return Book`, `Reserve Book`, `Access Digital Archives`, `Recommend Book for Purchase`, `Issue Library Card`, and `Manage Library Inventory`.

Identify the actor generalization hierarchy, draw the diagram, and explain the multiple inheritance scenario if applicable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the IS-A Test:**

- *Is `ResearchScholar` IS-A `Student`?* **Yes.** A Research Scholar is an advanced student category. `ResearchScholar → Student` (Generalization).
- *Is `Faculty` IS-A `Student`?* **No.** Faculty are staff members, not students. **No generalization between Faculty and Student.**
- *Is `ExternalVisitor` IS-A `Student`?* **No.** External visitors are not enrolled students. **No generalization.**
- *Is `LibraryStaff` IS-A `Student`?* **No.** Library staff are employees. **No generalization.**

**Step 2 — Multiple Inheritance Analysis:**

There is **no multiple inheritance** scenario in this problem because no single actor inherits from two distinct parents simultaneously. (To create one, we would need an actor like `ResearchScholarAndFaculty` which would inherit from both `ResearchScholar` and `Faculty` — but this is not present in the given list.)

**Step 3 — Use Case Assignment:**

- `Student`: Search Catalog, Borrow Book, Return Book, Reserve Book
- `ResearchScholar` (specialized): Access Digital Archives, Recommend Book for Purchase *(inherits Search Catalog, Borrow Book, Return Book, Reserve Book from Student)*
- `Faculty`: Search Catalog, Borrow Book, Reserve Book, Recommend Book for Purchase
- `LibraryStaff`: Issue Library Card, Manage Library Inventory
- `ExternalVisitor`: Search Catalog

**Step 4 — Diagram Description:**

Place `Student` as the parent. Draw a single solid line with hollow triangular arrowhead from `ResearchScholar` upward to `Student`. Place `Faculty`, `LibraryStaff`, and `ExternalVisitor` as standalone actors (no inheritance arrows). Inside the system boundary, place all eight use cases and connect them to the appropriate actors using association lines.

`(7 Marks — 3 for hierarchy identification, 2 for diagram, 2 for multiple-inheritance analysis)`

---

**Q: (b)** Explain any **four rules** that govern the use of actor generalization in UML. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Rule 1 — Direction of the Arrow:** The hollow triangular arrowhead of the generalization line must always point from the child (specialized) actor toward the parent (generalized) actor. Reversing this direction inverts the semantic relationship and is UML-invalid.

**Rule 2 — Inheritance of Associations:** A child actor automatically participates in every use case that its parent actor is associated with. The diagram should not redraw these inherited associations; otherwise the diagram becomes redundant.

**Rule 3 — Prohibition of Cycles:** The generalization hierarchy must form a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Circular inheritance (e.g., A inherits from B, and B inherits from A) is prohibited because it leads to undefined semantic behaviour.

**Rule 4 — Multiple Inheritance Permitted:** A single child actor may have more than one parent. The notation is multiple lines, each terminating in a hollow triangular arrowhead, fanning out from the child to each parent. The child inherits associations from all of its parents.

*(Additional Rule 5 — IS-A semantic requirement: For actor generalization to be valid, the child must semantically BE-A kind-of the parent. Mere overlap of use-case associations is not sufficient.)*

*(Additional Rule 6 — Substitution Principle: Wherever the parent actor is required in a scenario, the child actor may be substituted transparently.)*

`(7 Marks — 1.75 marks per rule, with 0.25 for example where required)`

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Reversing the arrow direction (–2 marks):** Drawing the hollow triangle pointing from parent to child. The arrow MUST point to the parent.
> 2. **Confusing actor generalization with use case generalization (–2 to –3 marks):** Using identical wording for both. Examiners specifically look for whether you state *what is being inherited* (associations vs. behaviour).
> 3. **Drawing redundant association lines from child to parent use cases (–1 mark):** Once inheritance is established, do NOT draw the inherited associations explicitly.
> 4. **Failing to justify the generalization (–1 mark):** In 7-mark sub-questions, you must explicitly apply the IS-A test and state the semantic reasoning.
> 5. **Using solid black arrowheads instead of hollow ones (–0.5 mark):** The hollow triangle is the UML standard. A filled arrow indicates association, not generalization.
> 6. **Forgetting the system boundary rectangle (–0.5 mark):** Use cases should be enclosed in a rectangle representing the system boundary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Actor Generalization:**

- **Definition:** A UML relationship between two actors where the child inherits the parent's use-case associations. Models the "IS-A" semantic.

- **Notation:** Solid line + **hollow triangular arrowhead** pointing **from child → parent**. Never use a filled (solid black) arrowhead.

- **Three Critical Rules:**
  1. Arrow direction: child to parent.
  2. Inheritance of associations is automatic (do not redraw).
  3. No cycles allowed — must be a Directed Acyclic Graph.

- **Inheritance Type:** A child inherits from ONE OR MORE parents (multiple inheritance is permitted). Draw multiple lines fanning out from the child.

- **Multiple Inheritance Example:** `TeachingAssistant → Student` AND `TeachingAssistant → FacultyMember` — both valid simultaneously.

- **Mandatory IS-A Test:** Before drawing actor generalization, confirm that the child is semantically a kind-of the parent (e.g., `PremiumCustomer` IS-A `Customer`).

- **Liskov Substitution Principle:** Wherever the parent actor is expected, the child actor may be transparently substituted.

- **Difference from Use Case Generalization:** Identical notation but applied between two use cases (not actors); what is inherited is behaviour (sequence of steps), not associations.

- **Where to Draw:** Only in **Use Case Diagrams**. Actor generalization is not a valid construct in class diagrams, sequence diagrams, or activity diagrams.

- **Redundancy Avoidance:** Do NOT draw association lines from a child actor to use cases already associated with its parent. The inheritance handles them implicitly.

- **Diagram Components:** Actor generalization arrows appear OUTSIDE the system boundary rectangle, while use cases and their associations appear INSIDE.

- **Stereotype (Optional):** Actors may be labelled with the `<<actor>>` stereotype, but this is not mandatory for KTU answers.

- **Common Examination Pitfalls:** Wrong arrow direction, redundant associations, confusion with use case generalization, missing system boundary, lack of IS-A justification.

- **High-Yield Examples to Remember:**
  - E-commerce: `PremiumCustomer → Customer`
  - University: `ResearchScholar → Student`
  - Healthcare: `Surgeon → Doctor → Staff`
  - Banking: `JointAccountHolder → Customer`
  - ERP: `SeniorManager → Manager → Employee`

- **Memory Hook:** *"Triangle tip touches the parent — the parent gives, the child receives."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Case Generalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Case Generalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Use Case Generalization** is a directed relationship between two use cases in which the **child (specialized) use case inherits the behavior, meaning, and associated actors** of the **parent (generalizing) use case**, and may further extend, override, or specialize that behavior to model a more specific variation of the parent scenario.

In UML 2.5 specification terminology, use case generalization establishes a **substitutability** contract: wherever the parent use case is referenced, the child use case can be substituted seamlessly because it conforms to the parent's purpose, scope, and contract. The relationship is unidirectional and is drawn from the **more specific (child) use case** to the **more general (parent) use case**.

### Key Terminology Mapping

| UML Term | Synonym | Role |
|----------|---------|------|
| **General Use Case** | Parent, Ancestor, Super-Use-Case | Defines the common, abstract behavior |
| **Specialized Use Case** | Child, Descendant, Sub-Use-Case | Adds or specializes behavior |
| **Generalization Arrow** | Hollow Triangle Notation | Visual connector |
| **Substitutability** | Liskov-style conformance | Behavioral guarantee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Use Case Generalization belongs to the broader category of **"Relationships between Use Cases"** in Module 1. Students must distinguish it precisely from `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` dependencies — a frequent 3-mark and 14-mark exam question in the KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Hierarchy" Intuition

Imagine a **transportation booking system**. The system supports a generic service called **"Book a Ride."** This is the parent use case.

- When a user books a regular taxi, we have a more specific scenario: **"Book a Standard Taxi."**
- When a user books a premium car, we have another variation: **"Book a Luxury Car."**
- When a user books a shared auto, we have yet another: **"Book a Shared Auto."**

All three specialized use cases are *kinds of* "Book a Ride." Each one **inherits** the core behavior of the parent (selecting destination, computing fare, confirming booking) and **specializes** it (luxury cars include chauffeur preference; shared autos include seat-sharing logic).

This "is-a-kind-of" relationship is exactly what **Use Case Generalization** models in UML. The arrow points from the **specific** use case **up to** the **general** use case — visually representing the "kind-of" inheritance direction.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notation Conventions

- **Notation**: A **solid line** with a **hollow (open) triangle arrowhead** pointing from the child use case to the parent use case.
- **Multiplicity**: Typically **1-to-many** (one parent, many children). A child may have multiple parents in rare multiple-inheritance scenarios.
- **Substitutability Rule**: A child use case must be **behaviorally consistent** with the parent — it cannot break the contract assumed by actors interacting with the parent.
- **No Stereotype Required**: Unlike `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`, generalization has **no stereotype keyword** above the line.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** "**Hollow Triangle = Hierarchy**." The same notation is used for class generalization in UML class diagrams. If you see an open triangle between use cases, it is **generalization**, never include or extend.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Use Case Generalization Works

Use case generalization is not just a visual line; it is a **semantic contract** with the following operational rules:

1. **Inheritance of Behavior**
   The child use case automatically inherits all flow steps, alternative flows, and post-conditions defined in the parent use case, unless explicitly overridden.

2. **Inheritance of Actors**
   Any actor associated with the parent use case is **implicitly associated** with the child use case, in addition to any new actors the child may introduce.

3. **Inheritance of Pre-conditions and Post-conditions**
   The child must satisfy **at least the same pre-conditions** as the parent and may impose **stricter post-conditions**, but never weaker ones (this is the **contract strengthening** rule).

4. **Behavioral Specialization**
   The child may:
   - **Add** new flow steps (additional behavior).
   - **Override** specific steps inherited from the parent with more refined logic.
   - **Restrict** the scope by adding constraints (e.g., "valid only for premium users").

5. **Substitutability Principle**
   An actor interacting with the parent use case **must be unaware** of the substitution. The child behaves as a *drop-in replacement* for the parent in any context where the parent is valid.

## 2.2 When to Choose Generalization vs. `<<include>>` vs. `<<extend>>`

This is the **most heavily tested comparison** in KTU Module 1. Memorize the decision matrix below.

| Criterion | Use Case Generalization | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Purpose** | Captures "is-a-kind-of" relationship | Captures **mandatory** shared sub-flow | Captures **optional/conditional** behavior |
| **Direction** | Child $\rightarrow$ Parent | Base $\rightarrow$ Included | Extending $\rightarrow$ Base |
| **Mandatory?** | Child inherits parent fully; both are valid scenarios | Included use case is **mandatory** for the base | Extension is **optional**, triggered by a condition |
| **Stereotype** | None (hollow triangle) | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
| **Example** | "Process Credit Card Payment" generalizes "Process Payment" | "Place Order" `<<include>>`s "Validate User" | "Place Order" `<<extend>>`s "Apply Discount" |
| **Reusability** | High (substitutable) | High (factoring common flows) | Medium (scenarios) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If your answer in the exam describes the relationship as "the use case **is a type of** another use case," it is **generalization**. If it says "the use case **uses** another use case as a mandatory sub-step," it is **include**. If it says "the use case **optionally adds behavior under a condition**," it is **extend**.

## 2.3 Properties of a Valid Use Case Generalization

For a generalization relationship to be **semantically valid** under UML 2.5 and KTU evaluation standards, the following must hold:

- The child use case's name must be **noun-phrase consistent** with the parent (e.g., "Verify *by OTP*" generalizes "Verify User").
- The child must **not violate the parent's contract** (no removal of mandatory steps, no weakening of guarantees).
- The relationship should be **bi-directionally substitutable** in actor-system interaction scenarios.
- Generalization should be used only when there is a **clear taxonomy** of scenarios, not merely for code reuse.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

| Property | Description | KTU Exam Weight |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Notation | Solid line + hollow triangle, child $\rightarrow$ parent | **High (3-mark questions)** |
| Substitutability | Child replaces parent seamlessly | **High (14-mark case studies)** |
| Actor Inheritance | Child inherits parent's actors | **Medium** |
| Pre/Post-condition Rules | Pre $\leq$ Parent, Post $\geq$ Parent (contract strengthening) | **High (theoretical questions)** |
| Comparison with Include/Extend | Distinguish use cases based on semantic intent | **Very High (favourite ESE question)** |
| Multi-level Generalization | Parent $\rightarrow$ Intermediate $\rightarrow$ Leaf | **Medium** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Use case generalization is applied in:

- **Banking Systems**: "Withdraw Funds" generalized by "Withdraw via ATM" and "Withdraw via Mobile App."
- **Healthcare Systems**: "Schedule Appointment" generalized by "Schedule In-Person Visit" and "Schedule Telemedicine."
- **E-commerce Platforms**: "Process Return" generalized by "Process Defective Return" and "Process Buyer's Remorse Return."
- **IoT Systems**: "Send Command to Device" generalized by "Send Command to Sensor" and "Send Command to Actuator."

In each case, the **parent use case captures the business essence**, and the **child use cases capture contextual variations** of that essence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Construction Process

## 3.1 Analytical Process — Building a Use Case Generalization Hierarchy

Below is the **step-by-step methodology** a KTU student must follow when constructing or analyzing a use case generalization in an exam answer.

### Step 1: Identify the Commonality

Begin by identifying the **abstract, common scenario** shared across multiple specific interactions. This becomes the **parent (general) use case**.

$$\text{Parent Use Case} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \text{Common Behavior}(U_i)$$

where $U_i$ represents the $i^{th}$ specific use case candidate and $\bigcap$ denotes the intersection of shared behavioral flow.

### Step 2: Identify the Variations

For each specific scenario, identify the **delta behaviors** — what does it do that the common scenario does not?

$$\Delta_i = \text{Behavior}(U_i) \setminus \text{Behavior}(\text{Parent})$$

These deltas become the **specialized additions** of the child use case.

### Step 3: Apply the Substitutability Test

Before establishing the generalization, verify that:

$$ \forall \text{ actor } A: A \text{ interacts with Parent} \Rightarrow A \text{ can interact with Child } U_i $$

If this fails, the relationship is **not a valid generalization** and should be modeled differently (perhaps as a separate, non-hierarchical use case).

### Step 4: Draw the Relationship

Draw a **solid line with an open triangle arrowhead** from each child use case to the parent use case. Do **not** label it with a stereotype.

### Step 5: Validate Contract Strengthening

Confirm:

$$ \text{Pre}(U_i) \subseteq \text{Pre}(\text{Parent}) \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Post}(U_i) \supseteq \text{Post}(\text{Parent}) $$

This ensures the child is a **stricter, more constrained** version of the parent — never a weaker one.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Online Food Delivery System

Consider an online food delivery platform like a KTU-flavored Swiggy/Zomato case study.

**Step 1 — Identify the Commonality:**
The abstract scenario common to all ordering is:

> **"Place an Order"** — the user selects items, confirms, and the system registers the request.

**Step 2 — Identify the Variations:**
- **"Place a Dine-In Pre-Order"** — same flow, but skips delivery logistics.
- **"Place a Takeaway Order"** — same flow, but the pickup is in-store.
- **"Place a Home Delivery Order"** — same flow, but adds delivery address validation and courier assignment.

**Step 3 — Substitutability Test:**
A "Customer" who can place an order can, in principle, place any kind of order. The contract holds.

**Step 4 — Draw the Generalization:**

```
                    [Place an Order]   ← General (Parent)
                    /        |        \
                   /         |         \
    [Place a        [Place a         [Place a
    Dine-In         Takeaway         Home Delivery
    Pre-Order]      Order]           Order]
```

**Step 5 — Contract Validation:**

| Use Case | Pre-condition | Post-condition |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Place an Order (Parent) | User is logged in | Order is registered |
| Place a Home Delivery Order (Child) | User is logged in **AND delivery zone is serviceable** | Order is registered **AND courier is assigned** |

Pre-condition: $\{ \text{logged in} \} \subseteq \{ \text{logged in, zone serviceable} \}$ ✓
Post-condition: $\{ \text{registered, courier assigned} \} \supseteq \{ \text{registered} \}$ ✓

Both rules are satisfied — the generalization is **valid**.

---

## 3.3 Multi-Level Generalization (Transitive Inheritance)

Use case generalization can be **multi-level**. Consider:

$$A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$$

where $\rightarrow$ denotes "generalizes." Then use case $C$ inherits from both $B$ and $A$ **transitively**. UML allows this, but KTU exam answers should be cautious — over-deep hierarchies are hard to maintain.

**Example:**

$$\text{Process Payment} \rightarrow \text{Process Card Payment} \rightarrow \text{Process Credit Card Payment}$$

The leaf "Process Credit Card Payment" inherits all flow from both "Process Card Payment" and "Process Payment." It adds CVV verification and credit-limit check as its own specializations.

---

## 3.4 Anti-Pattern: When NOT to Use Generalization

A common student mistake in KTU exams is using generalization where it does not belong. Avoid it in the following situations:

- **When the relationship is "uses"** (mandatory sub-flow) $\rightarrow$ use `<<include>>` instead.
- **When the relationship is "sometimes extends"** (optional, conditional) $\rightarrow$ use `<<extend>>` instead.
- **When the use cases share only an actor, not behavior** $\rightarrow$ do not use generalization; just connect both to the actor.
- **When the child has fundamentally different actors** $\rightarrow$ generalization is suspect.

A simple diagnostic question:

> *"Can a child use case substitute the parent in every scenario?"*
> If **yes** $\rightarrow$ generalization is valid.
> If **no** $\rightarrow$ the use cases are independent siblings, not in a hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & UML Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Use Case Diagram — Library Management System

Below is a complete, exam-ready use case diagram demonstrating generalization in the context of a Library Management System.

```mermaid
graph LR
    actor1((Student))
    actor2((Faculty))
    actor3((Librarian))

    uc1[/"Borrow Book"/]
    uc2[/"Return Book"/]
    uc3[/"Borrow Reference Book"/]
    uc4[/"Borrow Periodical"/]
    uc5[/"Return Reference Book"/]
    uc6[/"Return Periodical"/]
    uc7[/"Pay Fine"/]

    actor1 --- uc1
    actor1 --- uc2
    actor2 --- uc1
    actor2 --- uc2
    actor3 --- uc7

    uc3 -.->|is a kind of| uc1
    uc4 -.->|is a kind of| uc1
    uc5 -.->|is a kind of| uc2
    uc6 -.->|is a kind of| uc2

    uc1 ---|generalizes| uc3
    uc1 ---|generalizes| uc4
    uc2 ---|generalizes| uc5
    uc2 ---|generalizes| uc6
```

### Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Exams

- The **solid lines with hollow triangle arrowheads** (represented in the above as `-->|generalizes|` for Mermaid compatibility) connect child use cases to the parent.
- The **dashed lines with the "is a kind of" label** are alternative visual representations used in some textbooks.
- The **ellipse nodes** (drawn as `[/"..."/]` in Mermaid) represent use cases.
- The **stick figures** (drawn as `((...))`) represent actors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** The strict UML hollow-triangle arrowhead is not natively supported in Mermaid's `graph LR` syntax. In an actual KTU exam, you must hand-draw the **open triangle arrowhead** manually on the connecting line. In a digital submission, you may use the textual label `<<generalizes>>` along with a directional arrow.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

When a physical-style use case diagram is impractical, the following block-flow captures the **interaction logic** of the generalization relationship:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Actor: Customer] -->|initiates| B[Parent Use Case: Process Order]
    B -->|substitutable as| C[Child Use Case 1: Process Online Order]
    B -->|substitutable as| D[Child Use Case 2: Process In-Store Order]
    B -->|substitutable as| E[Child Use Case 3: Process Phone Order]

    C --> F[Inherits: Payment Validation]
    C --> G[Adds: Cart Total Computation]

    D --> F
    D --> H[Adds: POS Terminal Interaction]

    E --> F
    E --> I[Adds: IVR Menu Navigation]
```

### Interpretation of the Block Flow

- The **Parent Use Case** sits at the top, representing the abstract, business-level intent.
- The **three Child Use Cases** are substitutable variants.
- Each child **inherits** the parent's payment validation logic (`F`) and **specializes** with its own additional behavior (`G`, `H`, `I`).
- This block diagram is **KTU-acceptable** as a substitute for a complex use case diagram in written exams.

## 4.3 Symbol Reference Cheat Sheet

| UML Symbol | Mermaid Approximation | Meaning |
|------------|----------------------|---------|
| Ellipse with use case name | `[/"Name"/]` | Use case boundary |
| Stick figure | `((Actor))` | External actor |
| Solid line | `---` | Association (actor-to-use case) |
| Dashed line with `<<include>>` | `-.->\|<<include>>\|` | Mandatory sub-flow |
| Dashed line with `<<extend>>` | `-.->\|<<extend>>\|` | Optional extension |
| Solid line with hollow triangle | `-->\|generalizes\|` | Generalization |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define use case generalization. How is it represented in a UML use case diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

Use case generalization is a relationship between two use cases in which the **specialized (child) use case** inherits the behavior, actors, pre-conditions, and post-conditions of the **general (parent) use case**, and may add or override specific steps to model a more concrete variation. The relationship is **unidirectional** and is represented in UML by a **solid line with an open (hollow) triangle arrowhead** drawn from the child use case pointing toward the parent use case. No stereotype keyword is used. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between use case generalization and `<<include>>` relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Use Case Generalization | `<<include>>` |
|-------|------------------------|----------------|
| Purpose | Captures "is-a-kind-of" relationship | Captures mandatory shared sub-flow |
| Notation | Solid line with hollow triangle | Dashed line with `<<include>>` stereotype |
| Behavior Sharing | Child inherits full behavior from parent | Base use case delegates a sub-step to included use case |
| Substitutability | Child can replace parent seamlessly | No substitutability implied |
| Example | "Process Credit Card Payment" generalizes "Process Payment" | "Place Order" includes "Validate User" |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of use case generalization with a suitable example. Discuss the substitutability principle and contract strengthening rules in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast use case generalization, `<<include>>`, and `<<extend>>` relationships with neat UML diagrams and a real-world banking system example. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Concept of Use Case Generalization:**

Use case generalization is a UML mechanism to model a **taxonomy of use cases** where one use case (the child) represents a specific kind of another use case (the parent). The child inherits all attributes, behavior flows, and actor associations of the parent and can add new behavior or specialize inherited steps.

**Example — Banking System:**

Consider the parent use case **"Withdraw Funds."** Two specialized child use cases are:

1. **"Withdraw via ATM"** — adds card authentication and cash dispenser interaction.
2. **"Withdraw via Mobile App"** — adds biometric login and UPI/cardless flow.

Both children generalize "Withdraw Funds." The arrow from each child points to the parent.

**Substitutability Principle:**

The substitutability principle states that wherever the parent use case is invoked in any interaction, the child use case can be substituted **without the actor noticing any difference in the contract**. The actor who can perform the parent scenario must be able to perform the child scenario. If the actor cannot, the relationship is invalid.

Formally:

$$ \text{Valid Substitutability} \iff \forall \sigma \in \text{Scenarios}(\text{Parent}): \sigma \text{ is also a valid scenario for Child} $$

**Contract Strengthening Rules:**

The child use case may:

- Impose **stricter or additional pre-conditions** (e.g., "user must be within serviceable area").
- Guarantee **more detailed or stronger post-conditions** (e.g., "funds are debited AND SMS confirmation is sent").
- But it **must not weaken** the parent's guarantees.

$$\text{Pre}(\text{Child}) \supseteq \text{Pre}(\text{Parent})$$
$$\text{Post}(\text{Child}) \supseteq \text{Post}(\text{Parent})$$

Violating these rules invalidates the generalization. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Generalization | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|--------|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| Semantic | "is-a-kind-of" | "uses-as-mandatory-sub-step" | "optionally-adds" |
| Notation | Hollow triangle, solid line | Dashed line with `<<include>>` | Dashed line with `<<extend>>` |
| Trigger | Implicit, actor-driven | Always executed when base runs | Conditional, based on extension point |
| Reuse Direction | Child $\rightarrow$ Parent | Base $\rightarrow$ Included | Extender $\rightarrow$ Base |
| Frequency of Use | Low to medium | High (factoring common flows) | Medium (scenario variations) |

**Real-World Banking Example:**

Consider a banking application with the following use case relationships:

1. **Generalization:** "Withdraw via ATM" generalizes "Withdraw Funds." Both are valid scenarios; an ATM user is a special case of a funds-withdrawing user.

2. **`<<include>>`:** "Transfer Funds" `<<include>>`s "Authenticate User." Authentication is mandatory; the transfer cannot proceed without it.

3. **`<<extend>>`:** "Open Savings Account" `<<extend>>`s "Apply for Additional Services" under the condition that the customer opts in for a debit card.

**UML Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
   Withdraw via ATM  ───△───>  Withdraw Funds       (Generalization)
   
   Transfer Funds  ─ ─ <<include>> ─ ─>  Authenticate User
   
   Apply for Additional Services  ─ ─ <<extend>> ─ ─>  Open Savings Account
```

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Draw a use case diagram for an **Online Shopping System** that demonstrates use case generalization. Identify the parent and child use cases and justify the generalization relationships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of use case generalization in capturing variability in requirements. Provide a multi-level generalization example and explain the implications of deep hierarchies. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Use Case Diagram — Online Shopping System:**

```
                              Place Order  (Parent)
                              /     |     \
                             /      |      \
                  Place Online    Place     Place
                  Order           Phone      Walk-in
                                  Order      Order
```

**Identification of Use Cases:**

- **Parent Use Case:** "Place Order" — captures the abstract business scenario of a customer placing an order.
- **Child Use Cases:**
  1. "Place Online Order" — adds cart management, online payment gateway, and shipping address.
  2. "Place Phone Order" — adds IVR-based item selection and operator assistance.
  3. "Place Walk-in Order" — adds in-store POS interaction and cash/card handling.

**Justification:**

- Each child represents a **specific channel** for placing an order.
- The actor "Customer" who can place an order can, in principle, use any channel.
- Each child **inherits** the core flow (select items, confirm order, receive order ID) and **specializes** it with channel-specific steps.
- Pre-conditions and post-conditions are consistent with the contract strengthening rules.

**Notation:** Each child is connected to the parent by a solid line with a hollow triangle arrowhead pointing to "Place Order." **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Role in Capturing Variability:**

Use case generalization is a powerful UML mechanism to model **requirement variability** in a structured, substitutable manner. It allows analysts to:

1. Capture a **common core scenario** once (the parent).
2. Document **divergent behavior** in child use cases.
3. Maintain **consistency** through inherited contracts.
4. Support **plug-and-play** extension as new variations emerge.

**Multi-Level Generalization Example:**

Consider a hospital management system:

```
                          Manage Patient Record  (Level 1 Parent)
                                  △
                                  |
                          Manage In-Patient Record  (Level 2 Child)
                                  △
                                  |
                          Manage ICU Patient Record  (Level 3 Leaf)
```

The leaf "Manage ICU Patient Record" **transitively inherits** all flow from both ancestors and adds ICU-specific behavior (vital-signs monitoring, ventilator settings).

**Implications of Deep Hierarchies:**

While UML permits deep hierarchies, KTU-mature designs avoid them for the following reasons:

- **Maintenance overhead** — changes to a top-level parent cascade through all descendants.
- **Reduced clarity** — readers must trace inheritance chains to understand a leaf use case.
- **Increased contract complexity** — verifying contract strengthening at each level is non-trivial.
- **Recommendation:** Limit generalization depth to **2-3 levels** in practice. Beyond that, prefer composition with `<<include>>` or modular documentation. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong Notation** — Drawing a hollow triangle on the `<<include>>` or `<<extend>>` arrow is a guaranteed 1-2 mark deduction. Hollow triangle is **exclusively** for generalization.
> 2. **Confusing Substitutability with Reuse** — Writing that "the child reuses the parent's code" loses marks. Generalization is **behavioral substitutability**, not code reuse.
> 3. **Adding a Stereotype** — Writing `<<generalizes>>` on the line is incorrect and loses a mark. Generalization has **no stereotype**.
> 4. **Wrong Arrow Direction** — Arrow must point from **child to parent**, not the reverse. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up Include and Generalization** — If two use cases share an actor but do not share behavior, they are **not** in generalization. Do not draw the hollow triangle between them.
> 6. **Ignoring Contract Strengthening** — In 14-mark answers, examiners specifically look for the **pre/post-condition validation** discussion. Omitting it costs 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Use Case Generalization:**

- **Definition:** A directed relationship where the child use case inherits behavior, actors, and contract from the parent.
- **Notation:** Solid line with **hollow triangle arrowhead** — **no stereotype keyword**.
- **Direction:** Arrow points from **child (specific)** to **parent (general)**.
- **Substitutability:** Child can replace parent in all interaction scenarios without breaking the actor's contract.
- **Contract Strengthening:** Pre-conditions may be stricter; post-conditions may be stronger; but never weaker.
- **Actor Inheritance:** Child automatically inherits all actors associated with the parent.
- **Distinguish from `<<include>>`:** Include = mandatory sub-flow, not a "kind-of" relationship.
- **Distinguish from `<<extend>>`:** Extend = optional, conditional behavior addition.
- **Multi-level allowed:** Transitive inheritance works, but keep hierarchy depth **shallow (2-3 levels)**.
- **Valid generalization test:** "Can the child substitute the parent in every scenario?" — If yes, valid; if no, restructure.
- **Anti-patterns to avoid:** Using generalization for code reuse, drawing it between unrelated use cases, confusing it with include/extend, adding a stereotype label.
- **KTU Exam Cue:** If a question says "is-a-type-of," "kind-of," or "specialization" — the answer is **generalization**. If it says "uses," "requires," or "always performs" — the answer is **`<<include>>`**. If it says "optionally," "under condition," or "sometimes" — the answer is **`<<extend>>`**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objects and Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Objects and Classes**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Object?

In the context of Object-Oriented Modelling (OOM) and Unified Modelling Language (UML), an **object** is a fundamental runtime entity that encapsulates both **state** (data/attributes) and **behaviour** (operations/methods) within a single, well-defined boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**  
> *"An object is a discrete, identifiable entity with a well-defined role in the problem domain, characterised by a unique identity, a state that describes its current condition, and a behaviour that defines its dynamic responses to stimuli."*

In UML 2.5 (the standard adopted under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECGT411), every object possesses **four essential characteristics**:

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Identity** | Every object is uniquely distinguishable, even if its state matches another object. |
| 2 | **State** | The current snapshot of all attribute values held by the object. |
| 3 | **Behaviour** | The set of operations the object can perform in response to messages. |
| 4 | **Encapsulation** | Internal details are hidden; interaction happens only through a public interface. |

---

## 1.2 What is a Class?

A **class** is the **blueprint / template / abstraction** that describes the common structure (attributes), common behaviour (operations), and the meaning of a category of objects. Every object is said to be an **instance** of exactly one class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Class vs. Object — The Golden Rule:**  
> *Class is the concept; object is the reality.* A class exists in your model. An object exists in the running system at runtime.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **House Blueprint** drawn by an architect:

- The **Blueprint** = *Class* (defines structure: 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, 1 kitchen; defines behaviour: door opens, lights turn on).
- Every **House built from that blueprint** = *Object* (instance of the class).

Two houses built from the same blueprint look identical on paper, but once constructed, they are **different physical entities** (different addresses → different **identity**). Even if both have blue walls (same **state**), you can knock on one without disturbing the other.

In the same way:
- `class Student { ... }` is the blueprint.
- `student1`, `student2`, `student3` are distinct **objects** (instances) — each has its own roll number (identity), own marks (state), and can call its own `display()` method (behaviour).

---

## 1.4 Object Identity — Why It Matters

Identity is what makes objects distinguishable from raw values. For example, two integers with value `5` are **not identical** to two different `Student` objects both named "Rahul".

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Cue:** Whenever a question asks *"Differentiate between Class and Object"*, identity must appear in your answer. It is the **most frequently missed point**.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in UML Objects & Classes

| Metric | Standard Value (UML 2.5 Specification) |
|---|---|
| Default visibility for attributes | **Private (−)** unless explicitly declared |
| Default visibility for operations | **Public (+)** unless explicitly declared |
| Minimum number of instances a class can have | **0** (denoted by `0..*` in multiplicity) |
| Maximum number of attributes per compartment | **Unbounded**, but readability collapses above ~**15** |
| UML object name format | `instanceName : ClassName` (underlined) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Class

In a UML class diagram, a class is depicted as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**:

```
+--------------------------+
|        ClassName         |   ← Compartment 1: Name (mandatory)
+--------------------------+
|   - attribute1 : Type    |   ← Compartment 2: Attributes (optional)
|   - attribute2 : Type    |
+--------------------------+
|   + operation1() : Type  |   ← Compartment 3: Operations (optional)
|   + operation2() : Type  |
+--------------------------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **class name is mandatory**. Attributes and operations are optional, but at least one compartment other than the name is recommended for a meaningful model.

---

## 2.2 Visibility (Access Modifiers) in UML

UML uses **symbolic prefixes**, not Java/C++ keywords:

| Symbol | Visibility | Meaning | Equivalent Java Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+` | **Public** | Accessible from any class | `public` |
| `−` | **Private** | Accessible only from the class itself | `private` |
| `#` | **Protected** | Accessible from the class and its subclasses | `protected` |
| `~` | **Package** | Accessible only within the same package | *(default / package-private)* |

---

## 2.3 Structured Bulleted Logic — How an Object is Born

The lifecycle of an object in UML reasoning follows four steps:

1. **Declaration** — A class `Customer` is declared in the model with attributes and operations.
2. **Instantiation** — At runtime, memory is allocated and an object `c1 : Customer` is created. Identity is assigned (typically a unique memory address or handle).
3. **State Population** — Attributes are initialised. For example, `c1.name = "Anu"; c1.balance = 5000;`
4. **Message Dispatch** — Behaviour is invoked. For example, `c1.deposit(1000)` sends a message to `c1`, which executes its `deposit()` operation and updates state.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This four-step lifecycle is the conceptual backbone of every OOP language (Java, C++, Python) and is rigorously examined in KTU modules on UML-to-code translation.

---

## 2.4 Types of Objects

| Category | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Object** | Stores data but does not initiate actions; receives messages only. | A `BankAccount` object. |
| **Active Object** | Owns its own thread of control; can initiate actions without external stimulus. | A `TrafficController` object. |
| **Persistent Object** | Survives process termination; stored in a database or file. | A `Customer` record. |
| **Transient Object** | Exists only within a single process lifetime. | A loop counter object. |
| **Composite Object** | Built from other objects; has component parts. | A `Car` (composed of `Engine`, `Wheel`). |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Notation & Formulas

| Concept | UML Notation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Class | `<<class>> ClassName` or plain rectangle | `BankAccount` |
| Abstract Class | *Italicised* name | *`Shape`* |
| Object | `objectName : ClassName` (the **entire line is underlined**) | `acc101 : BankAccount` |
| Anonymous Object | `: ClassName` (no name, just underlined colon + class) | `: BankAccount` |
| Multiplicity | Lower-bound `..` upper-bound | `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `2..7` |
| Attribute syntax | `visibility name : type [multiplicity] = default` | `- balance : Real = 0.0` |
| Operation syntax | `visibility name(param : type) : returnType` | `+ deposit(amount : Real) : void` |
| Static attribute | *Underlined* | `- instanceCount : Integer` *(underlined)* |

### Multiplicity Reference Table

| Notation | Meaning | Allowed Instances |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one | Must always have exactly one. |
| `0..1` | Zero or one | Optional association. |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many | No upper bound. |
| `1..*` | One or many | At least one. |
| `n..m` | Between *n* and *m* | Range-bound. |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Objects & Classes

- **Software Engineering:** All major design patterns (Factory, Singleton, Observer) are expressed using class–object abstractions.
- **Database Modelling:** Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) tools like Hibernate convert UML classes directly into SQL tables.
- **Embedded Systems:** RTOS design models tasks as active objects with well-defined message-passing protocols.
- **AI / Agent Systems:** Intelligent agents are modelled as objects with autonomous behaviour (a direct extension of active objects).

> [!NOTE]
> For PECGT411 specifically, objects and classes are the **foundational vocabulary** for all subsequent modules — use cases, sequence diagrams, state machines — *everything* is built upon them.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Problem Statement to Class Diagram Structure

Consider the following KTU-style problem statement:

> *"A university manages Students. Each student has a roll number, name, and CGPA. Students register for Courses. A course has a course code, title, and credits."*

**Step 1 — Identify Nouns (Candidate Classes)**

Extract all nouns from the domain. Discard irrelevant ones (e.g., "university" may be context, not a class).

Candidates identified:
`Student`, `Course`

**Step 2 — Identify Attributes (Candidate Class Members)**

For each candidate class, extract descriptive properties.

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| `Student` | rollNumber, name, cgpa |
| `Course` | courseCode, title, credits |

**Step 3 — Identify Operations (Candidate Class Members)**

Extract verbs associated with each class.

| Class | Operations |
|---|---|
| `Student` | registerForCourse(), calculateGrade() |
| `Course` | addStudent(), getSyllabus() |

**Step 4 — Identify Relationships**

The verb phrase *"register for"* between `Student` and `Course` indicates an **association**.

**Step 5 — Determine Multiplicity**

- A *Student* can register for **many** courses → multiplicity `*`.
- A *Course* can have **many** students → multiplicity `*`.

This yields a **many-to-many association**.

**Step 6 — Final Derived Class Diagram Structure (logical form)**

$$
\boxed{
\text{Student} \xleftrightarrow{\text{registersFor, * .. *}} \text{Course}
}
$$

The binary multiplicity pair `* .. *` means **zero or more on both ends**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Class Decomposition

The complete classification of a class member is governed by the tuple:

$$
M = (V, N, T, M_u, D)
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Visibility | `+`, `−`, `#`, `~` |
| $N$ | Name (identifier) | `balance` |
| $T$ | Type | `Real`, `Integer`, `String`, or a class type |
| $M_u$ | Multiplicity (for attributes only) | `0..1`, `1`, `*` |
| $D$ | Default initial value (optional) | `= 0.0` |

**Example application — attribute `balance`:**

$$
M_{\text{balance}} = (\text{`−'}, \text{`balance'}, \text{`Real'}, \text{`1'}, \text{`0.0'})
$$

Rendered in UML:

```
- balance : Real = 0.0
```

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

1. Choose visibility $V$ = `−` (private, to enforce encapsulation).
2. Choose name $N$ = `balance` (a descriptive noun).
3. Choose type $T$ = `Real` (monetary values may have decimals).
4. Choose multiplicity $M_u$ = `1` (every account must have exactly one balance).
5. Choose default $D$ = `0.0` (a new account starts with no money).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Examiners often ask *"Write the full UML attribute signature for the following member…"* — the tuple $(V, N, T, M_u, D)$ is your guaranteed full-credit answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Object Identity vs. Object Equality

Two objects `o1` and `o2` are considered **identical (same identity)** if and only if they refer to the same memory handle:

$$
o_1 \equiv o_2 \iff \text{id}(o_1) = \text{id}(o_2)
$$

They are considered **equal in state** if their attribute values match pairwise:

$$
o_1 \cong o_2 \iff \forall a \in \text{Attributes}: o_1.a = o_2.a
$$

**Note:** Identity does not imply equality of state, and equality of state does not imply identity. For example:

- Two bank accounts each holding ₹5000 are *equal in state* but have *different identities* (different account numbers).
- An account before and after a deposit is the *same object* (same identity) but *unequal in state*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests this distinction with the question: *"Can two objects have the same state but different identities?"* The answer is **YES** — this is the difference between `==` and `.equals()` in Java.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Association Multiplicity Computation

Given a class pair $(A, B)$ connected by an association $R$, the multiplicity tuple $(m_A, m_B)$ is read **from the perspective of the opposite end**.

If "each $A$ is associated with $m_B$ instances of $B$, and each $B$ is associated with $m_A$ instances of $A$":

$$
\text{Multiplicity}(A \xrightarrow{R} B) = (m_A, m_B)
$$

**Example:** *"Each Department employs many Employees, and each Employee belongs to exactly one Department."*

$$
\text{Multiplicity}(\text{Department} \leftrightarrow \text{Employee}) = (1, *)
$$

Interpretation:
- From the **Employee** side: `1` (each employee has exactly one department).
- From the **Department** side: `*` (each department has zero or more employees).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Class Diagram — A `University` Domain

The following Mermaid block renders a clean UML-style class diagram for the University domain. All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are plain text inside double quotes.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        +name : String
        +location : String
        +addDepartment()
        +removeDepartment()
    }
    class Department {
        +deptId : String
        +deptName : String
        +head : String
        +addFaculty()
    }
    class Faculty {
        +facultyId : String
        +name : String
        +designation : String
        +teachCourse()
    }
    class Student {
        -rollNumber : String
        -name : String
        -cgpa : Real
        +registerForCourse()
        +calculateGrade()
    }
    class Course {
        +courseCode : String
        +title : String
        +credits : Integer
        +getSyllabus()
    }

    University "1" --> "*" Department : contains
    Department "1" --> "*" Faculty : employs
    Department "1" --> "*" Student : enrolls
    Student "*" --> "*" Course : registersFor
```

**Diagram interpretation (high-yield for exams):**

1. `University` has a one-to-many aggregation with `Department` (a university *contains* zero or more departments).
2. `Department` has a one-to-many association with `Faculty` (one department *employs* many faculty).
3. `Department` has a one-to-many association with `Student` (one department *enrolls* many students).
4. `Student` and `Course` share a many-to-many association (`* .. *`) — one student registers for many courses, and one course has many students.

---

## 4.2 Object Diagram — Concrete Snapshot

An **object diagram** captures actual instances and their values at a moment in time. It is the *runtime photograph* of a class diagram.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class acc101 {
        accountNumber = 101
        balance = 5000.0
        owner = Anu
    }
    class acc102 {
        accountNumber = 102
        balance = 7500.0
        owner = Rahul
    }
    class Bank {
        name = KTU Federal Bank
        branchCode = KTU001
    }

    Bank "1" --> "*" acc101 : owns
    Bank "1" --> "*" acc102 : owns
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Object diagram nodes are **always underlined** in standard UML. In the Mermaid rendering above, lowercase names are used to visually approximate the underline convention. In hand-drawn diagrams, use an underlined label such as `acc101 : BankAccount`.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Object Lifecycle Flow

This Mermaid flowchart captures the runtime lifecycle of an object from declaration through destruction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Class Declaration in Model] --> B[Instantiation Trigger]
    B --> C[Memory Allocated]
    C --> D[Identity Assigned]
    D --> E[Attributes Initialised to Defaults]
    E --> F{Active in System?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Message Dispatched to Object]
    G --> H[Operation Executes]
    H --> I[State Updated]
    I --> F
    F -- No --> J[Object Destroyed and Garbage Collected]
    J --> K[Memory Reclaimed]
```

**Step-by-step reading:**

1. **A → B**: A class `Customer` is declared in the UML model.
2. **B → C**: A client issues an instantiation request (e.g., `new Customer()`).
3. **C → D**: Memory is allocated and a unique handle (identity) is assigned.
4. **D → E**: All attributes are set to their default values.
5. **E → F**: The object is now active and waits for messages.
6. **F → G**: An external caller sends a message (e.g., `c1.placeOrder()`).
7. **G → H**: The operation body executes.
8. **H → I**: Internal state (attributes) is updated.
9. **I → F**: Control returns to the active state.
10. **F → J → K**: When no references remain, the object is garbage-collected.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following with respect to Object-Oriented Modelling:**
**(a)** Object  
**(b)** Class  
**(c)** Encapsulation

### Model Answer:

**(a) Object:**  
An object is a runtime entity that has a unique identity, a state (current values of its attributes), and a behaviour (set of operations it can perform). Objects are instances of classes and communicate by sending messages to one another.

**(b) Class:**  
A class is a blueprint or template that defines the common attributes, operations, and semantics for a set of similar objects. It describes *what* instances of that class will look like, but is not itself an instance.

**(c) Encapsulation:**  
Encapsulation is the principle of bundling an object's state (attributes) and behaviour (operations) together within a single unit, while restricting external access to the internal details. Only a well-defined public interface is exposed to other objects.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between an *abstract class* and a *concrete class* in UML. Can an abstract class be instantiated? Justify.**

### Model Answer:

| Feature | Abstract Class | Concrete Class |
|---|---|---|
| Name notation in UML | *Italicised* class name | Regular (Roman) class name |
| Instantiation | **Cannot be instantiated directly** | Can be instantiated |
| Purpose | Provides a common template for subclasses | Provides a complete, ready-to-use implementation |
| Methods | May contain abstract operations (no body) | All operations have full bodies |
| Usage in code | Subclasses must implement abstract methods | Used directly to create objects |

**Justification:** An abstract class cannot be instantiated because it represents an *incomplete* concept. For example, a class `*Shape*` is abstract — you cannot create an object that is *just* a shape; you must create a `Circle` or `Square`, which *are* shapes. Creating a `Shape` object would leave its `draw()` operation meaningless.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When drawing class diagrams, always include **three compartments** (Name, Attributes, Operations). Many students lose **2–3 marks** by drawing only the class name. Also, ensure you **underline the entire object name line** in object diagrams — partial underlining is a common deduction.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat UML class diagram, model the following domain. Include appropriate attributes, operations, visibility, and multiplicities. **(7 Marks)**

> *"An online shopping portal manages Customers and Orders. A customer has a customerId, name, email, and phone. An order has an orderId, orderDate, totalAmount, and status. A customer can place many orders, but each order is placed by exactly one customer. Each order contains one or more Products. Each product has a productId, name, price, and stockQuantity."*

**(b)** Draw the corresponding **object diagram** showing two specific customers, three orders, and four products with realistic data values. Explain how object identity is preserved. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**(a) Class Diagram:**

**Step 1 — Identify Classes:** `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`.

**Step 2 — Identify Attributes:**

- `Customer`: customerId, name, email, phone.
- `Order`: orderId, orderDate, totalAmount, status.
- `Product`: productId, name, price, stockQuantity.

**Step 3 — Identify Operations:**

- `Customer`: `register()`, `placeOrder()`, `viewOrderHistory()`.
- `Order`: `addProduct()`, `calculateTotal()`, `updateStatus()`.
- `Product`: `updateStock()`, `getPrice()`.

**Step 4 — Identify Relationships & Multiplicities:**

- `Customer` (1) → (0..*) `Order` — *places*.
- `Order` (1) → (1..*) `Product` — *contains*.

**Step 5 — Final Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -customerId : String
        -name : String
        -email : String
        -phone : String
        +register()
        +placeOrder()
        +viewOrderHistory()
    }
    class Order {
        -orderId : String
        -orderDate : Date
        -totalAmount : Real
        -status : String
        +addProduct()
        +calculateTotal()
        +updateStatus()
    }
    class Product {
        -productId : String
        -name : String
        -price : Real
        -stockQuantity : Integer
        +updateStock()
        +getPrice() : Real
    }

    Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order : places
    Order "1" --> "1..*" Product : contains
```

**Visibility justification:**
- Attributes are `−` (private) to enforce encapsulation — only the object itself can modify its data directly.
- Operations are `+` (public) — these form the interface through which other objects interact.

**(b) Object Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class c1 {
        customerId = C001
        name = Anu
        email = anu@ktu.in
        phone = 9876543210
    }
    class c2 {
        customerId = C002
        name = Rahul
        email = rahul@ktu.in
        phone = 9123456780
    }
    class o1 {
        orderId = O100
        orderDate = 2024-12-01
        totalAmount = 1500.0
        status = Confirmed
    }
    class o2 {
        orderId = O101
        orderDate = 2024-12-02
        totalAmount = 750.0
        status = Pending
    }
    class o3 {
        orderId = O102
        orderDate = 2024-12-03
        totalAmount = 2200.0
        status = Shipped
    }
    class p1 {
        productId = P01
        name = Notebook
        price = 50.0
        stockQuantity = 200
    }
    class p2 {
        productId = P02
        name = Pen
        price = 20.0
        stockQuantity = 500
    }
    class p3 {
        productId = P03
        name = Bag
        price = 800.0
        stockQuantity = 50
    }
    class p4 {
        productId = P04
        name = Laptop
        price = 1500.0
        stockQuantity = 10
    }

    c1 "1" --> "2" o1 : placed
    c1 "1" --> "1" o3 : placed
    c2 "1" --> "1" o2 : placed
    o1 "1" --> "2" p1 : contains
    o1 "1" --> "1" p2 : contains
    o2 "1" --> "1" p3 : contains
    o3 "1" --> "1" p4 : contains
```

**Explanation of Object Identity Preservation:**

Each object `c1`, `c2`, `o1`, `o2`, `o3`, `p1`, `p2`, `p3`, `p4` has a **unique identity** even though some objects share identical attribute values. For example:
- If two customers both had `name = "Anu"`, they would still be distinguishable as `c1` and a separate `c2` because their `customerId` (and underlying memory handle) differ.
- Object `o1` is uniquely identified by its memory handle regardless of its `totalAmount` being equal to `o3`'s `totalAmount`.
- This demonstrates that **identity is independent of state**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **four essential characteristics of an object** in detail. Why is *identity* considered the most fundamental property? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **Library Management System** with the following entities: `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`.  
Draw a UML class diagram with:
- At least **3 attributes and 2 operations per class**
- Appropriate **visibility markers**
- **Multiplicities** for relationships:  
  • A Member can borrow many Books.  
  • A Book is issued by exactly one Librarian.  
  • A Librarian serves many Members.  
Briefly justify each visibility choice. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**(a) Four Essential Characteristics of an Object:**

1. **Identity:**  
   Identity is the property that distinguishes one object from all others, regardless of whether their states are identical. In implementation terms, identity is realised by a unique memory address or a unique handle generated when the object is created. Identity persists for the entire lifetime of the object — even if all its attributes change, the object remains the *same* object.

2. **State:**  
   The state of an object is the cumulative values of all its attributes at a given moment in time. State is *dynamic* — it changes in response to operations. For example, a `BankAccount` object's state changes from `balance = 0` to `balance = 5000` after a deposit.

3. **Behaviour:**  
   Behaviour is the set of operations an object can perform and the reactions it produces when messages are received. Behaviour is what makes objects *active participants* rather than passive data containers.

4. **Encapsulation:**  
   Encapsulation hides the internal state behind a well-defined public interface. Other objects can only interact with the object by calling its operations, never by directly modifying its private attributes.

**Why Identity is the Most Fundamental Property:**

Identity is the most fundamental property because:
- Without identity, two objects with identical state would be *indistinguishable* — we could not tell `acc101` from `acc102` if both have `balance = 5000`.
- Identity enables **object references**, which are the foundation of all object relationships (association, aggregation, composition).
- State and behaviour are *properties of* an identifiable entity; without identity, there is no entity to possess them.

**(b) Class Diagram for Library Management System:**

**Step 1 — Attributes:**
- `Book`: bookId, title, author, availableCopies.
- `Member`: memberId, name, phone, borrowedCount.
- `Librarian`: empId, name, shift, booksIssued.

**Step 2 — Operations:**
- `Book`: `issueBook()`, `returnBook()`, `getAvailability()`.
- `Member`: `borrowBook()`, `returnBook()`, `viewHistory()`.
- `Librarian`: `issueBookToMember()`, `collectFine()`, `searchBook()`.

**Step 3 — Final Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
        -bookId : String
        -title : String
        -author : String
        -availableCopies : Integer
        +issueBook()
        +returnBook()
        +getAvailability() : Boolean
    }
    class Member {
        -memberId : String
        -name : String
        -phone : String
        -borrowedCount : Integer
        +borrowBook()
        +returnBook()
        +viewHistory()
    }
    class Librarian {
        -empId : String
        -name : String
        -shift : String
        -booksIssued : Integer
        +issueBookToMember()
        +collectFine()
        +searchBook()
    }

    Member "1" --> "*" Book : borrows
    Book "*" --> "1" Librarian : issuedBy
    Librarian "1" --> "*" Member : serves
```

**Visibility Justification:**

- All attributes are marked `−` (private) because internal data such as `memberId`, `empId`, and `availableCopies` must be protected from direct external modification. Only the owning object should be able to change them (e.g., only `Librarian.issueBookToMember()` should decrement `Book.availableCopies`).
- All operations are marked `+` (public) because they form the contract through which other parts of the system interact with these objects. Operations like `borrowBook()` and `returnBook()` must be callable by external actors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Objects and Classes":**

- ✅ An **object** is a runtime entity with **identity, state, behaviour, and encapsulation**.
- ✅ A **class** is a blueprint; objects are **instances** of a class.
- ✅ Class diagrams have **three compartments**: name, attributes, operations.
- ✅ **Visibility symbols:** `+` public, `−` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- ✅ **Abstract class** names are *italicised*; **concrete** names are in Roman.
- ✅ **Object notation** uses `objectName : ClassName` with the **entire line underlined**.
- ✅ **Multiplicity** is read from the *opposite* end: `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `n..m`.
- ✅ **Identity ≠ Equality**: Two objects can have the same state but different identities.
- ✅ **Active objects** initiate actions; **passive objects** only respond.
- ✅ Attribute syntax: `visibility name : type [multiplicity] = default`.
- ✅ Operation syntax: `visibility name(paramList) : returnType`.
- ✅ In UML, `*` is shorthand for `0..*` (zero or more).
- ✅ Always include **three compartments** when drawing a class — partial diagrams lose marks.
- ✅ **Underline** every object name line in object diagrams — partial underlining loses marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Unified Modeling Language (UML)**, a **Relationship** is a standardized semantic connection between two or more model elements (primarily classes, actors, use cases, packages, and components) that defines how these elements are structurally or behaviourally connected and how they exchange information, inherit properties, or depend on one another.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *Relationships in UML are the building blocks that bind UML elements together to express meaningful structural and behavioural semantics.
