They define the natural language of software blueprints by establishing how objects, classes, use cases, and components interact, depend, inherit, and realize each other within a system model.*

The UML 2.5 specification (governed by **OMG – Object Management Group**) classifies relationships into **four fundamental types**:

| # | Relationship Type | Category | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Association** | Structural | "Has-a" general connection |
| 2 | **Aggregation** | Structural (Weak) | Whole–Part, shared ownership |
| 3 | **Composition** | Structural (Strong) | Whole–Part, exclusive lifetime ownership |
| 4 | **Generalization** | Generalization | "Is-a" inheritance hierarchy |
| 5 | **Dependency** | Inter-Model | "Uses-a" weak semantic coupling |
| 6 | **Realization** | Inter-Model | Contract implementation |
| 7 | **Refinement / Trace** | Package/Diagram | Cross-level evolution link |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point for KTU Board Exam:** Always remember the symbolic notations — they are directly asked in 2-mark/3-mark questions as "draw the UML diagram for…" or "name the relationship symbol".

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college ecosystem**. Think of UML relationships like this:

- A **Student** *attends* a **Course** → that is an **Association** (they simply know each other).
- A **Department** *contains* many **Professors**, but if the department is closed, some professors might join another department → that is **Aggregation** (loose whole–part).
- A **House** *contains* **Rooms** — if the house is demolished, the rooms cease to exist → that is **Composition** (strong lifetime ownership).
- A **SportsCar** *is-a* **Car** → that is **Generalization** (inheritance).
- A **Client class** *uses* a **Server class** indirectly only during a method call → that is a **Dependency**.
- A **Button class** *implements* an **OnClickListener interface** → that is **Realization**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Board Favourite):** 
> **A**ggregation = "**A** empty diamond" (hollow).
> **C**omposition = "**C** losed/filled diamond" (solid).

## 1.3 Why Relationships Matter in Software Engineering

Relationships transform UML from a *glorified flowchart tool* into a **formal blueprinting language**. They allow:

- **Code Generation:** Modern CASE tools (Rational Rose, StarUML, Enterprise Architect) directly map associations → member variables, generalizations → `extends` keywords, dependencies → import statements.
- **Impact Analysis:** Multiplicity and navigation arrows help engineers understand ripple effects when a class is modified.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Tools like *Visual Paradigm* recover class skeletons from Java/C++ by detecting relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of UML Relationships

### 2.1.1 Association
A *structural relationship* describing a **bidirectional or unidirectional semantic connection** between classifiers (typically classes). It represents that objects of one classifier "know about," "communicate with," or "are linked to" objects of another.

**Key Sub-Concepts:**
- **Name:** A verb phrase like *works-for*, *owns*, *teaches*.
- **Role:** Each end has a *role name* describing how a class is perceived by the other (e.g., `employer` and `employee`).
- **Multiplicity:** Numerical range at each end (e.g., `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `n..m`).
- **Navigability:** Indicated by an open arrow `→`. Open arrow = only the source can access the target.
- **Association Class:** A class attached to an association to store metadata (e.g., a `Contract` between `Employee` and `Company`).
- **Qualified Association:** Uses a qualifier (key) to uniquely identify target objects (e.g., `bank[accountNo: String]`).
- **N-ary Association:** Connects **three or more** classes simultaneously (rare, drawn as a diamond).

### 2.1.2 Aggregation (`◇` — Hollow Diamond)
A *variant of association* representing a **whole–part relationship** where the part can exist **independently** of the whole. **Shared ownership** semantics.

- The diamond is drawn on the **whole** side.
- Lifetime of part ≠ lifetime of whole.
- Example: `Department ◇—— Professor` (a professor can switch departments).

### 2.1.3 Composition (`◆` — Filled Diamond)
A *strong form of aggregation* with **exclusive ownership** and **coincident lifetime**.

- Destroying the whole automatically destroys all its parts.
- Multiplicity on the whole side is typically `1` or `0..1`.
- Example: `House ◆—— Room`.

### 2.1.4 Generalization (`△` — Hollow Triangle)
A relationship between a **general class (superclass/parent)** and a **specialized class (subclass/child)**. Establishes **inheritance**.

- Subclass inherits attributes, operations, and relationships of the superclass.
- Can be **single** (Java-style) or **multiple** (C++-style, rarely drawn in clean UML).
- Supports **substitutability** (Liskov Substitution Principle).

### 2.1.5 Dependency (`⇢` — Dashed Arrow)
A **"uses" relationship** — one element requires another for its specification or implementation. It is the *weakest* of all UML relationships.

- Source is *client*, target is *supplier*.
- Always drawn as a **dashed line with an open arrowhead**.
- Common uses: parameter type, local variable, instantiation, static call.
- Example: A `Controller` class depends on a `Logger` utility.

### 2.1.6 Realization (`△` with Dashed Line)
A semantic relationship between two entities where one specifies a **contract** and the other **implements** it. Most commonly seen between:
- A **class** and an **interface** it implements.
- A **use case** and a **collaboration** that realizes it.
- A **component** and a **classifier** that implements it.

### 2.1.7 Refinement and Trace
- **Refinement:** A relationship between two descriptions of the same thing at different levels of abstraction (e.g., a business-level use case refined by a system-level use case).
- **Trace:** A relationship between elements in different models, typically used to track cross-model requirements (e.g., requirement → design).

## 2.2 Multiplicity Notation — The KTU Hot-Question Zone

Multiplicity defines the **cardinality constraint** of how many instances of one classifier are associated with one instance of the other.

| Notation | Meaning | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one | Every `Order` has **exactly one** `Customer` |
| `0..1` | Optional, zero or one | A `Person` may have **zero or one** `Passport` |
| `*` (or `0..*`) | Zero or many (unbounded) | A `Department` employs **zero or many** `Employees` |
| `1..*` | One or many | A `Team` has **at least one** `Player` |
| `n..m` | Range bound | A `Semester` has between `6` and `8` `Subjects` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse `*` with `1..*`. The former allows zero; the latter mandates a minimum of one. Examiners love testing this difference.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Relationship Syntax Summary

| Relationship | UML Symbol | Line Style | Arrow Head | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Association | Solid line | ——— | None (or open arrow) | Medium |
| Directed Association | Solid line | ——— | Open triangle `▶` | Medium |
| Aggregation | Solid line + hollow diamond | ———◇ | None | Weak Whole-Part |
| Composition | Solid line + filled diamond | ———◆ | None | Strong Whole-Part |
| Generalization | Solid line + hollow triangle | ———△ | Triangle | Strong (Inheritance) |
| Dependency | Dashed line + open arrow | ╌╌╌▶ | Open triangle | Weakest |
| Realization | Dashed line + hollow triangle | ╌╌╌△ | Triangle | Contract-binding |
| Refinement | Dashed line + stick arrow | ╌╌╌▷ | Stick | Cross-abstraction |
| Trace | Dashed line + open arrow | ╌╌╌▶ | Open arrow | Cross-model |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of UML Relationships

| Domain | Practical Application |
|---|---|
| **Database Design** | Composition → identifying 1:1, 1:N table relationships |
| **Microservices** | Dependency → bounded context communication contracts |
| **Design Patterns** | Generalization → Template Method; Aggregation → Observer subject-list |
| **Code Reverse-Engineering** | Tools extract relationships automatically from source |
| **Requirements Engineering** | Trace → links requirements to design to test cases |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Relationship Do I Use?"

When you are given a scenario in the exam, follow this **stepwise decision algorithm** to classify the relationship correctly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Does A *is-a* B? (e.g., Dog is-a Animal)} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{Use GENERALIZATION} \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Does A *implements/realizes* B (interface/contract)?} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{Use REALIZATION} \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Does A's lifetime control B's lifetime entirely?} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{Use COMPOSITION} \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{Does A *contain* B but B can survive without A?} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{Use AGGREGATION} \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Does A simply *use* B occasionally (e.g., as parameter)?} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{Use DEPENDENCY} \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{None of the above, but they are linked} \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{Use ASSOCIATION (default)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Library Management System

**Problem Statement:** Model a Library System where:
- A `Library` owns `Books`. If the Library shuts down, the books are destroyed (strong ownership).
- A `Library` has multiple `Members` who can `borrow` many `Books`.
- A `Book` may have an `Author`.
- A `ReferenceBook` *is-a* `Book`.
- A `LibrarySystem` (controller) *uses* a `DatabaseLogger` to log events.

**Step-by-Step Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Library} \longleftrightarrow \text{Book} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Is Book's lifetime tied to Library's lifetime?} \rightarrow \text{YES} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{COMPOSITION: Library} \blacklozenge\text{—— Book (1 to 1..*)} \\[6pt]
&\text{Library} \longleftrightarrow \text{Member} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Can a Member exist without a Library?} \rightarrow \text{YES (independent)} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{ASSOCIATION: Library —— Member (1 to *)} \\[6pt]
&\text{Member} \longleftrightarrow \text{Book (via Borrow)} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Is Borrow a metadata-rich link?} \rightarrow \text{YES} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{ASSOCIATION CLASS (Borrow: borrowDate, returnDate)} \\[6pt]
&\text{Book} \longrightarrow \text{Author} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Does Book need Author info in its data?} \rightarrow \text{YES (knows about)} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{DIRECTED ASSOCIATION: Book} \rightarrow \text{Author} \\[6pt]
&\text{ReferenceBook} \longleftrightarrow \text{Book} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Is-A relationship?} \rightarrow \text{YES} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{GENERALIZATION: ReferenceBook} \triangle\text{—— Book} \\[6pt]
&\text{LibrarySystem} \dashrightarrow \text{DatabaseLogger} \\
&\quad \text{Check: Used only as a logging dependency?} \rightarrow \text{YES} \\
&\quad \Rightarrow \textbf{DEPENDENCY (dashed arrow)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Multiplicity Derivation — Worked Numerical Exercise

**Scenario:** "Each `Instructor` teaches between `2` and `6` `Courses`. Each `Course` is taught by exactly `1` `Instructor`."

**Step 1 — Identify Directional Roles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Role A: Instructor} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Role B: Course} \\
\text{(teacher perspective)} \quad &\quad \text{(taught entity perspective)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Translate English to Multiplicity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instructor side:} \quad &\text{"Each Course has exactly 1 Instructor"} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Multiplicity at Instructor end} = 1 \\[6pt]
\text{Course side:} \quad &\text{"Each Instructor teaches 2 to 6 Courses"} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Multiplicity at Course end} = 2..6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final UML Snippet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instructor} \;\underset{\text{teaches}}{\overset{2..6}{------}}\; \text{Course} \quad \text{(Multiplicity: 1 instructor per course, 2..6 courses per instructor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Aggregation vs Composition — Lifetime Calculus

**Rule of Thumb (Lifetime Test):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Aggregation Test:} \quad \exists \; t \; \text{such that} \; \text{whole.destroy()} \; \text{at time } t \\
&\qquad \text{AND} \quad \text{part} \neq \text{null after } t \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{Aggregation} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Composition Test:} \quad \forall \; t \; : \; \text{whole.destroy()} \; \text{at time } t \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{part.destroy()} \; \text{also at time } t \\
&\qquad \Rightarrow \text{Composition}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quick Verification Table:**

| Whole destroys Part? | Whole keeps Part Reference After Self-Destruct? | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Yes | No (Part is GC'd) | **Composition** |
| No | Yes (Part survives) | **Aggregation** |
| Not Applicable | No direct ownership | **Association** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Relationship Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["UML Relationships"]
    rootB["Structural"]
    rootC["Behavioural / Inter-Model"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC

    rootB --> assoc1["Association"]
    rootB --> agg1["Aggregation"]
    rootB --> comp1["Composition"]

    assoc1 --> assoc1a["Unidirectional"]
    assoc1 --> assoc1b["Bidirectional"]
    assoc1 --> assoc1c["N-ary"]
    assoc1 --> assoc1d["Qualified"]
    assoc1 --> assoc1e["Association Class"]

    rootC --> gen1["Generalization"]
    rootC --> dep1["Dependency"]
    rootC --> real1["Realization"]
    rootC --> ref1["Refinement and Trace"]

    gen1 --> gen1a["Single Inheritance"]
    gen1 --> gen1b["Multiple Inheritance"]

    dep1 --> dep1a["Parameter Usage"]
    dep1 --> dep1b["Local Variable"]
    dep1 --> dep1c["Static Call"]

    real1 --> real1a["Class to Interface"]
    real1 --> real1b["Component to Classifier"]
```

## 4.2 Library System — Class Diagram with All Relationships

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Library {
        -name : String
        -location : String
        +addBook(b : Book) void
        +registerMember(m : Member) void
    }

    class Book {
        -ISBN : String
        -title : String
        -price : double
    }

    class ReferenceBook {
        -category : String
    }

    class Member {
        -memberId : int
        -name : String
    }

    class Author {
        -authorId : int
        -name : String
    }

    class Borrow {
        -borrowDate : Date
        -returnDate : Date
    }

    class LibrarySystem {
        +main() void
    }

    class DatabaseLogger {
        +log() void
    }

    Library "1" *-- "1..*" Book : composition owns
    Library "1" o-- "1..*" Member : aggregation has
    Book "1" --> "1..*" Author : directed association written-by
    ReferenceBook --|> Book : generalization is-a
    Member "0..*" -- "0..*" Book : association borrows
    LibrarySystem ..> DatabaseLogger : dependency uses
    LibrarySystem ..> Library : dependency manages
    Borrow ..> Book : trace
    Borrow ..> Member : trace
```

## 4.3 Relationship Strength Spectrum — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Strongest"] --> s2["Composition ◆"] --> s3["Generalization △"] --> s4["Realization ╌△"] --> s5["Aggregation ◇"] --> s6["Association ——"] --> s7["Dependency ╌▶"] --> s8["Weakest"]

    n1["Lifetimes coupled"]:::weak --> n2["Lifetime-coupled aggregation"]:::weak --> n3["Inheritance hierarchy"]:::weak --> n4["Contract binding"]:::weak --> n5["Loose whole-part"]:::weak --> n6["Structural link"]:::weak --> n7["Occasional reference"]:::weak --> n8["Token usage"]:::weak

    classDef weak fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#1f2937;
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is it an Is-A hierarchy?"}
    q2{"Does the supplier implement a contract?"}
    q3{"Does whole's destruction kill part?"}
    q4{"Is there loose containment but part survives?"}
    q5{"Is there only a temporary use / parameter reference?"}
    q6{"Just a knowledge link between classes?"}

    q1 -- YES --> r1["Use GENERALIZATION triangle"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> r2["Use REALIZATION dashed triangle"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> r3["Use COMPOSITION filled diamond"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4
    q4 -- YES --> r4["Use AGGREGATION hollow diamond"]
    q4 -- NO --> q5
    q5 -- YES --> r5["Use DEPENDENCY dashed arrow"]
    q5 -- NO --> q6
    q6 -- YES --> r6["Use ASSOCIATION solid line"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Aggregation** and **Composition** in UML with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | Hollow diamond | Filled diamond |
| **Strength** | Weak whole–part | Strong whole–part |
| **Lifetime** | Part survives destruction of whole | Part dies with the whole |
| **Ownership** | Shared | Exclusive |
| **Example** | `Department ◇—— Professor` | `House ◆—— Room` |

*(Award: 1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for example and lifetime distinction.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is a **Dependency** relationship in UML? How is it represented graphically?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Dependency** is a semantic relationship in which a change in the *supplier* element may require a change in the *client* element. It indicates that one element *uses* another — typically as a method parameter, local variable, or static method call.

**Graphical Notation:** A **dashed line** with an **open arrowhead** pointing from the client to the supplier.

**Example:** A `Controller` class invoking a `Logger` method.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark notation, 1 mark example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **structural relationships** in UML with neat diagrams. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Structural relationships describe the static composition of classes/objects in a system. The four primary structural relationships are:

**1. Association:**
A semantic connection between classifiers where objects are linked but neither owns the other.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Notation: Solid line between classes, optionally with role names and multiplicity.} \\
\text{Example: } \text{Student} \;\overset{enrolled}{\longleftrightarrow}\; \text{Course}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Aggregation:**
A "whole–part" relationship with **weak ownership**, indicated by a **hollow diamond** at the whole end.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Example: } \text{Team} \;\diamond\!-\!\!-\!\!-\; \text{Player} \quad (\text{Player can switch teams.})
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Composition:**
A "whole–part" relationship with **strong ownership**, indicated by a **filled diamond** at the whole end. Parts cannot exist without the whole.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Example: } \text{Window} \;\blacklozenge\!-\!\!-\!\!-\; \text{Frame} \quad (\text{Frame dies with Window.})
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Generalization (Is-A):**
An inheritance relationship; the subclass extends the superclass. Drawn with a **hollow triangle**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Example: } \text{SavingsAccount} \;\triangle\!-\!\!-\!\!-\; \text{Account}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student
    class Course
    class Team
    class Player
    class Window
    class Frame
    class SavingsAccount
    class Account

    Student --> Course : enrolled-in
    Team o-- Player : has
    Window *-- Frame : contains
    SavingsAccount --|> Account : extends
```

*(Awarding: 2 marks for each of the four explanations with examples, 1 mark for the diagram.)*

---

**(b)** Consider an **Online Shopping System**. Identify and draw all relationships among the following classes: `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`, `ShoppingCart`, `Payment`, `CreditCardPayment`, `UserAccount`. State the justification for each relationship chosen. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Source → Target | Relationship Chosen | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `Customer` → `UserAccount` | **Composition** ◆ | A UserAccount is owned exclusively by a Customer; deleting Customer deletes the account. |
| `ShoppingCart` ◇ `Product` | **Aggregation** | A ShoppingCart can contain Products, but Products exist independently in the catalogue. |
| `Customer` → `Order` | **Directed Association** | Customer places Order; bidirectional knowledge not needed. |
| `Order` ◇ `Product` | **Aggregation** | Order contains Products (line items), but Products live in the inventory independently. |
| `Order` → `Payment` | **Association** | Each Order is linked to exactly one Payment. |
| `CreditCardPayment` △ `Payment` | **Generalization** | CreditCardPayment **is-a** specific type of Payment. |
| `ShoppingCart` ⇢ `Payment` | **Dependency** | ShoppingCart uses Payment only for total calculation during checkout. |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer
    class UserAccount
    class Order
    class Product
    class ShoppingCart
    class Payment
    class CreditCardPayment

    Customer *-- UserAccount : owns
    ShoppingCart o-- Product : contains
    Customer --> Order : places
    Order o-- Product : line-items
    Order --> Payment : settled-by
    CreditCardPayment --|> Payment : is-a
    ShoppingCart ..> Payment : depends-on
```

*(Awarding: 1 mark per relationship identification + 1 mark for diagram = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Generalization**, **Dependency**, and **Realization** relationships in UML. For each, give the UML symbol and a real-world example. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Generalization:**
Captures the **"is-a"** inheritance hierarchy. The subclass inherits attributes, operations, and associations from the superclass.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Symbol: Solid line with a hollow triangle pointing toward the parent.} \\
\text{Example: Dog} \;\triangle\!-\!\!-\!\!-\; \text{Animal}
\end{aligned}
$$

Supports substitutability (Liskov Substitution Principle). Used to enable polymorphism.

**2. Dependency:**
The **weakest** semantic coupling. The client element does not hold a permanent reference to the supplier — it merely uses it transiently (parameter, local variable, return type, or static call).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Symbol: Dashed line with an open arrowhead toward the supplier.} \\
\text{Example: Controller} \;\dashrightarrow\; \text{Logger}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Realization:**
Binds a **specification** (interface or abstract class) to its **implementation**. The implementer *realizes the contract*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Symbol: Dashed line with a hollow triangle toward the specification.} \\
\text{Example: ArrayList} \;\dashv\!\!\triangle\!-\!\!-\!\!-\; \text{List (interface)}
\end{aligned}
$$

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal
    class Dog
    class Logger
    class Controller
    class List
    class ArrayList

    Dog --|> Animal : is-a
    Controller ..> Logger : uses
    ArrayList ..|> List : implements
```

*(Awarding: 2 marks per concept + 1 mark for symbol clarity.)*

---

**(b)** Design a UML class diagram for a **University Management System** involving classes: `University`, `Department`, `Professor`, `Course`, `Student`, `ExamController`. Show **at least four different types of relationships** with proper multiplicity and role names. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Source → Target | Relationship | Multiplicity | Role Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| `University` ◆ `Department` | Composition | 1 to 1..* | `contains` |
| `Department` ◇ `Professor` | Aggregation | 1 to 1..* | `employs` |
| `Professor` → `Course` | Directed Association | 1..* to 1..* | `teaches` |
| `Course` → `Student` | Directed Association | 1..* to 0..* | `enrolled-by` |
| `ExamController` ⇢ `Course` | Dependency | — | `manages` |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        -name : String
    }
    class Department {
        -deptId : int
    }
    class Professor {
        -profId : int
        -name : String
    }
    class Course {
        -courseCode : String
    }
    class Student {
        -rollNo : int
    }
    class ExamController {
        +scheduleExam() void
    }

    University "1" *-- "1..*" Department : contains
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : employs
    Professor "1..*" --> "1..*" Course : teaches
    Course "1..*" --> "0..*" Student : enrolled-by
    ExamController ..> Course : manages
```

*(Awarding: 1 mark per relationship with correct multiplicity + 2 marks for the diagram.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the diamond side:** Students often place the diamond on the *part* side instead of the *whole* side. Always remember: the diamond sits on the **whole**.
> 2. **Misusing the dashed line:** Dependency and Realization both use dashed lines, but Dependency uses an **open arrow** while Realization uses a **hollow triangle**. Mixing these will cost you 2 marks minimum.
> 3. **Forgetting multiplicity:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if multiplicity is absent in a class diagram drawn for full marks.
> 4. **Generalization direction:** The arrow/triangle points **toward the parent**, not the child. This is the most common directional mistake.
> 5. **Composition misuse:** Never use composition when parts can be shared. Composition mandates exclusive ownership.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Nine relationship types** exist in UML 2.5: Association, Directed Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Dependency, Realization, Refinement, Trace.
- ✅ **Aggregation = Hollow Diamond (weak whole-part).** Composition = **Filled Diamond (strong whole-part).**
- ✅ **Dependency = Dashed Line + Open Arrow.** Realization = **Dashed Line + Hollow Triangle.**
- ✅ **Generalization = Solid Line + Hollow Triangle** pointing to the parent.
- ✅ **Multiplicity** is mandatory in every association end for full marks — `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `n..m`.
- ✅ **Association Class** stores metadata about the link itself (e.g., `Borrow` between `Student` and `Book`).
- ✅ **Lifetime test:** If whole destroys part → Composition. If part survives → Aggregation.
- ✅ **Decision order:** Is-A → Generalization; Implements → Realization; Owns-strong → Composition; Owns-weak → Aggregation; Uses-temporary → Dependency; Default → Association.
- ✅ Always remember the **role names** on association ends for clarity in diagrams.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner's Eye:** Neat, well-labeled diagrams with multiplicity get 1–2 grace marks over plain text answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inheritance and Polymorphism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inheritance and Polymorphism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Inheritance (Generalization)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Inheritance is an object-oriented modeling mechanism in which one class (the **subclass** / **child class**) is defined to acquire the structural and behavioral features of another class (the **superclass** / **parent class**). In UML 2.5, this is depicted as a **Generalization** relationship, drawn as a solid line with a hollow triangular arrowhead pointing from the more specific (subclass) element to the more general (superclass) element.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Key Term:** UML uses the term **"Generalization"** for the inheritance relationship between classifiers. The OOP concept of inheritance is the realization of UML Generalization. Always write "Generalization" in your KTU exam answers, then explain it as inheritance.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
Think of a **family tree**. A child inherits the surname, certain facial features, and perhaps behavioral traits from a parent — but the child is also a *distinct* person with their own unique qualities. In UML, a `SavingsAccount` class *inherits* common attributes (like `accountNumber`, `balance`) and operations (like `deposit()`, `withdraw()`) from a generic `BankAccount` parent, but adds its own specialized feature (e.g., `interestRate`, `calculateInterest()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Generalization vs. Realization:**
> - **Generalization (Inheritance):** solid line + hollow triangle → between two classes.
> - **Realization:** dashed line + hollow triangle → between a class and an interface.
> Confusing these two is a guaranteed mark-loss in KTU valuations.

---

### 1.2 Polymorphism

**Formal Definition:**
Polymorphism (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface or supertype reference to invoke **different behaviors** depending on the **actual concrete type** of the object it refers to at runtime. In UML terms, polymorphism is enabled by the Generalization relationship combined with the substitution principle.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Consider the verb **`draw()`**. When you tell a `Circle` to `draw()`, you get a circle on the canvas. When you tell a `Square` to `draw()`, you get a square. When you tell a `Triangle` to `draw()`, you get a triangle. The *command* (the message) is identical, the *response* is different — because the *receiver* is a different concrete type. The call site doesn't need to know the exact type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Definition (Write Exactly This):**
> *"Polymorphism is the ability of an operation to behave differently based on the actual type of the object that invokes it, allowing a supertype reference to invoke subtype-specific implementations."*

---

### 1.3 The Three Forms of Polymorphism (Cardelli & Wegner Classification)

KTU 2024 specifically expects you to know the following typology:

| # | Type | Also Known As | Mechanism | UML Representation |
|---|------|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Inclusion Polymorphism** | Subtype / Pure Polymorphism | Method overriding via inheritance | Subclass overrides superclass operation |
| 2 | **Parametric Polymorphism** | Generic Polymorphism | Type parameters (templates) | UML Templates with `<<bind>>` |
| 3 | **Ad-hoc Polymorphism** | Overloading / Coercion | Same operation name, different signatures | Multiple operations with same name in one class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold units of measure / counts** required by the style guide for this topic: the inheritance hierarchy depth in UML is unbounded, but KTU exam diagrams usually limit to **3 levels** for clarity. Polymorphic dispatch latency is typically measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** in real OOP runtime engines (e.g., HotSpot JVM uses **~1–5 ns** for monomorphic inline cache hits).

topics.title: Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal UML Definition

In the Unified Modeling Language (UML) **2.5** specification, a **Package** is defined as a *namespace* and a *mechanism for organizing model elements into related groups*. Formally, a Package is a **NamedElement** that may itself own other **NamedElements** (such as Classes, Interfaces, Use Cases, Components, and even other Packages).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECGT411 Module 1):**
> A *Package* is a **general-purpose mechanism for organizing model elements into groups**. It provides a *namespace* so that two elements with identical simple names can coexist provided they belong to different packages. Packages form the topmost structural grouping unit in any large-scale UML model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a cupboard full of mixed items — clothes, books, medicines, tools. A *Package* is like a **labelled box or drawer** inside that cupboard:

| Real-World Analogy | UML Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A labelled drawer in a cupboard | A **Package** |
| Items placed inside the drawer | **Owned Model Elements** (Classes, Interfaces, etc.) |
| A smaller box kept inside the drawer | A **Nested Package** |
| The name written on the drawer label | The **Package Name** |
| "Only family members can open this drawer" | **Visibility Modifier** (private/protected) |
| A library card that lets you borrow from another branch | **Package Import / Access** |

The analogy makes the *Why* obvious:
- Without drawers, finding one shirt in a giant heap is impossible.
- Without Packages, locating one Class among thousands is impossible.
- Two different family members can be named "John" — but if one is `Kumar.John` and the other `Singh.John`, there is no conflict. Packages give us this *qualified naming*.

## 1.3 Primary Purposes of a Package

1. **Logical Grouping** — Cluster related classes (e.g., all `Controller` classes together).
2. **Namespace Control** — Resolve name clashes between identically named elements.
3. **Architectural Decomposition** — Break a large system into manageable layers / modules.
4. **Configuration Management** — Independent versioning, reuse, and packaging of units.
5. **Access Control** — Restrict visibility of elements via the `+`, `-`, `#` modifiers.

> [!NOTE]
> Packages are **structural** — they exist at design time and govern organization. They are *not* the same as physical deliverables (files, JARs, DLLs), although in practice a Package is often mapped 1-to-1 to a physical deployment artifact.

## 1.4 Visibility of Package Contents

Every element owned by a package carries a **visibility** indicator that controls how external packages may reference it.

| Symbol | UML Visibility Name | Semantics in a Package Context |
|---|---|---|
| **+** | Public | Visible to **all** packages that import this package |
| **-** | Private | Visible **only inside the declaring package** |
| **#** | Protected | Visible to packages that **specialize** (inherit) this one |
| *no symbol* | Package (default) | Visible only to packages that **explicitly import** this one |

> [!TIP]
> **Default behaviour for a top-level package** is *Public*. Always specify visibility explicitly when designing reusable libraries — KTU examiners look for this discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a UML Package

A Package is depicted as a **tabbed folder** (a rectangle with a small rectangular tab on its top-left corner). The package has three logical regions:

1. **Tab Region** — Contains the package *name* (and optionally a *stereotype*).
2. **Header Region** — Optional, shows the package's *contents signature* (list of element names).
3. **Body Region** — Contains the *owned elements* (Classes, Interfaces, even nested Packages).

$$
\boxed{\text{Package} = \langle \text{Name}, \text{Visibility}, \text{OwnedElements}, \text{Imports}, \text{NestedPackages} \rangle}
$$

## 2.2 Package Owns Elements (Owned-Element Rule)

A Package may directly own:
- **Classes** and **Interfaces**
- **UseCases**, **Actors**, **Components**, **Nodes**
- **Signals**, **DataTypes**, **Enumerations**, **Primitives**
- **Associations**, **Dependencies**, **State Machines**, **Activities**
- **Other Packages** (nested packages)
- **Constraints** and **Comments**

Every element can be owned by *at most one* Package — this is called the **Uniqueness of Ownership** rule. To share an element across packages, you must use **Import** or **Merge**.

## 2.3 Qualified Naming & Namespaces

Each Package establishes a **namespace**. A fully qualified name is constructed by concatenating package paths using the `::` separator (UML notation).

$$
\boxed{\text{ClassA} \in \text{Pkg1} \subseteq \text{Pkg2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{FullName} = \texttt{Pkg2::Pkg1::ClassA}}
$$

If a same-named element exists in two unrelated packages, both can coexist because their **fully qualified names differ**.

## 2.4 The Five Relationship Stereotypes (HIGH-YIELD)

These are the **most important** package relationships — guaranteed to appear in KTU exams.

| Stereotype | Direction | Semantic Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `<<import>>` | A → B | Adds the **public** elements of B into the namespace of A as *non-modifiable* aliases. A can use them but cannot redefine them. |
| `<<access>>` | A → B | A may use the **public** elements of B, but they are **NOT added** to A's namespace. Used for *runtime* access (e.g., transitive dependencies). |
| `<<merge>>` | A → B | **Content merger** — the contents of B are *merged* into A. Allows a package to *specialize* another. |
| `<<trace>>` | A → B | Loose semantic link indicating one model is a *refinement* or *historical successor* of another (typically across models). |
| `<<dependency>>` | A → B | Generic UML dependency — model element A uses B in some unspecified manner. |

### 2.4.1 PackageImport vs PackageAccess — The Subtle Distinction

This is a classic KTU favourite:

> **PackageImport (`<<import>>`)** → imported elements become **part of the importing namespace** as **aliases**. The importing package sees them as if they were local. Imported names can be used unqualified.

> **PackageAccess (`<<access>>`)** → the importing package can **refer to** the public elements but must always use the **fully qualified name**. The elements are **not** part of its private namespace.

### 2.4.2 ElementImport

A more granular form: instead of importing an entire package, you import a **single NamedElement** by aliasing it locally.

$$
\text{Example:} \;\; \texttt{<<element-import>>} \;\;\text{PkgB::ClassX} \;\;\mapsto \;\;\text{LocalX}
$$

## 2.5 PackageMerge — Deep Dive

`<<merge>>` is the most semantically rich package relationship. It is used to *merge* the contents of two packages into one logical view.

### 2.5.1 Purpose
A *base* package defines common content. A *specialized* package merges its own additional content on top of the base — the result is a *virtual merged package* that contains elements from **both**.

### 2.5.2 Algorithm (Simplified Trace)

For every element `e` in the merged package:

$$
\boxed{\text{Merged\_Contents} = \text{Contents}(\text{MergeTarget}) \cup \text{Contents}(\text{MergeSource})}
$$

- If `e` exists in **both** packages → the MergeSource version **overrides** the MergeTarget version.
- If `e` exists **only in MergeTarget** → it is **included as-is**.
- If `e` exists **only in MergeSource** → it is **added** to the merged view.

## 2.6 Standard UML Stereotypes for Packages

| Stereotype | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `<<framework>>` | A Package defining an *extensible skeleton*. Other packages may merge into it. |
|<<facade>> | A Package providing a *simplified unified view* of a complex subsystem. |
| `<<stereotype>>` | A Package whose contents form a UML **profile stereotype**. |
| `<<profile>>` | A Package of related stereotype definitions used in a UML profile. |
| `<<modelLibrary>>` | A reusable library of model elements. |

## 2.7 Nested Packages (Package Containment)

Packages can contain other packages, producing a **hierarchical tree**. This is the foundation of UML's logical architecture.

```
Root
 ├── Presentation
 │     ├── UI
 │     └── Reports
 ├── Business
 │     ├── Domain
 │     └── Services
 └── Data
       ├── Persistence
       └── Repositories
```

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Java** — A Package corresponds exactly to a Java package and folder structure.
- **C#** — A Package corresponds to a C# `namespace`.
- **C++** — Maps to a logical namespace block.
- **Architecture Frameworks** — The "4+1" View Model, TOGAF, and Zachman all rely on package-based hierarchical decomposition.
- **Microservices** — Each bounded context is essentially a UML package.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Qualified Name Construction

**Given:** Class `Customer` exists inside package `Domain`, which is nested inside package `Business`, which itself is nested inside root package `System`.

**Step 1 — Identify the package containment path**

$$
\text{Path} = \{\text{System} \to \text{Business} \to \text{Domain} \to \text{Customer}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply UML concatenation rule**

Each ancestor contributes its simple name separated by `::` (two colons). The leaf element contributes the final name segment.

$$
\text{QualifiedName} = \bigotimes_{i=1}^{n-1} \text{Ancestor}_i \, :: \, \text{LeafName}
$$

where $\bigotimes$ denotes concatenation with `::`.

**Step 3 — Substitute the specific path**

$$
\text{QualifiedName} = \texttt{System} :: \texttt{Business} :: \texttt{Domain} :: \texttt{Customer}
$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form**

$$
\boxed{\texttt{System::Business::Domain::Customer}}
$$

> This is exactly how Java prints it (`System.Business.Domain.Customer`) and how XML namespaces express it (`xmlns:sys="…/System"`).

## 3.2 Worked Example: PackageImport Semantics

**Scenario:**
- Package `BankUI` declares `<<import>>` from Package `BankServices`.
- `BankServices` contains classes `Account`, `Transaction`, and `Customer`, **all with `+` (public) visibility**.

**Step 1 — Identify publicly visible members of the target package**

$$
\text{PublicMembers}(\text{BankServices}) = \{\text{Account}^{+}, \text{Transaction}^{+}, \text{Customer}^{+}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply PackageImport transformation**

A PackageImport does NOT copy the elements; it creates **aliases** in the importer's namespace.

$$
\forall e \in \text{PublicMembers}(\text{BankServices}) : \text{CreateAlias}(\text{BankUI},\ e)
$$

**Step 3 — Verify name-clash resolution**

Suppose `BankUI` itself has a class `Customer` (local). After the import:

$$
\text{LocalAlias}(\text{BankUI}) = \texttt{BankUI::Customer} \quad \text{(local)}
$$

$$
\text{ImportedAlias}(\text{BankUI}) = \texttt{BankServices::Customer} \quad \text{(imported, non-modifiable)}
$$

**Step 4 — Resolution rule**

If `BankUI` references unqualified `Customer`, the **local** definition wins (UML 2.5 §7.8.3.3). To access the imported one, the full qualifier is mandatory:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{BankUI} \texttt{ uses } \texttt{BankServices::Customer} \texttt{ (fully qualified)}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: PackageMerge Trace

**Scenario:**
- Package `BaseReporting` contains classes `Report` and `Renderer`.
- Package `PDFReporting` declares `<<merge>>` from `BaseReporting`.
- `PDFReporting` defines its OWN class `PDFRenderer` (note the name overlap with `Renderer`).

**Step 1 — Enumerate elements in both packages**

$$
\text{BaseReporting} = \{\text{Report},\ \text{Renderer}\}
$$

$$
\text{PDFReporting} = \{\text{PDFRenderer}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply merge algorithm (Rule A — override on clash)**

There is **no direct name clash** (Renderer ≠ PDFRenderer), so both survive.

$$
\text{AfterMerge} = \text{BaseReporting} \cup \text{PDFReporting} = \{\text{Report},\ \text{Renderer},\ \text{PDFRenderer}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Modified scenario (PDFReporting also defines a `Report` class)**

$$
\text{PDFReporting}_{\text{new}} = \{\text{PDFRenderer},\ \text{Report}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply override-on-clash rule**

A clash occurs on `Report`. The **MergeSource** (`PDFReporting`) **overrides** the MergeTarget:

$$
\boxed{\text{Report}_{\text{visible}} = \text{Report}_{\text{PDFReporting}} \quad \text{(BaseReporting's Report is hidden)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Resulting merged package**

$$
\text{MergedContents} = \{\text{Report}_{\text{PDFReporting}},\ \text{Renderer},\ \text{PDFRenderer}\}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Relationship

| Design Need | Recommended Stereotype | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Reuse classes from a library package | `<<import>>` | Clean aliasing, no name pollution of internals |
| Need elements at runtime only, not at compile-time modelling | `<<access>>` | Prevents namespace pollution while allowing cross-package calls |
| Build a customizable framework with plug-in extensions | `<<merge>>` (with `<<framework>>`) | Allows controlled content override |
| Document cross-model relationships (requirements ↔ design) | `<<trace>>` | Loose, non-binding semantic link |
| Reuse a single class from another package | `<<element-import>>` | Granular import; avoids pulling entire package |
| Hide a helper class from all external packages | `Private (-)` visibility | Limits reach of the element |

## 3.5 Conflict Resolution Priority Order

When multiple stereotypes or aliases compete for the same name in a package, UML 2.5 mandates this resolution priority:

$$
\boxed{\text{Local} \gt \text{Imported (package)} \gt \text{Imported (element)}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
1. **Local** definition is always preferred first.
2. If none, look for an imported package's public element alias.
3. If none, look for an explicitly element-imported alias.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Basic Package Notation (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
graph TB
    pkgSys["SYSTEM PACKAGE"]
    pkgUI["USER INTERFACE PKG"]
    pkgBL["BUSINESS LOGIC PKG"]
    pkgDA["DATA ACCESS PKG"]

    pkgSys --- pkgUI
    pkgSys --- pkgBL
    pkgSys --- pkgDA

    pkgUI -->|"import"| pkgBL
    pkgBL -->|"access"| pkgDA
```

*Interpretation:*
- The outer rectangle `SYSTEM PACKAGE` represents the top-level architectural container.
- Inner rectangles represent nested sub-packages.
- Solid arrow with `import` stereotype = **PackageImport**.
- Dashed arrow with `access` stereotype = **PackageAccess**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Nested Package Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    rootPkg["ROOT PACKAGE ECOMMERCE"]
    level1a["PRESENTATION"]
    level1b["BUSINESS"]
    level1c["DATA"]

    subA1["UI WIDGETS"]
    subA2["REPORTS"]
    subB1["DOMAIN MODELS"]
    subB2["SERVICES"]
    subC1["PERSISTENCE"]
    subC2["REPOSITORIES"]

    rootPkg --- level1a
    rootPkg --- level1b
    rootPkg --- level1c

    level1a --- subA1
    level1a --- subA2
    level1b --- subB1
    level1b --- subB2
    level1c --- subC1
    level1c --- subC2
```

*Interpretation:* A three-level hierarchy — this is the canonical structure used in KTU diagrams to test whether students understand **nested package containment**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Five Relationship Types Compared

```mermaid
graph LR
    client["CLIENT PKG"]
    server["SERVER PKG"]
    util["UTILITY PKG"]
    base["BASE FRAMEWORK PKG"]
    special["SPECIALIZED PKG"]
    modelA["REQUIREMENTS MODEL"]
    modelB["DESIGN MODEL"]

    client -->|"import"| server
    client -.->|"access"| util
    special ==>|"merge"| base
    modelA -.->|"trace"| modelB
```

*Interpretation:*
- `client -->|import| server` — Solid arrow → **PackageImport** (names added to client's namespace).
- `client -.->|access| util` — Dashed arrow → **PackageAccess** (qualified access only).
- `special ==>|merge| base` — Thick arrow → **PackageMerge** (content merger).
- `modelA -.->|trace| modelB` — Dashed arrow → **PackageTrace** (cross-model loose link).

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Visibility Inside a Package

```mermaid
graph TB
    pkgMain["ACCOUNT PKG"]
    pubA["ACCOUNT PLUS"]
    privB["INTERNAL HELPER MINUS"]
    protC["BASE ACCOUNT HASH"]
    noVisD["LEGACY LOGGER"]

    pkgMain --- pubA
    pkgMain --- privB
    pkgMain --- protC
    pkgMain --- noVisD
```

*Interpretation (KTU frequently asked):*
- `+ Account` — Visible to **any** package importing `AccountPkg`.
- `- InternalHelper` — Visible **only inside AccountPkg**.
- `# BaseAccount` — Visible to descendants that specialize this package.
- *(no symbol) LegacyLogger* — Visible **only to packages that explicitly import it**.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Framework and Facade Stereotype Pattern

```mermaid
graph TB
    fw["FRAMEWORK PACKAGE FRAMEWORK"]
    facadeA["FACADE A"]
    facadeB["FACADE B"]
    impl1["IMPLEMENTATION X"]
    impl2["IMPLEMENTATION Y"]

    fw -.->|"mergeable by"| facadeA
    fw -.->|"mergeable by"| facadeB
    facadeA --> impl1
    facadeB --> impl2
```

*Interpretation:*
- `<<framework>>` Package exposes *extension points*.
- `<<facade>>` Packages offer a *simplified unified view* of complex subsystems.
- `mergeable by` annotation tells the reader that the framework package is **designed to be merged into** by client packages.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define a Package in UML. With a neat diagram, explain the notation used to represent a package.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Package** is a general-purpose UML grouping mechanism used to organize model elements into related sets and to provide a controlled namespace.

- **Diagram Notation:** A package is drawn as a **rectangle with a small rectangular tab** at the top-left corner (resembling a file folder).
- **Contents:** The body may contain classes, interfaces, use cases, and even other nested packages.
- **Label:** The package name is written inside the tab (or inside the rectangle for top-level packages).

```
+-----------------+
| PackageName     |
+-----------------+
| +ClassA         |
| -ClassB         |
+-----------------+
```

*(Marking split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark notation description, 1 mark diagram)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `<<import>>` and `<<access>>` package relationships with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `<<import>>` | `<<access>>` |
|---|---|---|
| Effect on Namespace | Imported elements **become aliases** in the importer's namespace | Imported elements are **NOT** added to namespace |
| Reference Style | May use **unqualified names** | Must use **fully qualified names** |
| Usage Scenario | Compile-time reuse of library classes | Runtime / transitive dependency |

**Example:** Package `OrderUI` `<<import>>`s `OrderServices`. Therefore, `OrderUI` can write `Order order = new Order();` using the unqualified `Order` class from `OrderServices`. If it were `<<access>>` instead, it would have to write `OrderServices::Order order = new OrderServices::Order();`.

*(Marking split: 2 marks difference, 1 mark example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Design the package structure for a Hospital Management System. Draw the complete package diagram showing: (a) at least 4 nested sub-packages with classes, and (b) all five types of package relationships (`<<import>>`, `<<access>>`, `<<merge>>`, `<<trace>>`, `<<framework>>`). Justify each relationship. (7 + 7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

The Hospital Management System is decomposed into the following package hierarchy:

```
HospitalManagementSystem                (<<framework>>)
 ├── PatientCarePkg
 │     ├── OutPatient
 │     └── InPatient
 ├── DiagnosticsPkg
 │     ├── Radiology
 │     └── Pathology
 ├── AdministrationPkg
 │     ├── Billing
 │     └── HR
 └── InfrastructurePkg
       ├── Database
       └── Communication
```

**Diagrammatic representation:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    root["HOSPITAL MS FRAMEWORK"]
    p1["PATIENT CARE PKG"]
    p2["DIAGNOSTICS PKG"]
    p3["ADMINISTRATION PKG"]
    p4["INFRASTRUCTURE PKG"]

    s1["OUT PATIENT"]
    s2["IN PATIENT"]
    s3["RADIOLOGY"]
    s4["PATHOLOGY"]
    s5["BILLING"]
    s6["HR"]
    s7["DATABASE"]
    s8["COMMUNICATION"]

    root --- p1
    root --- p2
    root --- p3
    root --- p4

    p1 --- s1
    p1 --- s2
    p2 --- s3
    p2 --- s4
    p3 --- s5
    p3 --- s6
    p4 --- s7
    p4 --- s8
```

**Justification of decomposition (3 marks):**
- **PatientCarePkg** groups all classes related to patient handling.
- **DiagnosticsPkg** isolates lab and imaging facilities.
- **AdministrationPkg** separates business support functions.
- **InfrastructurePkg** contains reusable technical utilities (DB, messaging).

**Visibility assignments (1 mark):**
- Domain classes (e.g., `InPatient`, `OutPatient`) → `+` Public.
- Helper classes (e.g., internal logger) → `-` Private.

**Class placement inside packages (3 marks):**
- `OutPatient`, `InPatient` belong to `PatientCarePkg`.
- `Radiology`, `Pathology` belong to `DiagnosticsPkg`.
- `Billing`, `HR` belong to `AdministrationPkg`.
- `Database`, `Communication` belong to `InfrastructurePkg`.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution — Applying the five relationships:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    patCare["PATIENT CARE PKG"]
    diag["DIAGNOSTICS PKG"]
    admin["ADMINISTRATION PKG"]
    infra["INFRASTRUCTURE PKG"]
    baseFw["BASE MS FRAMEWORK"]
    reqMod["REQUIREMENTS DOC"]
    billingExt["BILLING EXTENSION PKG"]
```

**1. `<<import>>` (1 mark):**
PatientCarePkg `<<import>>`s AdministrationPkg because patient registration directly uses the Billing class.

**2. `<<access>>` (1 mark):**
DiagnosticsPkg `<<access>>`es InfrastructurePkg because diagnostic reports are *written to* the database at runtime but no compile-time reference to DB classes is needed in DiagnosticsPkg's own code.

**3. `<<merge>>` (2 marks):**
The BillingExtensionPkg `<<merge>>`s AdministrationPkg.Billing to *override* the default billing algorithm with an insurance-aware version. After merge, the new `Billing` algorithm replaces the original.

**4. `<<trace>>` (1 mark):**
The Requirements Documentation Model `<<trace>>`s to the Design Model (PatientCarePkg), indicating that the design is a refinement of the requirement specification.

**5. `<<framework>>` (2 marks):**
The top-level `HospitalManagementSystem` package is stereotyped `<<framework>>` because it defines reusable extension points (PatientCare, Diagnostics) that external implementations can merge into. The BaseMSFramework is a `<<framework>>` template — concrete packages like the BillingExtensionPkg merge their customizations into it.

**Diagrammatic Final Form:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    patCare["PATIENT CARE PKG"] -->|"import"| admin["ADMINISTRATION PKG"]
    diag["DIAGNOSTICS PKG"] -.->|"access"| infra["INFRASTRUCTURE PKG"]
    billingExt["BILLING EXTENSION"] ==>|"merge"| admin
    reqMod["REQUIREMENTS MODEL"] -.->|"trace"| patCare
    baseFw["HOSPITAL MS FRAMEWORK"]
    billingExt -.->|"merges into"| baseFw
```

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Explain UML Packages in detail. With a suitable example, describe the following: (a) PackageMerge and its resolution rules with a worked example, and (b) the difference between PackageImport, PackageAccess, and ElementImport with examples. (7 + 7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks — PackageMerge**

**Definition (2 marks):** PackageMerge is a relationship between two packages where the contents of the **merge target** package are *combined* with the contents of the **merge source** package, forming a virtual merged package. Elements with the same name from the source **override** those of the target.

**Resolution Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. List contents of both packages.
2. For each element name appearing in both, the source version wins.
3. For each element unique to one package, it is included unchanged.
4. The merged package is a **virtual** view — no physical package is created.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

Let `BaseReporting` = `{ Report, Renderer }`.
Let `PDFReporting` declares `<<merge>>` on `BaseReporting`.
Let `PDFReporting` defines `PDFRenderer` and also `Report` (overriding).

$$
\text{Merged} = \{\text{Report}_{PDF},\ \text{Renderer},\ \text{PDFRenderer}\}
$$

Result: `Report` from PDF version replaces `Report` from Base; `Renderer` survives from Base; `PDFRenderer` is newly added.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks — Three Import Types**

**PackageImport `<<import>>` (2.5 marks):**
Adds all *public* members of the target package as **aliases** into the importer's namespace. Importer may use **unqualified** names. Example:

```text
BankUI  <<import>>  BankServices
→ BankUI can write:  Account a = new Account();
```

**PackageAccess `<<access>>` (2.5 marks):**
Allows the importer to *refer to* the public members of the target package, but the members are **NOT** added to its namespace. Must always use **fully qualified** names. Example:

```text
ReportingPkg  <<access>>  BillingPkg
→ ReportingPkg must write:  BillingPkg::Bill  billObj = new BillingPkg::Bill();
```

**ElementImport `<<element-import>>` (2 marks):**
Imports a **single NamedElement** by aliasing it locally without importing the whole package. Useful for selective reuse.

```text
<<element-import>>  BillingPkg::Invoice  →  LocalInvoice
```

Importer can then use `LocalInvoice` as if it were its own.

**Comparison Table (Bonus — for completeness):**

| Aspect | PackageImport | PackageAccess | ElementImport |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Whole package | Whole package | Single element |
| Added to namespace | Yes | No | Yes (single alias) |
| Unqualified use allowed | Yes | No | Yes (alias) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Package Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `<<import>>` with `<<access>>`** — students often draw the wrong arrow style. Remember: `import` = solid arrow (namespace added); `access` = dashed arrow (qualified use only).
> 2. **Forgetting visibility symbols** — a class without `+`/`-`/`#` inside a package loses marks for "incomplete diagram".
> 3. **Drawing Packages as folders with no tab** — UML 2.5 mandates the tab in the top-left corner. A plain rectangle is **incorrect**.
> 4. **Treating PackageMerge as PackageImport** — merge is *content override*, not aliasing. This is the single most common conceptual error.
> 5. **Not labelling stereotypes in `<<...>>` guillemets** — KTU answers must show `<<framework>>`, not just *framework*.
> 6. **Ignoring the framework package's role** — if the question mentions `<<framework>>`, students must explain that it is a *merge target* for client packages.
> 7. **Missing Qualified Names** — when discussing import/access, always demonstrate with a fully qualified name example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Packages (Module 1)**

- [ ] A **Package** is a *namespace* and a *grouping mechanism* for model elements.
- [ ] Notation: **rectangle with a top-left tab** (folder shape).
- [ ] A Package may contain Classes, Interfaces, Use Cases, Components, and **other Packages**.
- [ ] **Visibility modifiers**: `+` Public, `-` Private, `#` Protected, *(none)* Package-private.
- [ ] The **Uniqueness of Ownership** rule: each element has exactly **one** owning package.
- [ ] Qualified names use the `::` separator (e.g., `PkgA::PkgB::ClassX`).
- [ ] Five key relationships: `<<import>>`, `<<access>>`, `<<merge>>`, `<<trace>>`, `<<dependency>>`.
- [ ] `<<import>>` → adds aliases into importer's namespace; unqualified use allowed.
- [ ] `<<access>>` → does **NOT** add to namespace; fully qualified reference required.
- [ ] `<<merge>>` → merges source contents into target; **source overrides on clash**.
- [ ] `<<trace>>` → loose cross-model semantic link (no content transfer).
- [ ] `<<framework>>` → a package designed as a **merge target** with extension points.
- [ ] `<<facade>>` → provides a *simplified unified view* of a complex subsystem.
- [ ] `<<element-import>>` → imports a **single element** (not the whole package).
- [ ] **Conflict resolution priority**: Local $\gt$ PackageImport $\gt$ ElementImport.
- [ ] Packages are the **logical architecture unit** of UML — they map 1-to-1 to programming language namespaces.
- [ ] In KTU exams, always draw **labels on arrows** in `<<stereotype>>` format.
- [ ] Remember to show **nested packages** by drawing a folder inside a folder.
- [ ] PackageMerge is **virtual** — no new package is physically created; it's a *view*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Class Diagram for University Library Management Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Case Study: Class Diagram for University Library Management Software ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Class Diagram A **Class Diagram** is a static structural diagram in the Unified Modeling Language (**UML**) that represents the **system's vocabulary**, its **collaborations**, and the **logical schema** of a system by depicting the system's classes, their attributes, operations, and the relationships among objects. According to the **OMG UML 2.5.1 specification**, a class diagram is the most widely used type of UML diagram and forms the backbone of object-oriented analysis and design (**OOAD**). In the context of a **University Library Management Software (ULMS)**, a class diagram captures the static blueprint of the system — identifying *what* entities (books, members, librarians) exist, *what* data they hold, *what* actions they perform, and *how* they are connected. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Class diagrams are a high-weightage topic. Examiners expect students to (1) identify candidate classes using techniques like *noun–phrase analysis* and *CRC cards*, (2) define attributes with proper *visibility* modifiers (+, –, #, ~), and (3) draw relationships with correct *multiplicity* and *stereotypes* (≪interface≫, ≪entity≫, etc.). ### 1.2 The Three Tiers of a Class Diagram A UML class diagram for any system — including the University Library — is constructed on **three logical layers**: | Tier | Purpose | Library System Example | |------|---------|------------------------| | **Classes** | Identify the "actors" / "nouns" of the system | `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`, `Library` | | **Attributes** | Describe the properties / state of each class | `Book → title, ISBN, price, status` | | **Operations** | Define the behaviour / responsibilities of each class | `Book → issue(), return(), reserve()` | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library as a Blueprint > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine that you are an *architect* who has been asked to design a brand-new university library building. Before a single brick is laid, you draw a **floor plan**. This floor plan does not show real people walking or books moving — it shows the *static structure*: where the reception desk sits, how many reading rooms there are, where the stacks are located, and how the rooms are connected by doors and corridors. The **Class Diagram is exactly that floor plan** for software. The "rooms" are **classes** (Book, Member, Librarian), the "labels on the rooms" are **attributes** (title, ISBN), and the "purpose of each room" is captured in **operations** (issue, return, search). The "doors and corridors connecting rooms" are the **relationships** (association, aggregation, inheritance). This intuition is critical: a class diagram is **static** — it does not show time-ordered messages (that is the role of a *sequence diagram* in Module 3). It captures the *snapshot* of the system's vocabulary at design time. ### 1.4 Why Class Diagrams Matter in ULMS - They establish a **shared vocabulary** between the developer, the librarian, and the end-user (students/faculty). - They form the **input to code generation** in model-driven engineering (MDE) tools like *Rational Rose*, *StarUML*, and *Enterprise Architect*. - They enable **forward and reverse engineering**, which is central to modern DevOps pipelines. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Identify the classes, attributes, and relationships for a given case study."* (3 marks) and *"Draw a class diagram for the given scenario with proper multiplicity and stereotypes."* (7–14 marks). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Methodology to Construct a Class Diagram Below is the **rigorous, KTU-approved methodology** that examiners expect students to follow when tackling a case study: **Step 1 — Identify Classes (Noun-Phrase Analysis)** Read the problem statement and underline every **noun**. Filter out ambiguous, redundant, or implementation-specific nouns. Surviving nouns are *candidate classes*. **Step 2 — Identify Attributes (Adjective / Adverb Analysis)** For each candidate class, list the **data members** it must hold. Each attribute must have: - A **name** - A **type** (e.g., `String`, `int`, `Date`) - A **visibility** (the most common is `-` for *private*) **Step 3 — Identify Operations (Verb Analysis)** List the **responsibilities** of each class. Each operation should have a clear *signature*: name, parameters, and return type. **Step 4 — Identify Relationships** Map the connections between classes using the *five* UML relationship types: | Relationship | Symbol | Meaning | ULMS Example | |--------------|--------|---------|--------------| | **Association** | `———` | "uses-a" / structural connection | `Member` borrows `Book` | | **Aggregation** | `—◇—` | "has-a" (weak ownership) | `Library` ◇– `Book` | | **Composition** | `—◆—` | "owns-a" (strong lifecycle) | `Book` ◆– `Chapter` | | **Generalization** | `———▷` | "is-a" (inheritance) | `Member` ▷ `Student` | | **Dependency** | `- - -▷` | "depends-on" (transient) | `Report` ⇢ `Database` | **Step 5 — Assign Multiplicity** Add cardinality at both ends: `1`, `*` (many), `0..1`, `1..*`, `2..5`. Example: A `Member` borrows *0..5* `Book` instances. **Step 6 — Apply Stereotypes & Refine** Use `≪entity≫`, `≪control≫`, `≪boundary≫` (from *Jacobson’s* use-case analysis) and the GRASP patterns. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Element | UML Notation | Example in ULMS | |---------|--------------|-----------------| | Class | `+————————+`
`‖ Book ‖`
`+————————+` | `Book`, `Member` | | Attribute (private) | `- title : String` | `- isbn : String` | | Attribute (public) | `+ name : String` | `+ name : String` | | Operation (public) | `+ issue() : boolean` | `+ borrowBook() : void` | | Inheritance | `———▷` (hollow triangle) | `Student` extends `Member` | | Realization (interface) | `- - -▷` (dashed hollow triangle) | `Searchable` interface | | Aggregation (weak) | `—◇—` | `Library ◇— Book` | | Composition (strong) | `—◆—` | `Book ◆— Copy` | | Multiplicity | end-label `1`, `*`, `0..*` | `Member "1" — "*" Book` | | Abstract class | *Italic* class name | `Person` (abstract) | | Static member | *Underlined* | `- maxBooks : int` (static) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Class diagrams are the *primary artefact* fed into OO programming languages (Java, C++, Python). Modern IDEs like *IntelliJ*, *Eclipse*, and *VS Code* allow **forward engineering** (diagram → code) and **reverse engineering** (code → diagram). In the context of ULMS, this means a designer can sketch the library system on a whiteboard, generate a Java skeleton, and gradually fill in business logic — drastically reducing development time. ### 2.3 The Five Visibility Modifiers in UML | Symbol | Visibility | Equivalent in Java / C++ | |--------|------------|--------------------------| | `+` | Public | `public` | | `–` | Private | `private` | | `#` | Protected | `protected` | | `~` | Package | *default / package-private* | | *Underlined* | Static | `static` | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Analytical Matrix & Class Identification ### 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style Case Study) > *"The APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) wishes to develop a **Library Management Software** for its central library. The system should maintain information about **books, journals, members, librarians, and authors**. A member can search, issue, return, and reserve books. A librarian manages the catalogue, issues library cards, and computes fines for overdue returns. Each book has a unique ISBN, a title, an author, a publisher, a category, and multiple physical copies. Members are categorized into Students, Faculty, and External Users, each with different borrowing limits. The system must record every transaction with its issue date and due date."* ### 3.2 Step 1 — Noun-Phrase Class Identification (Analytical) From the problem statement, the **candidate classes** (nouns) are: $$ \text{Candidate Classes} = \{ \text{Library}, \text{Book}, \text{Journal}, \text{Member}, \text{Librarian}, \text{Author}, \text{Publisher}, \text{Category}, \text{Transaction}, \text{Copy}, \text{Account}, \text{Fine}, \text{Reservation} \} $$ We **filter out** non-class candidates: - "Information" → too abstract, dropped. - "Date" → treated as a **type/attribute**, not a class. - "Card" → rolled into `Account` class. - "User" → redundant with `Member`. The **final class list** (12 classes) is shown in the matrix below. ### 3.3 Step 2 & 3 — Attributes and Operations Matrix The complete **CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) matrix** for the ULMS: | Class | Key Attributes (with type & visibility) | Key Operations | |-------|------------------------------------------|----------------| | `Library` | `- libId : int`
`- name : String`
`- address : String`
`- phone : String` | `+ registerMember()`
`+ addBook()`
`+ searchBook()` | | `Book` | `- isbn : String`
`- title : String`
`- edition : int`
`- price : double`
`- category : Category` | `+ getDetails()`
`+ isAvailable() : boolean` | | `Author` | `- authorId : int`
`- name : String`
`- bio : String` | `+ getBooks()` | | `Publisher` | `- pubId : int`
`- name : String`
`- address : String` | `+ getBooks()` | | `Category` | `- catId : int`
`- genreName : String` | `+ listBooks()` | | `Copy` | `- copyId : int`
`- shelfLocation : String`
`- status : enum` | `+ issue()`
`+ returnCopy()` | | `Member` *(abstract)* | `- memberId : int`
`- name : String`
`- email : String`
`- phone : String`
`- joinDate : Date` | `+ borrowBook()`
`+ returnBook()`
`+ reserveBook()` | | `Student` *(extends Member)* | `- rollNo : String`
`- department : String`
`- semester : int` | `+ viewIssuedBooks()` | | `Faculty` *(extends Member)* | `- facultyId : String`
`- designation : String` | `+ requestInterLibraryLoan()` | | `ExternalUser` *(extends Member)* | `- organization : String`
`- idProof : String` | — | | `Librarian` *(extends Person)* | `- empId : int`
`- salary : double`
`- shift : String` | `+ issueBook()`
`+ collectFine()`
`+ addNewBook()` | | `Transaction` | `- transId : int`
`- issueDate : Date`
`- dueDate : Date`
`- returnDate : Date` | `+ calculateFine() : double` | | `Account` | `- accountId : int`
`- totalFine : double` | `+ payFine()`
`+ updateFine()` | | `Fine` | `- fineId : int`
`- amount : double`
`- reason : String` | `+ calculate()` | ### 3.4 Step 4 — Relationship Derivation with Multiplicity For each candidate pair of classes, we derive the **association type and multiplicity** as follows: **Rule of Multiplicity Derivation:** 1. Read the natural-language statement between the two classes. 2. Identify the *quantifiers* (one, many, each, several). 3. Place the multiplicity at the *opposite end* of the class that owns the reference. | # | Statement in Problem | Class A | Class B | Multiplicity | Relationship | |---|----------------------|---------|---------|--------------|--------------| | 1 | "A Library contains many Books" | `Library` | `Book` | `1` — `*` | **Aggregation** (weak ownership) | | 2 | "A Book is written by one or more Authors" | `Book` | `Author` | `*` — `*` | **Association** (many-to-many) | | 3 | "A Book is published by one Publisher" | `Book` | `Publisher` | `*` — `1` | **Association** | | 4 | "A Book belongs to one Category" | `Book` | `Category` | `*` — `1` | **Association** | | 5 | "A Book has multiple physical Copies" | `Book` | `Copy` | `1` — `1..*` | **Composition** (strong) | | 6 | "A Member can borrow multiple Books" | `Member` | `Book` | `1` — `0..5` | **Association** | | 7 | "Each borrowing is recorded as a Transaction" | `Member` | `Transaction` | `1` — `*` | **Association** | | 8 | "A Transaction involves exactly one Copy" | `Transaction` | `Copy` | `*` — `1` | **Association** | | 9 | "Each Member has one Account" | `Member` | `Account` | `1` — `1` | **Composition** | | 10 | "An Account may have many Fines" | `Account` | `Fine` | `1` — `*` | **Composition** | | 11 | "A Librarian manages a Library" | `Librarian` | `Library` | `1..*` — `1` | **Association** | | 12 | "Student, Faculty, ExternalUser are types of Members" | `Member` | Subclasses | `1` — `1` | **Generalization** | ### 3.5 Why Aggregation vs Composition? — The Lifecycle Test The *deepest* theoretical distinction examiners test: - **Aggregation (◇):** If the *container* is destroyed, the *contained* object can still exist. - *Example:* If `Library` shuts down, the `Book` ISBN records in the central KTU database may still exist digitally. - **Composition (◆):** If the *container* is destroyed, the *contained* object is **guaranteed** to be destroyed. - *Example:* If a `Book` is removed from the catalogue, all its `Copy` objects are deleted too. The formal test is: $$ \text{Lifecycle Dependency} = \begin{cases} \text{Composition (◆)} & \text{if } \forall\, c \in C,\; \exists!\, p \in P \text{ such that } \text{lifetime}(c) \subseteq \text{lifetime}(p) \\ \text{Aggregation (◇)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where $C$ is the child class and $P$ is the parent class. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram for ULMS > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid `classDiagram` adheres to the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with `c`, `i`, etc.) and avoids reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) as standalone node names. Multiplicity is shown as a label between the classes; abstract methods and abstract classes are tagged using the `{abstract}` decorator (in Mermaid this is rendered via *italics* in the class name). ```mermaid classDiagram class CLibrary { -libId : int -name : String -address : String -phone : String +registerMember() void +addBook() void +searchBook() Book } class CBook { -isbn : String -title : String -edition : int -price : double +getDetails() String +isAvailable() boolean } class CAuthor { -authorId : int -name : String -bio : String +getBooks() List } class CPublisher { -pubId : int -name : String -address : String +getBooks() List } class CCategory { -catId : int -genreName : String +listBooks() List } class CCopy { -copyId : int -shelfLocation : String -status : String +issue() boolean +returnCopy() boolean } class CMember { <> -memberId : int -name : String -email : String -phone : String -joinDate : Date +borrowBook() void +returnBook() void +reserveBook() void } class CStudent { -rollNo : String -department : String -semester : int +viewIssuedBooks() List } class CFaculty { -facultyId : String -designation : String +requestInterLibraryLoan() void } class CExternalUser { -organization : String -idProof : String } class CLibrarian { -empId : int -salary : double -shift : String +issueBook() void +collectFine() double +addNewBook() void } class CTransaction { -transId : int -issueDate : Date -dueDate : Date -returnDate : Date +calculateFine() double } class CAccount { -accountId : int -totalFine : double +payFine() void +updateFine() void } class CFine { -fineId : int -amount : double -reason : String +calculate() void } class ISearchable { <> +searchByTitle() List +searchByAuthor() List } %% Inheritance / Generalization CMember <|-- CStudent CMember <|-- CFaculty CMember <|-- CExternalUser %% Realization (Interface) CBook ..|> ISearchable CMember ..|> ISearchable %% Aggregations CLibrary o-- CBook : contains 1 to * CLibrary o-- CMember : registers 1 to * CLibrary o-- CLibrarian : employs 1 to 1..* %% Associations with multiplicity CBook "1..*" -- "1..*" CAuthor : written_by CBook "*" -- "1" CPublisher : published_by CBook "*" -- "1" CCategory : categorized_as CBook "1" *-- "1..*" CCopy : has_copies CMember "1" -- "0..5" CBook : borrows CMember "1" *-- "1" CAccount : owns CAccount "1" *-- "0..*" CFine : accumulates CTransaction "*" --> "1" CCopy : involves CTransaction "*" --> "1" CMember : records CLibrarian "1..*" --> "1" CLibrary : manages ``` ### 4.2 Diagram Legend (KTU-Compliant) | UML Symbol in Mermaid | Meaning | |------------------------|---------| | `<\|--` | Generalization (inheritance) — hollow triangle | | `..\|>` | Realization (interface) — dashed hollow triangle | | `o--` | Aggregation — hollow diamond | | `*--` | Composition — filled diamond | | `-->` | Directed Association — arrow | | `--` | Undirected Association — line | | `<>` | Abstract class | | `<>` | Interface | ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures how the *static* class diagram translates into the *runtime* workflow of a librarian issuing a book. This bridges Module 1 (class diagram) with Module 3 (sequence/activity diagrams): ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Student requests Book] --> B[ISearchable.searchByTitle] B --> C{Book Available?} C -- Yes --> D[Librarian.issueBook] C -- No --> E[Reservation Queue] D --> F[Create Transaction record] F --> G[Set issueDate and dueDate] G --> H[Update Copy.status to ISSUED] H --> I[Update Member.Account.totalFine = 0] I --> J[Confirmation Receipt] style A fill:#FFE4B5 style J fill:#90EE90 style E fill:#FFB6C1 style F fill:#ADD8E6 style H fill:#DDA0DD ``` **Reading the Flowchart:** - The *boundary* object (`Student`) triggers a *control* object (`ISearchable`). - The *entity* object (`Book`, `Transaction`, `Account`) is updated. - This is precisely the **Entity–Control–Boundary (ECB)** architectural pattern — a popular KTU Module 1 / Module 2 question. ### 4.4 Class Identification Diagram — CRC Card View ``` +————————————————+ +————————————————+ +————————————————+ | Class: Book | | Class: Member | | Class: Librarian| +————————————————+ +————————————————+ +————————————————+ | Responsibilities| | Responsibilities| | Responsibilities| | - hold data | | - borrowBook | | - issueBook | | - getDetails | | - returnBook | | - collectFine | | - isAvailable | | - reserveBook | | - addNewBook | +----------------+ +----------------+ +----------------+ | Collaborators | | Collaborators | | Collaborators | | - Author | | - Book | | - Book | | - Publisher | | - Account | | - Transaction | | - Copy | | - Transaction | | - Account | +----------------+ +----------------+ +----------------+ ``` CRC (*Class-Responsibility-Collaborator*) cards are an *informal* KTU-recommended technique for brainstorming before the formal class diagram is drawn. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any **four** components of a UML class diagram.
