For each, give **one** example from a Library Management System.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Component | Example in ULMS | |---|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | **Class Name** | `Book` | | 2 | **Attribute** | `- isbn : String` | | 3 | **Operation** | `+ issue() : boolean` | | 4 | **Relationship** | `Member` *borrows* `Book` (association) | > *Award 0.75 marks for each correct component + example combination.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between **Aggregation** and **Composition** in UML with a suitable example from a University Library Management System.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Aggregation (◇):** A *weak* "has-a" relationship. The child can exist independently of the parent. *Example:* `Library ◇— Book` — even if the `Library` building is demolished, the digital record of `Book` may still survive in a database. *(1.5 marks)* - **Composition (◆):** A *strong* "owns-a" relationship. The child's lifecycle is **completely tied** to the parent. *Example:* `Book ◆— Copy` — when a `Book` is permanently removed from the catalogue, all its physical `Copy` records are deleted automatically. *(1.5 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Choice (14 Marks) > **Internal-Choice Note:** Per the KTU 2024 Scheme, *exactly one* of the following two questions is to be answered. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Class Diagram + Justification **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Identify the **classes**, **attributes**, and **operations** for the following scenario: *"A university library maintains books, members, and librarians. Members can issue and return books. Librarians issue library cards and collect fines."* Use proper **visibility modifiers** and **types** for all attributes. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Draw the **complete UML Class Diagram** for the above scenario with proper **multiplicity** and **relationships** (association, aggregation, generalization). Use the **Mermaid** or any standard UML notation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Class Identification & Attribute-Operation Table (7 marks):** | Class | Attributes (with visibility & type) | Operations | |-------|-------------------------------------|-----------| | `Library` | `- libId : int`
`- name : String` | `+ addBook()`
`+ search()` | | `Book` | `- isbn : String`
`- title : String`
`- author : String`
`- price : double`
`- isAvailable : boolean` | `+ issueBook() : boolean`
`+ returnBook() : void`
`+ reserve() : void` | | `Member` | `- memberId : int`
`- name : String`
`- email : String`
`- phone : String` | `+ borrowBook() : void`
`+ returnBook() : void`
`+ viewFine() : double` | | `Librarian` | `- empId : int`
`- name : String`
`- salary : double` | `+ issueCard() : void`
`+ collectFine() : void`
`+ manageBooks() : void` | | `Fine` | `- fineId : int`
`- amount : double`
`- reason : String` | `+ calculate() : void` | | `Transaction` | `- transId : int`
`- issueDate : Date`
`- dueDate : Date` | `+ computeDueDate() : Date` | *Marking:* 1 mark per correctly identified class + 0.5 marks per correct attribute (capped at 7). **(b) Complete UML Class Diagram (7 marks):** ```mermaid classDiagram class CLibrary { -libId : int -name : String +addBook() void +search() Book } class CBook { -isbn : String -title : String -author : String -price : double -isAvailable : boolean +issueBook() boolean +returnBook() void +reserve() void } class CMember { -memberId : int -name : String -email : String -phone : String +borrowBook() void +returnBook() void +viewFine() double } class CLibrarian { -empId : int -name : String -salary : double +issueCard() void +collectFine() void +manageBooks() void } class CFine { -fineId : int -amount : double -reason : String +calculate() void } class CTransaction { -transId : int -issueDate : Date -dueDate : Date +computeDueDate() Date } CLibrary o-- CBook : contains CLibrary o-- CMember : registers CLibrary o-- CLibrarian : employs CMember "1" -- "0..5" CBook : borrows CMember "1" *-- "0..*" CFine : incurs CMember "1" -- "*" CTransaction : performs CTransaction "*" --> "1" CBook : records CLibrarian "1" -- "*" CTransaction : supervises ``` *Marking Scheme (7 marks):* | Element | Marks | |---------|-------| | Correct identification of 4+ classes | 2 | | Correct attributes with visibility | 1 | | Correct operations | 1 | | Correct multiplicity labels | 1 | | Correct relationship symbols (◇/◆) | 1 | | Neatness and proper notation | 1 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Inheritance & Interface Modelling **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Explain the concept of **generalization** and **realization** in UML with reference to the ULMS. Show how `Member` can be a *base class* for `Student`, `Faculty`, and `ExternalUser` and how the `ISearchable` interface can be realized by both `Book` and `Member`. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Draw the **UML class diagram** showing this inheritance and interface realization, including one **abstract method** in `Member` and one **abstract method** in `ISearchable`. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Theory (7 marks):** - **Generalization:** A *"is-a-kind-of"* relationship represented by a solid line with a **hollow triangle** pointing from the subclass to the superclass. It captures *inheritance* in OOP. In ULMS, `Student`, `Faculty`, and `ExternalUser` are all *kinds of* `Member`. They inherit the attributes (`name`, `email`) and operations (`borrowBook`, `returnBook`) of `Member` and may override or extend them. *(3 marks)* - **Realization:** A relationship between a *class* and an *interface* in which the class *promises* to implement the operations declared in the interface. Represented by a **dashed line with a hollow triangle**. The `ISearchable` interface declares methods like `searchByTitle()` and `searchByAuthor()`. Both `Book` and `Member` realize this interface because both need to support search functionality. *(3 marks)* - **Abstract Methods:** An abstract method has *no implementation* in the base class and must be overridden. In `Member`, `calculateBorrowLimit()` is abstract (each subclass defines its own — students get 5 books, faculty get 10). In `ISearchable`, both `searchByTitle` and `searchByAuthor` are abstract. *(1 mark)* **(b) Class Diagram with Inheritance & Interface (7 marks):** ```mermaid classDiagram class IMember { <> +searchByTitle() List +searchByAuthor() List } class CMember { <> -memberId : int -name : String -email : String -phone : String +borrowBook() void +returnBook() void +calculateBorrowLimit() int } class CStudent { -rollNo : String -department : String -semester : int +calculateBorrowLimit() int } class CFaculty { -facultyId : String -designation : String +calculateBorrowLimit() int } class CExternalUser { -organization : String -idProof : String +calculateBorrowLimit() int } class CBook { -isbn : String -title : String +searchByTitle() List +searchByAuthor() List } CMember <|-- CStudent CMember <|-- CFaculty CMember <|-- CExternalUser CBook ..|> IMember CMember ..|> IMember ``` *Marking Scheme (7 marks):* | Element | Marks | |---------|-------| | Correct use of `<>` stereotype | 1 | | Correct use of `<>` stereotype | 1 | | Three generalized subclasses with attributes | 2 | | Hollow triangle for inheritance | 1 | | Dashed hollow triangle for realization | 1 | | Abstract methods shown | 1 | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:** > > 1. **Forgetting Multiplicity:** Drawing a class diagram with *only lines* between classes and *no multiplicity labels* costs **at least 1.5 marks** per relationship. KTU examiners explicitly test whether you understand *one-to-many*, *many-to-many*, etc. > > 2. **Confusing Aggregation vs Composition:** Students frequently use the *filled diamond* (composition) where a *hollow diamond* (aggregation) is correct. **Memory trick:** *Hollow = Has-a (Heir can still live); Filled = Part-of (Part dies with whole).* > > 3. **Mixing Inheritance and Realization Arrows:** Inheritance is a **solid** line with a hollow triangle. Realization (interface) is a **dashed** line with a hollow triangle. Confusing them costs marks. > > 4. **No Visibility Modifiers on Attributes:** All attributes should ideally have a `+`, `–`, or `#` symbol. Omitting visibility implies *package-private* and loses you **0.5 mark per attribute** in a strict valuation. > > 5. **Over-Modelling:** Adding 20+ classes where 8 are enough. KTU expects a *minimal, complete* design — not a kitchen-sink model. > > 6. **Missing the `<>` or `<>` stereotype:** This is an *instant 1-mark deduction* in any diagram question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:** - A **class diagram** is a *static* structural diagram showing classes, attributes, operations, and relationships. - The **three-tier structure** of a class is: *Name* | *Attributes* | *Operations*. - The **five UML relationships** are: Association, Aggregation (◇), Composition (◆), Generalization (▷), Realization (- - -▷), Dependency (- - -▷). - **Aggregation** = weak "has-a"; **Composition** = strong "owns-a" with *lifecycle dependency*. - **Inheritance** is denoted by a *hollow triangle on a solid line*. **Interface realization** uses a *hollow triangle on a dashed line*. - **Multiplicity** is placed at the **opposite end** of the class that holds the reference. Common values: `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`. - **Visibility** modifiers: `+` (public), `–` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package). - **Stereotypes**: `<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`. - In ULMS, **core classes** are: `Library`, `Book`, `Copy`, `Author`, `Publisher`, `Category`, `Member` (abstract), `Student`, `Faculty`, `ExternalUser`, `Librarian`, `Transaction`, `Account`, `Fine`. - **Core relationships**: - `Library ◇— Book` (aggregation) - `Book ◆— Copy` (composition) - `Member ▷ Student, Faculty, ExternalUser` (generalization) - `Member — *— Book` (association, 1 to 0..5) - `Book *— *— Author` (many-to-many association) - **Identification technique**: *Noun-phrase analysis* for classes, *adjective analysis* for attributes, *verb analysis* for operations. - **CRC cards** (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) are an *informal* precursor to the formal class diagram. - **Entity-Control-Boundary (ECB) pattern** helps classify classes by *role* in the system. - The class diagram is the *backbone* of OOAD and feeds into *sequence diagrams* (Module 3) and *state-chart diagrams* (Module 4). - For KTU, the **14-mark question** typically asks: *"Identify classes/attributes/operations (7 marks) + Draw class diagram with multiplicity and relationships (7 marks)."* - Always label **direction** of association (use a verb) — e.g., `Member *— borrows — 1..5 Book`. - An **abstract class** name is written in *italics* in standard UML (or tagged `<>` in Mermaid). - A **static** attribute or operation is **underlined** — e.g., `- maxBorrowLimit : int` (static). > **End of Module 1 — Case Study: Class Diagram for University Library Management Software**

topics.title: Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Unified Modeling Language (UML) 2.5** specification adopted by KTU under the **2024 Scheme**, an **Interaction** is a behavioural unit that captures the **message-based collaboration** among a set of participating objects (lifelines) within a particular context, in order to fulfil a specific use-case or system operation. 

Formally, the UML 2.5 superstructure defines an Interaction as:

> *"An Interaction is a behavior that comprises a set of messages that are exchanged among a set of ConnectableInstances (typically Objects/Lifelines) within a Classifier/Collaboration context, together with their execution ordering constraints."*

Interactions are a sub-kind of **Behavior** in the UML metamodel and serve as the bridge between **structural models** (Class Diagrams) and **dynamic models** (State Machines, Activity Diagrams).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of *PECGT411* explicitly mandates study of the **four interaction diagrams**: **Sequence Diagram, Communication Diagram, Timing Diagram, and Interaction Overview Diagram**, along with **Combined Fragments** (interaction fragments) such as `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `seq`, `strict`, `neg`, `critical`, `break`, and `ref`.

---

## 1.2 Why "Interactions" Exist in UML

A Class Diagram alone tells us **what** the system contains. It says nothing about **how** objects collaborate at runtime. Interactions answer the dynamic question:

- **Which object initiates?** → Origin of message.
- **To which object?** → Receiver lifeline.
- **In what order?** → Sequencing via numbering or vertical time-axis.
- **Under what conditions?** → Branching via combined fragments.
- **For how long?** → Timing constraints and duration observations.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Stage Play 🎭**
> 
> Think of an OO software system as a **theatre stage play**.
> - Each **actor** entering the stage = an **Object lifeline** in UML.
> - Each **dialogue line spoken from one actor to another** = a **Message** in UML.
> - The **chronological order** of dialogues on the script = a **Sequence Diagram**.
> - The same play re-drawn with arrows between actors and **numbered scripts** instead of vertical time = a **Communication Diagram**.
> - A **scene-by-scene flowchart of when each sub-plot is enacted** = an **Interaction Overview Diagram**.
> - A **director’s timing sheet** showing when each spotlight is on/off = a **Timing Diagram**.
> - **Conditional scripts** ("If the hero enters, say A; otherwise, say B") = **Combined Fragments** (`alt`, `opt`).
> - **Looping rehearsals** = `loop` fragment.
> - **Concurrent sub-plots happening together** = `par` fragment.
> 
> UML Interactions are simply the **formal script notation** that UML provides to model such a play rigorously.

---

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of UML Interaction Diagrams

| Diagram | KTU 2024 Weightage | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence Diagram** | ★★★★★ (Most frequent in exams) | Emphasises **time-ordered** message exchange |
| **Communication Diagram** | ★★★☆☆ | Emphasises **object linkages** + numbered messages |
| **Timing Diagram** | ★★☆☆☆ | Emphasises **state/timing constraints** on lifelines |
| **Interaction Overview Diagram** | ★★★★☆ | **Activity-diagram** of nested interactions |

---

## 1.5 When to Use Which Interaction Diagram (KTU Recommendation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Heuristic:**
> - **Use Sequence Diagram** → when *time ordering* matters (default choice, always marks-full).
> - **Use Communication Diagram** → when *link/association structure* is more important than time.
> - **Use Timing Diagram** → in **real-time / embedded systems** with hard timing deadlines.
> - **Use Interaction Overview Diagram** → when one use-case contains **multiple sub-scenarios** that branch/loop/reuse.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an Interaction

Every UML 2.5 Interaction consists of the following **metamodel elements**:

1. **Lifeline** — A vertical line representing the existence of a participating instance over time. Each lifeline has:
   - **Head (UML 2 notation):** A rectangle (called a *head*) at the top containing the lifeline name (e.g., `:OrderProcessor`).
   - **Selector:** Optional instance name in front of the colon (e.g., `orderProc1:OrderProcessor`).
   - **Decomposition:** A lifeline may be *decomposed* into its internal interaction using the `ref` fragment.
2. **Message** — A communication between two lifelines carrying information or invoking an operation.
3. **Execution Specification (Activation)** — The thin/fat rectangle on a lifeline indicating the period during which an object is performing an action.
4. **Combined Fragment** — A rectangular enclosure containing one or more **Interaction Operands** guarded by an **Interaction Operator** (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `seq`, `strict`, `neg`, `critical`, `break`, `ref`).
5. **State Invariant** — A condition placed on a lifeline that must hold at that moment.
6. **Time Constraint / Duration Constraint** — Labelled curly-brace braces `{t < 200 ms}`.
7. **Gate** — The endpoint of an interaction used when it is referenced (nested or reused).

---

## 2.2 Types of Messages

| Message Kind | UML Symbol | Semantics | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Solid arrowhead → filled triangle | Sender waits for reply | `getBalance()` |
| **Asynchronous** | Solid arrowhead → open arrow | Sender proceeds immediately | `fireEvent()` |
| **Return / Reply** | Dashed arrow → open arrow | Return value of synchronous call | Implicit (often omitted) |
| **Self-Message (Recursive)** | Solid arrowhead looping back on same lifeline | Object calls its own operation | `this.validate()` |
| **Found Message** | Arrow originating from a filled black circle | Message whose sender is outside scope | Actor → System |

---

## 2.3 Combined Fragment Operators (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is a **favourite KTU question** — be ready to draw any of these fragments in your exam.

| Operator | Operand Count | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `alt` | ≥ 2 | **Alternative** — mutually exclusive guarded fragments (like `switch-case`) |
| `opt` | 1 | **Optional** — runs only when guard is true (like `if` without `else`) |
| `loop` | ≥ 1 | **Repeated** execution; supports `[min..max]` iteration bounds |
| `par` | ≥ 1 | **Parallel** — operands execute concurrently |
| `seq` | ≥ 2 | **Weak sequencing** — weak ordering among operands |
| `strict` | ≥ 2 | **Strict sequencing** — every message in operand A before any in operand B |
| `neg` | 1 | **Negative** — shows an invalid trace (used in assertions/tests) |
| `critical` | 1 | **Atomic region** — cannot be interleaved with other concurrent traces |
| `break` | 1 | **Break** — exits enclosing interaction |
| `ref` | 1 | **Reference** — invokes a nested sub-interaction |

---

## 2.4 Interaction Fragment Guards

Each `alt`/`opt`/`loop` operand may carry a **guard expression** in square brackets at the top-left of its divider line. Guards must be **mutually exclusive** and **collectively exhaustive** in a properly written `alt`.

Example: `[balance >= amount]` vs `[balance < amount]`.

---

## 2.5 Interaction Overview Diagram (IOD) — Internal Structure

An **Interaction Overview Diagram** is **syntactically an Activity Diagram** in which the *actions* are replaced by **`InteractionUse`** (ref) elements pointing to sub-interactions. Control flow follows the activity edge semantics (initial node, decision, fork, join, merge, final node).

IOD operators:

- **`ref`** → references a nested sequence/communication diagram.
- **`InteractionUse`** with parameter passing: `ref("subFlow")` plus binding `parameter=actual`.
- **Decision / Merge nodes** → drive conditional selection.
- **Fork / Join nodes** → drive concurrency.
- **Activity-final node** → terminates the IOD.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Interaction-Diagram Use |
|---|---|
| **Banking / FinTech** | Validating transaction flow, exception branches |
| **Telecom (4G/5G call setup)** | Sequencing of protocol messages (SIP/SDP) |
| **Embedded / Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Timing deadlines in ECU communication |
| **IoT / Smart Home** | Async event-driven sensor-to-actuator flows |
| **Healthcare (HL7/FHIR)** | Patient-record retrieval sequence |
| **E-commerce checkout** | Order processing + parallel payment validation |

---

## 2.7 KTU 2024 Reference Notation Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Always draw diagrams left-to-right with time flowing TOP → DOWN.** This is the KTU convention expected by examiners.

| Notation | Visual |
|---|---|
| Lifeline head | Rectangle `[instanceName : ClassName]` |
| Sync message | `───────▶` (filled triangle) |
| Async message | `───────▷` (open arrow) |
| Return message | `─ ─ ─ ─ ▷` (dashed, open arrow) |
| Activation bar | Tall thin/fat rectangle on lifeline |
| Self message | Looped arrow back to same lifeline |
| Combined fragment | Rectangle enclosing labelled pentagon tag (`alt`, `opt`, ...) |
| Time constraint | `{now1 – now2 < 500 ms}` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — ATM Withdrawal Scenario

This exhaustive worked example demonstrates **every element** of UML Interactions as expected in KTU 2024 evaluation. The use-case is: **"Customer withdraws cash from an ATM"**.

---

## 3.1 Identify the Lifelines (Participants)

| # | Lifeline | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | `customer : Customer` | Actor | Initiates the flow |
| L2 | `atm : ATMController` | Boundary | UI / hardware interface |
| L3 | `bank : BankServer` | Entity | Network-side bank |
| L4 | `account : Account` | Entity | Persistent account record |

---

## 3.2 Identify the Message Flow (Sequence)

The flow consists of these steps in order:

1. `insertCard(cardInfo)` → from Customer to ATM
2. `requestPIN()` → from ATM to Customer *(asynchronous prompt)*
3. `enterPIN(pin)` → from Customer to ATM *(asynchronous, fill-the-pin pad)*
4. `validate(pin)` → synchronous call to Bank
5. `lookupAccount(cardNo)` → synchronous from Bank to Account
6. `getBalance()` → Account → Bank
7. `selectWithdrawal(amount)` → Customer → ATM
8. `debit(amount)` → Bank → Account
9. `dispenseCash(amount)` → ATM (self-invocation of mechanical unit)
10. `ejectCard()` → ATM self-message
11. `printReceipt()` → ATM self-message
12. Reply (implicit return values at each synchronous call).

---

## 3.3 Identify the Branching / Looping

- **`opt`**: PIN is verified only after `validate(pin)` returns success → guard `[valid]`.
- **`alt`**: 
  - Branch A: `[balance >= amount]` → dispense cash.
  - Branch B: `[balance < amount]` → show "Insufficient Funds".
- **`loop`**: The customer may retry PIN entry up to 3 times.
- **`par`**: While cash is being dispensed, the receipt may be printed concurrently.

---

## 3.4 Derived Sequence Diagram Specification (Textual Notation)

Below is the **UML 2.5 textual/structured specification** of the interaction, written in the formal interaction-fragment style. This is often a 14-mark KTU Part B sub-question.

```text
sd ATM_Withdrawal {
    lifeline customer : Customer
    lifeline atm      : ATMController
    lifeline bank     : BankServer
    lifeline account  : Account

    customer → atm : insertCard(cardInfo)
    atm → customer : requestPIN()  (async)
    loop [retries < 3] {
        customer → atm : enterPIN(pin)  (async)
        atm → bank  : validate(pin)
        bank → account : lookupAccount(cardNo)
        account → bank : balance
        opt [valid] {
            customer → atm : selectWithdrawal(amount)
            alt [balance >= amount] {
                bank → account : debit(amount)
                account → bank : ok
                par {
                    atm → atm : dispenseCash(amount)
                } and {
                    atm → atm : printReceipt()
                }
            } else [balance < amount] {
                atm → customer : displayMessage("Insufficient Funds")
            }
        } else [!valid] {
            atm → customer : displayMessage("Invalid PIN")
        }
        atm → customer : ejectCard()   (async)
    }
}
```

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Communication Diagram Equivalent

A **Communication Diagram** shows the **same messages** but emphasises **associations between objects** with **sequence numbers** instead of vertical time. The numbering rules are:

> **Rule:** Predecessor's number → current message's number.
> - First message from `customer`: `1: insertCard(cardInfo)`
> - Synchronous call from `atm` to `bank`: `1.1: validate(pin)` (because it is nested inside message 1)
> - Bank to Account: `1.1.1: lookupAccount(cardNo)`
> - Account back to Bank: `1.1.1.1: balance`

Complete numbering:

```
customer → atm      : 1  : insertCard(cardInfo)
atm      → customer : 1.1: requestPIN()
customer → atm      : 2  : enterPIN(pin)
atm      → bank     : 2.1: validate(pin)
bank     → account  : 2.1.1: lookupAccount(cardNo)
account  → bank     : 2.1.1.1: balance
customer → atm      : 3  : selectWithdrawal(amount)
bank     → account  : 3.1: debit(amount)
account  → bank     : 3.1.1: ok
atm      → atm      : 3.2: dispenseCash(amount)
atm      → atm      : 3.3: printReceipt()
atm      → customer : 4  : ejectCard()
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Numbering convention (UML 2.5):** Each nested message appends one digit level. The leading number denotes call depth. This is a **frequent KTU short-answer question**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Elements to Diagram Types

| UML Element | Sequence | Communication | Timing | IOD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lifeline | ✔ Head + dashed line | ✔ Head + line | ✔ Head + state line | ✘ (lives inside refs) |
| Time-order emphasis | ✔ Vertical axis | ✘ | ✔ X-axis = time | ✔ Flow |
| Message numbering | ✘ | ✔ Decimal labels | ✘ | ✘ |
| Combined fragments | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ |
| Timing constraint `{t}` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ (primary) | ✘ |
| Ref to sub-interaction | ✔ (`ref`) | ✔ (`ref`) | ✔ (`ref`) | ✔ (action) |
| Activity-node decision/merge | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |

---

## 3.7 Validating the Interaction Against UML 2.5 Semantics

A complete KTU answer must verify:

1. **Total order** of message events within a single lifeline.
2. **Partial order** across different lifelines.
3. **Causality**: an asynchronous reply cannot precede its synchronous call.
4. **Guard mutual exclusivity** in `alt`.
5. **Loop bounds** `[min..max]` or guard expression.
6. **Synchronous message return value** semantics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of UML Interaction Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Behavior Hierarchy] --> B0[Interaction]
    B0 --> C0[Sequence Diagram]
    B0 --> D0[Communication Diagram]
    B0 --> E0[Timing Diagram]
    B0 --> F0[Interaction Overview Diagram]

    C0 --> C1[Lifeline]
    C0 --> C2[Message]
    C0 --> C3[Execution Spec]
    C0 --> C4[Combined Fragment]
    C0 --> C5[State Invariant]

    D0 --> D1[Link between lifelines]
    D0 --> D2[Numbered message]

    E0 --> E1[State-line vs Time-axis]
    E0 --> E2[Duration constraint]

    F0 --> F1[Activity nodes]
    F0 --> F2[InteractionUse ref]

    C4 --> G0[alt]
    C4 --> G1[opt]
    C4 --> G2[loop]
    C4 --> G3[par]
    C4 --> G4[seq]
    C4 --> G5[strict]
    C4 --> G6[neg]
    C4 --> G7[critical]
    C4 --> G8[break]
    C4 --> G9[ref]
```

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram Schematic — ATM Withdrawal Flow

The following block-level functional flow reproduces the vertical time-axis semantics of a UML Sequence Diagram for the **ATM Withdrawal** use case.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant cust as Customer
    participant atmCtl as ATM Controller
    participant bank as Bank Server
    participant acct as Account

    cust ->> atmCtl: 1 insertCard cardInfo
    atmCtl -->> cust: 1.1 requestPIN async
    loop retry up to 3 times
        cust -->> atmCtl: 2 enterPIN
        atmCtl ->> bank: 2.1 validate pin
        bank ->> acct: 2.1.1 lookupAccount
        acct -->> bank: 2.1.1.1 balance
        alt valid
            cust ->> atmCtl: 3 selectWithdrawal amount
            alt balance sufficient
                bank ->> acct: 3.1 debit amount
                acct -->> bank: 3.1.1 ok
                par concurrent
                    atmCtl ->> atmCtl: 3.2 dispenseCash
                    atmCtl ->> atmCtl: 3.3 printReceipt
                end
            else balance insufficient
                atmCtl -->> cust: show insufficient funds
            end
        else invalid
            atmCtl -->> cust: show invalid PIN
        end
        atmCtl -->> cust: 4 ejectCard
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Communication Diagram Schematic — Same Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cNode["customer Customer"]:::actor
    aNode["atm ATM Controller"]:::boundary
    bNode["bank Bank Server"]:::entity
    acNode["account Account"]:::entity

    cNode -- "1 insertCard cardInfo" --> aNode
    aNode -- "1.1 requestPIN async" --> cNode
    cNode -- "2 enterPIN" --> aNode
    aNode -- "2.1 validate pin" --> bNode
    bNode -- "2.1.1 lookupAccount" --> acNode
    acNode -- "2.1.1.1 balance" --> bNode
    cNode -- "3 selectWithdrawal amount" --> aNode
    bNode -- "3.1 debit amount" --> acNode
    acNode -- "3.1.1 ok" --> bNode
    aNode -- "3.2 dispenseCash" --> aNode
    aNode -- "3.3 printReceipt" --> aNode
    aNode -- "4 ejectCard" --> cNode

    classDef actor fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef boundary fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef entity fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0b6b0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class cNode actor
    class aNode boundary
    class bNode,acNode entity
```

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram Schematic — Real-Time Cash Dispense

```mermaid
gantt
    title Timing Diagram — ATM Cash Dispense and Receipt Print
    dateFormat  s
    axisFormat  %S.%L ms

    section Cash Mechanism
    Pickup request received :done, pickup, 0, 50ms
    Count notes              :active, count, 50, 300ms
    Move notes to slot       :slot, 300, 200ms
    Eject to customer        :eject, 500, 100ms

    section Receipt Printer
    Buffer transaction       :buffer, 0, 80ms
    Render receipt           :render, 80, 250ms
    Print and cut            :print, 330, 200ms
    Eject receipt            :receipt, 530, 100ms

    section Card Reader
    Read card data           :crit, read, 0, 60ms
    Return card to slot      :ret, 60, 100ms
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Gantt chart emulates a UML Timing Diagram. In actual KTU exams, draw the **state-line vs. time-axis** rectangle diagram with explicit `{duration}` constraints on transitions.

---

## 4.5 Interaction Overview Diagram Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode([init]):::start
    nodeA[/"ref AuthenticateUser"/]:::ref
    nodeB[/"ref CheckBalance"/]:::ref
    nodeD{Daily limit OK?}:::decision
    nodeE[/"ref ProcessWithdrawal"/]:::ref
    nodeF[/"ref PrintReceipt"/]:::ref
    nodeG[/"ref DeclineTransaction"/]:::ref
    finalNode([end]):::end

    initNode --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD -- yes --> nodeE
    nodeD -- no  --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> finalNode
    nodeG --> finalNode

    classDef start fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#0b6b0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end   fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#a30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ref   fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.6 Combined Fragment — Operator Selection Heuristic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Need conditional branching?] -->|Yes| A2[Two or more mutually exclusive paths?]
    A2 -->|Yes| A3[Use alt fragment]
    A2 -->|No single path| A4[Use opt fragment]
    A1 -->|No| A5[Need repetition?]
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Use loop with min..max]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Need concurrency?]
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Use par fragment]
    A7 -->|No| A9[Need strict ordering?]
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Use strict or seq fragment]
    A9 -->|No| A11[Reference sub-interaction?]
    A11 -->|Yes| A12[Use ref fragment]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read before answering!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Communication Diagram numbering with time.** Numbers are **call-nesting levels**, *not* seconds.
> 2. **Synchronous messages MUST show an activation rectangle** on the receiver lifeline. Drawing only the arrow → 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Combined fragments must carry the operator tag** (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`) inside a small pentagon at the top-left corner of the fragment rectangle.
> 4. **Guards in `alt` must be mutually exclusive.** `[x>0]` and `[x>5]` will be marked down — use `[x>0]` and `[x≤0]`.
> 5. **`par` (parallel) vs `seq` (weak sequence)** is frequently interchanged wrongly. Remember: `par` = truly concurrent; `seq` = ordering allowed but not strictly enforced.
> 6. **Return messages are OPTIONAL** in UML 2.5; drawing them is acceptable but not compulsory. Drawing them wrong (filled arrow) costs marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE combined fragments used in UML interaction diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

A **combined fragment** in UML 2.5 is a region of an interaction whose execution is governed by an interaction operator. Three commonly used fragments are:

1. **`alt` (Alternative):** Models mutually exclusive choices. The interaction is divided into multiple operands separated by dashed lines, each with a guard condition. Only the operand whose guard evaluates to `true` executes. Analogous to a `switch-case` construct.

2. **`opt` (Optional):** Models a single conditional path. The operand executes *only if* its guard condition evaluates to `true`. Analogous to an `if` without an `else` branch.

3. **`loop` (Loop):** Models repeated execution of an operand. May be specified with explicit iteration bounds `[min..max]` (e.g., `[1..5]`) or with a guard expression. Used to represent retry logic, polling, or batched operations.

**(Each fragment ≈ 1 mark = 3 marks.)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Sequence Diagram and a Communication Diagram in UML.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sequence Diagram | Communication Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Axis** | Time (vertical, top-down) | Object topology (links) |
| **Message Order** | Visual (top→bottom) | Numerical (e.g., `1.1.1`) |
| **Object Links** | Implicit | Explicit (drawn as edges) |
| **Best For** | Temporal flow analysis | Structural/architectural reasoning |
| **Activation Bars** | Shown on lifelines | Not typically shown |
| **Combined Fragments** | Fully supported | Fully supported |
| **KTU Preference** | More marks (≥7) | Usually asked as supportive (≤5) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, Module 2 offers an **internal choice** between two full questions. Either Q.A or Q.B must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q.A.** *Consider the use-case "User logs in to a web-based Learning Management System (LMS)". The flow involves a User, a Login Page (boundary), an Authenticator (controller), and a User Database (entity). The system must:*
*(a)* Draw a complete **UML Sequence Diagram** showing login success and failure branches, including a **loop fragment for up to 3 retries**, an **alt fragment** for valid vs invalid credentials, and **parallel printing of audit log and email notification** on success. *(**7 marks**)*
*(b)* Convert the same scenario into a **UML Communication Diagram** with proper message numbering, and briefly explain the meaning of the prefix `2.1.1` on one of the messages. *(**7 marks**)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Sequence Diagram**

**Lifelines:**

- `u : User` (actor)
- `lp : LoginPage` (boundary)
- `auth : Authenticator` (controller)
- `db : UserDB` (entity)
- `mail : MailService` (entity)

**Step-by-step construction:**

```text
sd LMS_Login {
    lifeline u : User
    lifeline lp : LoginPage
    lifeline auth : Authenticator
    lifeline db : UserDB
    lifeline mail : MailService

    u → lp : enterCredentials(username, password)
    loop [retryCount < 3] {
        lp → auth : validate(u, p)
        auth → db : fetchUser(username)
        db → auth : userRecord
        alt [credentials valid] {
            auth → lp : sessionToken
            par {
                auth → db : writeAuditLog(userId, timestamp)
            } and {
                auth → mail : sendLoginNotification(email)
            }
            lp → u : displayDashboard()
        } else [credentials invalid] {
            auth → lp : errorMessage
            lp → u : displayError("Invalid credentials")
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark-split guide:**
- Correct lifelines drawn → **2 marks**
- Correct sync vs async arrowheads → **1 mark**
- `loop` fragment with guard `[retryCount < 3]` → **1 mark**
- `alt` fragment with two mutually exclusive guards → **1 mark**
- `par` fragment for audit + mail → **1 mark**
- Return messages / overall readability → **1 mark**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Communication Diagram**

**Lifelines (same as above).** Numbered messages:

```
u    → lp   : 1  : enterCredentials(username, password)
lp   → auth : 1.1 : validate(u, p)
auth → db   : 1.1.1 : fetchUser(username)
db   → auth : 1.1.1.1 : userRecord
auth → lp   : 1.2 : sessionToken
auth → db   : 1.3 : writeAuditLog(userId, ts)
auth → mail : 1.4 : sendLoginNotification(email)
lp   → u    : 1.5 : displayDashboard()
```

**Explanation of prefix `2.1.1`:**
The prefix encodes the **call nesting hierarchy**:
- `2` → outer message number 2.
- `.1` → first nested message invoked as a result of message 2.
- `.1` (final) → second-level nested message, invoked as a result of `2.1`.

Thus `2.1.1` indicates the **third-level depth** in the interaction: a message triggered from within the reply of a call that itself was triggered by call number 2. *(3 marks for this explanation.)*

**Mark-split guide (Q.A(b)):** Correct link drawing → 2 marks, Correct message numbering → 3 marks, Explanation of `2.1.1` → 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q.B.** *(a)* Construct a **UML Interaction Overview Diagram** for an Online Food Ordering use-case with the following nested interactions:
1. Authenticate User
2. View Restaurant Menu
3. **Decision:** Item in stock?
   - **Yes** → Add to Cart → Make Payment → Send Order Confirmation
   - **No** → Display "Out of Stock" → Suggest Alternatives
4. End.
Use `ref` interaction-uses appropriately. *(**7 marks**)*

*(b)* Draw a **UML Timing Diagram** for an embedded temperature-monitoring system where the sensor samples every 100 ms, the controller processes for 40 ms, and the actuator toggles a fan for 200 ms whenever temperature exceeds 30 °C. Show one full cycle including the duration constraint `{now2 − now1 ≤ 50 ms}` between sample-acquisition and controller-receive. *(**7 marks**)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Interaction Overview Diagram**

**Nodes (actions → InteractionUses):**

| Node | Kind | Content |
|---|---|---|
| N1 | Initial node | `●` |
| N2 | `ref AuthenticateUser` | references a sub-sequence |
| N3 | `ref ViewRestaurantMenu` | references a sub-sequence |
| N4 | Decision node | `Item in stock?` |
| N5 | `ref AddToCart` | |
| N6 | `ref MakePayment` | |
| N7 | `ref SendOrderConfirmation` | |
| N8 | `ref DisplayOutOfStock` | |
| N9 | `ref SuggestAlternatives` | |
| N10 | Activity-final node | `◉` |

**Edges:**
- N1 → N2 → N3 → N4
- N4 —[yes]→ N5 → N6 → N7 → N10
- N4 —[no] → N8 → N9 → N10

**Mark-split:** Correct initial/final nodes (1), Decision with two edges and labelled guards (2), All five `ref` nodes correctly named (2), Activity edges in correct sequence (2).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Timing Diagram**

**State-lines (Y-axis participants, X-axis time):**

```
Sensor       : idle ──── sampling ───── idle ──── sampling ───── idle ...
                │           │                              │
                t0          t1                             t2

Controller   : ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ processing ──── idle ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ processing
                                  │                            │
                                  t1'                           t2'

Actuator     : ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ OFF ─ ─ ─ ON (200 ms) ─ ─ OFF
```

**Time / Duration Constraints (written along the transitions):**

- Sample period: `{now1 − now0 = 100 ms}`
- Sample-to-controller latency: `{now2 − now1 ≤ 50 ms}`
- Actuator toggle window: `{now3 − now2 = 200 ms}`
- Threshold predicate on controller state-line: `[temp > 30 °C]`.

**Mark-split:** Three state-lines correctly named (2), Time axis with units and labels (2), One duration constraint correctly shown (2), Conditional threshold note (1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 / Interactions**

- ✅ **Interaction** = a Behaviour describing message exchange among lifelines.
- ✅ **Four interaction diagrams**: Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview.
- ✅ **Sequence Diagram** = vertical time axis; **Communication Diagram** = numbered arrows on object graph.
- ✅ **Synchronous message** → filled triangle; **Asynchronous** → open arrow; **Return** → dashed open arrow.
- ✅ **Activation rectangle** MUST be drawn on receiver for synchronous calls.
- ✅ **`alt`** → mutually exclusive guards (like `if-else if`).
- ✅ **`opt`** → single guarded path (like `if`).
- ✅ **`loop`** → repetition; supports `[min..max]` and `[condition]` guards.
- ✅ **`par`** → parallel concurrency; **`seq`** → weak sequencing.
- ✅ **`strict`** → strict ordering; **`neg`** → invalid trace (testing); **`critical`** → atomic region; **`break`** → exit enclosing; **`ref`** → reference sub-interaction.
- ✅ **Message numbering rule** in Communication Diagram: each nested call appends one level (`1` → `1.1` → `1.1.1`).
- ✅ **Interaction Overview Diagram** = Activity Diagram whose actions are `InteractionUse (ref)` nodes.
- ✅ **Timing Diagram** = state-line vs time; used for real-time/embedded systems; supports `{duration}` constraints.
- ✅ **KTU examiners expect diagrams drawn TOP-DOWN for Sequence** and **LEFT-RIGHT for Communication**, with **time clearly labelled**.
- ✅ **Guard expressions** must be enclosed in **square brackets `[ ... ]`** at the top-left of the divider line.
- ✅ **Pentagon tag** at the top-left corner of a combined fragment rectangle indicates the operator.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Combined Fragments → "ALOOPS-CS-NCB":**
> **A**lt, **L**oop, **O**pt, **O**ops (*seq*), **P**ar, **S**trict — **C**ritical, **S**trict (alt already covered) — **N**eg, **C**ritical (dup), **B**reak. Memorise in that order for quick recall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Messaging - Synchronous and Asynchronous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Messaging - Synchronous and Asynchronous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5)**, *messaging* is the fundamental mechanism through which objects (lifelines) communicate within **Interaction diagrams** (Sequence and Communication diagrams). A **message** is a named, asynchronous or synchronous communication between two lifelines that conveys information with the expectation that action will be initiated upon receipt.

According to the OMG UML 2.5.1 specification, messages are formally classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition**
> A **Synchronous Message** is a message in which the **caller is blocked** and waits for the operation to complete before continuing its own thread of execution. A **Return Message** is implicitly generated.
>
> An **Asynchronous Message** is a message in which the **caller does not wait** for a response; it dispatches the request and continues execution immediately on a separate thread of execution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy (Phone Call vs. Email)**
> - **Synchronous = Phone Call**: You call the waiter, hold the line, and **wait** until the waiter confirms your order. You cannot do anything else while waiting. The call **must be completed** before you place the next one.
> - **Asynchronous = WhatsApp Message**: You send a "place order" message to the waiter and **immediately go back to chatting** with your friend. When the food is ready, the waiter *may* notify you (an optional callback return), but you were never blocked.

In the context of UML, this distinction is **critical** for modelling real-time, concurrent, and distributed systems (e.g., IoT, microservices, event-driven architectures).

### 1.3 UML Notational Conventions (Visual Markers)

| Message Type | Arrow Style | Line Style | Blocking? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Filled (solid) arrowhead | Solid line | **Yes** |
| **Asynchronous** | Stick (open) arrowhead | Solid line | **No** |
| **Return** | Open arrowhead | **Dashed** line | N/A |
| **Self / Recursive** | Filled or stick arrowhead | Solid line (looped back) | Depends |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **shape of the arrowhead** is the *only* visual differentiator between synchronous (filled triangle) and asynchronous (stick/V) messages in a UML Sequence Diagram. This is a frequently tested 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Synchronous Messaging — Operational Breakdown

When object `A` sends a synchronous message to object `B`:

1. **Invocation**: `A` invokes an operation on `B` and the **call stack** of `A` is paused.
2. **Activation**: The **focus of control** (the rectangular activation bar) shifts to `B`.
3. **Execution**: `B` executes the requested operation on its own thread (which may be `A`'s thread).
4. **Return**: Control and (optionally) a return value flow back to `A` via a **dashed return arrow**.
5. **Resumption**: The activation bar on `B` ends, and `A` resumes execution from where it was suspended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** In Java, this maps to a direct method call. In distributed systems (e.g., CORBA, RMI, gRPC unary calls), this is the standard **request-response** pattern.

### 2.2 Asynchronous Messaging — Operational Breakdown

When object `A` sends an asynchronous message to object `B`:

1. **Dispatch**: `A` posts the message to a queue (or invokes a non-blocking I/O) and **continues immediately**.
2. **Concurrency**: `B` may execute on a separate thread, process, or even a separate machine.
3. **No Implicit Wait**: `A` does **not** receive an automatic return value.
4. **Optional Callback**: If a response is needed, `B` must send a *separate* message (often via an observer/listener pattern) back to `A`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** In modern software, this maps to message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka), event buses, AJAX callbacks, JavaScript Promises, and `async/await` constructs. It is the **backbone of reactive and event-driven architectures**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Parameter | Synchronous Message | Asynchronous Message |
|---|---|---|
| **Notation (Arrowhead)** | Filled (solid black) triangle | Open stick (V) |
| **Line Style** | Solid | Solid |
| **Caller Behavior** | Blocked, waits for response | Non-blocking, continues execution |
| **Return Message** | Implicit (shown as dashed arrow) | Not implicit; requires explicit reply |
| **Threading** | Typically same thread | Separate thread / process |
| **Time-Criticality** | Tightly coupled timing | Loosely coupled (decoupled) |
| **Failure Handling** | Exceptions propagate immediately | Needs explicit error-event mechanism |
| **Programming Analogue** | Function call (C, Java method) | Event, callback, Promise, Queue |
| **Activation Bar** | Starts on caller, transfers to callee | Starts on caller, may end immediately |
| **Use Case** | Real-time control, transactions | Notifications, background tasks, pub/sub |
| **Coupling** | **Tight** coupling | **Loose** coupling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Synchronous**: Used in **safety-critical embedded systems** (e.g., automotive ECUs, pacemakers) where deterministic response times are mandatory.
- **Asynchronous**: Used in **scalable web servers** (Node.js), **microservices** (Kafka), and **IoT systems** (MQTT) where decoupling producer and consumer is essential for high throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — From Code to UML

### 3.1 Deriving the UML Diagram from Source Code

This section walks you through the **logical process** examiners expect when you are given a scenario and asked to "draw the sequence diagram with sync/async messages."

**Step 1: Identify the Objects (Lifelines).**
Look for nouns in the problem statement that hold state or perform actions.
- *Example (ATM Withdrawal):* `Customer`, `ATM Machine`, `Bank Server`, `Printer`.

**Step 2: Identify the Messages (Verbs / Actions).**
Trace the flow of control:
- *Customer inserts card* → Message from Customer to ATM.
- *ATM validates PIN* → Sync call to Bank Server (must wait for approval).
- *Bank Server sends SMS notification* → Async event (ATM does not wait for it).
- *ATM prints receipt* → Self-message or sync call to Printer.

**Step 3: Classify Each Message.**

$$
\text{Message Type} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Synchronous} & \text{if caller MUST block and await result} \\
\text{Asynchronous} & \text{if caller can proceed without result} \\
\text{Return} & \text{if it is the dashed response to a sync call}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Place Activation Bars.**
- Every **synchronous** message creates an activation bar on the callee.
- An **asynchronous** message may show a brief activation (or no bar at all if it's fire-and-forget).

**Step 5: Apply the Correct Arrowhead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Visual Rule:} \quad & \blacktriangleright \ \text{(filled triangle)} = \text{sync} \\
& \rightarrow \ \text{(stick/V)} = \text{async} \\
& \dashv\!\!\rightarrow \ \text{(dashed open)} = \text{return}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — ATM Withdrawal Logic Flow

Let us model the sequence of interactions step-by-step:

**Step A: Customer initiates the session.**
The `Customer` lifeline sends `insertCard(cardNumber)` to the `ATM Machine`. This must be synchronous because the ATM must acknowledge acceptance before the customer proceeds.

$$
\text{Customer} \xrightarrow{\;\blacktriangleright\;\text{insertCard()}\;} \text{ATM}
$$

**Step B: PIN validation is a remote, blocking transaction.**
The `ATM Machine` must contact the `Bank Server` to validate the PIN. It **must wait** for an "approved/denied" decision before dispensing cash. This is a **classic synchronous call**.

$$
\text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\;\blacktriangleright\;\text{validatePIN(pin)\;}\;\blacktriangleright\;\text{return status}\;} \text{Bank Server}
$$

**Step C: SMS notification is asynchronous.**
After approval, the `Bank Server` triggers an SMS to the customer's phone. The ATM's transaction must **not** be blocked by SMS gateway latency. Hence, this is an **asynchronous fire-and-forget message**.

$$
\text{Bank Server} \xrightarrow{\;\text{V (stick)}\;\text{notifySMS(phoneNo, amount)}\;} \text{SMS Gateway}
$$

**Step D: Receipt printing is a synchronous self-operation.**
The `ATM Machine` calls its own `printReceipt()` method, which blocks the ATM's main thread for a few hundred milliseconds.

$$
\text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\;\blacktriangleright\;\text{printReceipt()}\;} \text{ATM (self-loop)}
$$

**Step E: Cash dispensing concludes the flow.**
Finally, the `ATM Machine` returns control to the `Customer` with a `dispenseCash()` operation, completing the synchronous handshake.

$$
\text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\;\blacktriangleright\;\text{dispenseCash()}\;} \text{Customer}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always look for words like *"and then"*, *"waits for"*, *"gets back"* → these are clues for **synchronous**. Words like *"notifies"*, *"fires event"*, *"in the background"*, *"does not wait"* → these are clues for **asynchronous**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant cliObj as Client Object
    participant srvObj as Server Object
    participant bgJob as Background Worker

    rect rgb(230, 245, 255)
    Note over cliObj,srvObj: SYNCHRONOUS BLOCKING FLOW
    cliObj->>srvObj: processRequest(data)
    activate srvObj
    srvObj-->>cliObj: returnResult(value)
    deactivate srvObj
    Note over cliObj: Execution resumes ONLY after returnResult
    end

    rect rgb(255, 245, 230)
    Note over cliObj,bgJob: ASYNCHRONOUS NON-BLOCKING FLOW
    cliObj-)bgJob: publishEvent(payload)
    activate bgJob
    Note over cliObj: Continues immediately
    bgJob--)cliObj: optionalCallback(status)
    deactivate bgJob
    end
```

**Diagram Interpretation Guide:**

- `->>` (solid line + filled triangle) = **Synchronous** message
- `-->>` (dashed line + open arrow) = **Return** message
- `-)` (solid line + stick arrow) = **Asynchronous** message
- `--)` (dashed line + stick arrow) = **Optional asynchronous reply / callback**

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Control Flow Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Caller Thread Initiates] --> B{Message Type?}
    B -->|Synchronous| C[Block Caller Thread]
    C --> D[Activate Callee]
    D --> E[Execute Operation]
    E --> F[Return Value via Dashed Arrow]
    F --> G[Resume Caller Thread]

    B -->|Asynchronous| H[Dispatch to Message Queue]
    H --> I[Return Immediately to Caller]
    I --> J[Caller Continues Next Statement]
    H --> K[Spawn Worker Thread]
    K --> L[Execute Operation Concurrently]
    L --> M{Reply Needed?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Send Separate Callback Message]
    M -->|No| O[Fire and Forget - End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above (e.g., `B`, `C`, `D`) are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and all labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting, strictly complying with the engine's Mermaid safeguards.

### 4.3 Activation Bar Visualization Logic

The **activation bar** (focus of control) is a thin vertical rectangle placed atop a lifeline. Its operational rules are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sync Message: } & \text{Activation starts on callee, ends on return.} \\
\text{Async Message: } & \text{Activation may be brief or absent on callee.} \\
\text{Stacked Sync: } & \text{Multiple sync calls produce nested activation bars.}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a synchronous message in UML. State its notational representation and one example from a real-time system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **synchronous message** is a communication in which the **sender waits** (is blocked) until the receiver has completed the operation and returned a value. In UML Sequence Diagrams, it is represented by a **solid line with a filled (solid black) arrowhead**, and is typically followed by a dashed return arrow.

**Example:** A `Pilot` sending a `releaseLandingGear()` command to an `Aircraft Control System` — the pilot must wait for confirmation that the gear is locked before touchdown. *(1 mark each: definition + notation + example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous messages in UML with suitable diagram notations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Arrowhead | Filled triangle | Stick (open) |
| Caller | Blocked | Non-blocked |
| Return | Implicit (dashed) | Explicit (separate message) |

*(1 mark each for arrowhead, blocking behavior, and return semantics.)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each module carries one **14-mark question** with **internal choice (A or B)**. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various types of messages used in UML 2.5 sequence diagrams. Discuss synchronous, asynchronous, return, and self-messages with their notations.

**Model Solution (a):**

The UML 2.5 specification defines messages as the units of communication between lifelines. The major types are:

1. **Synchronous Message** *(2 marks)*: The caller halts execution and waits. Notation: `→▸` (solid line + filled arrow). The callee's activation bar remains active until a return message is sent.

2. **Asynchronous Message** *(2 marks)*: The caller does not wait; it dispatches and continues. Notation: `→|` (solid line + stick/V arrow). Used in multithreaded and event-driven systems.

3. **Return Message** *(1.5 marks)*: The dashed line with an open arrowhead (`--▷`) used to return control (and a value) from a callee back to the caller. It is implicit for sync calls.

4. **Self-Message** *(1.5 marks)*: A message that a lifeline sends to itself, used to model internal operations (e.g., `this.calculate()`). Notation: a looped solid arrow originating and ending on the same lifeline.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Draw a UML sequence diagram for an **Online Book Purchase System** showing a synchronous call to verify payment and an asynchronous call to send a confirmation email. Use proper activation bars and notations.

**Model Solution (b):**

The diagram should depict four lifelines: `User`, `WebApp`, `PaymentGateway`, `EmailService`.

1. `User` → `WebApp`: `placeOrder(cart)` (Sync)
2. `WebApp` → `PaymentGateway`: `verifyPayment(card)` (Sync) — followed by `-->>` return.
3. `WebApp` → `EmailService`: `sendConfirmation(email)` (**Async** — stick arrow) — WebApp does not block.
4. `WebApp` → `User`: `showOrderConfirmation()` (Sync return).

> A student drawing all four lifelines with correct arrowheads (filled for sync, stick for async), a dashed return arrow, and a proper activation bar on `PaymentGateway` earns full marks. Adding the activation bar on `EmailService` as short/brief (since async) earns bonus credit.

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **difference between synchronous and asynchronous messaging**. Discuss their relative advantages and suitable engineering use-cases.

**Model Solution (a):**

A **synchronous message** (filled triangle arrow) blocks the sender until the receiver completes the operation and returns a response. It enforces **strict ordering** and is simpler to reason about but reduces **throughput** in I/O-bound systems.

An **asynchronous message** (stick arrow) does not block the sender; the response (if any) arrives later as a separate callback. It enables **parallelism** and **decoupling** but introduces complexity in error handling and ordering.

**Advantages Comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sync Pros: } & \text{Simpler control flow, immediate error propagation.} \\
\text{Sync Cons: } & \text{Low scalability, caller stalls on slow I/O.} \\
\text{Async Pros: } & \text{High scalability, non-blocking, ideal for distributed systems.} \\
\text{Async Cons: } & \text{Complex debugging, eventual consistency, no immediate return.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Use-Cases:** Sync → ATM PIN check, railway signal control. Async → Email dispatch, push notifications, Kafka event streaming.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **UML notation for a self-message** and a **return message**. How does the activation bar behave for nested synchronous calls?

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Self-Message** *(2.5 marks)*: A lifeline calls its own operation. Notation: a solid arrow that loops from the lifeline back to itself, with an activation bar that shows nested execution. Example: a `Calculator` object calling `this.round()` before returning a result.

2. **Return Message** *(2.5 marks)*: A dashed line with an open (V) arrowhead originating from the callee's activation bar and ending at the caller's lifeline. It is the **standard UML way** to depict the value flowing back from a synchronous call.

3. **Nested Sync Activation** *(2 marks)*: When object `A` calls `B`, and `B` in turn calls `C` synchronously, the activation bars are **stacked**: the topmost bar belongs to `C` (innermost), the middle to `B`, and the bottom to `A`. They unwind in **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** order — exactly like a program call stack.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Arrowhead confusion**: Students frequently draw **dashed lines for async** and **solid for sync return**. Remember: *solid line + filled triangle = sync*; *solid line + stick = async*; *dashed line = return*.
> 2. **Forgetting the dashed return arrow**: A synchronous call without its return arrow is incomplete and will cost you **at least 1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Activation bar misuse**: Drawing an activation bar on an asynchronous receiver as if it were a long-running sync call is wrong. Async activations are typically **brief or absent**.
> 4. **Confusing "Asynchronous" with "Parallel"**: Asynchronous means *non-blocking*; it does not necessarily mean *simultaneous*. Marks are deducted if these are used interchangeably.
> 5. **Skipping lifeline labels**: Every lifeline MUST be labeled (e.g., `:PaymentGateway`), or 0.5 marks are deducted per unlabeled lifeline.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **Synchronous message** = filled arrowhead + solid line + **caller is blocked**.
- 🔹 **Asynchronous message** = stick (V) arrowhead + solid line + **caller continues**.
- 🔹 **Return message** = dashed line + open arrowhead + flows **only after a sync call**.
- 🔹 **Self-message** = looped arrow on the same lifeline + nested activation bar.
- 🔹 **Activation bars** represent the *focus of control*; they **stack** for nested sync calls (LIFO).
- 🔹 **Programming analogues**: Sync → method call, RPC; Async → callbacks, Promises, message queues (Kafka, MQTT).
- 🔹 **Engineering rule of thumb**: Use **synchronous** when ordering and immediate response matter (e.g., financial transactions). Use **asynchronous** when throughput and decoupling matter (e.g., notifications, log shipping).
- 🔹 **KTU frequency**: This topic is asked at least **once per ESE** as either a Part A (3 marks) or Part B sub-question (7 marks). Master the **arrowhead notations** and the **activation bar semantics**.
- 🔹 **Lifeline naming convention in UML**: `:ClassName` for instances, `objectName:ClassName` for named instances.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sequence Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sequence Diagram** is a type of **Interaction Diagram** in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) that depicts object interactions arranged in a **time-sequenced manner**. It visualizes the flow of messages exchanged between objects (or actors) participating in a particular scenario, focusing on the **chronological order of message passing** rather than the static structure of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A sequence diagram is an interaction diagram that emphasizes the time-ordering of messages exchanged between cooperating objects. It shows objects, their lifelines, and the messages they send to one another to accomplish a particular task."

In KTU parlance, sequence diagrams fall under the **dynamic modeling** (behavioral) view of UML, specifically within the **interaction** category of the UML 2.5 metamodel (OMG specification).

| UML Diagram Category | Type | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Structure Diagrams | Class, Object, Component | Static skeleton |
| **Interaction Diagrams** | **Sequence, Communication** | **Dynamic message flow** |
| State Diagrams | State Machine | Internal object lifecycle |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Script" Metaphor

Imagine a **movie screenplay**:
- Each **actor in the script** is an *object* in the diagram.
- The **director shouts "Action!"** and the scene unfolds **left-to-right and top-to-bottom** — that vertical descent represents **time advancing**.
- Every **line of dialogue** between actors is a **message** sent.
- The **script doesn't show what the actors are thinking** — it only shows *what they say to each other* and *in what order*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** A sequence diagram is essentially a **time-ordered conversation log** between a group of objects. Read it like a comic strip where each panel shows a message being passed.

## 1.3 Purpose in Software Engineering (KTU-Weightage)

Sequence diagrams are the **primary tool** for:
1. **Use-case realization** — detailing the flow of a specific use case (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*).
2. **Method signature discovery** — exposing the public methods each class must expose.
3. **Contract specification** — showing pre-conditions, post-conditions, and message arguments.
4. **Detecting design flaws** early — overly long arrows or deep nesting indicate poor cohesion.
5. **Reverse-engineering legacy code** — reconstructing dynamic behavior from static class diagrams.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says *"model the dynamic behavior of scenario X"*, the expected answer is almost always a **Sequence Diagram** (or its sibling, the Communication Diagram). For 14-mark design questions, sequence diagrams dominate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components — The Anatomy

A sequence diagram is built from **eight core building blocks**. Each carries a precise UML 2.5 semantic meaning.

### 2.1.1 Actor / Object (Lifeline Header)

The **rectangle at the top** of each vertical dashed line.

**Notation rules:**
- Format: `ObjectName : ClassName` (e.g., `customer : Customer`)
- If anonymous: `:ClassName` (e.g., `:ATM`)
- **Actor** (stick figure) for external entities, **Object rectangle** for internal system objects.

### 2.1.2 Lifeline

The **vertical dashed line** descending from each object header. Represents the **existence of the object over time**.

> [!NOTE]
> An object's lifeline begins at the moment it is created (via a `<<create>>` message) and ends at object destruction (`<<destroy>>` marked with an **X**).

### 2.1.3 Activation / Execution Occurrence (Focus of Control)

The **thin vertical rectangle** placed on a lifeline. Represents the **time period during which an object is performing an action** — i.e., it has control and is executing a method.

- Activation **starts** when a message is received.
- Activation **ends** when the method returns.

### 2.1.4 Messages — The Heart of the Diagram

Messages are **horizontal arrows** connecting two lifelines. UML 2.5 defines **five message types**, each with distinct arrowheads:

| # | Message Type | Arrowhead | Semantic Meaning | KTU Usage Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Synchronous** | Solid line, filled triangular arrowhead | Sender **waits** for reply | ★★★★★ (Most common) |
| 2 | **Asynchronous** | Solid line, open stick arrowhead | Sender **does not wait** | ★★★☆☆ |
| 3 | **Return** | Dashed line, open stick arrowhead | Reply to a prior call | ★★★★☆ |
| 4 | **Self / Recursive** | Solid loop arrow on same lifeline | Object calls its own method | ★★☆☆☆ |
| 5 | **Create** | Dashed line to new object header | `<<create>>` instantiation | ★★★☆☆ |

### 2.1.5 Combined Fragments — Control Logic

Used to model **loops, conditionals, alternatives, and parallel execution**. Enclosed in a **rectangle** with a fragment operator.

| Operator | Name | Purpose | Syntax |
|---|---|---|---|
| `alt` | Alternative | **if / else** branching | `alt [condition1]` ... `else [condition2]` ... `end` |
| `opt` | Optional | **Single-branch** if | `opt [condition]` ... `end` |
| `loop` | Loop | **while / for** repetition | `loop [condition]` ... `end` |
| `par` | Parallel | **Concurrent** execution | `par [condition]` ... `end` |
| `critical` | Critical Region | Atomic block | `critical` ... `end` |
| `neg` | Negative | Invalid scenario | `neg` ... `end` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Combined fragments appear in **Part B (14-mark) design questions**. Drawing a sequence diagram *without* `alt` and `loop` for a real scenario will cost **2–3 marks**.

### 2.1.6 State Invariant

A small **curly-braced condition** `{condition}` placed on a lifeline. Asserts that the object must be in a given state at that point in time.

### 2.1.7 Time Constraints

`{t1..t2}` annotation on a message — bounds the delivery time. Mostly theoretical at KTU level.

## 2.2 Message Numbering Convention (KTU Exam Convention)

Two accepted schemes for numbering messages in nested calls:

**Scheme A — Hierarchical Decimal:**
```
1  : initial call
   1.1 : nested call from object A
   1.2 : another nested call
2  : second top-level call
```

**Scheme B — Sequential with Indent Depth:** (KTU-Examiner-Preferred for legibility)
```
1 : firstMessage()
  1.1 : secondMessage()
  1.2 : thirdMessage()
2 : fourthMessage()
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat-Sheet

| UML Notation | Meaning | KTU Symbol Hint |
|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow$ filled triangle | Synchronous call | Solid filled arrow |
| $\dashrightarrow$ open arrow | Asynchronous / Return | Dashed open arrow |
| $\xrightarrow{\text{<<create>>}}$ | Object creation | Dashed arrow to new box |
| `*` (guard) | Iteration guard | `* [i=1..n]` |
| `{condition}` | State invariant | Braced text on lifeline |
| `alt / opt / loop / par` | Combined fragment operators | Top-left of fragment box |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Modeling SIP call-flow signaling.
- **Banking:** ATM transaction flows with PIN verification, balance check, dispense, log.
- **IoT:** Sensor → Gateway → Cloud ingestion pipelines.
- **Microservices:** REST/RPC choreography between distributed services.
- **Game Development:** NPC interaction scripts (Unity/Unreal behavior trees).
- **Embedded Systems:** State-driven firmware message exchanges (CAN bus).

> [!TIP]
> In modern **Model-Driven Development (MDD)**, sequence diagrams can be **auto-executed** via tools like **IBM Rational Rhapsody**, **Enterprise Architect (Sparx)**, and **Papyrus**, generating skeleton code directly from the interaction model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The KTU-Recommended 5-Step Construction Procedure

When a 14-mark question asks *"Draw a sequence diagram for scenario X"*, follow this **deterministic, mark-securing procedure**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Scenario Boundary
- Read the use-case description.
- **List every object/actor** that participates, in order of their first appearance.
- Discard peripheral objects (logging, audit) unless explicitly demanded.

> **Decision Rule:** "Does this object receive a message in the scenario?" → If **No**, exclude it.

### Step 2 — Lay Out Lifelines Left-to-Right
- **Actors** (external) on the **far left**.
- **Boundary / UI objects** next.
- **Controller objects** in the middle.
- **Entity / Database objects** on the **far right**.
- This left-to-right placement mirrors **MVC architecture** — examiners expect it.

### Step 3 — Map the Happy Path
- Number messages **1, 2, 3, ...** from top to bottom.
- Each message corresponds to a method invocation: `messageName(arg1, arg2)`.
- Place an **activation bar** on the receiver's lifeline for the duration of its method execution.

### Step 4 — Add Return Arrows
- For every synchronous call, add a **dashed return arrow** with the return value: `returnValue : Type`.
- Omit return arrows only when the return is `void` (still, KTU examiners prefer them for clarity).

### Step 5 — Layer in Combined Fragments
- Identify decision points → wrap in `alt` with `else`.
- Identify repetitions → wrap in `loop` with guard.
- Identify concurrency → wrap in `par`.
- Identify exception paths → wrap in `neg` or `alt [else]`.

## 3.2 Worked Example: ATM Cash Withdrawal (Happy Path + Failure Path)

**Scenario:** *"A customer inserts a card, enters the PIN, requests ₹5000, the ATM verifies PIN with the bank, checks balance, dispenses cash, prints receipt, and ejects the card."*

### Object Identification (Step 1)

| # | Object | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `customer` | Actor | External initiator |
| 2 | `atmUI` | Boundary | Card reader, keypad, screen |
| 3 | `atmController` | Control | Orchestrates workflow |
| 4 | `bankServer` | Entity | PIN/balance verification |
| 5 | `cashDispenser` | Entity | Physical cash payout |
| 6 | `receiptPrinter` | Entity | Receipt generation |

### Message Trace (Step 3 & 4)

```
1   customer → atmUI          : insertCard(cardNo)
2   atmUI → atmController     : validateCard(cardNo)
3   atmController → bankServer : verifyCard(cardNo)
3.1 bankServer → bankServer   : lookupAccount(cardNo)
4   bankServer → atmController : return cardValid : Boolean  [RETURN]
5   atmController → atmUI     : promptForPIN()
6   customer → atmUI          : enterPIN("****")
7   atmUI → atmController     : submitPIN("****")
8   atmController → bankServer : authenticate(cardNo, pin)
9   bankServer → atmController : return pinValid : Boolean  [RETURN]
10  atmController → bankServer : getBalance(accountId)
11  bankServer → atmController : return balance = 25000  [RETURN]
12  atmController → cashDispenser : dispense(5000)
13  atmController → receiptPrinter : print(transactionId)
14  atmController → atmUI     : ejectCard()
```

### Failure-Path Branching (Step 5)

If `pinValid == false` → wrap messages 7–15 inside:

```
alt [pinValid]
    : (proceed with messages 10–14)
else [!pinValid]
    atmController → atmUI : displayError("Invalid PIN")
    atmController → atmUI : ejectCard()
end
```

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Diagram

| Scenario Characteristic | Recommended Diagram | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Message ordering is the focus** | **Sequence Diagram** | Time flows top-down naturally |
| **Object-link topology is the focus** | Communication Diagram | Shows collaboration graph explicitly |
| **Complex conditional/loop logic** | **Sequence Diagram** | `alt`, `loop` fragments are cleaner |
| **Distributed system message passing** | Sequence (with timing) | Shows latency and asynchronous I/O |
| **Real-time embedded control** | **Sequence Diagram** | Maps directly to call stacks |
| **Brainstorming object collaborations** | Communication Diagram | Faster to sketch link topology |

## 3.4 Common Calculation: Activation Bar Nesting Depth

A **key design-health metric** in sequence diagrams is the **Maximum Activation Depth (MAD)**:

$$\text{MAD} = \max_{t \in \text{time}} \left( \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[\text{activation}_i \text{ active at } t] \right)$$

Where:
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function.
- $\text{activation}_i$ is the $i$-th activation bar.

**Interpretation Matrix:**

| MAD Value | Design Quality | KTU Examiner Note |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{MAD} \leq 3$ | **Excellent** | Clean layered architecture |
| $3 < \text{MAD} \leq 5$ | **Acceptable** | Some coupling, justify with comments |
| $\text{MAD} > 5$ | **Poor** | Likely violates Single Responsibility Principle — **deduct marks** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Drawing activation bars **incorrectly nested** (e.g., the inner bar ends *after* the outer bar) is one of the top three reasons students lose marks on sequence-diagram design questions. Always remember: **inner activation ⊂ outer activation** (temporal subset).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdrawal (Full Annotated)

Below is the **fully-annotated sequence diagram** for the ATM withdrawal scenario described in Section 3, rendered in Mermaid. The diagram deliberately incorporates the **happy path** and an `alt` fragment for the PIN-failure path, alongside activation bars and return messages.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor CUST as customer
    participant UI as atmUI : ATMScreen
    participant CTRL as atmController : ATMController
    participant BANK as bankServer : BankServer
    participant CASH as cashDispenser
    participant PRN as receiptPrinter

    CUST ->> UI : insertCard(cardNo)
    activate UI
    UI ->> CTRL : validateCard(cardNo)
    activate CTRL
    CTRL ->> BANK : verifyCard(cardNo)
    activate BANK
    BANK -->> CTRL : return cardValid : Boolean
    deactivate BANK
    CTRL -->> UI : promptForPIN()
    deactivate CTRL
    deactivate UI

    CUST ->> UI : enterPIN(****)
    activate UI
    UI ->> CTRL : submitPIN(****)
    activate CTRL

    alt pinValid == true
        CTRL ->> BANK : authenticate(cardNo, pin)
        activate BANK
        BANK -->> CTRL : return pinValid : true
        deactivate BANK
        CTRL ->> BANK : getBalance(accountId)
        activate BANK
        BANK -->> CTRL : return balance : Double
        deactivate BANK
        CTRL ->> CASH : dispense(amount)
        activate CASH
        CASH -->> CTRL : return dispenseStatus : Boolean
        deactivate CASH
        CTRL ->> PRN : print(transactionId)
        activate PRN
        PRN -->> CTRL : return receiptOK : Boolean
        deactivate PRN
    else pinValid == false
        CTRL ->> UI : displayError(Invalid PIN)
        CTRL ->> UI : ejectCard()
    end

    CTRL -->> UI : ejectCard()
    deactivate CTRL
    UI -->> CUST : takeCard()
    deactivate UI
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

To complement the message-level view, the following block diagram maps the **architectural layers** that the sequence diagram's objects belong to, illustrating how the **MVC + Entity** decomposition maps onto the interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT[External Layer]
        A1[customer Actor]
    end
    subgraph BDR[Boundary Layer]
        B1[atmUI : ATMScreen]
    end
    subgraph CTL[Control Layer]
        C1[atmController : ATMController]
    end
    subgraph ENT[Entity Layer]
        E1[bankServer : BankServer]
        E2[cashDispenser]
        E3[receiptPrinter]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> E1
    C1 --> E2
    C1 --> E3
    E1 -.return values.-> C1
    C1 -.return values.-> B1
    B1 -.UI update.-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Solid arrows = synchronous message flow (downward). Dashed arrows = return values (upward). Each layer talks **only to its adjacent layer** — a hallmark of the **GOF Façade / Layered Architecture** pattern.

### 4.3 Combined Fragment Decision Tree

The following flow chart is the **exam-day decision procedure** for choosing the correct combined fragment operator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need control structure?] --> Q1{Is there a choice between two or more paths?}
    Q1 -- Yes, mutually exclusive --> Q2{Both paths must be modeled?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ALT[Use alt with else]
    Q2 -- No, only one path --> OPT[Use opt]
