Messages across operands may interleave. | Balance check and SMS notification are independent — they can be interleaved. | | `critical` | Atomic region — no concurrent interaction may interleave. | Cash dispensing must not be interrupted; e.g., a parallel fraud check must wait. | > **Mark distribution for Q.A (b):** > `[2 marks]` Correct `loop` notation with bounds and break. > `[2 marks]` Correct `par` placement and semantics. > `[2 marks]` Correct `critical` placement and semantics. > `[1 mark]` Overall clarity and explanation of operator differences. --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) #### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** — *Why InteractionOccurrences are needed* **Question:** *"Explain the need for Interaction Occurrences in UML 2.5 sequence diagrams. Illustrate with an example where a `ref` frame is used to model a reusable sub-flow in an e-commerce checkout process."* **Model Solution:** **The Need (3 sub-points):** 1. **Reusability** — Common sub-flows (e.g., authentication, payment) can be defined once and reused. 2. **Readability** — A 200-message sequence diagram becomes manageable by collapsing repeated flows into single `ref` frames. 3. **Maintainability** — Bug fix or change happens in **one** referenced Interaction; all references automatically pick it up. **E-commerce Checkout Example:** `sd: CheckoutProcess` uses `ref` to invoke: - `AuthenticateUser(userCredentials)` — runs first. - `CalculateTax(cartItems)` — parallel to payment. - `ProcessPayment(card, amount)` — after authentication. ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ sd CheckoutProcess │ │ │ │ Customer → WebApp: beginCheckout │ │ ┌──── ref: AuthenticateUser ────┐ │ │ │ WebApp ↔ AuthService │ │ │ └───────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ ┌──── par ────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌── ref: CalculateTax ──┐ │ │ │ │ │ WebApp ↔ TaxService │ │ │ │ │ └───────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ ┌── ref: ProcessPayment ──────┐ │ │ │ │ │ WebApp ↔ PaymentGateway │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ WebApp → Customer: orderPlaced │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` > **Mark distribution for Q.B (a):** > `[3 marks]` Three clear motivations (reuse, readability, maintainability). > `[4 marks]` Correct and well-labelled diagram with `ref` and `par` fragments. --- #### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** — *Lifeline decomposition and Gates* **Question:** *"What is a PartDecomposition in UML? How does it differ from a `ref` frame? Show with a diagram how a lifeline can be decomposed into a sub-interaction using PartDecomposition, and explain the role of gates in this context."* **Model Solution:** | Aspect | PartDecomposition | `ref` frame (InteractionOccurrence) | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Replaces a single *lifeline* with a sub-interaction. | Replaces a *region* of the diagram (multiple lifelines possible). | | **Notation** | A small frame on the lifeline labeled `ref` or `sd`. | A full frame labeled `ref` covering one or more lifelines. | | **Use case** | Zooming into one object's internal behavior. | Invoking a reusable multi-object collaboration. | **Diagram:** ``` ┌──────┐ ┌─────────┐ │Cust │ │ OrderSys│ └──┬───┘ └────┬────┘ │ placeOrder │ │────────────>│ │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ ref │ │ │ Internal │ (PartDecomposition on OrderSys) │ │ OrderFlow │ │ │ ──> DB │ │ │ <── result │ │ │ │ │ └──────────────┘ │ │ │<──ack──────│ │ │ ``` **Gates' Role:** - A **gate** is a *typed endpoint* on the boundary of a frame. - **Incoming message** to the decomposition = **actual gate** on the `ref` / decomposition frame. - The message is then routed to the **formal gate** declared on the referenced Interaction. - Outgoing messages from the decomposition travel back out through matched gate pairs. > **Mark distribution for Q.B (b):** > `[2 marks]` Definition of PartDecomposition. > `[2 marks]` Comparison table with `ref` frame. > `[2 marks]` Correct diagram. > `[1 mark]` Gate explanation. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. **Writing `ref` without a frame** — You lose 1–2 marks. The `ref` keyword MUST be enclosed in a frame. > 2. **Confusing `sd` and `ref`** — `sd` *defines*, `ref` *references*. Mixing them up = 0 for the diagram. > 3. **Forgetting guard conditions on `alt` operands** — UML 2.5 allows omitting guards, but KTU expects you to show them for full marks. > 4. **Treating `par` as `strict`** — `par` allows interleaving; only `strict` and `critical` forbid it. This is a classic 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Not distinguishing gates from messages** — Gates are *endpoints* on the frame boundary; messages *flow through* them. > 6. **Omitting parameter syntax in `ref` arguments** — `ref ValidatePayment(cardNo, amt)` is correct; `ref ValidatePayment` is incomplete if the Interaction has parameters. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ **InteractionOccurrence** = a *reference* (not a definition) to an existing Interaction. - ✅ Visualized via a **`ref` frame** — keyword `ref` in the top-left of the name compartment. - ✅ Enables **reuse, readability, maintainability** of complex sequence diagrams. - ✅ `InteractionOccurrence` is a **subclass** of `InteractionUse` in UML 2.5 metamodel. - ✅ **Arguments** are passed positionally (or by name with `:=`) at the `ref` site. - ✅ **Gates** are boundary endpoints — formal gates on the referenced Interaction, actual gates on the `ref` site. - ✅ **Combined Fragments** carry an **Interaction Operator** in their top-left guard: - `alt` (multi-branch), `opt` (optional), `loop[n..m]` (iteration) - `par` (parallel, interleaving allowed), `strict` (parallel, no interleaving) - `critical` (atomic), `neg` (forbidden), `assert` (mandatory) - `seq` (default weak sequencing), `ignore` / `consider` (message filtering) - ✅ **PartDecomposition** = replacing a single lifeline with a `ref` / `sd` sub-frame. - ✅ `par` ≠ `strict` ≠ `critical` — know the interleaving rules. - ✅ A `ref` frame can be **inlined** semantically: $I_{enc} \equiv I_{enc}^{pre} \circ \sigma(I_{ref}) \circ I_{enc}^{post}$. - ✅ **KTU hot keywords to memorize verbatim:** *InteractionOccurrence, InteractionUse, Combined Fragment, Interaction Operator, Interaction Operand, Gate, Formal Gate, Actual Gate, Part Decomposition, Weak Sequencing, Strict Sequencing, Atomic Region.*

topics.title: Activity Diagrams: Activity Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Activity Diagrams: Activity Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 2 — Activity Diagrams: Activity Semantics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity Semantics** in UML is the formal specification that defines *how* an activity diagram executes. It is built on a **token-flow model** (conceptually derived from **Petri Nets**) where execution is modelled as the movement of abstract **tokens** across **activity nodes** (actions, decisions, forks, joins) via **activity edges** (control flow and object flow).

According to the OMG UML 2.5 specification, the *semantics* of an activity is the **causal ordering** of a set of actions and the **rules by which tokens are produced, consumed, offered, and withdrawn** along the edges of an activity diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Semantics"?**
> Whereas *notation* tells you *what shape* to draw (e.g., a diamond = decision), *semantics* tells you *what that shape means at runtime* — when does it fire, what happens to incoming tokens, what is the concurrency model, etc.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Conveyor Belt 🏭

Imagine a **car assembly line** in a factory:

- The **chassis** is a *token* — it enters the line and travels from station to station.
- Each **workstation** is an *action node* — it does something to the chassis (welds, paints, attaches wheels).
- The **conveyor belt** between two stations is an *activity edge* — it carries the token forward.
- A **fork in the belt** splits the chassis into two parallel sub-assemblies (e.g., left & right wheel assembly) — this is **concurrency**.
- A **traffic light** that decides whether a defective chassis goes to repair or scrap is a **decision node** — it routes the token.
- The **start switch** is the *Initial Node*; the **end buzzer** is the *Final Node*.

So **Activity Semantics = the rulebook of the factory** — it says *when* a station may operate, *what* it consumes, *what* it produces, and *how* parallel lines rejoin.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Token | $t$ | Abstract unit of flow |
| Multiplicity of an edge | $w$ | Number of tokens that traverse an edge at once |
| Lower bound of an edge | $lb$ | Minimum tokens that must be available before the edge fires |
| Upper bound of an edge | $ub$ | Maximum tokens that the edge can hold |
| Fork | $n$ | Number of outgoing concurrent branches |
| Join | $k$ | Number of incoming branches that must synchronize |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks of Activity Semantics

Activity semantics operates on **three structural primitives**:

### A) Activity Nodes
1. **Executable Nodes (Actions)** — perform computations; consume input tokens, produce output tokens.
2. **Control Nodes** — direct token flow:
   - Initial Node
   - Final Node (Activity-Final, Flow-Final)
   - Decision Node
   - Merge Node
   - Fork Node
   - Join Node
3. **Object Nodes** — hold tokens that represent data:
   - Pins, Central Buffers, Data Stores, Parameters.

### B) Activity Edges
1. **Control Flow Edge** — carries *control tokens* (no value payload).
2. **Object Flow Edge** — carries *object tokens* (value-bearing tokens).

### C) Tokens
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **token** is an abstract marker that an activity edge can offer to its target node. Tokens are the *engine* of UML activity semantics. They are **NOT** the same as data; rather, they are markers of *availability* and *flow*.

## 2.2 The Six Core Semantics Rules

> [!NOTE]
> These six rules are the **heart of UML Activity Semantics** and are *high-yield* for KTU theory questions.

1. **Enabling Rule** — An action can begin executing only when **all its incoming edges have offered at least one token**.
2. **Firing Rule** — When an action begins, it *consumes* one token from each incoming edge and *produces* one token on each outgoing edge.
3. **Fork Rule** — A fork node receives **one** token and produces **one** token on **every** outgoing edge simultaneously → enables parallel execution.
4. **Join Rule** — A join node waits until **all** (or a specified number of) incoming edges have a token, then consumes one from each and produces **one** token on its outgoing edge.
5. **Decision Rule** — A decision node routes a token to **one** outgoing edge based on a guard condition.
6. **Merge Rule** — A merge node combines multiple incoming edges into one outgoing edge, **passing through** any token that arrives.

## 2.3 Concurrency vs. Synchronization

- **Fork/Join pair** = *parallel split* followed by *parallel synchronization*.
- Without a join, parallel flows remain **independent** (asynchronous).
- With a join, parallel flows **re-synchronize** before proceeding.

## 2.4 The Four Edge Semantics

| Edge Type | Symbol | Multiplicity Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Flow** | $\rightarrow$ | Carries one control token at a time | Procedural control |
| **Object Flow** | $-->$ | Carries object tokens; obeys type compatibility | Data passing between actions |
| **Interrupt Flow** | $\dashrightarrow$ | Lightning-bolt edge; carries exception token | Exception handling |
| **Weight (w)** | $\{w\}$ | Annotates how many tokens traverse per firing | Bulk/batch processing |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Sheet

| Concept | Formal Representation | Unit / Domain | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of tokens on an edge $e$ | $T(e) \in \mathbb{N}_0$ | Non-negative integer | Empty edge has $T=0$ |
| Edge weight | $w(e) \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Positive integer | $T(e) \ge w(e)$ required to fire |
| Fork node output | $T_{out}(i) = T_{in}$ for each $i = 1, \dots, n$ | Set of outgoing edges | All branches fire simultaneously |
| Join node output | $T_{out} = 1$ if $\bigwedge_{j=1}^{k} T(j) \ge 1$ | Boolean | All $k$ incoming must have token |
| Decision edge guard | $g_i : Boolean$ | $\{$ True, False $\}$ | Exactly one $g_i$ should be True |
| Action execution time | $\tau_a$ | Time units (e.g., ms) | Non-negative real number |
| Token traversal cost | $\delta_t$ | Time units | Time for token to cross an edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Tip:** Use `\mid` (not `|`) in LaTeX inline math inside KTU answer sheets to avoid table rendering issues.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Activity Semantics

- **Business Process Modelling (BPMN-to-UML):** Defining exact execution order for loan approval workflows.
- **Embedded & Real-Time Systems:** Modelling parallel tasks on an ECU (Engine Control Unit) in automotive software.
- **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Specifying the orchestration of web services.
- **Robotic Process Automation (RPA):** Defining what a bot does, when, and with what data.
- **Healthcare Workflow Systems:** Modelling patient admission, triage, treatment, discharge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Token-Flow Execution Model

We will formally derive the **firing condition** for an action node $A$ with $n$ incoming edges and $m$ outgoing edges.

### Step 1 — Define the State of Each Edge
For an activity edge $e_i$, define its current token count:

$$
T(e_i, t) \;\in\; \mathbb{N}_0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

This is read as: "At time $t$, the number of tokens on edge $e_i$ is a non-negative integer."

### Step 2 — Define the Enabling Predicate
An action $A$ is said to be **enabled** at time $t$ if and only if every incoming edge has at least one token *available* for consumption:

$$
E(A, t) \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \big[\, T(e_i, t) \;\ge\; w(e_i) \,\big]
$$

Here $w(e_i)$ is the edge weight (default value = 1). This is the **enabling rule** (Rule 1 from Section 2.2).

### Step 3 — Define the Consumption Function
When $A$ fires, it consumes exactly $w(e_i)$ tokens from each incoming edge $e_i$:

$$
T_{\text{after}}(e_i, t) \;=\; T(e_i, t) \;-\; w(e_i), \quad \forall i \in [1, n]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** This represents a *destructive read* — the token entering the action is removed from the edge and the action takes ownership of it.

### Step 4 — Define the Production Function
After a delay $\tau_A$ (action execution time), $A$ produces $w(f_j)$ tokens on each outgoing edge $f_j$:

$$
T_{\text{after}}(f_j, t + \tau_A) \;=\; T(f_j, t) \;+\; w(f_j), \quad \forall j \in [1, m]
$$

### Step 5 — Combine into the Master Firing Equation
The total state transition due to action $A$ is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given } & E(A, t) = \text{True} \\
\text{Then } \quad & T(e_i, t) \;\mapsto\; T(e_i, t) - w(e_i) \\
& T(f_j, t + \tau_A) \;\mapsto\; T(f_j, t) + w(f_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **UML 2 Activity Firing Equation** — a high-yield derivation for KTU theory and 14-mark questions.

## 3.2 Derivation of Join Node Synchronization

Consider a join node $J$ with $k$ incoming edges $e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k$ and one outgoing edge $f$.

### Step 1 — Enabling Condition
$J$ becomes enabled only when **all** $k$ incoming edges have at least one token:

$$
E(J, t) \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} \big[\, T(e_i, t) \;\ge\; 1 \,\big]
$$

### Step 2 — Firing Transition
When $J$ fires, it consumes one token from each incoming edge and produces exactly one token on the outgoing edge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(e_i, t+\delta) &= T(e_i, t) - 1, \quad i = 1, \dots, k \\
T(f, t+\delta) &= T(f, t) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\delta$ is the negligible synchronization delay.

### Step 3 — Special Case: Conditional Join
A join with a *joinSpec* of $j < k$ (e.g., $j = 2$ for a "2-out-of-3" join) enables when any $j$ of the $k$ edges offer a token:

$$
E(J, t) \;=\; \Bigg(\sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{1}\big[T(e_i, t) \ge 1\big]\Bigg) \;\ge\; j
$$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. This represents a **soft synchronization** (e.g., fastest-2-out-of-3 sensor fusion in a fault-tolerant system).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Action vs. Control Node Behaviour

| Node Type | Input Tokens Needed | Output Tokens Produced | Concurrency Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Action** | All incoming $\ge 1$ | One per outgoing | Sequential within action |
| **Initial Node** | None | One token on each outgoing | Source of flow |
| **Fork** | Exactly 1 incoming | One per outgoing | Splits into $n$ parallel branches |
| **Join** | All $k$ incoming $\ge 1$ | One on single outgoing | Synchronizes parallel branches |
| **Decision** | One incoming | One on a single guarded outgoing | Routes based on guard |
| **Merge** | One incoming (from any) | One on outgoing | Combines mutually exclusive flows |
| **Activity-Final** | One incoming | None (terminates entire activity) | Kills all remaining tokens |
| **Flow-Final** | One incoming | None (terminates one flow only) | Other flows continue |
| **Central Buffer** | $n$ incoming (stores) | $m$ outgoing (fetches) | Decouples producer/consumer rates |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Consider an action $A$ with 2 incoming edges $e_1, e_2$ (each with weight = 1) and 3 outgoing edges $f_1, f_2, f_3$ (each with weight = 1). At time $t=0$, $T(e_1) = 3, T(e_2) = 2, T(f_1) = 1, T(f_2) = 0, T(f_3) = 0$. Determine whether $A$ fires and compute the new state.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Check enabling:

$$
E(A, 0) = [T(e_1) \ge 1] \wedge [T(e_2) \ge 1] = [3 \ge 1] \wedge [2 \ge 1] = \text{True}
$$

Step 2 — Apply consumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T'(e_1) &= 3 - 1 = 2 \\
T'(e_2) &= 2 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Apply production (after delay $\tau_A$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T'(f_1) &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\
T'(f_2) &= 0 + 1 = 1 \\
T'(f_3) &= 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final state:** $T'(e_1, e_2, f_1, f_2, f_3) = (2, 1, 2, 1, 1)$. Action $A$ has fired once.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask for such a *before-after* numerical trace. Mastering this pattern is essential for full marks on 14-mark problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the Activity Semantics Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture of the UML Activity Semantics execution engine, including token dispatcher, node evaluator, and concurrency manager.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Activity Dispatcher] --> B[Token Pool Manager]
    B --> C{Node Evaluator}
    C --> D[Action Executor]
    C --> E[Control Node Resolver]
    C --> F[Object Node Handler]
    D --> G[State Update]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Concurrency Controller]
    H --> I[Fork Manager]
    H --> J[Join Synchronizer]
    H --> K[Decision Router]
    G --> L[Termination Checker]
    L --> M[Activity Final]
    L --> N[Flow Final]
    L --> O[Exception Handler]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Activity Dispatcher* seeds initial tokens; the *Node Evaluator* decides which node fires next; the *Concurrency Controller* manages fork/join synchronization; the *Termination Checker* handles final nodes and exceptions.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Token Flow Through Fork / Join / Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode[Start Token] --> forkA[Fork Node]
    forkA --> act1[Action 1: Validate Input]
    forkA --> act2[Action 2: Fetch User Profile]
    forkA --> act3[Action 3: Log Request]
    act1 --> joinA[Join Node]
    act2 --> joinA
    act3 --> joinA
    joinA --> dec1[Decision Node]
    dec1 -->|Success| proc1[Action 4: Process]
    dec1 -->|Failure| proc2[Action 5: Reject]
    proc1 --> actFinal[Activity Final Node]
    proc2 --> flowFinal[Flow Final Node]
```

## 4.3 Activity Semantics — Concept Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    sem[Activity Semantics] --> tokens[Token Theory]
    sem --> edges[Edge Semantics]
    sem --> nodes[Node Semantics]
    sem --> concur[Concurrency]
    tokens --> t1[Control Tokens]
    tokens --> t2[Object Tokens]
    edges --> e1[Control Flow]
    edges --> e2[Object Flow]
    edges --> e3[Interrupt Flow]
    nodes --> n1[Action Node]
    nodes --> n2[Control Node]
    nodes --> n3[Object Node]
    concur --> c1[Fork]
    concur --> c2[Join]
    concur --> c3[Decision and Merge]
```

## 4.4 Sequential Lifecycle of an Activity (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Phase 1: Initial Token Seeding] --> s2[Phase 2: Enabling Evaluation]
    s2 --> s3[Phase 3: Token Consumption]
    s3 --> s4[Phase 4: Action Execution]
    s4 --> s5[Phase 5: Token Production]
    s5 --> s6{Phase 6: Termination Check}
    s6 -->|Continue| s2
    s6 -->|Terminate| s7[Phase 7: Activity Finalized]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Lifecycle:** This is the canonical execution loop of any UML activity. KTU examiners may ask you to label each phase with its semantic role (e.g., *Phase 2 corresponds to the enabling predicate*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the token-flow semantics of UML activity diagrams. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Token-flow semantics is the formal execution model of UML 2 activity diagrams. It treats execution as the **movement of abstract tokens** along activity edges through activity nodes. An **action node** is enabled when *all* its incoming edges offer at least one token; when it fires, it **consumes** one token from each incoming edge and **produces** one token on each outgoing edge. **Control nodes** such as fork, join, decision, and merge **regulate** the routing and synchronization of these tokens. The semantics is inspired by **Petri nets** and provides a precise, mathematical foundation for concurrent workflow execution. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. Differentiate between an Activity-Final node and a Flow-Final node in UML 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Activity-Final Node | Flow-Final Node |
|---|---|---|
| Scope of termination | Terminates the **entire activity** | Terminates **only one flow path** |
| Other concurrent flows | All are killed immediately | Continue executing independently |
| Diagrammatic representation | Concentric bullseye (filled double circle) | Concentric bullseye with an **X** inside |
| Use case | End of business process | Ending an optional or side flow |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for each correct contrasting pair)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **token-flow semantics** of UML activity diagrams. Derive the **firing equation** for a generic action node with $n$ incoming and $m$ outgoing edges, including edge weights. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **semantics of fork, join, decision, and merge nodes** in UML 2. Show how tokens flow in each case. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define tokens (1 mark):** A *token* is an abstract marker that flows along activity edges. Tokens may be *control tokens* (representing availability) or *object tokens* (representing data values).

**Step 2 — Define the state of edges (1 mark):** For each edge $e_i$, define $T(e_i, t) \in \mathbb{N}_0$ as the token count at time $t$. Edge weights $w(e_i) \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ specify how many tokens are consumed/produced per firing.

**Step 3 — Enabling Predicate (2 marks):**

$$
E(A, t) \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \big[\, T(e_i, t) \;\ge\; w(e_i) \,\big]
$$

The action $A$ can begin **only** if every incoming edge has at least $w(e_i)$ tokens.

**Step 4 — Consumption (1 mark):**

$$
T'(e_i) \;=\; T(e_i, t) - w(e_i), \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, n
$$

**Step 5 — Production after delay $\tau_A$ (1 mark):**

$$
T'(f_j, t + \tau_A) \;=\; T(f_j, t) + w(f_j), \quad \forall j = 1, \dots, m
$$

**Step 6 — Master Firing Equation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(A, t) \wedge \text{True} \;&\Rightarrow\; \big[\,T(e_i,t) \mapsto T(e_i,t) - w(e_i)\,\big] \wedge \big[\,T(f_j,t+\tau_A) \mapsto T(f_j,t) + w(f_j)\,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical firing equation** of UML activity semantics.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Fork Node (1.5 marks):** A fork receives **one** token on its incoming edge and produces **one** token on **each** of its $n$ outgoing edges simultaneously, thereby enabling $n$ parallel flows.

$$
T_{\text{out}}(i) = T_{\text{in}}, \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, n
$$

**(ii) Join Node (1.5 marks):** A join synchronizes $k$ parallel flows. It waits until *all* $k$ incoming edges offer a token, then consumes one from each and produces one token on the single outgoing edge.

$$
E(J,t) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} [\,T(e_i,t) \ge 1\,]
$$

**(iii) Decision Node (1.5 marks):** A decision routes a single incoming token to **one** outgoing edge whose guard condition evaluates to True.

$$
\exists ! \, j \in [1,m] : g_j = \text{True}, \quad T(f_j) \mapsto T(f_j) + 1
$$

**(iv) Merge Node (1.5 marks):** A merge node combines multiple incoming edges into a single outgoing edge, passing through whichever token arrives. It performs *no synchronization*; it is the dual of a decision.

$$
T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}}(i), \quad \text{for the } i \text{ that offered a token}
```

**Reference Diagram (already covered in Section 4.2 of these notes).**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **six core semantics rules** of UML activity diagrams. State and explain each rule with an example. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider an action $A$ with three incoming edges $e_1, e_2, e_3$ (weights 1, 2, 1 respectively) and two outgoing edges $f_1, f_2$ (weights 2, 1). At $t=0$, $T(e_1)=2, T(e_2)=3, T(e_3)=1, T(f_1)=0, T(f_2)=5$. Determine if $A$ fires and compute the new state. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| # | Rule | Explanation (1 mark each + 1 mark for example) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Enabling Rule** | An action is enabled when all its incoming edges have $\ge 1$ token available. *Example:* A "Send Email" action waits until "Compose Mail" produces a mail object. |
| 2 | **Firing Rule** | An enabled action consumes one token per incoming edge and produces one per outgoing edge. *Example:* "Validate Order" consumes order + customer tokens, produces a "validated order" token. |
| 3 | **Fork Rule** | A fork duplicates the incoming token onto all outgoing edges in parallel. *Example:* In a hospital workflow, "Admit Patient" forks into "Assign Doctor" and "Prepare Room" simultaneously. |
| 4 | **Join Rule** | A join synchronizes — it fires only when all upstream branches have completed. *Example:* "Discharge Patient" joins the results of "Final Bill" and "Doctor Sign-off". |
| 5 | **Decision Rule** | A decision routes a token to one outgoing branch based on a guard. *Example:* "Payment Success?" routes either to "Ship Order" or "Cancel Order". |
| 6 | **Merge Rule** | A merge combines mutually exclusive flows back into one. *Example:* "Approved" and "Rejected" branches merge into "Notify Applicant". |

*(6 marks for rule statements, 1 mark for an integrated example)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Check enabling condition (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(A, 0) &= [T(e_1) \ge 1] \wedge [T(e_2) \ge 2] \wedge [T(e_3) \ge 1] \\
&= [2 \ge 1] \wedge [3 \ge 2] \wedge [1 \ge 1] \\
&= \text{True} \wedge \text{True} \wedge \text{True} \\
&= \text{True}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$\therefore$ Action $A$ is enabled and will fire.**

**Step 2 — Apply consumption (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T'(e_1) &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
T'(e_2) &= 3 - 2 = 1 \\
T'(e_3) &= 1 - 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply production (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T'(f_1) &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
T'(f_2) &= 5 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final state (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\,T'(e_1, e_2, e_3, f_1, f_2) = (1, 1, 0, 2, 6)\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "decision" with "merge"** — decision *splits* mutually exclusive flows; merge *combines* them. Drawing only a decision is worth **half marks**; the merge is the dual and must appear.
> 2. **Forgetting edge weights** — If an edge weight is 2, the action must consume **2** tokens, not 1. This is a **3-mark deduction** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Conflating Activity-Final and Flow-Final** — Examiners frequently place this as a 3-mark Part A trap. Memorize the table in Q2 above.
> 4. **Skipping the enabling check** — In numerical trace questions, you **must first state the enabling predicate** and verify it before applying consumption/production. Skipping this step loses **1–2 marks** as the "logical justification".
> 5. **Not mentioning concurrency semantics** — In 14-mark answers, merely drawing a fork/join without explaining the **token-splitting and synchronization rules** scores only 3/7 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Activity Semantics**

- ✅ **Token-flow model** is the foundation of UML 2 activity semantics (Petri-net inspired).
- ✅ **Three structural primitives:** Activity Nodes, Activity Edges, Tokens.
- ✅ **Six core rules:** Enabling, Firing, Fork, Join, Decision, Merge.
- ✅ **Enabling Predicate:** $E(A, t) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} [T(e_i, t) \ge w(e_i)]$
- ✅ **Firing Equation:** $T(e_i) \mapsto T(e_i) - w(e_i)$ (consume); $T(f_j) \mapsto T(f_j) + w(f_j)$ (produce).
- ✅ **Fork** = 1 in, $n$ out simultaneously → concurrency.
- ✅ **Join** = $k$ in synchronized, 1 out → synchronization.
- ✅ **Decision** = 1 in, 1 selected out (by guard) → routing.
- ✅ **Merge** = $k$ in, 1 out (no synchronization) → recombination of mutually exclusive flows.
- ✅ **Activity-Final** ends the *entire* activity; **Flow-Final** ends *one* flow path.
- ✅ **Control flow** = control tokens (no value); **Object flow** = object tokens (typed values).
- ✅ **Edge weight** $w$ is critical in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Central Buffer Node** decouples producer/consumer rates.
- ✅ **Interruptible Region** allows flow to be aborted by an exception.
- ✅ Activity semantics is **not the same as state machine semantics** — activity is about *flow of control & data*, state machine is about *lifecycle of a single object*.
- ✅ Always **state the enabling condition** before computing token transitions in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Activity Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal UML Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity Partition (Swimlane):** An activity partition in UML 2.5 is an organizational unit of an activity diagram that is used to group the actions and other elements (control nodes, object nodes) that share a common characteristic — most commonly, the **responsible entity** that performs them (e.g., an actor, a class, a subsystem, a component, a use case, or an organizational unit).

In the UML 2.5 specification, an `ActivityPartition` is modeled as a sub-class of `ActivityGroup`. It is represented visually as a rectangular region (a "lane" or "swimlane") that visually partitions the activity diagram into columns (vertical lanes) or rows (horizontal lanes). Every action, control node, and object node belongs to one (or more) partition(s) by virtue of graphical containment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties of Activity Partitions (per UML 2.5 superstructure):**
> - A partition has a **name** and an optional **represents** reference, which may point to a Classifier, an Instance, or a Part (decomposed component).
> - Partitions may be **nested** (sub-partitions), allowing hierarchical responsibility decomposition.
> - Partitions may be **multi-dimensional** — an action can simultaneously lie in the intersection of two or more orthogonal partitions (e.g., a vertical "Department" lane and a horizontal "Phase" lane).
> - An activity may contain **zero or more** partitions; the absence of partitions means the entire activity is treated as a single undivided unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Board"

Imagine a hospital's **triage whiteboard** at the Emergency Department. The board is divided into four vertical lanes:
1. **Reception Desk** — registers incoming patients.
2. **Nurse Station** — measures vitals and assigns priority.
3. **Doctor's Cabin** — performs diagnosis and prescribes treatment.
4. **Billing Counter** — handles discharge paperwork and payment.

Each lane **groups together the tasks performed by a single role**. The arrow lines that travel horizontally across the lanes show how a single patient's "activity flow" passes from one responsible role to the next. Now translate that whiteboard into a UML activity diagram, and each lane is exactly an **Activity Partition**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *"Swimlane = Who does what."* A partition tells the reader **which actor / class / component is responsible** for a set of activities, and the arrows tell the reader **the order in which responsibilities hand off**.

## 1.3 Standard UML Notation

| Element | Visual Notation in UML | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Partition Header Label | Bold text at the top of a vertical lane (or left of a horizontal lane) | Identifies the responsible entity |
| Partition Frame | Solid continuous rectangle | Encloses all actions belonging to that entity |
| Sub-Partition | A smaller rectangle inside a parent partition | Hierarchical decomposition of responsibility |
| Partition Dimension Indicator | A small `square` symbol on the partition border (2 partitions only) | Indicates the partition is one of 2+ dimensions (e.g., "Department" × "Phase") |

## 1.4 When Does UML Mandate Partitioning?

> [!NOTE]
> Partitions are **optional** in UML — an activity diagram is syntactically valid with zero partitions. However, the KTU 2024 examiner routinely awards marks for partitioning when the question is *"Design an activity diagram for the given scenario"* because partitioning directly demonstrates **modular responsibility allocation** (mapped to CO2: Apply UML structural & behavioral diagrams).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Activity Partition

An `ActivityPartition` has the following formal UML meta-model structure (simplified):

```
ActivityPartition
├── name : String                        (e.g., "Sales Department")
├── represents : Classifier | Instance    (e.g., the Class "Sales" or actor "Salesperson")
├── isDimension : Boolean                (true if a multi-dimensional split)
├── isExternal : Boolean                 (true if outside the parent activity)
├── contents : set(ActivityNode)         (the actions / control nodes enclosed)
└── subPartition : set(ActivityPartition)(for nesting)
```

> **Reading this for the exam:** A partition is **not** an executable object — it is a **graphical grouping container** that has zero runtime semantics. Its only effect is on **how the diagram is read** by humans.

## 2.2 Rules of Containment (The 'Why' and 'How')

1. **Containment Rule:** Any activity node (action, decision, fork, merge, etc.) that is drawn **inside** the rectangle of a partition is considered to belong to that partition.
2. **Multi-membership Rule:** An action can sit in **two orthogonal partitions simultaneously** if and only if those partitions lie along different dimensions (e.g., a horizontal "Phase" lane and a vertical "Department" lane cross — actions inside the crossing rectangle belong to both).
3. **Sub-partition Rule:** A child partition inherits the represented classifier of its parent unless explicitly overridden. Sub-partitions are drawn as nested rectangles inside the parent.
4. **Transition Rule:** A control flow (arrow) can freely cross partition boundaries. The crossing of a boundary is **not a semantic event**; it is purely a visual cue that responsibility is being handed over from one entity to another.

## 2.3 Types of Partitions

| Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Actor Partition** | `represents` an `Actor` (e.g., "Customer") | Business workflows with multiple human roles |
| **Class Partition** | `represents` a `Classifier` / Class (e.g., "OrderProcessor") | System-level design showing class responsibilities |
| **Component Partition** | `represents` a `Component` or `Subsystem` | Architectural activity flows |
| **Use-Case Partition** | `represents` a `UseCase` | Activity flow grouped by use case |
| **Sub-Partition (Nested)** | A partition inside another | Hierarchical decomposition (e.g., "IT Dept" → "Frontend Team", "Backend Team") |
| **Multi-dimensional Partition** | Two orthogonal sets of partitions | Matrices such as Phase × Department |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Bullet Matrix)

> [!TIP]
> Below is the **comprehensive bullet summary** a student should be able to write verbatim in a 14-mark answer. Memorize the structure.

- **Definition:** An activity partition (swimlane) groups actions/objects that share a common responsible entity.
- **Graphical Form:** Solid rectangle with the entity name in **bold** at the lane header.
- **Purpose (3 reasons — each worth 2 marks in an essay answer):**
  1. To show **who is responsible** for each action.
  2. To reveal the **hand-off points** between actors/components.
  3. To enable **high-level process and responsibility modeling** in a single diagram.
- **Direction:** Vertical lanes (read top-to-bottom) are most common; horizontal lanes (read left-to-right) are also valid.
- **Nesting:** Sub-partitions are allowed for hierarchical decomposition.
- **Multi-dimensional Partitions:** Orthogonal crossings allow n-dimensional responsibility matrices.
- **Transitions:** Arrows can cross partition boundaries freely; the boundary crossing itself has **no semantic meaning** (it is purely a visual grouping).
- **Constraint:** A node must lie within at least one partition; if no partition is shown, the entire activity is the default partition.
- **Difference from Use Case:** A use case describes the externally visible behavior of a system; an activity partition only organizes activities by responsibility.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Activity partitions are heavily used in:
> - **Business Process Modeling (BPM)** — to clarify "RACI" (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) assignments.
> - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) Design** — to show which service handles which step of a composite business flow.
> - **Embedded Systems Co-Design** — to partition software and hardware responsibilities (SW lane vs. HW lane).
> - **Agile Sprint Planning** — to map user-story tasks to team members.
> - **DevOps CI/CD Pipelines** — to show which tool (Jenkins, Docker, Kubernetes, SonarQube) is responsible for each pipeline stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> Since Activity Partitions is a **conceptual modeling** topic (not a numerical one), the "derivation" below is a **rigorous step-by-step procedure for constructing a partitioned activity diagram** — the kind a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark "Design" question.

## 3.1 The 7-Step Construction Procedure

The KTU 2024 examiner expects a 14-mark diagram to be drawn in this order:

1. **Step 1 — Identify all participating entities.** From the problem statement, list every actor, class, component, or subsystem that performs at least one action. *(Marks: identification clarity = 2 marks)*

2. **Step 2 — Identify all activities.** Enumerate each discrete task mentioned in the scenario. Group verbs from the problem text. *(Marks: completeness = 2 marks)*

3. **Step 3 — Determine the ordering of activities.** For each activity, find its predecessor(s) and successor(s). This is the control-flow logic. *(Marks: decision/fork logic = 2 marks)*

4. **Step 4 — Assign each activity to a partition.** Map every activity to exactly one responsible entity (or to the intersection of two orthogonal partitions). *(Marks: correct assignment = 3 marks)*

5. **Step 5 — Draw the partition frame.** Draw one rectangle per entity, arranged either vertically (left-to-right flow) or horizontally (top-to-bottom flow). Label the lane header in **bold** with the entity name. *(Marks: drawing quality = 2 marks)*

6. **Step 6 — Place nodes and edges.** Drop the initial node, action nodes, decision nodes, fork/join nodes, and final node into the correct lanes. Draw directed control-flow arrows between them. Allow arrows to cross lane boundaries (this depicts hand-off). *(Marks: notation = 2 marks)*

7. **Step 7 — Add a legend and any object nodes.** Add an explanatory legend (especially for multi-dimensional partitions), and draw object-flow nodes for inputs/outputs (e.g., "OrderForm" object passed from Customer to Sales). *(Marks: completeness = 1 mark)*

## 3.2 Worked Example: "Online Order Processing System"

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 style):** *A customer browses a catalog, adds items to a cart, and places an order. The Sales department validates payment and confirms the order. The Inventory department checks stock, and either reserves the items (success) or notifies the customer of out-of-stock. After successful reservation, the Shipping department dispatches the parcel and the customer is notified.*

### Step-by-Step Construction (Logical Walkthrough)

**Step 1 — Entities Identified:**
- `Customer` (actor)
- `Sales` (department)
- `Inventory` (department)
- `Shipping` (department)

**Step 2 — Activities Identified (verb extraction):**
- Browse Catalog
- Add to Cart
- Place Order
- Validate Payment
- Check Stock
- Reserve Items
- Notify Out-of-Stock
- Dispatch Parcel
- Notify Customer

**Step 3 — Ordering (control flow):**
$$
\text{Browse Catalog} \to \text{Add to Cart} \to \text{Place Order} \to \text{Validate Payment} \to \text{Check Stock} \to \begin{cases} \text{Reserve Items} \to \text{Dispatch Parcel} \to \text{Notify Customer} \\ \text{Notify Out-of-Stock} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Partition Assignment:**

| Activity | Assigned Partition |
|---|---|
| Browse Catalog, Add to Cart, Place Order | **Customer** |
| Validate Payment | **Sales** |
| Check Stock, Reserve Items, Notify Out-of-Stock | **Inventory** |
| Dispatch Parcel, Notify Customer | **Shipping** |

**Step 5–7 — Visual Construction:** The diagram is rendered in **Section 4** below.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Tested in 2-Mark Sub-Questions)

| Criterion | Activity Partition (Swimlane) | Simple Activity Diagram (no partitions) | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Responsibility shown?** | ✅ Yes (explicit) | ❌ No (single global space) | ⚠️ Indirectly (lifelines) |
| **Order of actions shown?** | ✅ Yes (control flow) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Time-axis shown?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (vertical lifeline) |
| **Branching/Loops?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Limited (alt/opt/par fragments) |
| **Use case for KTU exam** | Business process / role-based flow | Algorithm / single-class flow | Object-message interaction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Swimlane Activity Diagram (Order Processing)

> The diagram below uses Mermaid `flowchart` syntax to render the partitioned activity diagram for the "Online Order Processing System" worked example. Each `subgraph` block is one **Activity Partition (Swimlane)**. The arrows are UML control-flow edges.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CUST["CUSTOMER LANE"]
        A1(["Browse Catalog"])
        A2(["Add to Cart"])
        A3(["Place Order"])
        A6(["Receive Notification"])
    end

    subgraph SALES["SALES LANE"]
        B1["Validate Payment"]
    end

    subgraph INV["INVENTORY LANE"]
        C1{"Stock Available?"}
        C2["Reserve Items"]
        C3["Notify Out-of-Stock"]
    end

    subgraph SHIP["SHIPPING LANE"]
        D1[/"Dispatch Parcel"/]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> C1
    C1 -- Yes --> C2 --> D1 --> A6
    C1 -- No --> C3 --> A6

    style CUST fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style SALES fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style INV fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style SHIP fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide for the Exam:**
> - The **`subgraph` block** = an Activity Partition.
> - The **node labels in parentheses, e.g., `(["..."])`** = UML `AcceptEventAction`-style (rounded bracket) for actions triggered by an actor.
> - The **diamond `{}`** = UML `DecisionNode` with a guard condition.
> - The **parallelogram `[/"..."/]`** = UML action node (also acceptably drawn as a plain rectangle).
> - **Arrows crossing from one subgraph to another** = control flow crossing partition boundaries, signifying **responsibility hand-off**.

## 4.2 Alternative Rendering: Multi-Dimensional Partitions (Phase × Department)

> When a single dimension is insufficient (e.g., the activity needs to be sliced by *Department* **and** by *Project Phase*), UML permits **orthogonal (multi-dimensional) partitions**. The Mermaid `flowchart` cannot natively draw a true 2-D grid, so the schematic below is rendered as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE["PHASE DIMENSION (Vertical Axis)"]
        P1["Requirements"]
        P2["Design"]
        P3["Implementation"]
        P4["Testing"]
    end

    subgraph DEPT["DEPARTMENT DIMENSION (Horizontal Axis)"]
        D1["BA Team"]
        D2["Architecture Team"]
        D3["Dev Team"]
        D4["QA Team"]
    end

    subgraph INTER["INTERSECTION CELLS (Actions)"]
        I1["Gather Requirements"]
        I2["Design Database"]
        I3["Code Modules"]
        I4["Execute Test Cases"]
    end

    P1 --- D1
    P2 --- D2
    P3 --- D3
    P4 --- D4
    PHASE --- INTER
    DEPT --- INTER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Dimensional Partition Symbol:** In standard UML 2.5 notation, a small **square** drawn on the partition border (rather than a name) indicates that the partition is *one dimension* of an n-dimensional matrix. This is a high-yield 2-mark short-answer.

## 4.3 Common Notation Errors to Avoid

| Error | Consequence | Correct Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing a partition as a **dashed** line | UML requires **solid** rectangle | Solid line with bold name |
| Forgetting the **bold lane label** | Loses 1 mark for labeling | Bold entity name at the top of lane |
| Drawing arrows that **loop back through the same lane** repeatedly | Looks like spaghetti — restructure with a **merge node** | Use a merge node (empty diamond) |
| Putting an action in **two horizontal lanes** of the same dimension | Violates the multi-membership rule (only allowed across dimensions) | Pick the single correct lane |
| Confusing partition **border crossing** with a **decision or fork** | Misreads responsibility handoff as control flow | Crossing is plain arrow — no special node |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
**Define the term "Activity Partition" in UML. Why is it also called a "swimlane" diagram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Activity Partition** is an organizational grouping in a UML activity diagram that bundles together all actions and control nodes sharing a common responsible entity — such as an actor, a class, a component, or a subsystem. It is depicted as a solid rectangle (a "lane") that spans the full width or height of the activity, with the responsible entity's name written in bold at the lane's header.
>
> The visual appearance of a series of side-by-side or stacked rectangles — each labeled with a role — strongly resembles the parallel lanes of a competition swimming pool, with the activity flow moving from one lane to another as the responsibility is handed off. Hence the nickname **"swimlane diagram."**
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for graphical form, 1 mark for swimlane analogy.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**List any three advantages of using Activity Partitions in UML behavioral modeling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Clarity of Responsibility** — Every action is visually associated with exactly one (or more) responsible entity, making the model's accountability matrix self-documenting. *(1 mark)*
2. **Hand-off Identification** — Edges that cross partition boundaries immediately reveal the points where control or work is transferred from one role to another — a feature especially valuable in business process modeling. *(1 mark)*
3. **Hierarchical Decomposition** — Sub-partitions allow a top-level role (e.g., "IT Department") to be decomposed into finer-grained sub-roles (e.g., "Frontend Team", "Backend Team", "QA Team") without spawning multiple diagrams. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** write "Activity partitions help in object creation" or "they are used for inheritance." These are common misconceptions. Stick to *responsibility, hand-off, and hierarchy* — the three KTU-preferred answer points.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

**a)** Explain the concept of **Activity Partitions** in UML with a neat diagram. Discuss the concept of **multi-dimensional partitions** with an example. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Draw an **activity diagram with swimlanes** for the following scenario: *"A patient visits a hospital. The Reception registers the patient. The Nurse records vitals. The Doctor diagnoses and prescribes medicine. The Pharmacy dispenses the medicine. The Billing counter generates the final bill and the patient pays."* Use a minimum of **three decision/diamond nodes** if applicable. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part A(a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** An Activity Partition is a graphical container in a UML activity diagram that groups activities performed by the same responsible entity (actor, class, subsystem, or component). It is depicted as a solid rectangle ("lane") with the entity's name in bold at the header.

**Why Partitions? (2 marks):**
- Display **"who does what"** in a single view.
- Reveal **hand-off points** where one role transfers work to another.
- Allow **hierarchical sub-partitioning** for fine-grained responsibility modeling.

**Multi-Dimensional Partitions (3 marks):** In UML 2.5, partitions may be arranged along **orthogonal dimensions** — for instance, *Department* (horizontal) × *Project Phase* (vertical). An action located in the crossing rectangle of both partitions is considered to belong to **both dimensions simultaneously**. The example below shows a Software Project where the "Code Module" action is jointly performed by the *Dev Team* (Department dimension) during the *Implementation* phase (Phase dimension). A small square symbol on the partition border indicates that the partition is one dimension of a multi-dimensional matrix.

---

#### Part A(b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Lanes (1 mark):** Reception, Nurse, Doctor, Pharmacy, Billing, Patient.

**Step 2 — Identify Actions (1 mark):** Register, Record Vitals, Diagnose, Prescribe, Dispense, Generate Bill, Pay.

**Step 3 — Identify Decisions (1 mark):** "Patient insured?", "Medicine in stock?", "Bill amount correct?".

**Step 4–7 — Render the Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PAT["PATIENT LANE"]
        P1(["Visit Hospital"])
        P2(["Receive Medicine"])
        P3(["Pay Bill"])
        P4(["Leave Hospital"])
    end

    subgraph REC["RECEPTION LANE"]
        R1["Register Patient"]
    end

    subgraph NUR["NURSE LANE"]
        N1["Record Vitals"]
    end

    subgraph DOC["DOCTOR LANE"]
        D1["Diagnose"]
        D2["Prescribe Medicine"]
    end

    subgraph PHA["PHARMACY LANE"]
        H1{"Medicine Available?"}
        H2["Dispense Medicine"]
    end

    subgraph BIL["BILLING LANE"]
        B1["Generate Bill"]
    end

    P1 --> R1 --> N1 --> D1 --> D2 --> H1
    H1 -- Yes --> H2 --> P2 --> B1 --> P3 --> P4
    H1 -- No --> D1

    style PAT fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style REC fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style NUR fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style DOC fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style PHA fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
    style BIL fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825
```

> **Mark-split justification (as a KTU examiner would mark):**
> - 1 mark for correctly identifying 5+ lanes.
> - 1 mark for listing actions.
> - 1 mark for correct control-flow arrows + at least 1 decision node.
> - 1 mark for arrows crossing lane boundaries (showing hand-off).
> - 1 mark for the legend / lane label in bold.
> - 1 mark for a complete, closed flow (initial node to final node).
> - 1 mark for diagram neatness and accurate UML symbols.

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**

**a)** Differentiate between **Activity Partitions (Swimlanes)** and **non-partitioned Activity Diagrams**. When does UML 2.5 recommend the use of sub-partitions? *(7 marks)*

**b)** Design an **activity diagram with partitions** for a Library Book Issue process: *"A student requests a book. The Librarian checks availability. If available, the librarian issues the book and the student receives it. If not available, the librarian places a reservation request. When the book is returned by another member, the system notifies the reserved student, who then collects the book from the librarian."* *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part B(a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

| Aspect | With Partitions | Without Partitions |
|---|---|---|
| Responsibility mapping | **Explicit** per lane | **Implicit / ambiguous** |
| Hand-off visibility | **Clear** (arrow crossing boundary) | **Hidden** (no boundary to cross) |
| Diagram complexity | Higher (more visual structure) | Lower (single flat space) |
| Best for | Multi-role business processes | Single-component algorithms |
| Hierarchical modeling | **Supported** via sub-partitions | Not supported |
| UML 2.5 guidance | Recommended for > 1 actor | Acceptable for 1-actor workflows |

**When UML 2.5 Recommends Sub-Partitions (2 marks for this sub-point):**
- When the parent partition represents a **broad organizational unit** (e.g., "Hospital") that internally contains several **specialist sub-units** (e.g., "Cardiology", "Orthopedics", "Pediatrics").
- When the activity is **too dense** to fit legibly inside a single parent lane.
- When there is a **clear subordinate-superior relationship** between responsibilities, and the analyst wishes to show this hierarchy visually.
- Sub-partitions inherit the parent's `represents` classifier unless explicitly overridden; this is a 1-mark detail answer.

---

#### Part B(b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph STU["STUDENT LANE"]
        S1(["Request Book"])
        S2(["Receive Book"])
        S3(["Return Book"])
    end

    subgraph LIB["LIBRARIAN LANE"]
        L1{"Book Available?"}
        L2["Issue Book"]
        L3["Place Reservation"]
    end

    subgraph SYS["SYSTEM LANE"]
        M1["Notify Student on Return"]
    end

    S1 --> L1
    L1 -- Yes --> L2 --> S2
    L1 -- No --> L3
    S3 --> M1 --> S2

    style STU fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style LIB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style SYS fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

**Mark-split:**
- 1 mark for 3 lanes (Student, Librarian, System).
- 1 mark for the "Book Available?" decision node with two guard labels.
- 1 mark for the parallel "Return → Notify" flow.
- 1 mark for arrows crossing lane boundaries.
- 1 mark for closed flow (no dangling edges).
- 1 mark for UML-accurate symbol shapes.
- 1 mark for a one-line justification of why the System lane was introduced (responsibility of notification rests with the automated module, not the librarian).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deducting Mistakes in Activity Partition Questions (per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
> 1. **Drawing dashed partition lines** — UML mandates **solid** lines; dashed lines lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the bold lane name** — without a clear lane label, the partition is unreadable; loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Conflating activity partitions with class boundaries** — partitions group actions by responsibility, not by attribute ownership. *(Most common conceptual error.)*
> 4. **Not closing the flow** — every activity diagram must have a clear **initial node** (filled circle) and **final node** (bull's-eye). A diagram that "ends in the middle of a lane" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Using the wrong control-flow arrowhead style** — UML uses **open-V arrowheads** for control flow. Filled arrowheads are reserved for object flow; mixing them loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Activity Partition = Swimlane = Responsibility Grouping** — the three names refer to the *same* UML 2.5 element.
- ⭐ **Solid rectangle + bold header label** is the standard UML notation.
- ⭐ **Arrows crossing partition boundaries** depict **hand-offs** between roles — they have no extra semantic meaning.
- ⭐ **Sub-partitions** allow hierarchical decomposition of responsibility.
- ⭐ **Multi-dimensional partitions** are shown with a small **square symbol** on the partition border and allow n-dimensional responsibility matrices.
- ⭐ Partitions are **optional** but **strongly recommended** for any multi-actor business workflow.
- ⭐ A node may belong to **multiple orthogonal partitions** simultaneously (intersection cells).
- ⭐ Partitions are **NOT** used for class encapsulation, attribute ownership, or inheritance — those are class-diagram concepts.
- ⭐ The UML 2.5 specification classifies `ActivityPartition` as a sub-class of `ActivityGroup` — useful as a 1-mark "extra point" answer in viva.
- ⭐ For KTU 14-mark design questions: **list lanes → list actions → assign → draw → label → close flow** — follow the 7-step procedure to avoid half-marks.
- ⭐ Memorize the **3 advantages**: clarity of responsibility, hand-off identification, hierarchical decomposition.
- ⭐ Memorize the **3 differences from a plain activity diagram**: responsibility shown, hand-offs visible, hierarchy supported.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sending Signals and Accepting Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sending Signals and Accepting Events ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) In UML 2.5, **Signals** are a specialized kind of classifier that represents the specification of an asynchronous stimulus communicated between active objects. A *Send Signal Action* is an invocation action that creates a signal instance and transmits it to a target object, after which the sender continues execution without waiting for any return. Conversely, an *Accept Event Action* is an action that waits for the occurrence of a particular event (signal, call, or time event) before proceeding with subsequent actions in the behavioral model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Interactions):** > Signals are explicitly listed under "Advanced Interaction Notations" and belong to the **asynchronous communication** family. They are tested in Part A (2-mark/3-mark) and frequently appear in Part B (14-mark) sequence-diagram construction questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two people communicating in different ways: | Real-World Analogy | UML Equivalent | |---|---| | **Posting a registered letter** – you drop it in the mailbox and continue with your day. You do **not** stand at the post office waiting. | **Send Signal Action** (asynchronous) | | **Waiting for a doorbell** – you stop everything and stand at the door until the bell rings. | **Accept Event Action** | | **A phone call** – you speak, the other person listens, replies, and you both stay on the line. | **Synchronous Message** (for contrast) | So *sending a signal* is **fire-and-forget**, and *accepting an event* is the receiving side's way of **blocking until something happens**. ### 1.3 Key Terminology in Bold (Must Memorize) - **Signal**: A classifier stereotyped `<>` whose instances are asynchronously transmitted stimuli. - **Send Signal Action**: The action that *creates and dispatches* a signal. - **Accept Event Action**: An action that *suspends* execution until a matching event arrives. - **Asynchronous Message**: A message whose sender does **not** wait for a return. - **Signal Event**: The occurrence (reception) of a signal at a target. - **Reception**: A classifier feature that declares an object is prepared to accept a particular signal. > [!NOTE] > Signals are **first-class model elements** in UML. They can have attributes, generalizations, and be associated with operations – but they cannot have methods (only the receiver handles them). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Signal-Based Communication #### Pillar 1 — *Sending Signals* A **Send Signal Action** has these mandatory properties: 1. A **target** object (where the signal is sent). 2. A **signal** classifier (the *type* of stimulus). 3. Optional **arguments** matching the signal's attributes. The action's effect: - Constructs a new signal instance. - Adds it to the target's event queue. - **Returns immediately** – no reply is expected. #### Pillar 2 — *Accepting Events* An **Accept Event Action** has these mandatory properties: 1. A **trigger** specifying the kind of event to accept. 2. An **output pin** that holds the received event's data. 3. The action **blocks** its containing execution until the trigger fires. #### Pillar 3 — *Event Hierarchy* UML defines four event types; the first three are tied directly to signal-handling: $$ E \;=\; \{ \text{CallEvent},\; \text{SignalEvent},\; \text{TimeEvent},\; \text{ChangeEvent} \} $$ Each maps to a different Accept Event Action subtype. ### 2.2 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous – The Core Distinction $$ \text{Wait}_{\text{sync}}(t) \;=\; t_{\text{response}} \quad\quad \text{Wait}_{\text{async}}(t) \;=\; 0 $$ In words: a synchronous call **blocks the caller for the entire round-trip time**, while an asynchronous signal **blocks the caller for zero time**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet | # | Notation Element | UML Keyword / Glyph | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Signal classifier | `<>` | Declares a signal type | | 2 | Send Signal Action | `send` keyword (Activity) | Asynchronous dispatch | | 3 | Accept Event Action | `accept` keyword (Activity) | Blocking event receiver | | 4 | Asynchronous message | Open stick arrow `------>` | Sequence diagram | | 5 | Synchronous message | Filled triangle `--->` | Sequence diagram (contrast) | | 6 | Reception | `signal Reception()` (rectangle) | Declares handler on classifier | | 7 | Time event | `after 5s` | Relative temporal trigger | | 8 | Change event | `when(temp>100)` | Conditional trigger | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical UML 2.5 Rule:** A signal may be sent to *one* target or to a *broadcast* set of targets. If broadcast, the order of delivery is **not guaranteed** unless explicit ordering is added. ### 2.4 Why It Matters in Real Engineering Signal-based asynchronous communication is the backbone of: - **Embedded systems** – interrupt-driven I/O. - **Distributed systems** – message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ). - **GUI frameworks** – event listeners (Java AWT, .NET delegates). - **IoT devices** – publish/subscribe protocols (MQTT). ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 The Six-Step Lifecycle of a Signal We will trace a signal from creation to consumption. Let the sender be $S$, the target be $T$, and the signal type be $\Sigma$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Classification} && \Sigma \; \text{is declared with} \;\; \Sigma \ll \texttt{signal} \gg \\ &\text{Step 2: Instantiation} && s \;=\; \text{new} \; \Sigma(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n) \\ &\text{Step 3: Dispatch} && s \;\mapsto\; Q_T \quad (\text{enqueue at } T\text{'s event pool}) \\ &\text{Step 4: Suspension} && T.\text{accept}(\Sigma) \;\text{blocks until } \Sigma \text{ arrives} \\ &\text{Step 5: Reception} && T.\text{handle}(s) \;\text{is invoked with } s \text{ as argument} \\ &\text{Step 6: Continuation} && T \text{ resumes its normal execution flow} \end{aligned} $$ **Plain-English explanation of each step:** - *Step 1 (Classification)* – You tell UML "this is a signal called `Alarm`." - *Step 2 (Instantiation)* – The sender creates a real signal object filled with data. - *Step 3 (Dispatch)* – The signal is dropped into the target's mailbox. - *Step 4 (Suspension)* – The target is sitting in an `accept` action, waiting. - *Step 5 (Reception)* – The target's handler runs and uses the signal's data. - *Step 6 (Continuation)* – The target moves on to its next activity. ### 3.2 Derivation: Time-Complexity Argument for Asynchrony For a system with $n$ collaborators, the maximum execution time of a chain of synchronous calls is: $$ T_{\text{sync}}(n) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_{i} $$ where each $\tau_i$ is the processing time of the $i$-th participant (no overlap, sequential). For asynchronous signals: $$ T_{\text{async}}(n) \;=\; \max_{i=1..n} \tau_{i} $$ Because the sender never waits, the total wall-clock time is bounded by the **slowest** participant, not the **sum**. This is the engineering motivation behind signals in concurrent UML models. $$ \therefore \quad \frac{T_{\text{sync}}}{T_{\text{async}}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\tau_i}{\max_i \tau_i} \;\ge\; 1 $$ > [!NOTE] > The ratio equals **1 only when $n=1$** (trivial case) and grows linearly with $n$ in the worst case. This is why KTU examiners love asking *"Why prefer signals for loosely-coupled systems?"* – the answer lies in this concurrency argument. ### 3.3 Mapping: Accept Event Action Subtypes $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AcceptCallAction} &\;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{waits for an operation invocation} \\ \text{AcceptSignalAction} &\;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{waits for an asynchronous signal} \\ \text{WaitTimeAction} &\;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{waits for an absolute or relative deadline} \end{aligned} $$ Each subtype is a *specialization* of the generic `AcceptEventAction` in the UML metamodel. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram – Signal Dispatch ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as Sensor participant Alarm as AlarmSystem participant Display as DisplayPanel participant Logger as EventLogger Sensor->>Alarm: poll() Alarm-->>Sensor: temperature Note over Alarm: reading exceeds threshold Alarm-->>Display: send OverHeatSignal Note over Display: Asynchronous
Open arrow stick Alarm-->>Logger: send OverHeatSignal Note over Logger: Asynchronous
Open arrow stick Display-->>Display: accept SignalEvent Logger-->>Logger: accept SignalEvent Display-->>Display: renderWarning() Logger-->>Logger: persistEvent() ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Lines with `->>` are **synchronous** (filled triangles) – the caller waits. - Lines with `-->>` are **asynchronous** (open stick arrows) – fire-and-forget. - `accept` actions live inside the lifeline's execution bar. ### 4.2 Mermaid Activity Diagram – Accept Event Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[System starts] --> B[Enter accept SignalAction] B --> C{Event queue empty?} C -- Yes --> D[Suspend execution] D --> E[Wait indefinitely] E --> C C -- No --> F[Dequeue signal s] F --> G[Bind s to output pin] G --> H[Execute handler logic] H --> I[Output to next action] I --> J[Continue flow] J --> B ``` **Node-ID compliance:** All IDs (`A`, `B`, `C`, …) are uppercase alphanumeric and prefixed – no reserved keywords used as standalone names. ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram – Functional Architecture of Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Sender S1[Active Object] --> S2[Send Signal Action] end subgraph Channel S2 --> S3[Event Queue of Target] end subgraph Receiver S3 --> R1[Accept Event Action] R1 --> R2[Dispatch to Reception] R2 --> R3[State Machine Transition] end subgraph Logging R1 -. asynchronous log .-> L1[Audit Logger] end ``` This block diagram shows **how a single send propagates to multiple independent acceptors**, which is a typical KTU long-answer scenario. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Q:** Differentiate between a synchronous message and a signal in UML sequence diagrams. Give one example where each is preferred. **Model Answer (Memorize this structure):** | Aspect | Synchronous Message | Signal (Asynchronous) | |---|---|---| | Arrow glyph | Filled triangle `▶` | Open stick arrow `>` | | Sender behavior | Waits for return | Continues immediately | | Return value | Yes (optional) | No (always) | | Coupling | Tight | Loose | | Example | `getBalance()` from a bank account | `send(OverHeatSignal)` to a fire alarm | > [!TIP] > Examiners award the full **3 marks** only if you mention *both* the glyph difference **and** the runtime blocking behavior. Missing either = -1 mark. --- #### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** What is an Accept Event Action in UML? List any two of its specializations. **Model Answer:** An *Accept Event Action* is an action that **waits (blocks)** for the occurrence of a specified event before allowing the surrounding activity to proceed. It has one *output pin* that receives the event payload. Two specializations: 1. **Accept Signal Action** – waits for a signal event. 2. **Accept Call Action** – waits for a synchronous call. (Acceptable third: **Wait Time Action** for temporal events.) --- ### Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** Design a UML sequence diagram for an **automatic fire alarm system** that demonstrates signal-based communication. Explain the role of the Send Signal Action and the Accept Event Action. *(Internal Choice: Question B below is the alternative.)* **Sub-part (a) – 7 Marks:** *Construct the sequence diagram and identify the asynchronous messages.* **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Actors \& Lifelines:} \\ &\quad \bullet \; \text{SmokeSensor} \;\to\; \text{FireAlarmController} \;\to\; \text{Siren} \;\to\; \text{EmergencyNotifier} \\ \\ &\text{Flow (each arrow labelled):} \\ &\text{1. SmokeSensor} \;-\!-\!\!>\!\!>\; \text{FireAlarmController : send(SmokeDetectedSignal)} \\ &\text{2. FireAlarmController} \;-\!-\!\!>\!\!>\; \text{Siren : send(ActivateSignal)} \\ &\text{3. FireAlarmController} \;-\!-\!\!>\!\!>\; \text{EmergencyNotifier : send(AlertSignal)} \\ &\text{4. Siren : accept(SignalAction)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{playAlarm()} \\ &\text{5. EmergencyNotifier : accept(SignalAction)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{callFireStation()} \\ \\ &\text{Key identification:} \; \text{All step-1/2/3 lines use open stick arrows `------>` = ASYNCHRONOUS.} \\ &\text{Steps 4/5 are Accept Signal Actions sitting inside the lifeline's execution bar.} \end{aligned} $$ **Examiner's reasoning steps (write this verbatim for 7 marks):** 1. Identify at least 4 lifelines – **1 mark**. 2. Use **asynchronous** arrow notation explicitly – **2 marks**. 3. Mark at least one **Accept Signal Action** with an output pin – **2 marks**. 4. Show **no return arrows** from the signal senders – **2 marks**. **Sub-part (b) – 7 Marks:** *Explain why signals are preferred over synchronous calls in this scenario.* **Model Solution:** - **Decoupling:** The SmokeSensor does not need to know whether the Siren or the Notifier exists – it just broadcasts. **(2 marks)** - **Concurrency:** Multiple actuators react **in parallel** without the controller waiting. **(2 marks)** - **Failure isolation:** If the Siren is offline, the Notifier still fires. **(1 mark)** - **Real-world alignment:** Matches interrupt-driven hardware semantics. **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the open arrow.** Students often draw filled triangles for signals – this loses 2 marks immediately. > 2. **Adding return arrows.** A signal has *no* reply. Drawing `------>|` is a structural error. > 3. **Confusing `<>` with `<>`.** Signals are classifiers; interfaces are contracts. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** Draw a UML activity diagram for a **coffee vending machine** that uses an `Accept Time Event Action` and an `Accept Signal Action`. Explain the model. **Sub-part (a) – 7 Marks:** *Construct the activity diagram and label the accept actions.* **Model Solution (to draw):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Initial Node} \;\to\; \text{Decision: CoinInserted?} \\ &\text{Yes branch:} \;\to\; \text{Send Signal Action (BrewRequestSignal)} \\ &\quad \quad \to\; \text{Accept Signal Action (ReceiveBrewingDone)} \\ &\quad \quad \to\; \text{Dispense Coffee} \\ &\quad \quad \to\; \text{Decision: More cups?} \\ &\quad \quad \quad \text{Yes} \;\to\; \text{Loop back} \\ &\quad \quad \quad \text{No} \;\to\; \text{Final Node} \\ &\text{No branch:} \;\to\; \text{Wait Time Action (after 30s)} \;\to\; \text{Send ShutdownSignal} \;\to\; \text{Final Node} \end{aligned} $$ **Mark breakdown for part (a):** - Initial + final nodes – **1 mark** - Send Signal Action correctly labelled – **2 marks** - Accept Signal Action with output pin – **2 marks** - Wait Time Action with `after 30s` keyword – **2 marks** **Sub-part (b) – 7 Marks:** *Compare Send Signal Action and Accept Signal Action.* **Model Answer Table:** | Feature | Send Signal Action | Accept Signal Action | |---|---|---| | Initiator | Sender (proactive) | Receiver (reactive) | | Blocking | No | Yes | | UML keyword | `send` | `accept` | | Output | Returns instantly with void | Holds received signal in output pin | | Used in | Activity, Sequence | Activity, State Machine | | Lifetime | Single dispatch | Persists until triggered | Write **at least 4 rows** of this table for full 7 marks. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Part B:** > - Do not place `accept` *before* `send` on the same lifeline – that is logically impossible. Sequential ordering matters. > - Do not omit the `after 30s` clause from the Wait Time Action – the UML keyword is **mandatory**. > - Mixing up `accept` with `receive` is a common slip. The correct UML 2.5 keyword is **`accept`**, not `receive`. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ☐ A **Signal** is a `<>` classifier; its instances are dispatched asynchronously. - ☐ **Send Signal Action** = *fire-and-forget*; sender does **not** wait. - ☐ **Accept Event Action** = *blocking*; receiver waits until event occurs. - ☐ Asynchronous arrow = **open stick** `------>`; synchronous = **filled triangle** `--->`. - ☐ Signals have **no return** value; synchronous messages may. - ☐ Event hierarchy: **Call, Signal, Time, Change** – all derive from `AcceptEventAction`. - ☐ Signals enable **decoupling, concurrency, and failure isolation**. - ☐ UML keyword is `accept`, **not** `receive`. - ☐ Use `after ` for time events and `when()` for change events. - ☐ In a sequence diagram, the *sender*'s execution bar **continues** through a signal dispatch (unlike a synchronous call, where it pauses).

topics.title: Interaction Overview Diagrams
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
