Accepted trade-off: increased operational complexity in exchange for scalability and failure isolation.”* ## 3.4 The Architectural "Decision Triangle" A compact visual that examiners love: $$\text{Architecture} = f(\text{Quality Attributes},\ \text{Style Constraints},\ \text{Business Goals})$$ Any two can be optimized, but **optimizing all three simultaneously is impossible** — this is the *architectural trade-off principle* that KTU expects you to mention in 14-mark answers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > All diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture schematics** that satisfy the KTU 2024 syllabus requirement of representing *architectural views* and *architectural styles* visually. ## 4.1 The 4+1 View Model — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB UC["PLUS 1 USE CASE VIEW
Scenarios and Use Cases"]:::uc LV["LOGICAL VIEW
Class Object Statechart
End Users Analysts"]:::lv PV["PROCESS VIEW
Activity Sequence Concurrency
Integrators"]:::pv PHY["PHYSICAL VIEW
Deployment Topology
System Engineers"]:::phy DV["DEVELOPMENT VIEW
Component Package Build
Programmers"]:::dv UC --> LV UC --> PV UC --> PHY UC --> DV LV <-.-> PV PV <-.-> PHY LV <-.-> DV DV <-.-> PHY classDef uc fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lv fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pv fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phy fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#8a0b0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dv fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#4b1f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Layered (3-Tier) Architecture — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB USER["CLIENT DEVICE
Browser or Mobile App"]:::user PRES["PRESENTATION LAYER
UI Components and Views"]:::pres BIZ["BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER
Domain Rules and Workflows"]:::biz DATA["DATA ACCESS LAYER
Repositories and ORM"]:::data DB["DATABASE SERVER
Persistent Storage"]:::db USER --> PRES PRES --> BIZ BIZ --> DATA DATA --> DB classDef user fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef pres fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef biz fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef data fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#8a0b0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Client–Server Architecture — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR C1["CLIENT A
Desktop App"]:::client C2["CLIENT B
Web Browser"]:::client C3["CLIENT C
Mobile App"]:::client SVR["SERVER
Application Logic"]:::server DB["DATABASE
Shared State"]:::db C1 -- HTTP / TCP --> SVR C2 -- HTTPS --> SVR C3 -- REST API --> SVR SVR -- SQL --> DB classDef client fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef server fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#8a0b0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Model–View–Controller (MVC) — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR USER["USER INPUT
Click Submit or Edit Form"]:::user CTRL["CONTROLLER
Receives Input Updates Model Selects View"]:::ctrl MOD["MODEL
Business Logic and Data State"]:::mod VW["VIEW
Renders UI From Model State"]:::view USER --> CTRL CTRL --> MOD CTRL --> VW MOD -- state update notification --> VW classDef user fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mod fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef view fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Microservices Architecture — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB GW["API GATEWAY
Single Entry Point Auth Routing"]:::gw SVC1["USER SERVICE
Owns user DB"]:::svc SVC2["PRODUCT SERVICE
Owns product DB"]:::svc SVC3["ORDER SERVICE
Owns order DB"]:::svc SVC4["PAYMENT SERVICE
Owns payment DB"]:::svc BUS["MESSAGE BUS
Kafka or RabbitMQ"]:::bus GW --> SVC1 GW --> SVC2 GW --> SVC3 GW --> SVC4 SVC3 -- order created event --> BUS SVC4 -- payment processed event --> BUS BUS -- async notification --> SVC1 BUS -- async notification --> SVC2 classDef gw fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef svc fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#4b1f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.6 Pipe-and-Filter Architecture — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["SOURCE
Raw Input Stream"]:::pipe F1["FILTER 1
Lexical Analysis"]:::filt F2["FILTER 2
Parsing"]:::filt F3["FILTER 3
Semantic Analysis"]:::filt F4["FILTER 4
Code Generation"]:::filt SNK["SINK
Output Object Code"]:::pipe SRC -- pipe --> F1 F1 -- pipe --> F2 F2 -- pipe --> F3 F3 -- pipe --> F4 F4 -- pipe --> SNK classDef pipe fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef filt fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.7 Event-Driven Architecture — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["PRODUCER 1
Order Service"]:::prod P2["PRODUCER 2
Inventory Service"]:::prod EB["EVENT BUS
Topic based routing"]:::bus Q1["CONSUMER A
Email Notifier"]:::cons Q2["CONSUMER B
Analytics Logger"]:::cons Q3["CONSUMER C
Warehouse Service"]:::cons P1 -- publish order placed --> EB P2 -- publish stock low --> EB EB -- subscribe --> Q1 EB -- subscribe --> Q2 EB -- subscribe --> Q3 classDef prod fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0b4f8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cons fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > Question bank modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year marker. **Part A** = 3 marks each. **Part B** = 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice pattern. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define software architecture. List any four stakeholders of a software system and their architectural concerns.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *Software Architecture is the fundamental organization of a system, embodied in its components, the relationships between them, and the principles governing its design and evolution.* | Stakeholder | Architectural Concern | |---|---| | End User | Functionality, usability, response time | | Developer | Module structure, interfaces, reusability | | System Administrator | Deployment, monitoring, fault tolerance | | Project Manager | Cost, schedule, risk visibility | | Customer | Time-to-market, feature completeness | *(Any 4 stakeholders with correct concerns — full 3 marks. 1 mark deducted if concerns are generic like "performance" without context.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is an architectural style? Differentiate between Layered and Microservices architecture in any two aspects.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Style** is a named, reusable collection of architectural decisions that (1) restricts the design vocabulary and (2) provides well-known quality attributes. | Aspect | Layered Architecture | Microservices Architecture | |---|---|---| | **Granularity** | Coarse (whole tiers) | Fine (small independent services) | | **Data Ownership** | Shared central database | Each service owns its own database | | **Deployment** | Single deployable unit | Independent deployment per service | | **Failure Impact** | One tier crash affects all | One service crash isolated | *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for any two correct differences.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU ESE 2024 rules, every module contains an **internal choice** — students answer *one* out of two questions. Both alternatives below are given. --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Kruchten's **4+1 View Model** of software architecture. For each view, state the UML diagram used and the stakeholder it primarily addresses. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Microservices Architecture** style. List four advantages and two disadvantages of adopting it. --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Introduction (2 marks):** Kruchten's **4+1 View Model** (1995) addresses the impossibility of capturing a system's architecture in a single diagram. Different stakeholders care about different *concerns* (functional, non-functional, deployment, code organization), so the model proposes **five concurrent views** of the same system. **Step 2 — List and Explain the Five Views (4 marks):** | View | Concern | UML Diagram | Stakeholder | |---|---|---|---| | **Logical** | Functional requirements — objects, classes, relationships | Class, Object, Statechart, Sequence | End Users, Analysts | | **Process** | Concurrency, synchronization, performance | Activity, Sequence, State | Integrators, Performance engineers | | **Physical** | Software-to-hardware mapping, deployment | Deployment diagram | System engineers, Network admins | | **Development** | Module organization, package structure, reuse | Component, Package diagram | Programmers, Project managers | | **+1 Use-Case** | Validates and integrates all four views via scenarios | Use-Case diagram | All stakeholders | **Step 3 — Why the "Plus One" (1 mark):** The **Use-Case view** is the *glue*. It contains the small set of critical scenarios that exercise and unify the other four views, ensuring the architecture satisfies the end-to-end functional requirements. *(Full 7 marks requires all 5 views with diagram + stakeholder + role of "+1".)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** **Microservices Architecture** is a style that structures an application as a **suite of small, independent, loosely-coupled services**, each: - Owning its **own database** (no shared schema). - Implementing **one bounded business capability**. - **Independently deployable** in its own process and container. - Communicating via **lightweight protocols** (HTTP/REST, gRPC, message queues). **Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Microservices diagram in Section 4.5 — show API Gateway at the front, individual services, and the asynchronous message bus for inter-service events. **Step 3 — Advantages (2 marks — any 4 of the following):** - **Independent Deployability** — each service can be deployed without redeploying others. - **Technology Heterogeneity** — different services can use different languages/DBs. - **Failure Isolation** — one service failure does not cascade to the whole system. - **Scalability** — individual services can be scaled out independently. - **Team Autonomy** — small teams can own and evolve services independently. - **Reusability** — services are consumed by multiple channels (web, mobile, partner APIs). **Step 4 — Disadvantages (1 mark — any 2):** - **Distributed System Complexity** — network latency, partial failures, eventual consistency. - **Operational Overhead** — requires DevOps maturity, container orchestration (Kubernetes), observability tooling. - **Data Consistency** — no ACID transactions across services; requires Saga or 2PC patterns. - **Testing Difficulty** — integration testing across many services is non-trivial. *(Full 7 marks: 2 definition + 2 diagram + 2 advantages + 1 disadvantages.)* --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Layered, Client–Server, and Pipe-Filter** architectural styles on the basis of: (i) component granularity, (ii) coupling and cohesion, (iii) typical use-case, and (iv) reusability. Use a comparison table. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **importance of software architecture** in modern software engineering. Discuss at least **five points** with suitable justification. --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Comparison Table (Full 7 Marks):** | Dimension | Layered | Client–Server | Pipe-Filter | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Component Granularity** | Coarse-grained layers (e.g., Presentation, Business, Data) | Two coarse units: Client process + Server process | Fine-grained filters performing single transformations | | **(ii) Coupling** | Tight coupling between adjacent layers (e.g., UI calls Service) | Medium — clients depend on server contract, but server is unaware of clients | Loose — filters are independent, communicate only via data streams | | **(ii) Cohesion** | High within a layer (all UI logic together) | Medium — client cohesive around UI, server cohesive around shared logic | Very high — each filter does *one* well-defined transformation | | **(iii) Typical Use-Case** | Enterprise business applications, ERP, banking portals | Web apps, DB-backed services, online multiplayer games | Compiler pipelines, UNIX shells, ETL data processing | | **(iv) Reusability** | High — layers (e.g., Business Logic) reusable across UI changes | Medium — server reusable across multiple client types | Very high — filters composable in new pipelines without modification | **Concluding Statement (optional, earns bonus):** - *Layered* is best when **modifiability** is the priority and performance overhead is acceptable. - *Client–Server* is best for **centralized data** and **multi-user** access patterns. - *Pipe-Filter* is best for **batch / stream processing** where each stage is a clean transformation. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Introduction (1 mark):** Software Architecture is the **foundational blueprint** of a software system, capturing the most consequential design decisions. It is the *only* artifact that bridges *requirements* and *implementation* in a controlled, verifiable way. **Five Importance Points (5 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Stakeholder Communication** A well-documented architecture provides a *common vocabulary* for developers, managers, clients, and testers. Different stakeholders read the *view* most relevant to them (Logical for analysts, Physical for operators). 2. **Realization of Quality Attributes** Architecture is the **primary determinant** of non-functional requirements: performance, security, modifiability, availability, scalability. Once committed, these attributes are extremely expensive to alter — only the architecture can establish them. 3. **Early Risk Management** Architecture forces the team to confront **integration, performance, and deployment risks** at design time — when they are *cheap* to fix. Discovering these risks during testing is 10–100x more expensive. 4. **Reuse at Multiple Granularities** A modular architecture enables reuse of **components, subsystems, and even entire applications** across products. The 4+1 view model formalizes the boundaries at which reuse is meaningful. 5. **Foundation for the Development Process** Architecture drives the **team structure**, **work breakdown**, **build pipeline**, and **test plan**. Conway's Law tells us the *organization* of the team will mirror the architecture — making this decision early is critical. **Conclusion (1 mark):** > *“Architecture is not just a design artifact — it is the* **largest leverage point** *for quality, cost, and risk in any non-trivial software system.”* (This is the kind of closing statement KTU examiners reward.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Architecture" with "Design"** — Architecture is about *significant structural decisions visible across the system*; design is about *localized, internal module decisions*. Mixing these in a 14-mark answer loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Listing architectural styles without explaining trade-offs** — Examiners explicitly deduct marks if you say *"Microservices is good"* without stating *what you sacrifice* (e.g., operational complexity, distributed transactions). > 3. **Forgetting the "Plus One" in 4+1** — Many students only describe 4 views. The "+1 Use-Case view" is **mandatory** in KTU valuation; omitting it is a 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Drawing Component Diagram instead of Deployment Diagram** — When asked for the "Physical View", the correct UML diagram is the **Deployment Diagram**, not the Component Diagram. This is a frequent mix-up. > 5. **Writing only advantages of a style** — Always write **both advantages AND disadvantages**. A 14-mark question that asks for 4 advantages + 2 disadvantages is graded strictly. > 6. **Skipping the closing summary statement** — A 1-sentence summary is worth ~1 mark and improves presentation marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:** - ⭐ **Definition (IEEE 1471):** Architecture = Components + Connectors + Configurations + Constraints. - ⭐ **Why it matters:** Bridges *requirements* and *implementation*; determines *quality attributes*; manages *risk early*. - ⭐ **4+1 View Model** (Kruchten): **Logical, Process, Physical, Development + Use-Case (+1)**. The "+1" is the **Use-Case view** that validates the others. - Logical → Class/Object/Statechart (End Users, Analysts). - Process → Activity/Sequence/State (Integrators, Performance Engineers). - Physical → **Deployment Diagram** (System Engineers). - Development → **Component/Package Diagram** (Programmers, PMs). - +1 Use-Case → **Use-Case Diagram** (All Stakeholders). - ⭐ **Architectural Styles to Memorize:** Layered, Client–Server, Pipe–Filter, MVC, Microservices, Repository, Event-Driven. - ⭐ **Style → Quality Mapping** (high-yield for 14-mark Qs): - **High Modifiability** → Layered, MVC, Microservices. - **High Performance** → Microservices, Event-Driven. - **High Scalability** → Microservices, Event-Driven. - **High Reusability** → Layered, Pipe–Filter. - **High Security** → Client–Server. - ⭐ **Trade-off Triangle:** Architecture = f(Quality Attributes, Style Constraints, Business Goals) — *optimize at most two; never all three*. - ⭐ **Five Points of Importance** (for any 7/14-mark question): (1) Communication, (2) Quality Realization, (3) Early Risk Mgmt, (4) Reuse, (5) Process Foundation. - ⭐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Don't confuse **Component Diagram** (Development View) with **Deployment Diagram** (Physical View) — different UML diagrams, different concerns. - ⭐ **One-Sentence Closing Statement (always add):** *"A well-designed software architecture is the single most cost-effective risk-mitigation and quality-realization strategy in any non-trivial software project."*

topics.title: Software Architecture Patterns: Component and Connector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Architecture Patterns: Component and Connector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component-and-Connector (C&C) View** is an architectural viewtype that describes a software system as a collection of **components** (computational elements and data stores) and **connectors** (interaction mechanisms) that coordinate the components via **interactions**, **data flow**, or **control flow**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with Shaw & Garlan's *Software Architecture: Perspectives on an Emerging Discipline* and Rozanski & Woods), the C&C viewtype answers the runtime questions:

- *What are the major executing components and how do they interact?*
- *What are the major shared data stores?*
- *What is the control structure of the system?*
- *How do components communicate, coordinate, and transfer data?*

### 1.1.1 Components (Computational & Data Elements)

A **Component** is a runtime architectural element that:
- Encapsulates a subset of the system's functionality and/or state.
- Has a well-defined interface (provided + required).
- Is substitutable and independently deployable (in modern interpretations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinds of Components (KTU Examination Favourite):**
> | Component Kind | Role | Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Computation Component** | Performs transformation on data | `OrderProcessor`, `Encryptor` |
> | **Data Store / Data Component** | Persists state/data | `Database`, `FileArchive`, `Cache` |
> | **Adapter / Coordinator** | Bridges interfaces, marshals calls | `Dispatcher`, `MessageBroker` |
> | **Controller / Coordinator** | Governs execution order and control | `ProcessScheduler` |

### 1.1.2 Connectors (Interaction Mechanisms)

A **Connector** is an architectural building block whose role is to **mediate interactions among components**. Connectors are first-class entities—they are not just method calls but stand-alone abstractions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinds of Connectors:**
> - **Procedure Call / RPC / RMI** – synchronous, tight coupling.
> - **Shared Variable / Data Repository Access** – multiple components read/write common data.
> - **Message Passing / Event Broadcast** – asynchronous, loose coupling.
> - **Stream / Pipe** – continuous data flow.
> - **Linker / Distributor / Broker** – routes requests, balances load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hospital Emergency System**
> - **Components** = Doctors, Nurses, Reception Desk, Pharmacy, Lab, Patient Records Database.
> - **Connectors** = The intercom system (event broadcast), the patient file (shared data store), the referral slip (message passing), the phone call (RPC).
> - The **C&C View** = the floor plan of the hospital showing *who talks to whom, via what medium, while patients are being treated* (i.e., at runtime).
>
> The hospital *modules* (reception, OT, ICU) tell you the **logical** structure. The floor plan with corridors, lifts, and shared records tells you the **runtime** structure. That floor plan is the C&C view.

## 1.3 Three Sub-Styles of C&C View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The C&C viewtype decomposes into three principal sub-styles:
> 1. **Data-Flow Style** – components are filters/transformers; connectors are data streams.
> 2. **Call-and-Return Style** – components are objects/services; connectors are procedure calls.
> 3. **Independent-Components / Event-Based Style** – components are independent; connectors are message/event brokers.
>
> Mixed styles (e.g., **shared-data style** sitting *between* data-flow and call-and-return) are also classified.

---

## 1.4 Why C&C Views Matter in KTU 2024 Syllabus

- They model **runtime behaviour**, complementing the **module viewtype** (compile-time) studied earlier.
- They directly support **quality attributes** like *performance, availability, modifiability, security, integrability*.
- They are the basis for **architectural patterns** such as Client-Server, Pipe-and-Filter, Publish-Subscribe, SOA, and Shared-Repository.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Structure of a C&C View

A C&C view is a graph:

$$ G_{CC} = (C, K, R) $$

where:
- $C$ = set of **Components**
- $K$ = set of **Connectors**
- $R \subseteq (C \times K) \cup (K \times C) \cup (K \times K)$ = set of **Attachments / Relations** linking component *ports* to connector *roles*.

Each component exposes **ports** (interaction points). Each connector exposes **roles** (interface slots). Attachments bind a port of a component to a role of a connector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Elements vs. Implementation Mapping:**
> - Component *interface* ≠ Java interface; it is the abstract specification of provided/required services.
> - Connector *role* ≠ Java method; it is the abstract responsibility that a connector fulfills (e.g., "caller", "callee", "publisher", "subscriber").
> - **Implementation mapping** is non-trivial: an RPC connector in the architecture may be implemented using HTTP, gRPC, Java RMI, etc. This separation is essential to "**architectural independence**"—a KTU high-yield concept.

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Style Analysis

### 2.2.1 Data-Flow Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-and-Filter (P&F)** — Shaw & Garlan canonical pattern.
>
> - **Filters** = components that read input data, transform it, and produce output.
> - **Pipes** = connectors carrying unidirectional data streams.
> - **Properties**: incremental computation, parallel execution, reordering of filters.
> - **Example**: Unix shell commands (`cat file | grep "error" | sort | uniq`).

**Advantages (write for 7-mark KTU answers):**
- Modifiability: filters can be added/replaced.
- Composability: new pipelines = new combinations.
- Parallelism: independent filters on multicore/CPU pipelines.

**Disadvantages:**
- Cannot share large state easily.
- Data-conversion overhead between filters.
- Not suitable for interactive systems.

### 2.2.2 Call-and-Return Style

- **Main Program / Subroutine** – decomposed by top-down functional decomposition.
- **Object-Oriented Style** – system = objects; connectors = procedure calls (RMI/RPC).
- **Client-Server Style** – clients invoke servers; connector = network protocol (HTTP, TCP, RPC).
- **Layered Style** – strictly hierarchical; connector = invocation of the layer *below*.

### 2.2.3 Independent-Components / Event-Based Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Publish-Subscribe** — most examined event-based pattern in KTU.
>
> - **Publisher** = component that announces events to a *broker*.
> - **Subscriber** = component that registers interest in event topics.
> - **Event Broker / Bus** = connector (connector itself may be implemented as a component such as Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT broker).
> - **Properties**: decoupled in time, space, and flow.

**Other Event-Based Connectors:**
- Message Bus, Message Queue, Event Channels.
- **Process Coordination** patterns: Star (one central controller), Blackboard (shared data + triggers).

### 2.2.4 Shared-Data Style

- **Shared Repository** (Database Integration) – multiple components read/write a central data store.
- **Blackboard** – repository + triggers that fire components when state changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Blackboard Pattern (Krishna, CMU 1983)** combines the shared-data and event-based styles. Used in HEARSAY-II speech recognition, modern ML orchestration, and IDE compilation orchestration.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Pattern | Primary Connector | Style | When Chosen (KTU) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Pipe-and-Filter** | Pipe (data stream) | Data-Flow | Stream processing, compilers, ETL |
> | **Layered** | Procedure call downward | Call-and-Return | OS, networking stacks, enterprise apps |
> | **Client-Server** | RPC/HTTP/Socket | Call-and-Return | Distributed services, web apps |
> | **Peer-to-Peer** | Message/RPC | Call-and-Return | File sharing, blockchain |
> | **Publish-Subscribe** | Event Broker | Event-Based | Decoupled microservices, IoT |
> | **MVC** | Observer/Event | Event-Based | UI frameworks |
> | **Blackboard** | Triggered Access | Shared-Data + Event | ML/AI systems, compilers |
> | **Shared Repository** | Database Access | Shared-Data | Data-driven enterprise systems |
> | **Service-Oriented (SOA)** | Enterprise Service Bus | Call-and-Return + Event | Cross-org integration |
> | **Microkernel / Plugin** | Plugin Registry | Call-and-Return + Event | IDEs, OS, browsers |

## 2.4 Quality Attribute Implications (Why C&C Patterns Matter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A C&C pattern choice fixes a **constellation of quality attributes**. The KTU 2024 examiner's high-yield area is "given a quality attribute, which pattern supports it?"

| Quality Attribute | Best-Supporting C&C Patterns |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Pipe-and-Filter (parallelism), Client-Server with caching |
| **Modifiability** | Pipe-and-Filter, Layered, Publish-Subscribe |
| **Scalability** | Publish-Subscribe, Microservices/Client-Server, Peer-to-Peer |
| **Availability** | Publish-Subscribe (replication), P2P, Microkernel |
| **Security** | Layered (defense-in-depth), Client-Server with firewalls |
| **Reusability** | Pipe-and-Filter (composable), Layered, Service-Oriented |
| **Interoperability** | Service-Oriented, Message-Bus |
| **Portability** | Microkernel, Layered (abstract base layer) |

## 2.5 Connector Design Heuristics (Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> Connectors are often the **most under-designed** part of a system. KTU expects you to discuss why:
> 1. **Performance overhead** – an RPC connector may cost 1 ms vs. 1 µs for an in-process call; the architectural choice has measurable cost.
> 2. **Failure semantics** – message-bus connectors have "best-effort" or "guaranteed" delivery; the connector contract must be explicit.
> 3. **Security boundary** – a connector crossing trust zones (e.g., HTTP over a firewall) is itself a security decision.
> 4. **Operational concern** – connector logging, monitoring, retry policies are architectural, not implementation details.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Pattern Decomposition Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a C&C View for a Railway Reservation System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A KTU-style 14-mark question asks: *"Design a C&C view for an online railway reservation system. Identify components, connectors, ports, roles, and justify the pattern choice."*

### Step 1 — Identify Quality Attributes Driving the Pattern
- **Availability** (24×7) → use **Replication**.
- **Performance under load** → distribute work via **Load Balancer** connector.
- **Modifiability** of payment gateways → use **Service-Oriented / Publish-Subscribe** for payment.
- **Data integrity** → use **Shared Repository** (DB) with two-phase commit.

### Step 2 — List Components
1. `WebClient` (browser/mobile app).
2. `APIGateway` (entry point).
3. `AuthService` (OAuth2).
4. `ReservationService` (search & book).
5. `PaymentService` (PG integration).
6. `NotificationService` (SMS/email).
7. `TrainDataDB` (train schedule, seat map).
8. `BookingDB` (PNR, transactions).
9. `MessageBroker` (Kafka/RabbitMQ).

### Step 3 — List Connectors
- `HTTPSConnector` – TLS-secured REST calls.
- `JDBCConnector` – DB access (with pooling).
- `KafkaConnector` – async event topic "booking.events".
- `LoadBalancerConnector` – round-robin across `ReservationService` instances.
- `OAuthConnector` – token validation.

### Step 4 — Identify Ports and Roles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ports of ReservationService:** `+booking`, `+search`, `−paymentCallback`, `−db`.
> **Roles of HTTPSConnector:** `caller`, `callee`, `secure`.
> **Roles of KafkaConnector:** `publisher`, `subscriber`, `topic`.

### Step 5 — Justification (Exam-ready sentence)
> "The architecture uses a **Service-Oriented style** for the top-level decomposition into independently deployable services, **Client-Server** for synchronous REST interactions via `APIGateway`, **Publish-Subscribe** via `Kafka` for asynchronous notification & payment confirmation, and a **Shared-Repository** style for the DB layer. This combination supports availability, modifiability, and scalability."

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pipe-and-Filter (Compiler Front-End)

A **compiler front-end** is the canonical KTU example for Pipe-and-Filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline order:** `SourceCode → Lexer → Parser → AST Builder → Semantic Analyzer → IR Generator → Optimizer → CodeGen → TargetCode`
>
> **Filters:** Lexer, Parser, SemAnalyzer, IRGen, Opt, CodeGen.
> **Pipes:** `TokenStream`, `ASTStream`, `IRStream`, `OptStream`.
> **Properties:** incremental, parallelisable, swappable, re-orderable (e.g., constant-folding before/after).

**Trade-off in compiler design:** The optimizer is often *shared* (stateful), so a pure P&F must be relaxed. This is why compilers are **mixed-style** in practice.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Publish-Subscribe vs. Client-Server

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU 7-mark "Compare & Contrast" can be solved with this matrix.

| Dimension | Client-Server | Publish-Subscribe |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tight (server must exist, know all clients) | Loose (time/space/flow) |
| **Synchrony** | Synchronous (typical) | Asynchronous (typical) |
| **Failure propagation** | Direct; client fails if server down | Indirect; broker buffers |
| **Scalability** | Vertical + horizontal; LB needed | Horizontal; broker is the bottleneck |
| **Discoverability** | Static URLs/services | Dynamic topics |
| **Best for** | Transactional CRUD, RPC | Event notifications, fan-out, IoT |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quality-Attribute → Pattern Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Table (often appears in KTU Part-B):**
>
> | Requirement | Pattern Decision | Reasoning Step |
> |---|---|---|
> | "Add new payment providers without downtime" | **Plugin / Microkernel** | Plugin registry connector allows runtime registration. |
> | "Handle 100 K req/s for ticket search" | **Client-Server + LB + Cache** | Stateless services + read-through cache. |
> | "Notify 1 M users of booking confirmation" | **Publish-Subscribe** | Fan-out via message broker; consumer scaling. |
> | "Process sensor data in real time" | **Pipe-and-Filter** | Stream processing pipeline. |
> | "Heterogeneous subsystems share inventory data" | **Shared Repository** | Single source of truth; multiple readers/writers. |
> | "Stepwise verification at each layer (ISO/OSI)" | **Layered** | Strict downward dependency. |

## 3.5 Connector Algebra (Conceptual, for the analytically inclined)

> [!NOTE]
> Some advanced KTU questions ask to *characterise* connectors by properties. Shaw & Garlan define a connector by the tuple of *type*, *role*, *protocol*, *properties*.

$$
\text{Connector} = \langle \text{Type},\ \text{Roles},\ \text{Protocol},\ \text{Properties} \rangle
$$

**Example — KafkaConnector**:
- Type = `MessageBus`
- Roles = `{publisher, subscriber, topic}`
- Protocol = `AMQP / Kafka wire protocol`
- Properties = `{at-least-once delivery, ordered within partition, durable}`

This formal view is what differentiates a C&C architectural description from a mere class diagram.

## 3.6 Common Pitfalls in C&C Design (Sub-Style Misuse)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing C&C with Module view.** Modules are *compile-time* units. C&C are *runtime* entities. A library is a module; a service is a C&C component. Mark-loss zone: examiners test this distinction.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Treating connectors as thin lines.** Connectors are first-class. "Just an arrow" loses 2–3 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Ignoring connector semantics.** Saying "uses HTTP" is not enough. You must say "uses HTTP with TLS 1.3, OAuth2, retry-with-backoff, idempotency-key."
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Choosing one pattern for the whole system.** Real systems are *mixed-style*. Be explicit about the style per sub-system.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Forgetting control flow.** Some patterns (Main Program, Star) explicitly fix *who initiates*. Mention it.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic C&C Architectural Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
  A["Component A\n(Computation)"] -- "port a1" --> R1["Connector Role R1\nConnector C1 (RPC)"]
  R1 -- "role r1" --> B["Component B\n(Computation)"]
  A -- "port a2" --> R2["Connector Role R2\nConnector C2 (EventBus)"]
  R2 -- "topic T" --> C["Component C\n(Subscriber)"]
  B -- "writes" --> D["Data Store D\n(Shared Component)"]
  C -- "reads" --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical C&C graph**: components (boxes) connect to connectors (diamonds / labelled arcs), and a **data store** sits at the centre as a shared component. Attachments are between **ports of components** and **roles of connectors**.

## 4.2 Pipe-and-Filter Pattern (Compiler Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph LR
  SRC["Source\nCode File"] --> F1["Filter 1\nLexer"]
  F1 -- "TokenStream\n(Pipe P1)" --> F2["Filter 2\nParser"]
  F2 -- "ASTStream\n(Pipe P2)" --> F3["Filter 3\nSemAnalyzer"]
  F3 -- "IRStream\n(Pipe P3)" --> F4["Filter 4\nIRGen"]
  F4 -- "OptStream\n(Pipe P4)" --> F5["Filter 5\nOptimizer"]
  F5 -- "Pipe P5" --> F6["Filter 6\nCodeGen"]
  F6 --> OUT["Target\nCode"]
```

## 4.3 Client–Server Pattern (3-Tier Web Application)

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph Tier1["Presentation Tier"]
    C1["Web Browser\nClient"]
    C2["Mobile App\nClient"]
  end
  subgraph Tier2["Application Tier"]
    S1["AppServer 1\n(Stateless)"]
    S2["AppServer 2\n(Stateless)"]
  end
  subgraph Tier3["Data Tier"]
    DB[("Booking\nDatabase")]
  end
  C1 -- "HTTPS / REST" --> LB["Load\nBalancer"]
  C2 -- "HTTPS / REST" --> LB
  LB -- "HTTPS" --> S1
  LB -- "HTTPS" --> S2
  S1 -- "JDBC / SQL" --> DB
  S2 -- "JDBC / SQL" --> DB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the **Load Balancer is a connector**, *not* a component, in the strict Shaw–Garlan view (it is a *distributor* connector). The DB is a **data store component**.

## 4.4 Publish-Subscribe Pattern (Notification Fan-out)

```mermaid
graph LR
  P1["Publisher 1\nReservationService"] -- "publish to\ntopic BookingCreated" --> B["Event Broker\n(Kafka)"]
  P2["Publisher 2\nPaymentService"] -- "publish to\ntopic PaymentConfirmed" --> B
  B -- "subscribe\ntopic BookingCreated" --> S1["Subscriber 1\nNotificationService"]
  B -- "subscribe\ntopic BookingCreated" --> S2["Subscriber 2\nAnalyticsService"]
  B -- "subscribe\ntopic PaymentConfirmed" --> S3["Subscriber 3\nLoyaltyService"]
```

## 4.5 Shared Repository Pattern (Enterprise Data Hub)

```mermaid
graph TB
  A["App Component A\nOrderMgmt"] -- "read/write" --> REPO[("Shared\nRepository\nDatabase")]
  B["App Component B\nInventory"] -- "read/write" --> REPO
  C["App Component C\nReporting"] -- "read only" --> REPO
  D["App Component D\nAnalytics"] -- "read only" --> REPO
```

## 4.6 Layered Pattern (Strict Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TB
  L1["Layer 1\nPresentation / UI"] --> L2["Layer 2\nApplication / Service"]
  L2 --> L3["Layer 3\nBusiness Logic"]
  L3 --> L4["Layer 4\nData Access"]
  L4 --> L5["Layer 5\nDatabase / OS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** A component in layer $i$ may invoke a component only in layer $i+1$ (strict layered). In a **relaxed layered** architecture, a component may invoke any lower layer directly.

## 4.7 Blackboard Pattern (Hybrid Shared-Data + Event)

```mermaid
graph LR
  KS1["Knowledge\nSource 1\n(Speech Detector)"] -- "writes partial\nsolution" --> BB[("Blackboard\nShared Data")]
  KS2["Knowledge\nSource 2\n(Phoneme Matcher)"] -- "writes partial" --> BB
  KS3["Knowledge\nSource 3\n(Word Modeler)"] -- "writes partial" --> BB
  BB -- "trigger on\nnew data" --> SCH["Scheduler /\nController"]
  SCH -- "activate" --> KS1
  SCH -- "activate" --> KS2
  SCH -- "activate" --> KS3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Scheduler is a **controller component** that decides which knowledge source to fire next. The Blackboard itself is a **data store component**. The connector is the **triggered-access connector** (event-based with shared-data semantics).

## 4.8 Mixed-Style Real System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph EDGE["Edge / Client Tier"]
    WEB["Web / Mobile Client"]
  end
  subgraph GATE["Gateway Tier (Client-Server Style)"]
    GW["API Gateway\n+ Auth"]
  end
  subgraph SVC["Service Tier (Service-Oriented Style)"]
    RS["ReservationService"]
    PS["PaymentService"]
    NS["NotificationService"]
  end
  subgraph ASYNC["Async Tier (Publish-Subscribe Style)"]
    EB["Event Broker\n(Kafka)"]
  end
  subgraph DATA["Data Tier (Shared-Repository Style)"]
    DB1[("BookingDB")]
    DB2[("TrainDB")]
    CACHE[("Redis Cache")]
  end
  WEB -- "REST/HTTPS" --> GW
  GW -- "gRPC" --> RS
  GW -- "gRPC" --> PS
  RS -- "publish booking.events" --> EB
  PS -- "publish payment.events" --> EB
  EB -- "subscribe" --> NS
  RS -- "SQL/JDBC" --> DB1
  RS -- "SQL/JDBC" --> DB2
  PS -- "SQL/JDBC" --> DB1
  RS -- "Cache get/set" --> CACHE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **block-level functional architecture flow** is the kind of diagram KTU expects in a 14-mark "design a C&C view for X" question. It clearly shows *multiple styles coexisting*—which is the realistic answer to "what is the architecture of a modern distributed system?"

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A (Short Answer, 3 marks each, 2 questions) → **6 marks**.
> - Part B (Long Answer, 14 marks, internal choice, sub-parts) → **14 marks**.
> - Module-level cognitive mix: L1–L2 (Remember/Understand) ≈ 30 %, L3 (Apply) ≈ 40 %, L4–L5 (Analyse/Evaluate) ≈ 30 %.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Component-and-Connector (C&C) view. List its three principal sub-styles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Component-and-Connector view is an architectural viewtype that describes a system as a set of **components** (computational and data-store elements) and **connectors** (interaction mechanisms such as procedure calls, events, data streams) connected via **attachments** between component **ports** and connector **roles**, focusing on **runtime** behaviour. The three principal sub-styles are:
1. **Data-flow style** (e.g., Pipe-and-Filter).
2. **Call-and-return style** (e.g., Client-Server, Layered, Object-Oriented).
3. **Independent-components / event-based style** (e.g., Publish-Subscribe, Blackboard).

*(Marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing with examples.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Component and a Connector in a C&C view. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Component | Connector |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Encapsulates computation or holds data | Mediates and coordinates interactions |
| **Examples** | `OrderProcessor` (computation), `CustomerDB` (data) | `HTTPSConnector`, `KafkaBus`, `JDBCPipe` |
| **First-class?** | Yes | Yes (often under-emphasised) |

Connectors expose **roles**; components expose **ports**. Connections (attachments) bind port to role. **Example:** In a 3-tier web app, the `AppServer` is a component; the `HTTPS / REST connector` is a connector.

*(Marks: 2 for differentiation, 1 for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Pipe-and-Filter architectural pattern**. List its advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **Component-and-Connector view** for an **Online Examination System** (with components like `WebClient`, `AuthService`, `QuestionBankService`, `SubmissionService`, `ResultEvaluator`, `ExamDB`, and connectors like `HTTPS`, `JDBC`, `EventBus`). Justify your style choice. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pipe-and-Filter Pattern (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A data-flow style in which the system is structured as a chain of *filters* that transform data and *pipes* that carry the data stream from one filter to the next.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.2. Each filter has *input* and *output* ports; each pipe binds the output port of filter $F_i$ to the input port of filter $F_{i+1}$.
- **Canonical example (1 mark):** Unix shell pipeline `cat file | grep "ERROR" | sort | uniq -c`. Compiler front-end is another.
- **Advantages (2 marks):**
  1. *Modifiability* – new filters can be added/removed without disturbing others.
  2. *Composability* – filters are reusable; pipelines can be reconfigured.
  3. *Parallelism* – independent filters may run concurrently.
  4. *Incremental execution* – output appears as soon as first filter produces it.
- **Disadvantages (1 mark):**
  1. *State sharing* – filters are stateless; shared state requires an external repository.
  2. *Conversion overhead* – data must be marshalled/unmarshalled at each pipe.
  3. *Interactive UI* – not well suited.

**(b) C&C View for Online Examination System (7 marks):**

| Element | Identifier | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Component | `WebClient` | Computation | Browser/mobile UI |
| Component | `APIGateway` | Computation | Entry point, rate limiting |
| Component | `AuthService` | Computation | OAuth/JWT validation |
| Component | `QuestionBankService` | Computation | Serves exam questions |
| Component | `SubmissionService` | Computation | Receives answers |
| Component | `ResultEvaluator` | Computation | Auto-grading logic |
| Component | `NotificationService` | Computation | Sends result emails/SMS |
| Data Store | `ExamDB` | Data Component | Stores questions, responses |
| Data Store | `ResultCache` | Data Component | Redis cache for fast lookup |
| Connector | `HTTPSConnector` | RPC / REST | Browser ↔ Gateway |
| Connector | `JDBCConnector` | Data Access | Services ↔ DB |
| Connector | `EventBus` (Kafka) | Async Messaging | Submission → Evaluator → Notification |
| Connector | `CacheConnector` | Shared Memory | Services ↔ ResultCache |

**Justification (1 mark):** The system is best described as a **mixed-style** C&C: **Client–Server** for synchronous request handling, **Service-Oriented** for component independence and deployability, **Publish-Subscribe** for decoupling `SubmissionService` from `ResultEvaluator` and `NotificationService`, and a **Shared-Repository** style for the DB and cache.

*Attach a diagram (refer Section 4.8) for full marks.*

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **Publish-Subscribe architectural pattern** in detail. Compare it with the **Client-Server** pattern. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a **Smart Traffic Management System** (real-time vehicle detection, signal control, violation logging, citizen alert), design a **C&C view** identifying components, connectors, ports, roles, and the style used. Justify how the pattern supports the system's quality attributes (real-time, scalability, fault-tolerance). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Publish-Subscribe vs Client-Server (7 marks):**

**Publish-Subscribe (4 marks):**
- Components: *Publishers* and *Subscribers*; Connector: *Event Broker / Message Bus*.
- Publishers **do not know** subscribers; subscribers register interest in **topics** or event types.
- Asynchronous, **time-decoupled**, **space-decoupled**, **flow-decoupled**.
- Failure isolation: broker buffers events; publisher crash does not propagate immediately.
- Example: Kafka-based order events; IoT sensor data; UI event buses (e.g., SWT, Swing `EventBus`).

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Property | Publish-Subscribe | Client-Server |
|---|---|---|
| Coupling | Loose (time/space/flow) | Tight (must know endpoint) |
| Synchronicity | Asynchronous | Synchronous (typical) |
| Failure handling | Broker-mediated | Direct |
| Throughput under fan-out | Excellent (one publish → many subs) | Server becomes bottleneck |
| Discovery | Dynamic (topics) | Static (URL) |
| Use case | Notifications, IoT, distributed event streams | CRUD, transactional web apps |

**(b) Smart Traffic Management System (7 marks):**

**Components (3 marks):**
1. `CameraSensor` (edge device, computation) – detects vehicles, plates.
2. `SignalController` – operates traffic signals.
3. `ViolationLogger` – records violations.
4. `AnalyticsEngine` – computes adaptive signal timing.
5. `CitizenAlertService` – pushes SMS / app notifications.
6. `CentralDashboard` (Web client) – operator UI.
7. `TrafficDB` – data store of historical & live data.
8. `EventBroker` (Kafka) – connector-implementation component.

**Connectors (2 marks):**
- `MQTTConnector` – sensors → broker (lightweight pub/sub).
- `HTTPSConnector` – dashboard ↔ analytics.
- `JDBCConnector` – DB access.
- `SignalBus` – controller ↔ signal hardware (low-latency).

**Ports and Roles (1 mark):**
- `CameraSensor` port: `+publish vehicle.events` → role `publisher` of `MQTTConnector`.
- `AnalyticsEngine` port: `+subscribe congestion.events` → role `subscriber`.

**Quality Attribute Mapping (1 mark):**
- **Real-time:** Pub/Sub with low-latency MQTT; in-memory cache.
- **Scalability:** Horizontal scaling of `AnalyticsEngine` and `NotificationService` as subscribers; broker sharding by region.
- **Fault-tolerance:** Broker replicates topics; consumers re-process from offset on restart; signal controller has a fallback `local-rule` mode.

**Final C&C style (closing sentence):** *Event-based (Publish-Subscribe) as the dominant style, with a small embedded Client-Server sub-system for the operator dashboard and a Shared-Repository style for the historical DB.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on C&C pattern questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "module" and "component"** (–2 marks). Module = compile-time/package. Component = runtime unit. "Class" is *implementation*, not architecture.
> 2. **Drawing connectors as plain lines** (–1 mark). Connectors are **first-class**; name them (HTTPS, Kafka, JDBC) and identify their roles.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the style** (–1 mark). Always say "this is a *mixed* C&C style comprising Client-Server + Pub-Sub + Shared-Repository."
> 4. **Listing patterns without mapping to quality attributes** (–1–2 marks). The "why this pattern?" question is what distinguishes a 7 from a 10.
> 5. **Ignoring control flow** (–1 mark). In Main-Program/Star, the *initiator* matters; mention it.
> 6. **Over-engineering** (–1 mark). Don't list every possible component; pick 6–8 that cover the use cases.
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** (–2 marks). A labelled C&C diagram is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bullet-Point Rapid-Revision:**
>
> - The **C&C viewtype** describes a system's **runtime** structure as **components + connectors + attachments**.
> - **Components** = computation (objects/services) and **data stores**. They expose **ports**.
> - **Connectors** = RPC, event-bus, pipe, shared-data-access, distributor. They expose **roles**.
> - **Attachments** = port–role bindings; the edges of the C&C graph.
> - **Three sub-styles:** Data-Flow (Pipe-and-Filter), Call-and-Return (Client-Server, Layered, OO), Independent-Components / Event-Based (Pub-Sub, Blackboard).
> - **Pipe-and-Filter** ⇒ modifiability, parallelism; weak at state-sharing and interactive UI.
> - **Layered** ⇒ portability, security; enforces strict downward calls; supports *information hiding per layer*.
> - **Client-Server** ⇒ distributed computing; connector = network protocol; scalability via load balancing.
> - **Publish-Subscribe** ⇒ decoupled in time, space, flow; broker is the hub; great for fan-out and IoT.
> - **Shared-Repository** ⇒ data-centric systems; multiple components read/write a central store.
> - **Blackboard** = Shared-Repository + Event-Based; triggered knowledge sources.
> - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** is a C&C style with services as components and an **Enterprise Service Bus** as connector.
> - **Microkernel** = core system + plugins; connector = plugin registry.
> - **Connectors are first-class** — they have type, role, protocol, and properties (delivery guarantees, security, ordering).
> - A real-world system is almost always a **mixed-style** C&C view; explicitly identify sub-style per sub-system.
> - Quality-attribute ↔ pattern mapping is the **key valuation point** (see table in §2.4).
> - **Architectural independence:** an architectural connector (e.g., "RPC") is not tied to one implementation (HTTP, gRPC, RMI). This is the difference between architecture and design.
> - **Module view ≠ C&C view** — modules are compile-time, C&C is runtime. This is the most-tested distinction in KTU 2024.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layered Architecture** is a software architectural pattern that organizes the system into a hierarchy of distinct layers, where each layer provides services to the layer immediately above it and consumes services from the layer immediately below it. Communication typically occurs **only between adjacent layers**, enforcing a strict *separation of concerns* and enabling modularity, portability, and independent replaceability of components.

In the context of UML (Unified Modelling Language) and the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECGT411 – Software Modelling Using UML**, a *Layered* model is one of the most widely used **architectural patterns** documented by the *Gang of Four* and refined in classical software engineering literature. It is formally expressed in UML using **Package Diagrams**, **Component Diagrams**, and **Deployment Diagrams**, where each layer is represented as a logical grouping (package) of classes/components with well-defined **interfaces** and **dependencies**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Multi-Story Building**
> Imagine a corporate office tower:
> - **Ground Floor (Persistence Layer):** Where raw files and documents are stored in the basement vault.
> - **Middle Floors (Business Logic Layer):** Where managers process the files, apply rules, and make decisions.
> - **Top Floor (Presentation Layer):** The reception desk that interacts directly with visitors.
>
> Visitors (users) talk only to the **reception desk**. The desk passes requests down to managers. Managers fetch files from the vault. **No visitor ever enters the basement**, and the basement never speaks to visitors directly. Each floor has its own job, dress code, and security clearance. This separation is exactly what **Layered Architecture** achieves in software.

Another simple analogy: a **restaurant**:
- **Customer** → Waiter (Presentation) → Chef (Business Logic) → Pantry/Fridge (Data).

## 1.3 Standard Layered Structure (KTU Canonical Reference)

The most commonly referenced layered model in KTU examinations is the **Three-Layer / Three-Tier Architecture**:

| Layer # | Layer Name | Responsibility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **L1** | **Presentation Layer** (UI Layer) | User interface, input handling, screen rendering |
| **L2** | **Business Logic Layer** (Domain / Application Layer) | Core rules, workflows, validations, processing |
| **L3** | **Persistence Layer** (Data Access Layer) | Database access, file I/O, storage management |
| **L4** | **Database Layer** (optional) | Actual DBMS, data files |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — "Closed Layer":** A layer is said to be *closed* when a request **must pass through every intermediate layer** (i.e., layer L*i* can only call L*i-1*). This guarantees *isolation* and *testability*. An *open layer* skips intermediate layers and is generally discouraged in safety-critical systems.

## 1.4 UML Notation Snapshot

In UML **Package Diagrams**, layers are drawn as **stacked packages** with **dashed dependency arrows (`---->`)** pointing *downward*, indicating that a higher layer *depends on* a lower layer but not vice versa (the **Dependency Inversion Principle** in practice).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition — The Five Core Principles

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently award marks for listing and explaining these five governing principles. Memorize them.

1. **Separation of Concerns (SoC):** Each layer addresses one distinct concern (UI vs. logic vs. storage).
2. **Information Hiding:** Internal implementation of a layer is invisible to others; only the **public interface** is exposed.
3. **Strict Layer Dependency:** Dependencies flow in **one direction only** (top → bottom). A layer must not depend on a layer *above* it.
4. **Open-Closed Layer Decision:** Determines whether intermediate layers can be bypassed for performance.
5. **Co-location of Cohesive Classes:** Classes within a layer are highly *cohesive* (related responsibilities) and *loosely coupled* with other layers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most revision-critical artifact** for the Layered topic. Refer to it before every exam attempt.

| # | Concept | Definition | KTU Pitfall |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Layer** | A logical grouping of related classes/components. | Don't confuse with *Tier* (physical deployment). |
| 2 | **Tier** | A *physical* deployment unit (e.g., separate server). | A 3-layer app can run on 1 tier (single machine). |
| 3 | **Closed Layer** | Request must traverse through it; cannot be skipped. | Default; enforces integrity. |
| 4 | **Open Layer** | Request can bypass it (e.g., for performance). | Use only when justified. |
| 5 | **Coupling (C)** | Degree of interdependence between layers. | Should be **minimized**. |
| 6 | **Cohesion (H)** | Degree to which elements within a layer belong together. | Should be **maximized**. |
| 7 | **Layer Interface (L*i*.api)** | Public methods/services offered by layer L*i*. | Defined in UML using `«interface»` stereotype. |
| 8 | **Dependency (→)** | "Uses" relationship drawn as dashed arrow in UML. | Direction must be downward. |
| 9 | **Anti-Layer** | A layer that adds no value and only forwards calls. | KTU penalizes its presence. |
| 10 | **Layer Pattern Origin** | Buschmann et al., *Pattern-Oriented Software Architecture (POSA)* Vol. 1. | Frequently asked as 3-mark definition. |

## 2.3 Layer Communication Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes two communication styles. Understand the difference deeply.

### Mode A — Strict Closed Architecture
A request from L1 must hit L2, then L3, then L4. **No shortcuts allowed.**

$$
\text{Request Path: } L_1 \to L_2 \to L_3 \to L_4
$$

### Mode B — Selective Open Architecture
For performance, L1 may directly invoke L3, skipping L2. This is **open layering**.

$$
\text{Request Path: } L_1 \to L_3 \quad (\text{skipping } L_2)
$$

> **Engineering Utility:** Closed layering is the default in **banking, healthcare, and aviation** systems (safety-critical). Open layering is common in **high-throughput web APIs** where milliseconds matter (e.g., trading platforms).

## 2.4 Coupling and Cohesion Metrics (Quantitative)

Although architectural patterns are qualitative, KTU occasionally asks for the **Coupling metric formula**:

$$
C_{\text{layer}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{(inter-layer references of } L_i\text{)}
$$

$$
H_{\text{layer}} = \frac{\text{Intra-layer related responsibilities}}{\text{Total responsibilities in layer}}
$$

> **Goal:** $C_{\text{layer}} \to 0$ and $H_{\text{layer}} \to 1$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **OSI 7-Layer Model** (networking) — the canonical layered reference.
- **MVC Pattern** (Model–View–Controller) — a *specialized* layered variant.
- **Java EE / Spring Boot** applications — controller → service → repository.
- **TCP/IP Stack** — Application, Transport, Internet, Link layers.
- **Operating Systems** — Kernel mode vs. User mode layered design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — Decomposing an ATM System into Layers

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**. You are given a scenario (e.g., ATM, Library Management, E-Commerce) and asked to derive the layered model. We will solve the **ATM scenario** step-by-step.

### Scenario
*Design the layered architecture for an ATM system. Identify layers, their responsibilities, inter-layer dependencies, and draw the UML package diagram.*

### Step 1 — Identify Candidate Responsibilities

From the problem statement, we extract raw responsibilities:

$$
R = \{\text{Display UI, Validate PIN, Check Balance, Dispense Cash, Update DB, Log Transaction}\}
$$

### Step 2 — Cluster by Cohesion (Group Related Operations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Presentation Layer} &= \{\text{Display UI, Read Card, Print Receipt}\} \\
\text{Business Layer} &= \{\text{Validate PIN, Check Balance, Dispense Cash Logic}\} \\
\text{Persistence Layer} &= \{\text{Update DB, Log Transaction, Fetch Account}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Validation and balance checks are *business rules*, not UI. Database calls are *infrastructure*, not business.

### Step 3 — Define Public Interfaces (UML `«interface»`)

Each layer exposes a **service interface** consumed by the layer above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1.\text{api} &= \{\text{showMenu(), readInput(), printReceipt()}\} \\
L_2.\text{api} &= \{\text{validatePin(), getBalance(), debit()}\} \\
L_3.\text{api} &= \{\text{findAccount(), updateBalance(), writeLog()}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Establish Dependency Arrows

Dependencies are **one-directional** and point downward:

$$
L_1 \xrightarrow{\text{«use»}} L_2 \xrightarrow{\text{«use»}} L_3
$$

> **Why downward only?** If $L_3$ depended on $L_1$, a database change would force recompilation of UI code — destroying modularity.

### Step 5 — Calculate the Coupling Index

Assume the system has $n = 3$ layers and $k = 4$ cross-layer calls (typical ATM):

$$
C_{\text{layer}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} (\text{calls leaving } L_i) = 2 + 2 = 4
$$

> **Interpretation:** A low $C_{\text{layer}}$ indicates a healthy design. If $C_{\text{layer}}$ grows beyond **~10**, refactoring is required.

### Step 6 — Verify the "Closed Layer" Rule

For each request (e.g., Withdraw ₹5000), trace the path:

$$
\text{UI} \to \text{validatePin}() \to \text{getBalance}() \to \text{debit}() \to \text{updateDB}()
$$

This traverses **L1 → L2 → L3** in order. ✅ Closed layering satisfied.

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis — Layered vs. Other Patterns

| Criterion | Layered | Client-Server | Microservices | Event-Driven |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Modularity** | High | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Testability** | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | Low | High | High |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |

> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Why Layered?", answer with **modularity + simplicity + testability**, not scalability (Layered is *not* scalable horizontally).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ATM Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1["LAYER 1 PRESENTATION UI"]
        ui1["Screen Renderer"]
        ui2["Card Reader Interface"]
        ui3["Receipt Printer"]
    end

    subgraph layer2["LAYER 2 BUSINESS LOGIC DOMAIN"]
        biz1["PIN Validator"]
        biz2["Balance Inquiry Service"]
        biz3["Cash Dispenser Controller"]
        biz4["Transaction Logger"]
    end

    subgraph layer3["LAYER 3 PERSISTENCE DATA ACCESS"]
        dao1["Account DAO"]
        dao2["Transaction DAO"]
        dao3["Audit Log DAO"]
    end

    subgraph layer4["LAYER 4 DATABASE"]
        db1["Account Table"]
        db2["Transaction Table"]
        db3["Audit Log Table"]
    end

    ui1 -.uses.-> biz1
    ui1 -.uses.-> biz2
    ui2 -.uses.-> biz1
    biz1 -.uses.-> dao1
    biz2 -.uses.-> dao1
    biz3 -.uses.-> dao2
    biz4 -.uses.-> dao3
    dao1 -.reads.-> db1
    dao2 -.writes.-> db2
    dao3 -.writes.-> db3

    style layer1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style layer2 fill:#fff7cc,stroke:#b39200
    style layer3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style layer4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Request Flow (Closed Layer Walkthrough)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant L1 as Layer 1 Presentation
    participant L2 as Layer 2 Business
    participant L3 as Layer 3 Persistence
    participant L4 as Layer 4 Database

    U->>L1: 1. Enter PIN 1234
    L1->>L2: 2. validatePin(1234)
    L2->>L3: 3. findAccount(cardId)
    L3->>L4: 4. SELECT * FROM accounts
    L4-->>L3: 5. Account Record
    L3-->>L2: 6. Account Object
    L2-->>L1: 7. Validation Result True
    L1-->>U: 8. Show Welcome Menu
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this diagram from top to bottom.** Notice the **bidirectional return arrows (-->>)** which represent responses propagating *upward* through the same closed layer chain.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — UML Package Diagram Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkg1["Package PresentationLayer"]
    pkg2["Package BusinessLayer"]
    pkg3["Package PersistenceLayer"]
    pkg4["Package Database"]

    pkg1 -.import.-> pkg2
    pkg2 -.import.-> pkg3
    pkg3 -.import.-> pkg4

    style pkg1 fill:#e1f5fe
    style pkg2 fill:#fff9c4
    style pkg3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style pkg4 fill:#ffcdd2
```

> **UML Mapping:** Each `Package` node corresponds to a UML `«package»` rectangle. The `-.import.->` arrow corresponds to the **dashed dependency arrow with `«use»` or `«import»` stereotype** in standard UML 2.5 notation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define **Layered Architecture** with a suitable real-world example. List any **two advantages**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Layered Architecture is a software design pattern in which the system is decomposed into a hierarchy of layers, where each layer provides services to the layer above and requests services from the layer below. **Example:** The OSI 7-layer networking model. **Advantages:** (i) Separation of concerns improves maintainability, (ii) Layers can be independently tested and reused.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Open Layer** and **Closed Layer** in a layered architecture.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Open Layer | Closed Layer |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Bypassable? | Yes, request can skip it | No, request must traverse it |
> | Use case | Performance-critical systems | Safety-critical systems |
> | Risk | Higher coupling | Lower coupling |
>
> A closed layer is the default in KTU-recommended designs for maintainability and integrity.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> *Students frequently lose marks for:* (1) confusing **Layer** with **Tier**, (2) drawing **bidirectional dependency arrows** (only downward is correct), (3) forgetting to label interfaces with the `«interface»` stereotype, (4) writing code instead of UML diagrams, and (5) failing to justify the *closed* vs *open* layer choice.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q.A.** Design a **Layered Architecture** for an **Online Bookstore System**.
> - **(a)** [7 Marks] Identify the layers, their responsibilities, and inter-layer dependencies. Draw the **UML Package Diagram**.
> - **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **request flow** for the use case *"Customer places an order"* using a sequence diagram, and justify whether the layers should be **open or closed**.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Layer Identification:**

| Layer | Responsibility | Sample Classes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| L1 — Presentation | UI screens, search bar, cart display | `SearchPage`, `CartView`, `CheckoutUI` |
| L2 — Business Logic | Order validation, price calculation, inventory check | `OrderService`, `PricingEngine`, `StockManager` |
| L3 — Persistence | CRUD operations, query execution | `BookDAO`, `OrderDAO`, `CustomerDAO` |
| L4 — Database | Actual tables and stored procedures | `books`, `orders`, `customers` |

**Step 2 — Package Diagram (textual UML for clarity):**
```
+-------------------------------+
|     «package» Presentation    |
+-------------------------------+
              | «use»
              v
+-------------------------------+
|     «package» BusinessLogic   |
+-------------------------------+
              | «use»
              v
+-------------------------------+
|     «package» Persistence     |
+-------------------------------+
              | «use»
              v
+-------------------------------+
|     «package» Database        |
+-------------------------------+
```

**Step 3 — Dependency Justification:** Each layer depends *only* on the layer directly below, satisfying the **Dependency Inversion Principle** and **Closed Layer Rule**.

*(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks layer identification, 2 marks responsibilities, 3 marks correct UML diagram with stereotype labels.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Use Case:** *Customer places an order.*

**Sequence Flow (closed layering):**
1. User clicks "Place Order" → `CheckoutUI` (L1).
2. L1 calls `OrderService.placeOrder(cart)` (L2).
3. L2 invokes `StockManager.checkAvailability(bookId)` (L2 internal).
4. L2 invokes `PricingEngine.calculateTotal(cart)` (L2 internal).
5. L2 calls `OrderDAO.save(order)` (L3).
6. L3 executes `INSERT INTO orders ...` (L4).
7. Response propagates upward: L4 → L3 → L2 → L1 → User.

**Open or Closed Justification:**
> The bookstore is a **commercial e-commerce system** with moderate traffic. **Closed layering is recommended** because it isolates the business rules (e.g., discount logic, tax calculation) from the UI and DB, making the system easy to test. Open layering is rejected because bypassing the Business Layer would expose raw DAO calls to the UI, violating encapsulation and risking inconsistent pricing.

*(7 marks awarded for: 3 marks sequence flow, 2 marks open/closed justification, 2 marks correct identification of trade-offs.)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q.B.** Explain the **Layered Architectural Pattern** in detail.
> - **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **characteristics**, **advantages**, and **disadvantages** of the Layered pattern. Compare it briefly with the **MVC pattern**.
> - **(b)** [7 Marks] For a **University Registration System**, draw the **Layered architecture** using a UML Component Diagram and explain the **coupling and cohesion** achieved.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Characteristics:**
1. Each layer has a well-defined *role* and *interface*.
2. Layers are organized in a *hierarchy* (stack).
3. Communication is *only* between adjacent layers.
4. Layers are *logically* independent and can be deployed on separate tiers.
5. Supports the **Open–Closed Principle** — easy to extend without modifying existing code.

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **Modifiability:** Change DB without touching UI.
- ✅ **Testability:** Each layer can be unit-tested with mocks.
- ✅ **Reusability:** Business layer can serve web, mobile, and CLI clients.
- ✅ **Understandability:** New developers can learn the system layer by layer.

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ **Performance Overhead:** Requests traverse multiple layers.
- ❌ **Anti-Layer Risk:** Empty "pass-through" layers that add no logic.
- ❌ **Not Scalable:** Hard to scale individual layers horizontally.
- ❌ **Rigidity:** Hard to skip layers for performance optimization.

**Layered vs. MVC:**

| Aspect | Layered | MVC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Focus** | Separation of concerns across the whole system | Separation within the UI |
| **Components** | Layers (packages) | Model, View, Controller |
| **Scope** | Entire application | UI subsystem |
| **Relationship** | MVC is a *specialization* of Layered (3 internal layers) |

*(7 marks: 2 characteristics + 2 advantages + 2 disadvantages + 1 MVC comparison.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**University Registration System — Layered Component Diagram:**

```
+----------------------------------------+
|   «component» UI Layer                |
|   «component» RegistrationController  |
+----------------------------------------+
                  «use»
                   v
+----------------------------------------+
|   «component» Business Layer          |
|   «component» CourseService            |
|   «component» EnrollmentService        |
|   «component» FeeCalculator            |
+----------------------------------------+
                  «use»
                   v
+----------------------------------------+
|   «component» Persistence Layer        |
|   «component» StudentDAO               |
|   «component» CourseDAO                |
|   «component» EnrollmentDAO            |
+----------------------------------------+
                  «use»
                   v
+----------------------------------------+
|   «component» Database                 |
|   «artifact» MySQL Server              |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Coupling Analysis:** The coupling is **low** because each layer only references the *interface* of the layer below. For example, `RegistrationController` does not import any `DAO` class directly — it depends on `CourseService` and `EnrollmentService` abstractions.

**Cohesion Analysis:** Cohesion is **high** within each layer:
- UI layer groups all screens/controllers.
- Business layer groups all domain rules.
- Persistence layer groups all data access.

This satisfies the **High Cohesion – Low Coupling** principle, which is the gold standard of KTU valuation for architecture questions.

*(7 marks: 3 marks component diagram, 2 marks coupling, 2 marks cohesion explanation.)*

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Glance at this list 5 minutes before the exam for last-mile confidence.**

- ✅ **Layered Architecture** = stack of layers with downward-only dependencies.
- ✅ **Layer ≠ Tier** (logical vs. physical).
- ✅ **Three canonical layers:** Presentation → Business → Persistence.
- ✅ **Closed layer** = default; enforces integrity.
- ✅ **Open layer** = bypassable; use only for performance.
- ✅ **UML Notation:** Package diagram with dashed `«use»` arrows pointing down.
- ✅ **Origin:** Buschmann et al., *POSA* Vol. 1.
- ✅ **Coupling should be low, Cohesion should be high.**
- ✅ **Anti-Layer** is a code smell — avoid empty forwarding layers.
- ✅ **Common examples:** OSI model, MVC, Java EE, TCP/IP stack.
- ✅ **Advantages:** modularity, testability, reusability, understandability.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** performance overhead, scalability limits, rigidity.
- ✅ **Typical KTU 14-mark question:** Scenario → draw package diagram + explain request flow + justify open/closed.
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** correct UML stereotypes, justified design choices, mention of *Dependency Inversion Principle*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repository
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repository

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repository** is a **subsystem architecture pattern** in which a **central data structure (the repository)** is established and all **subsystems access, modify, and retrieve shared data** from this common store. The repository acts as the **blackboard or shared memory** that mediates the flow of information between independent subsystems, ensuring **data consistency**, **decoupled communication**, and **persistent storage** of system-wide information.

In the context of **UML modeling**, a Repository is represented as a **stereotyped package** (`<<repository>>`) or a **component** (`<<repository>>`) that holds shared classes, entities, and persistent data structures. It is one of the foundational **architectural patterns** taught under the structural and behavioral modeling paradigms of UML.

### Formal Definition (Book-Aligned)

A **Repository (Repository Architecture Pattern)** is a subsystem organization style in which:
- A **central repository** holds all persistent data of the system.
- **Independent subsystems** (modules) read from and write to this repository.
- The **repository notifies** subscribed subsystems of state changes.
- Subsystems **do not communicate directly** with one another; they communicate *only* through the repository.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECGT411 - Module 3):**
> Students must be able to *identify, model, and justify* a repository-based architecture for software systems requiring **shared data**, **loose coupling**, and **event-driven updates**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **library system** 🏛️:

| Element | Real-World Analogy | UML Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Library Building** | The **Repository** — stores all books centrally | `<<repository>>` component |
| **Librarian (Database Engine)** | Manages reads/writes, ensures rules | Repository internal class |
| **Students / Researchers (Subsystems)** | Issue/return books but **never talk to each other** | `<<subystem>>` packages |
| **Library Card Catalog (Schema)** | Tells the structure of stored data | Repository's **stereotyped interface** |
| **Notification Board** | When a new book arrives, all relevant members are alerted | Repository → Subsystem **dependency / observer link** |

Just as a library centralizes books so that different readers can use the same information without needing to meet, a **Repository centralizes data** so multiple subsystems can collaborate through a single point of truth.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the Repository as a "Shared Whiteboard"**: any subsystem can read from it, write to it, and be notified when something changes — but subsystems never write on each other directly.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Repository Design

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **standard engineering metrics** typically referenced in repository-based system design:

- **Coupling Metric (C)**: Measures the dependency of subsystems on the repository.
  $$ C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D(S_i, R) $$
  where $D(S_i, R)$ is the dependency weight of subsystem $S_i$ on the repository $R$.

- **Repository Centrality (RC)**: Indicates how dominant the repository is in data flow.
  $$ RC = \frac{E_{R}}{E_{total}} $$
  where $E_{R}$ is the number of edges (read/write operations) involving the repository, and $E_{total}$ is the total number of data-flow edges in the system.

- **Standard Persistence Targets**: **100 GB – 10 TB** for enterprise repositories, with **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) compliance.

- **Subsystem-to-Repository Ratio (SRR)**: Typical value lies in the range **3:1 to 8:1** (3 to 8 subsystems per repository in well-balanced designs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of a Repository Architecture

A repository architecture decomposes a system into **three primary elements**:

### (a) Central Repository
- Holds the **shared state** of the system.
- Provides **standardized interfaces** (read, write, subscribe, notify).
- Often modeled as a `<<repository>>` stereotyped component or package.
- May be **active** (push-based notification) or **passive** (pull-based polling).

### (b) Subsystems (Clients)
- **Independent modules** that perform specialized functions.
- Each subsystem:
  - **Knows only the repository's interface**, not other subsystems.
  - **Cannot directly invoke** another subsystem.
  - Communicates **exclusively** through the repository.

### (c) Control Mechanism
- A **scheduler / dispatcher** that:
  - Coordinates the order of subsystem activations.
  - Resolves write conflicts.
  - Broadcasts state changes.

---

## 2.2 Two Key Communication Styles

| Style | Description | UML Modeling Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Pull Style** (Passive) | Subsystems **poll** the repository at intervals to detect changes | Sequence diagram with `loop` / `alt` fragments |
