| **Push Style** (Active) | Repository **notifies** subscribers when data changes (Observer pattern) | Sequence diagram with `async` messages from repository to subsystem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **push style** corresponds to the **Observer / Publish-Subscribe pattern**, where the repository is the *Subject* and subsystems are *Observers*.

---

## 2.3 UML Notation for Repository

In UML, a Repository is denoted using **stereotypes** on a package or component:

```
<<repository>>
+-----------------------+
|   Data Repository     |
+-----------------------+
| - dataStore: Map      |
| - subscribers: List   |
+-----------------------+
| + read(key): Object   |
| + write(key, Object)  |
| + notify()            |
+-----------------------+
```

The corresponding **package diagram** uses:
- A package with stereotype `<<repository>>`
- Dependency arrows (`---->`) from subsystem packages to the repository
- No direct arrows between subsystem packages

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Notation | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repository Package | `<<repository>>` | Holds shared data |
| 2 | Subsystem Package | `<<subsystem>>` | Independent functional unit |
| 3 | Dependency Arrow | `------>` (dashed) | Subsystem *uses* repository |
| 4 | Interface | `<<interface>>` (lollipop) | Defines access contract |
| 5 | Notification | `async` arrow (sequence) | Push-style update |
| 6 | Polling | `loop` fragment (sequence) | Pull-style update |
| 7 | Stereotype | `<<blackboard>>` | Alternate name (AI/blackboard systems) |
| 8 | Component | `<<component>>` with `<<repository>>` | Physical/architectural view |
| 9 | Centrality (RC) | $RC = E_R / E_{total}$ | Coupling metric |
| 10 | SRR (recommended) | $3:1$ to $8:1$ | Subsystem-to-Repo ratio |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Repository architectures are extensively used in:

- **Banking Systems** 🏦 — central ledger (repository) used by ATM, Internet Banking, Mobile App subsystems.
- **Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)** 💻 — AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) acts as repository for parser, type-checker, formatter, debugger.
- **Air Traffic Control Systems** ✈️ — flight data is shared across radar, communication, scheduling subsystems.
- **Compiler Design** ⚙️ — symbol table is the repository accessed by lexical, syntax, semantic, and code generation phases.
- **Hospital Information Systems** 🏥 — central EHR (Electronic Health Record) accessed by billing, pharmacy, lab, OPD modules.

> [!TIP]
> In **KTU board exams**, banking and hospital examples are the most commonly asked scenarios for repository diagrams.

---

## 2.6 Advantages and Disadvantages (Frequently Tested)

| Advantages ✅ | Disadvantages ❌ |
|---|---|
| **Loose coupling** between subsystems | Repository becomes a **single point of failure** |
| **Centralized data management** | **Performance bottleneck** under heavy load |
| Easy to add new subsystems | **Difficult to distribute** across multiple servers |
| **Data integrity** enforced at one place | Subsystems must agree on the **data model** |
| Supports **event-driven** updates | High **dependency** on repository's availability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Method — How to Construct a Repository Model

When asked to **"design a repository architecture for a given scenario"** in KTU exams, follow this **5-step analytical framework**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Step Repository Modeling Framework (K-RAM):**
> 1. **K**ey shared data → Identify the central data structure
> 2. **R**oles → Identify subsystems (consumers/producers)
> 3. **A**ccess pattern → Decide push (observer) or pull (poller)
> 4. **M**ap UML → Draw package/component diagram
> 5. **V**erify → Check SRR, coupling, and single-point-of-failure risks

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Hospital Management System (HMS)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *Design a repository-based architecture for a Hospital Management System where the central patient record must be accessed by OPD, Pharmacy, Laboratory, and Billing subsystems.*

### Step 1: Identify Central Repository
- The **Patient Health Record (PHR)** is the shared data.
- Modeled as: `<<repository>> PatientRepository`

### Step 2: Identify Subsystems
- `<<subsystem>> OPD_Module`
- `<<subsystem>> Pharmacy_Module`
- `<<subsystem>> Lab_Module`
- `<<subsystem>> Billing_Module`

### Step 3: Access Pattern
- **Push-style notification**: When a Lab test result is added, the OPD module (prescribing doctor) and Billing module are auto-notified.

### Step 4: UML Package Diagram (Textual Sketch)

```
        <<subsystem>>         <<subsystem>>          <<subsystem>>        <<subsystem>>
        OPD_Module            Pharmacy_Module         Lab_Module          Billing_Module
              \                    |                       |                    /
               \                   |                       |                   /
                \                  v                       v                  /
                 \------>  <<repository>>  <--------------------------------/
                            PatientRepository
                            ----------------------
                            - patientRecords : Map
                            - subscribers : List
                            ----------------------
                            + readPatient(id) : Record
                            + writePatient(id, Record)
                            + subscribe(sub)
                            + notifyChange()
```

### Step 5: Verification
- $SRR = 4 : 1$ ✅ (within 3:1–8:1 range)
- Subsystems do **not** communicate directly ✅
- Repository is a single point of failure ⚠️ (must be backed up)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Coupling Metric Calculation

**Problem:** A repository system has 5 subsystems, each with 3 dependencies on the repository. Total data flow edges in the system diagram is 30. Calculate the **Repository Centrality (RC)**.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- Number of edges involving the repository: $E_R = 5 \times 3 = 15$
- Total edges: $E_{total} = 30$

**Step 1:** Apply the centrality formula.

$$
RC = \frac{E_R}{E_{total}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
RC = \frac{15}{30}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$
RC = 0.5 \; (= 50\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** Half of all data flow in the system passes through the repository — a *moderately central* architecture. If $RC > 0.7$, the repository is a **performance bottleneck**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Conversion of Push to Pull (Sequence Diagram Logic)

**Problem:** Convert a push-style repository interaction into a pull-style interaction for the HMS example.

### Push Style (Original):

```
Lab_Module  -->  PatientRepository : writePatient(id, labResult)
PatientRepository  -->  OPD_Module : async notify(newLabResult)
```

### Pull Style (Converted):

```
OPD_Module  -->  PatientRepository : readPatient(id)  [inside loop]
```

**Conversion Logic Step-by-Step:**
1. Replace the `async notify` message with a `loop` fragment.
2. Inside the `loop`, the subsystem issues a `readPatient` call.
3. The repository responds with the current data.
4. The subsystem checks if the data is newer than its cached version.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write *both* the push and pull flows in the same diagram, which is **logically incorrect**. Choose one access style and stick to it.

---

## 3.5 Repository vs. Client–Server vs. Pipe-and-Filter (Analytical Comparison)

| Criterion | Repository | Client–Server | Pipe-and-Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Sharing** | Central repository | Server holds data | Each filter has local data |
| **Subsystem Coupling** | Loose (via repository) | Tight (client ↔ server) | Very loose (stream) |
| **Notification** | Push/Pull | Request/Response | Stream-based |
| **Failure Impact** | Repository down → system down | Server down → clients fail | One filter fail → partial output |
| **Best For** | Data-intensive, collaborative systems | Distributed, network-based systems | Data transformation pipelines |
| **KTU Example** | Banking ledger, HMS | Web app + DB | Compiler phases, image processing |

> [!NOTE]
> If a KTU question asks *"Compare Repository with Client–Server architecture"*, the above matrix is the **expected answer format**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Repository Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYS["Software System Boundary"]
        direction TB
        S1["Subsystem 1\n<<subystem>>\nOPD Module"]
        S2["Subsystem 2\n<<subystem>>\nPharmacy Module"]
        S3["Subsystem 3\n<<subystem>>\nLab Module"]
        S4["Subsystem 4\n<<subystem>>\nBilling Module"]
        REPO["Central Repository\n<<repository>>\nShared Data Store\n+ read, write\n+ subscribe, notify"]
    end
    S1 -->|read write dependency| REPO
    S2 -->|read write dependency| REPO
    S3 -->|read write dependency| REPO
    S4 -->|read write dependency| REPO
    REPO -.->|push notification| S1
    REPO -.->|push notification| S2
    REPO -.->|push notification| S3
    REPO -.->|push notification| S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are *dependencies* (subsystem uses the repository). Dashed arrows are *notifications* (repository pushes updates to subsystems). **No arrows exist between subsystems** — this is the **defining property** of repository architecture.

---

## 4.2 UML Package Diagram for the Repository

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Package\n<<subsystem>>\nUserInterface"]
    P2["Package\n<<subsystem>>\nBusinessLogic"]
    P3["Package\n<<subsystem>>\nReporting"]
    P4["Package\n<<subsystem>>\nNotification"]
    RP["Package\n<<repository>>\nCoreDataRepository\n---\n+ users: Table\n+ transactions: Table\n+ auditLog: Table\n+ read, write, notify"]
    P1 -.->|depends on| RP
    P2 -.->|depends on| RP
    P3 -.->|depends on| RP
    P4 -.->|depends on| RP
```

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Push-Style Notification Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Subsystem A
    participant R as Repository
    participant S2 as Subsystem B
    Note over S1,S2: Push Style - Repository Initiates Notification
    S1->>R: writeData(key, newValue)
    R->>R: updateInternalState
    R-->>S2: async notifyChange(key, newValue)
    Note over S2: Subsystem B processes new value
    S2->>R: readData(key) [optional confirmation]
    R-->>S2: return currentValue
```

---

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Pull-Style Polling Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Subsystem A
    participant R as Repository
    Note over S1,R: Pull Style - Subsystem Polls Repository
    loop Every T seconds
        S1->>R: readData(key)
        R-->>S1: return currentValue
        Note over S1: If value changed, process update
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Component Diagram of Repository Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPS["System Components"]
        direction TB
        C1["Component\n<<subsystem>>\nAuthService"]
        C2["Component\n<<subsystem>>\nOrderService"]
        C3["Component\n<<subsystem>>\nPaymentService"]
        CREPO["Component\n<<repository>>\nDataStore\n---\nProvided Interfaces:\n+ IRead\n+ IWrite\n+ INotify"]
    end
    C1 -.->|requires| CREPO
    C2 -.->|requires| CREPO
    C3 -.->|requires| CREPO
```

> [!TIP]
> In **KTU 14-mark questions**, students are often asked to draw **both a package diagram AND a sequence diagram** for the same repository scenario. The sequence diagram should clearly show whether push or pull is being used.

---

## 4.6 Decision Flowchart — When to Use a Repository Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System has shared persistent data?"] -->|Yes| B["Multiple subsystems need access?"]
    A -->|No| Z["Use Pipe-and-Filter or Layered"]
    B -->|Yes| C["Subsystems independent of each other?"]
    B -->|No| Z
    C -->|Yes| D["Data changes must be broadcast?"]
    C -->|No| Y["Use Client-Server"]
    D -->|Yes - Push| E["Use Repository with Observer"]
    D -->|Yes - Pull| F["Use Repository with Polling"]
    D -->|No| G["Use Layered Architecture"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Repository architecture pattern. List any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Repository Architecture Pattern** is a subsystem organization style in which a **central data structure (the repository)** holds all shared persistent data, and **independent subsystems** access/modify this data through a standardized interface. Subsystems do not communicate directly with each other — they communicate **exclusively** through the repository.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Loose coupling** between subsystems, making them easier to develop, test, and maintain independently.
2. **Centralized data management** ensures data integrity, consistency, and easy backup/recovery.

*(Alternative valid advantages: easy addition of new subsystems, supports event-driven updates, single source of truth.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Push-style and Pull-style notification in a Repository architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Push Style | Pull Style |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Repository notifies subsystems | Subsystem polls repository |
| **Timing** | Real-time (on change) | Periodic (intervals) |
| **Coupling** | Repository knows subscribers | Repository is unaware of pollers |
| **Overhead** | Higher (state change broadcast) | Lower (no broadcast, just reads) |
| **Latency** | Low (instant) | High (depends on poll interval) |
| **Pattern** | Observer / Publish-Subscribe | Polling / Client-pull |
| **UML Sequence Hint** | `async` messages from repository | `loop` fragment on read message |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Design a Repository-based architecture for an online Banking System. The system must support the following subsystems: (i) ATM Module, (ii) Internet Banking Module, (iii) Loan Processing Module, (iv) Customer Service Module. Draw the UML package diagram and explain the data flow.**

#### (a) UML Package Diagram and Identification of Components (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Central Repository Identified**
- The **Account & Transaction Repository** is the shared data store.
- Stereotype: `<<repository>>`
- Holds: customer accounts, transaction logs, loan records, audit trail.

**Step 2: Subsystems Identified**
- `<<subsystem>>` ATM_Module
- `<<subsystem>>` InternetBanking_Module
- `<<subsystem>>` LoanProcessing_Module
- `<<subsystem>>` CustomerService_Module

**Step 3: Package Diagram (Textual Representation)**

```
+------------+   +-------------------+   +-----------------+   +------------------+
|  <<sub-    |   |   <<subsystem>>   |   |  <<subsystem>>  |   |  <<subsystem>>   |
|  system>>  |   |   InternetBanking |   |  LoanProcessing |   |  CustomerService |
|  ATM_Mod.  |   |   _Module         |   |  _Module        |   |  _Module         |
+-----+------+   +---------+---------+   +--------+--------+   +---------+--------+
      |                    |                      |                     |
      |  (dependency)      |  (dependency)        |   (dependency)     | (dependency)
      v                    v                      v                     v
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  <<repository>>  Account_Transaction_Repository               |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|  - accounts : PersistentMap                                                    |
|  - transactions : PersistentList                                               |
|  - auditTrail : Log                                                            |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|  + getAccount(accNo) : Account                                                 |
|  + updateBalance(accNo, amount)                                                 |
|  + recordTransaction(tx)                                                       |
|  + subscribe(observer)                                                         |
|  + notifyChange(event)                                                         |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Step 4: Explanation of Data Flow**

- The **ATM Module** reads the account balance and updates it after withdrawal. It writes the transaction record to the repository.
- The **Internet Banking Module** performs the same operations but via a web interface.
- The **Loan Processing Module** reads the customer's account history from the repository to evaluate loan eligibility.
- The **Customer Service Module** reads transaction history and account details for dispute resolution.
- When a transaction is written, the repository **notifies** the relevant subsystems (e.g., Internet Banking may be notified of an ATM withdrawal so it can update its UI).
- **No direct communication** exists between the four subsystems.

#### (b) Sequence Diagram for ATM Withdrawal (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

```
ATM_Module          Account_Repository         Notification_Service
    |                       |                          |
    |---authenticate-------->|                          |
    |<--authSuccess----------|                          |
    |                       |                          |
    |---getAccount(accNo)--->|                          |
    |<--accountDetails-------|                          |
    |                       |                          |
    |--withdraw(amount)----->|                          |
    |   (validates balance)  |                          |
    |<--transactionSuccess---|                          |
    |                       |--notify(event)----------->|
    |                       |                          |--async notify InternetBanking
```

**Key points to mention in the exam answer:**
- Step 1: ATM authenticates user against repository.
- Step 2: Repository returns account details.
- Step 3: ATM sends withdrawal request.
- Step 4: Repository updates balance and logs transaction.
- Step 5: Repository pushes notification to other subscribed subsystems (Internet Banking, Customer Service).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Explain the Repository Architecture Pattern with reference to a Compiler Design system. Identify the repository and subsystems, and justify why repository is a suitable architecture for compilers.**

#### (a) Repository Identification and Subsystem Decomposition (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Central Repository:** The **Symbol Table** (and Intermediate Code Repository) — stores identifiers, types, scopes, and generated intermediate code.

**Subsystems:**
1. **Lexical Analyzer** — reads source code, creates tokens.
2. **Syntax Analyzer (Parser)** — checks grammar, builds AST.
3. **Semantic Analyzer** — type checking, scope resolution.
4. **Intermediate Code Generator** — produces three-address code.
5. **Code Optimizer** — optimizes IR using repository data.
6. **Target Code Generator** — emits final machine code.

**Justification for Repository Suitability:**

- **Shared Data:** All phases share the symbol table, scope information, and intermediate representation.
- **Sequential Pipeline with Shared State:** Each phase consumes the IR left by the previous phase and updates the repository.
- **Decoupling:** Each phase can be developed, tested, and replaced independently.
- **Consistency:** Centralized symbol table ensures no duplicate/conflicting definitions.

#### (b) UML Component Diagram with Justification (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

```
<<subsystem>>   <<subsystem>>   <<subsystem>>   <<subsystem>>   <<subsystem>>   <<subsystem>>
 Lexical         Syntax         Semantic         IR Generator    Optimizer       Target Code
 Analyzer        Analyzer       Analyzer                                          Generator
     \              |              |                |               |              /
      \             |              |                |               |             /
       \            v              v                v               v            /
        \------>  <<repository>>  <------------------------------------------------/
                   Symbol_Table_and_IR_Repository
                   ----------------------------------------------
                   - symbolTable : HashMap
                   - intermediateCode : List
                   - errorLog : List
                   ----------------------------------------------
                   + lookup(name) : Symbol
                   + insert(name, type, scope)
                   + getIR() : IR
                   + updateIR(ir)
                   + reportError(msg)
```

**Key advantages in compiler context:**
- **Phases communicate only through the repository** (symbol table + IR).
- Easy to insert a new optimization pass — it just reads from and writes to the repository.
- Error reporting is centralized.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Repository-based Questions:**
> 1. **Drawing direct arrows between subsystems** — This is the *biggest* mark-losing mistake. In a repository architecture, **NO subsystem-to-subsystem direct arrows are allowed**. Subsystems communicate *only* via the repository. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Forgetting the stereotype `<<repository>>`** — Always use the `<<repository>>` stereotype on the central component/package. Omission = loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Repository with Client–Server** — A repository has **multiple heterogeneous subsystems** (e.g., ATM, Internet Banking) sharing the *same* central store. Client–Server has *one type* of client accessing one server. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Not specifying push vs. pull** — Always state (and draw) whether the repository uses **push-style notification** or **pull-style polling**. Vague answers lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting disadvantages** — In "explain" questions, mention **at least two disadvantages** (single point of failure, performance bottleneck) to show balanced understanding.
> 6. **No interface definition** — Draw the repository with a clear **interface (lollipop) or explicit method list** (read, write, notify). Bare boxes without methods lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Recap of "Repository" (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Repository is a subsystem architecture pattern with a **central shared data store** accessed by **independent subsystems**.
- **Three key elements:** Central Repository, Subsystems, Control/Notification mechanism.
- **No direct subsystem-to-subsystem communication** — this is the **defining rule**.
- **Two notification styles:** Push (Observer pattern, `async` messages) and Pull (Polling, `loop` fragment).
- **UML Notation:** `<<repository>>` stereotype on a package or component; `---->` (dashed) dependency arrows from subsystems.
- **Centrality Metric:** $RC = E_R / E_{total}$; values > 0.7 indicate bottleneck.
- **Subsystem-to-Repository Ratio (SRR):** Ideal range is **3:1 to 8:1**.
- **Coupling is LOOSE** between subsystems, but **TIGHT** between each subsystem and the repository.
- **Advantages:** Loose coupling, centralized data, easy extensibility, data integrity.
- **Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, performance bottleneck, distribution difficulty.
- **Real-world examples:** Banking systems, Hospital Management, IDEs (AST), Compilers (Symbol Table), Air Traffic Control.
- **Confusion Watch:** Repository ≠ Database alone — repository is an *architectural pattern* that **uses** a database, but also includes **notification, control, and consistency** logic.
- **Draw it right:** Always include the **stereotype, methods, and dashed dependency arrows**; never draw arrows between subsystems.
- **Examiner's favourite scenarios:** Banking, HMS, Library, Compiler — prepare at least **two** of these for the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Client-Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Client-Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Client-Server Architecture** is a **distributed computing model** in which the workload is partitioned between **service providers (servers)** and **service requesters (clients)**. The client initiates requests over a network using a well-defined protocol, and the server processes these requests and returns the corresponding responses. In UML 2.5, this architecture is represented using **Deployment Diagrams**, **Component Diagrams**, and **Sequence Diagrams** under the modelling discipline of software architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Client-Server style belongs to the category of **distributed architectural patterns** (one of the fundamental software architecture patterns studied in Module 3). It is characterized by:
- **Decoupling of presentation and processing logic**
- **Centralized resource management**
- **Asymmetric communication** (client always initiates)

The three mandatory participants are:
1. **Client** — A process or device that requests a service.
2. **Server** — A process or device that provides the requested service.
3. **Network + Protocol** — The medium (TCP/IP, HTTP, RMI, RPC) carrying the request/response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis**: Examiners frequently test the difference between **1-tier, 2-tier, 3-tier, and n-tier** architectures. Always draw a labelled deployment diagram with **«artifact»** and **«component»** stereotypes when explaining.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition 🍽️

Think of a **restaurant**:
| Restaurant Role | Client-Server Role |
|---|---|
| Customer (you) sitting at a table | **Client** (e.g., web browser, mobile app) |
| Waiter taking the order | **Network / Middleware** |
| Kitchen preparing the food | **Server** (e.g., Web/App server) |
| Food delivered back to table | **Response** (HTML page, JSON data) |
| The Menu | **API Contract / Protocol** |

- You (the **client**) never go into the kitchen. You give your order to the **waiter** (network).
- The **kitchen** (server) doesn't know about you until an order arrives. It serves many tables.
- The **menu** (protocol/API) defines what you can ask for.

> [!TIP]
> The restaurant analogy is a high-scoring **2–3 line introduction** in KTU descriptive answers. It instantly earns the "explanation clarity" rubric marks.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded metrics and constants** standard to Client-Server analysis:
> - **RTT (Round-Trip Time)** — Time taken for a request to reach the server and response to return.
> - **Throughput (λ)** — Number of requests processed per second.
> - **Latency (L)** — Delay between request initiation and response arrival.
> - **Concurrency (N)** — Number of simultaneous client connections handled.
> - **Bandwidth (B)** — Maximum data transfer rate, in **bits/second (bps)**.
> - **Server Utilization (U)** — Fraction of time the server is busy, $0 \leq U \leq 1$.

---

## 1.4 UML Standard View of Client-Server

In UML 2.5, the Client-Server style is best modelled using:
- **Deployment Diagram** → shows physical nodes (devices, servers) and the software artifacts running on them.
- **Component Diagram** → shows the server-side and client-side components and their interfaces («provide»/«require»).
- **Sequence Diagram** → captures the **time-ordered message exchange** between client and server.
- **Communication Diagram** → captures structural links and message numbering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step

The Client-Server interaction follows a precise **6-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Client Bootstrapping** — The client application launches, loads configuration (server URL/IP, port number, credentials).
2. **Connection Establishment** — Client opens a socket (e.g., TCP **3-way handshake**: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK).
3. **Request Formulation** — Client packages the request according to a protocol (HTTP GET, SQL query, RPC call).
4. **Network Transmission** — The request traverses the network with latency $\ L_{\text{net}}\ $.
5. **Server Processing** — Server authenticates, dispatches to handler, executes business logic, and queries backend if needed.
6. **Response Delivery** — Server returns data (HTML/JSON/XML), and client renders/uses it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **server is stateless or stateful**:
> - **Stateless** (e.g., HTTP REST) — Each request is independent; server holds no session.
> - **Stateful** (e.g., FTP session, database connection) — Server maintains session context.

---

## 2.2 Architecture Tiers — The KTU Board Favourite

### Tier 1 — 1-Tier Architecture
- Everything on a single machine.
- No network involved. Example: Standalone desktop application.
- UML: Single **device** node containing all **artifacts**.

### Tier 2 — 2-Tier Architecture (Classic Client-Server)
- **Client Tier** ↔ **Server Tier** (usually a database).
- **Fat Client** does presentation + business logic; server only stores data.
- Example: MS Access front-end talking to Oracle DB.

### Tier 3 — 3-Tier Architecture
- **Presentation Layer** (Client) ↔ **Application/Logic Layer** (App Server) ↔ **Data Layer** (DB Server).
- The **Kerala KTU textbook standard** for enterprise applications.

### Tier 4 — N-Tier Architecture
- Adds layers: **Load Balancer**, **Cache**, **Message Queue**, **Microservices**.
- Each layer is an independent deployable unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tier up?** — Separation of concerns, scalability, fault isolation. The more tiers, the more flexibility, but also more network hops → higher latency.

---

## 2.3 Client Variants — Fat vs Thin vs Hybrid

| Variant | Where Processing Lives | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fat Client** | Mostly on the client | Fast UI, offline capable | Hard to update, heavy install |
| **Thin Client** | Mostly on the server | Easy maintenance, low client HW | High network dependence |
| **Hybrid Client** | Distributed | Balance of both | Complex design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Confusing **"Tier"** (architectural layer) with **"Layer"** (logical module like MVC's View/Controller/Model). They are *not* the same. 3-tier ⇒ 3 physical machines; 3-layer ⇒ 3 logical modules, possibly on 1 machine.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All high-yield formulas for Client-Server performance and design — **memorize these for ESE**.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = requests in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = time in system | requests, req/s, s |
| **Throughput Bound** | $X_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{server}}}$ | Max server throughput | requests/s |
| **Response Time** | $R = T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{server}}$ | Total client-perceived delay | seconds (s) |
| **Utilization (M/M/1 queue)** | $U = \lambda \cdot T_{\text{server}}$ | Server busy fraction | dimensionless |
| **Mean Response Time (M/M/1)** | $R = \dfrac{T_{\text{server}}}{1 - U}$ | Queueing-theoretic result | seconds (s) |
| **Bandwidth Requirement** | $B_{\min} = N \cdot R_{\text{req}} \cdot S_{\text{pkt}}$ | $N$ clients, $R_{\text{req}}$ request rate, $S_{\text{pkt}}$ packet size | bits/s (bps) |
| **Data Transfer Time** | $T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{\text{Payload}}{B}$ | Time to push payload over link $B$ | seconds (s) |
| **TCP 3-Way Handshake Cost** | $T_{\text{h}} \approx 1.5 \cdot \text{RTT}$ | Initial connection setup | seconds (s) |
| **Connection Pool Size** | $P = \lceil U \cdot N_{\text{conc}} \rceil$ | Recommended DB pool size | integer |
| **Amdahl's Speedup** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = servers | dimensionless |

> **Note on notation**: Use $U$ for utilization, $\rho$ is also valid in queueing textbooks. KTU accepts both.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking** — ATM (client) ↔ Bank Server (authorization + ledger).
- **E-Commerce** — Browser ↔ Web Server ↔ Order DB ↔ Payment Gateway.
- **Healthcare** — Hospital Workstation ↔ HIS (Hospital Information System) ↔ Patient DB.
- **IoT** — Sensor (client) ↔ Cloud Ingestion Server.
- **Telecom** — Mobile App ↔ 5G Core Network Functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Response Time of an M/M/1 Server Queue

The single-server queue is the canonical model for a Client-Server system where requests arrive randomly and are served FIFO.

**Given:**
- $\lambda$ = mean arrival rate of client requests (req/s)
- $\mu = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{server}}}$ = mean service rate (req/s)
- $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \lambda \cdot T_{\text{server}}$ = server utilization

**Step 1:** State the steady-state birth-death balance equation for an M/M/1 queue:

$$P_{n} = \rho^{n} \cdot P_{0}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the normalization condition $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_{n} = 1$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^{n} \cdot P_{0} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $P_{0}$, the probability that the server is idle:

$$P_{0} = 1 - \rho$$

**Step 4:** Compute the mean number of requests in the system $L$:

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_{n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \rho^{n} \cdot (1 - \rho)$$

$$L = (1 - \rho) \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \rho^{n} = (1 - \rho) \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{(1 - \rho)^{2}}$$

$$L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$$

**Step 5:** Apply **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda \cdot W$ where $W$ is the mean time in system = response time $R$):

$$\lambda \cdot R = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \dfrac{\rho}{\lambda(1 - \rho)}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back $\rho = \lambda / \mu$:

$$R = \dfrac{\lambda / \mu}{\lambda \cdot (1 - \lambda/\mu)} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{T_{\text{server}}}{1 - \rho}$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$R = \dfrac{T_{\text{server}}}{1 - U}$$
> where $U = \lambda \cdot T_{\text{server}}$ is the server utilization.

**Interpretation:** As utilization $U \to 1$, the response time $R \to \infty$ — a critical insight for **capacity planning**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Requirement for Concurrent Clients

**Given:**
- $N$ = number of concurrent clients
- $R_{\text{req}}$ = request rate per client (req/s)
- $S_{\text{req}}$ = average request size (bits)
- $S_{\text{res}}$ = average response size (bits)

**Step 1:** Total request bandwidth (incoming):

$$B_{\text{req}} = N \cdot R_{\text{req}} \cdot S_{\text{req}}$$

**Step 2:** Total response bandwidth (outgoing) — usually the dominant direction in web traffic:

$$B_{\text{res}} = N \cdot R_{\text{req}} \cdot S_{\text{res}}$$

**Step 3:** Total bandwidth link must support (assume full-duplex, take the max):

$$B_{\min} = \max(B_{\text{req}},\ B_{\text{res}}) \cdot (1 + H)$$

where $H$ is a **headroom factor** (typically **0.20 to 0.40**, i.e., 20–40% spare capacity) to absorb bursts.

**Step 4:** Example numerical evaluation:

> Suppose $N = 500$ clients, $R_{\text{req}} = 2$ req/s, $S_{\text{res}} = 20$ KB = $160{,}000$ bits, $H = 0.30$:

$$B_{\text{res}} = 500 \cdot 2 \cdot 160{,}000 = 1.6 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} = 160 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$B_{\min} = 1.6 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1.30 = 2.08 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} = 208 \text{ Mbps}$$

> **Decision:** Provision a **250 Mbps** link to give additional safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Connection Pool Sizing

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{conc}} = 200$ concurrent users
- Each user holds a DB connection for $T_{\text{hold}} = 0.5$ s
- Each user makes a DB call every $T_{\text{think}} = 4$ s

**Step 1:** Utilization of a single DB connection from one user:

$$U_{\text{user}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{hold}}}{T_{\text{hold}} + T_{\text{think}}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5 + 4} = \dfrac{0.5}{4.5} \approx 0.111$$

**Step 2:** Total connection-time demand per second:

$$\text{Demand} = N_{\text{conc}} \cdot U_{\text{user}} = 200 \cdot 0.111 = 22.22 \text{ connection-seconds/s}$$

**Step 3:** Pool size = demand divided by 1 (one connection serves one unit at a time):

$$P = \lceil 22.22 \rceil = 23 \text{ connections}$$

> **Engineering verdict:** Provision a connection pool of size **25** for buffer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | 2-Tier | 3-Tier | N-Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | 2 | 3 | $\geq 4$ |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Good | Excellent |
| **Security** | Low (DB exposed) | Higher (DB hidden) | Highest |
| **Latency** | Lowest (1 hop) | Medium (2 hops) | Higher (multiple hops) |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Use Case** | Small LAN apps | Enterprise web | Cloud microservices |
| **UML Stereotype** | «client», «server» | + «application server» | + «load balancer», «queue», «cache» |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — High-Level Client-Server Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor clientA as Client Browser
    participant webA as Web Server
    participant appA as App Server
    participant dbA as Database Server
    clientA->>webA: HTTP GET /login
    webA->>appA: Authenticate(user, pass)
    appA->>dbA: SELECT * FROM users WHERE ...
    dbA-->>appA: User Record
    appA-->>webA: JWT Token
    webA-->>clientA: 200 OK + Token
    clientA->>webA: HTTP GET /dashboard
    webA->>appA: FetchDashboard(user)
    appA->>dbA: SELECT orders ...
    dbA-->>appA: Orders List
    appA-->>webA: JSON
    webA-->>clientA: 200 OK HTML
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — UML Deployment Diagram (3-Tier)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[ClientWorkstation\n«device»\nPC Browser]
    node2[WebServerNode\n«device»\nApache Tomcat]
    node3[AppServerNode\n«device»\nJBoss WildFly]
    node4[DatabaseNode\n«device»\nMySQL Server]
    node5[LoadBalancerNode\n«device»\nHAProxy]
    art1[ClientArtifact\nWAR file]
    art2[WebArtifact\nWebApp.war]
    art3[AppArtifact\nBusinessLogic.jar]
    art4[DBArtifact\nschema.sql]
    proto1[/«TCP/IP»/]
    proto2[/«JDBC»/]
    proto3[/«SQL over TCP»/]
    node1 -.->|HTTPS| node5
    node5 -.->|HTTP| node2
    node2 -.->|RMI| node3
    node3 -.->|JDBC| node4
    node1 --- art1
    node2 --- art2
    node3 --- art3
    node4 --- art4
    node1 -.- proto1
    node2 -.- proto2
    node3 -.- proto3
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — UML Component Diagram (Client-Server Interfaces)

```mermaid
graph LR
    compA[ClientUI\n«component»]
    compB[BusinessLogic\n«component»]
    compC[DataAccess\n«component»]
    intA[/IUserService\n«interface»/]
    intB[/IRepository\n«interface»/]
    compA -.->|«require»| intA
    compB -.->|«provide»| intA
    compB -.->|«require»| intB
    compC -.->|«provide»| intB
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Layer\nPresentation and Input] -->|Request| B[Middleware Layer\nAuth, Routing, Logging]
    B -->|Dispatch| C[Application Layer\nBusiness Rules, Workflows]
    C -->|Query Command| D[Data Layer\nPersistence and Retrieval]
    D -->|Result Set| C
    C -->|Domain Object| B
    B -->|Response Payload| A
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Fat vs Thin vs Hybrid Client Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FA[FAT CLIENT]
        FA1[Presentation]
        FA2[Business Logic]
        FA3[Local Cache]
        FAS[Remote Server\nData Only]
    end
    subgraph TH[THIN CLIENT]
        TH1[Thin UI\nBrowser]
        THS[Server\nPresentation + Logic + Data]
    end
    subgraph HY[HYBRID CLIENT]
        HY1[Rich UI + Local Logic]
        HYS[Server\nShared Logic + Data]
    end
    FA --> FAS
    TH1 --> THS
    HY1 --> HYS
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip**: When asked to "draw a UML diagram for Client-Server", a **Deployment Diagram** with clear «device», «executionEnvironment», and «artifact» stereotypes is the **highest scoring** choice. Always label the **communication paths** with the protocol used (e.g., «HTTP», «RMI», «JDBC»).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Client-Server architectural style. List any two advantages of the 2-tier architecture over the 1-tier architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Client-Server is a distributed architecture in which the application is decomposed into two logical roles — a **client** that issues requests and a **server** that provides services or resources. The client and server communicate over a network using a defined protocol (HTTP, TCP/IP, RPC, etc.). The server may serve multiple clients concurrently, and clients do not share resources directly with each other.

> **Advantages (1 mark – any two):**
> 1. **Centralized data management** — Data is stored on a single server, reducing redundancy and easing backup.
> 2. **Resource sharing** — Expensive peripherals (printers, DB engines) are shared by many clients.
> 3. **Scalability of clients** — Adding a new client does not require changes to the server side.
> 4. **Security** — Server can enforce access control centrally.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between a Fat Client and a Thin Client with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Fat Client | Thin Client |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing location** | Bulk of business logic on client | Bulk of business logic on server |
| **Resource usage** | Heavy client hardware | Minimal client hardware |
| **Example** | Desktop Photoshop, MS Excel with macros | Web-based Gmail, Google Docs |
| **Offline capability** | High (logic local) | Low (server needed) |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult to update | Server-side single update |

---

## 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve **ONE full question** from the OR choice.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model – Module 3 Pattern]

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **3-tier Client-Server architecture** with a neat UML deployment diagram. Discuss the role of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A web application server has a mean service time of **20 ms** per request and receives requests at a rate of **30 requests/second**. Compute the **mean response time** experienced by clients and comment on what happens when the request rate rises to **45 requests/second**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A 3-tier Client-Server architecture partitions the system into three logical and physical tiers — **Presentation Tier**, **Application/Logic Tier**, and **Data Tier**. The tiers communicate over a network, providing clean separation of concerns, improved scalability, and enhanced security.

**Tier Roles (3 marks):**
1. **Presentation Tier (Client Layer)** — Provides the user interface. Handles input capture, validation, and rendering of responses. Examples: web browser, mobile app, desktop GUI. Communicates with the application tier via HTTP/HTTPS.
2. **Application/Logic Tier (Middleware)** — Hosts the business logic, workflow rules, and processing. Stateless or stateful. Examples: Java EE application server, .NET IIS with code-behind, Node.js server. Often talks to the data tier using JDBC, ODBC, or ORM.
3. **Data Tier (Database Server)** — Stores, retrieves, and manages persistent data. Examples: Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB. Implements ACID properties, indexing, and query optimization.

**UML Deployment Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1[ClientPC\n«device»]
    p2[AppServerNode\n«device»\nJBoss]
    p3[DBNode\n«device»\nMySQL]
    a1[BrowserArtifact\n«artifact»]
    a2[WAR Artifact\n«artifact»]
    a3[SchemaArtifact\n«artifact»]
    proto1[/«HTTPS»/]
    proto2[/«JDBC»/]
    p1 --- a1
    p2 --- a2
    p3 --- a3
    p1 -.->|HTTPS| p2
    p2 -.->|JDBC| p3
    p1 -.- proto1
    p2 -.- proto2
```

**Advantages (any 1, for full marks):**
- Security: DB is hidden behind the application tier.
- Scalability: Each tier can scale independently (vertical/horizontal).
- Maintainability: UI changes don't affect logic or data.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{server}} = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.02 \text{ s}$
- $\lambda_{1} = 30 \text{ req/s}$
- $\lambda_{2} = 45 \text{ req/s}$

**Step 1: Compute service rate $\mu$**

$$\mu = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{server}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute utilization at $\lambda_{1} = 30$ req/s**

$$U_{1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\mu} = \dfrac{30}{50} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Mean response time (M/M/1 formula)**

$$R_{1} = \dfrac{T_{\text{server}}}{1 - U_{1}} = \dfrac{0.02}{1 - 0.60} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.40} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: At $\lambda_{2} = 45$ req/s**

$$U_{2} = \dfrac{45}{50} = 0.90$$

$$R_{2} = \dfrac{0.02}{1 - 0.90} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.10} = 0.20 \text{ s} = 200 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Comment**

At 30 req/s, the response time is a comfortable 50 ms. As load rises to 45 req/s, utilization hits **90%** and response time **quadruples to 200 ms**. Beyond 50 req/s, $U \to 1$ and $R \to \infty$ — the system becomes unstable. The **knee point** is around 70–80% utilization. **Recommendation**: scale the server (vertical scaling, load balancing, caching) before reaching 45 req/s sustained load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - **Forgetting to convert ms to s** in the response-time formula → full-mark loss.
> - **Not commenting on stability** beyond $\mu$ → loses 1 mark for "engineering insight".
> - **Mixing Little's Law with M/M/1 formula incorrectly** → ensure you state which formula you are using.

---

### 📝 Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**Q.B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **n-tier Client-Server architecture**. Discuss why modern cloud applications prefer n-tier over 2-tier. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** An e-commerce site has **1000 concurrent users** browsing. Each user issues **1 request every 5 seconds** with an average response size of **30 KB**. Calculate the **minimum outbound bandwidth** required from the web server, assuming a **30% headroom factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** N-tier architecture generalizes the 3-tier model by introducing additional specialized tiers such as **load balancers, caches, message queues, search servers, and microservices**. Each tier has a single well-defined responsibility and communicates with adjacent tiers through protocols/APIs.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[Client\n«device»]
    n2[CDN\n«device»]
    n3[LoadBalancer\n«device»]
    n4[AppServer1\n«device»]
    n5[AppServer2\n«device»]
    n6[Cache\nRedis\n«device»]
    n7[Database\n«device»]
    n8[MessageQueue\nKafka\n«device»]
    n1 --> n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n3 --> n5
    n4 --> n6
    n5 --> n6
    n4 --> n7
    n5 --> n7
    n4 --> n8
    n5 --> n8
```

**Why n-tier over 2-tier in cloud (3 marks):**
1. **Independent scaling** — Web tier can scale on traffic spikes; DB tier scales on data volume.
2. **Fault isolation** — A failure in cache does not bring down the DB.
3. **Performance via caching/CDN** — Static content served from edge nodes reduces latency.
4. **Asynchronous processing** — Message queues decouple long-running tasks (email, payment).
5. **Polyglot persistence** — Different tiers can use SQL, NoSQL, search engines as appropriate.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B (b)

**Given:**
- $N = 1000$ concurrent users
- $R_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2$ req/s per user
- $S_{\text{res}} = 30 \text{ KB} = 30 \times 1024 \times 8 = 245{,}760 \text{ bits}$
- $H = 0.30$ headroom

**Step 1: Total request rate from all clients**

$$\Lambda = N \cdot R_{\text{req}} = 1000 \cdot 0.2 = 200 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 2: Outbound (response) bandwidth**

$$B_{\text{res}} = \Lambda \cdot S_{\text{res}} = 200 \cdot 245{,}760 = 4.9152 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 49.15 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3: Apply 30% headroom**

$$B_{\min} = 4.9152 \times 10^{7} \cdot (1 + 0.30) = 6.3898 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 63.90 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4: Engineering decision**

Provision a **100 Mbps** link to handle peak bursts (e.g., 2× average) with margin. This also covers the smaller inbound request traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes in Q.B (b)**:
> - **Forgetting the headroom factor** → 0.5 to 1 mark lost.
> - **Converting KB to bits incorrectly** (using $1 \text{ KB} = 1000$ bytes vs $1024$ bytes) → check the question's convention; KTU usually uses 1024.
> - **Not stating the final engineering decision** (round up to standard link size) → loses "practical insight" mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Client-Server (Module 3):**

- ✅ **Definition**: Distributed architecture with **request-initiating client** and **service-providing server**.
- ✅ **Three mandatory participants**: **Client, Server, Network/Protocol**.
- ✅ **Tier classification**:
  - **1-Tier** = single machine (no network).
  - **2-Tier** = client + DB server.
  - **3-Tier** = client + app server + DB server.
  - **N-Tier** = adds load balancers, caches, queues, microservices.
- ✅ **Fat Client** = logic on client; **Thin Client** = logic on server; **Hybrid** = balanced.
- ✅ **UML Diagrams used**: **Deployment**, **Component**, **Sequence**, **Communication**.
- ✅ **Key stereotypes**: «device», «executionEnvironment», «artifact», «component», «interface».
- ✅ **Key protocols**: HTTP/HTTPS, TCP/IP, RMI, RPC, JDBC, SOAP, REST.
- ✅ **Performance formulas**:
  - Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$
  - M/M/1 response: $R = \dfrac{T_{\text{server}}}{1 - U}$
  - Utilization: $U = \lambda \cdot T_{\text{server}}$
  - Bandwidth: $B_{\min} = N \cdot R_{\text{req}} \cdot S_{\text{pkt}} \cdot (1 + H)$
- ✅ **Stateful vs Stateless** servers — stateless (REST) scales better.
- ✅ **Always label** communication paths in UML with the **protocol name** («HTTP», «RMI»).
- ✅ **Connection pool sizing** = $\lceil N_{\text{conc}} \cdot U_{\text{user}} \rceil$.
- ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid**: confusing tier vs layer, forgetting unit conversion (ms↔s, KB↔bits), missing headroom factor, omitting the UML diagram.
- ✅ **2-mark boosters**: Mention real-world example (ATM, Gmail, Amazon), mention security/scalability trade-off, draw at least one stereotype-labelled node.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Strategy**: For 14-mark questions, structure the answer as **Definition → Diagram → Explanation → Advantages/Limitations → Formula Application (if any) → Conclusion**. This mapping hits every rubric row in the KTU valuation key.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Publish-Subscribe Pattern: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Publish-Subscribe (Pub-Sub) Pattern** is a distributed, asynchronous messaging architectural style in which senders of messages, called **Publishers**, do not program the messages to be sent directly to specific receivers, called **Subscribers**. Instead, the published messages are categorized into classes (or **Topics/Events**) without knowledge of which subscribers, if any, will consume them. A central event-bus or message broker acts as a routing intermediary to decouple the producers from the consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *PECGT411 – Software Modelling Using UML*, the Pub-Sub pattern is taught as a behavioural message-passing paradigm used to model large-scale distributed, event-driven, and service-oriented systems using UML **Sequence**, **Component**, and **Deployment** diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The YouTube Channel Subscription**
>
> Imagine a YouTube channel ("TechReviewHub"). A viewer **subscribes** to the channel by clicking the subscribe button. The viewer does **not** know exactly when the next video will arrive. When the channel owner **publishes** a new video, YouTube's **notification system (Event Bus / Message Broker)** pushes a notification to every subscriber automatically. The publisher (channel owner) never has to manage a list of subscribers, and subscribers can join or leave at any time. This is exactly how the **Pub-Sub Pattern** operates in software systems.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Publisher** | A software component that emits messages/events without addressing any specific consumer. |
| **Subscriber** | A software component that expresses interest in receiving specific categories of events. |
| **Event / Topic** | The categorical label (e.g., `"ORDER_PLACED"`, `"TEMPERATURE_HIGH"`) used to filter messages. |
| **Message Broker / Event Bus** | The middleware routing engine that accepts publications and delivers them to matching subscribers. |
| **Event Channel** | A logical pipeline through which events of a given topic flow. |
| **Asynchronous Delivery** | The publisher returns control immediately, without blocking until a subscriber reads the message. |

## 1.4 Why Pub-Sub Matters in Modern Software Engineering

- **Decoupling:** Publisher and Subscriber never reference each other (zero compile-time dependency).
- **Scalability:** Can support **thousands of publishers and millions of subscribers** simultaneously.
- **Heterogeneity:** Subscribers can be written in different languages running on different platforms.
- **Reliability & Fan-Out:** One event can be delivered to **N** subscribers concurrently (1-to-N fan-out).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Architecture: Three Logical Tiers

The Pub-Sub architecture is decomposed into three logical layers, each performing a distinct responsibility:

1. **Producer Tier (Publishers):** Generates domain events. Examples — `OrderService`, `IoT Sensor`, `StockTradeEngine`.
2. **Routing Tier (Message Broker):** Filters, persists (optionally), and dispatches events. Examples — **Apache Kafka**, **RabbitMQ**, **Amazon SNS**, **Google Pub/Sub**, **Redis Pub/Sub**.
3. **Consumer Tier (Subscribers):** Registers interest in a topic, then acts upon receipt. Examples — `EmailService`, `AnalyticsEngine`, `InventoryUpdater`.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU board examinations, a common mistake is to draw the *Publisher* and *Subscriber* communicating directly. **Never do this.** They must always communicate *indirectly* through the broker, because direct coupling defeats the entire purpose of the pattern.

## 2.2 Variants of the Publish-Subscribe Pattern

There are **four classical variants** recognized in the *Distributed Systems* literature (Muhl et al., 2006) that KTU examiners frequently test:

### 2.2.1 Topic-Based Pub-Sub
The publisher tags the event with a string **topic name** (e.g., `"Sports"`, `"Politics"`). Subscribers receive **all events** published under topics they have subscribed to. This is the simplest and most widely-used variant.

### 2.2.2 Content-Based Pub-Sub
Subscribers register interest using **boolean predicates over the event content** (e.g., `event.temperature > 80 AND event.sensorType == "Boiler"`). The broker evaluates the predicate against each event to determine delivery.

### 2.2.3 Channel-Based (Subject-Based) Pub-Sub
A direct mapping where each subscriber specifies a specific **channel ID**. It is functionally a hybrid between topic-based and point-to-point messaging.

### 2.2.4 Type-Based Pub-Sub
Subscribers express interest in a specific **object class or event type** (e.g., all `OrderEvent` instances). Common in object-oriented middleware (CORBA Notification Service).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table: Pub-Sub vs. Point-to-Point

| Property | **Publish-Subscribe** | **Point-to-Point (Queue)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Receivers** | One to Many (1 : N) | One to One (1 : 1) |
| **Coupling** | **Anonymous** (no direct link) | **Direct** (queue name known) |
| **Message Persistence** | Usually **ephemeral** (transient) | Usually **persistent** until consumed |
| **Delivery Semantics** | **Broadcast** / Fan-Out | **Load-balanced** (round-robin) |
| **Use Case** | Notifications, event streaming, telemetry | Task distribution, job processing |
| **Failure Impact** | If a subscriber fails, others **still receive** events | If a consumer fails, message **stays in queue** |
| **Example Middleware** | Kafka Topics, Redis Pub/Sub, MQTT | RabbitMQ Queues, JMS Queue, SQS |

## 2.4 Formal Mathematical Model of the Pattern

Let the system be modelled as a tuple $\mathcal{P} = \langle P, S, T, B, f, g \rangle$ where:

$$
P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\} \quad \text{(Set of Publishers)}
$$

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\} \quad \text{(Set of Subscribers)}
$$

$$
T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k\} \quad \text{(Set of Topics / Event Channels)}
$$

$$
B \quad \text{(The Message Broker)}
$$

$$
f : P \times T \rightarrow M \quad \text{(Publish function producing messages)}
$$

$$
g : S \times T \rightarrow \{\text{true}, \text{false}\} \quad \text{(Subscription filter function)}
$$

A message $m$ produced by publisher $p_i$ on topic $t_j$ is delivered to subscriber $s_l$ **if and only if**:

$$
\text{Deliver}(m, s_l) = \big(f(p_i, t_j) = m\big) \wedge \big(g(s_l, t_j) = \text{true}\big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formal model is exactly what KTU examiners expect when a question asks: *"Explain the publish-subscribe model with a suitable diagram."* The Boolean filter function $g$ is the **heart of decoupling**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Stock Trading Platforms:** Price updates are published; trading bots subscribe to specific tickers.
- **IoT & Smart Cities:** Sensors publish telemetry; dashboards and alerting systems subscribe.
- **Microservices Architectures:** Domain events (e.g., `OrderShippedEvent`) are published and consumed by Inventory, Notification, and Analytics services.
- **Chat & Collaboration Tools:** User-presence and message-routing events.
- **Log Aggregation Pipelines:** Applications publish logs; central collectors subscribe for indexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Operational Flow

## 3.1 The End-to-End Message Lifecycle (10 Logical Steps)

To understand the pattern deeply, the message flow can be deconstructed into ten explicit, ordered steps. KTU frequently awards marks for these sequential correctness.

**Step 1 — Publisher Bootstraps:**
The publisher component $p_i$ is initialized by the system. It acquires a **logical connection handle** to the message broker $B$. No knowledge of any subscriber is loaded into memory.

**Step 2 — Subscriber Declares Interest:**
Subscriber $s_l$ sends a subscription request $r = \langle s_l, t_j \rangle$ to the broker. The broker stores this in its internal **subscription registry** $\mathcal{R}$, a hash-map structure typically keyed by topic.

**Step 3 — Registry Update:**
The broker updates its registry as:

$$
\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \mathcal{R} \cup \{\langle t_j, \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_l\} \rangle\}
$$

**Step 4 — Publisher Constructs Event:**
An internal trigger (e.g., a database write, an HTTP request, a timer tick) causes $p_i$ to construct an event payload $m$ with a topic header $t_j$.

**Step 5 — Publisher Invokes `Publish()` API:**
The publisher calls $f(p_i, t_j)$, which returns the marshalled message $m$. Control is **immediately returned** to the publisher (non-blocking, asynchronous).

**Step 6 — Broker Receives Message:**
The broker receives $m$ on topic $t_j$ and queries the registry $\mathcal{R}$ for all matching subscribers:

$$
\text{MatchList}(m, \mathcal{R}) = \{ s_l \mid \langle t_j, s_l \rangle \in \mathcal{R} \}
$$

**Step 7 — Fan-Out Dispatch:**
For each subscriber $s_l$ in MatchList, the broker initiates a delivery channel $c_l$. This may be a TCP socket, a webhook POST, or a push notification.

**Step 8 — Message Delivered to Subscriber:**
The subscriber's listener callback is invoked with $m$ as the argument. The subscriber processes it asynchronously.

**Step 9 — Acknowledgement (Optional):**
If the broker uses **at-least-once delivery**, the subscriber sends an `ACK` back to the broker; otherwise, delivery is **fire-and-forget**.

**Step 10 — Event Lifecycle Ends:**
The event may be discarded, persisted to a log (Kafka), or archived, depending on the broker's retention policy.

## 3.2 Quantitative Fan-Out Analysis

Suppose a publisher emits messages at a sustained rate of $\lambda$ messages per second on topic $t_j$, and there are $N$ active subscribers to $t_j$. The total work performed by the broker is:

$$
W_{broker} = \lambda \times N \quad \text{(deliveries per second)}
$$

The **average latency** perceived by a single subscriber, assuming the broker's internal queue is an M/M/1 queue, is given by the queueing-theory formula:

$$
L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho}, \quad \text{where} \quad \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}
$$

$$
T_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

Where $\mu$ is the broker's service rate. This **Little's Law** derivation is occasionally asked in KTU university examinations for distributed-systems electives.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** The fan-out formula $\lambda \times N$ shows that broker load grows **linearly with subscriber count**, which is the primary reason production-grade systems use partitioned topics and consumer groups to parallelize delivery.

## 3.3 Coupling Analysis: The "Why" of Decoupling

The decoupling can be quantified using **dependency graph theory**. Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed graph where:
- $V = P \cup S$ (all publishers and subscribers)
- $E$ contains an edge $e$ if any component holds a hard reference to another.

**In Tightly-Coupled Systems:**
$$
\lvert E \rvert \to \mathcal{O}(m \times n) \quad \text{(every publisher knows every subscriber)}
$$

**In Pub-Sub Systems:**
$$
\lvert E \rvert = m + n \quad \text{(each node only references the broker)}
$$

This quadratic-to-linear reduction in coupling is the **single most important architectural benefit** of the pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural UML & Architectural Diagrams

## 4.1 UML Component Diagram — High-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *publishers*, *broker*, *subscribers*, and their interfaces as a UML component architecture. It corresponds to the deployment logic that KTU examiners expect under Module 3 architectural-modelling topics.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Producer Tier"
        P1["OrderService Publisher"]
        P2["PaymentService Publisher"]
        P3["IoTSensor Publisher"]
    end

    subgraph "Routing Tier"
        B1["Event Bus Message Broker"]
        R1["Subscription Registry"]
        T1["Topic ORDER_PLACED"]
        T2["Topic PAYMENT_SUCCESS"]
    end

    subgraph "Consumer Tier"
        S1["Inventory Subscriber"]
        S2["Email Notification Subscriber"]
        S3["Analytics Subscriber"]
        S4["Warehouse Subscriber"]
    end

    P1 -- publish ORDER_PLACED --> B1
    P2 -- publish PAYMENT_SUCCESS --> B1
    P3 -- publish TELEMETRY --> B1
    B1 --> R1
    B1 --> T1
    B1 --> T2
    T1 -- deliver --> S1
    T1 -- deliver --> S2
    T2 -- deliver --> S2
    T2 -- deliver --> S3
    T1 -- deliver --> S4
```

## 4.2 UML Sequence Diagram — Message Lifecycle

The following sequence diagram captures the chronological interaction between a publisher, broker, and two subscribers for a single `ORDER_PLACED` event. This is the **single most frequently drawn diagram** in KTU Pub-Sub exam questions.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Pub as OrderService Publisher
    participant Brk as Message Broker
    participant Sub1 as Inventory Subscriber
    participant Sub2 as Email Subscriber

    Sub1->>Brk: subscribe(topic: ORDER_PLACED)
    Brk-->>Sub1: ACK subscription registered
    Sub2->>Brk: subscribe(topic: ORDER_PLACED)
    Brk-->>Sub2: ACK subscription registered

    Note over Pub: User places an order
    Pub->>Brk: publish(topic, payload)
    activate Brk
    Brk->>Brk: match subscribers in registry
    Brk-->>Sub1: deliver(event payload)
    Brk-->>Sub2: deliver(event payload)
    Sub1-->>Brk: ACK processed
    Sub2-->>Brk: ACK processed
    deactivate Brk

    Note over Pub,Sub2: Publisher is decoupled; returns control immediately
```

## 4.3 UML Deployment Diagram — Physical Node Mapping

The following block diagram shows how the components are distributed across physical nodes, mapping Pub-Sub onto actual servers, brokers, and consumer clusters.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Application Server Node 1"
        AP1["OrderService Component"]
    end
    subgraph "Application Server Node 2"
        AP2["PaymentService Component"]
    end
    subgraph "Broker Cluster Node"
        BK1["Message Broker Process"]
        BK2["Subscription Registry"]
        BK3["Topic Partition Log"]
    end
    subgraph "Consumer Worker Node A"
        CW1["Inventory Worker"]
    end
    subgraph "Consumer Worker Node B"
        CW2["Email Worker"]
    end
    subgraph "Consumer Worker Node C"
        CW3["Analytics Worker"]
    end

    AP1 -- TCP Publish Channel --> BK1
    AP2 -- TCP Publish Channel --> BK1
    BK1 -- Internal --> BK2
    BK1 -- Internal --> BK3
    BK3 -- Push Delivery --> CW1
    BK3 -- Push Delivery --> CW2
    BK3 -- Push Delivery --> CW3
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Fallback)

For a condensed, single-image summary of the entire flow that is easy to memorize for the KTU exam, use the following functional block flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Event Source Domain Trigger"] --> B["Publisher Component"]
    B --> C{"Message Broker Routing Engine"}
    C --> D["Subscriber A Filter Match"]
    C --> E["Subscriber B Filter Match"]
    C --> F["Subscriber C No Match"]
    D --> G["Subscriber A Action Handler"]
    E --> H["Subscriber B Action Handler"]
    F --> I["Event Discarded by Broker"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define the Publish-Subscribe messaging pattern. List any two advantages of using it over point-to-point communication.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Publish-Subscribe is an asynchronous messaging pattern in which **publishers** send messages to a central **message broker** categorized by **topics**, and **subscribers** receive only those topics for which they have registered interest, without the publisher or subscriber being aware of each other.

**Advantages over Point-to-Point (1.5 marks each, any two):**
1. **One-to-Many Fan-Out:** A single event can be delivered to multiple subscribers simultaneously, whereas point-to-point delivers to exactly one consumer.
2. **Loose Coupling:** Publishers and subscribers are completely anonymous to each other; new subscribers can be added without modifying the publisher's code.
3. **Dynamic Topology:** Subscribers can join or leave at runtime without disturbing the publishers.
4. **Better Scalability for Event-Driven Systems:** Suited for broadcast-style scenarios like notifications, IoT, and analytics.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is the role of a message broker in the Publish-Subscribe pattern? Name two real-world middleware systems that implement it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Role of the Message Broker (2 marks):**
A message broker is the **central routing engine** that decouples publishers from subscribers. It maintains a subscription registry, accepts published messages, applies filter predicates, and dispatches the messages to all matching subscribers. It may also provide **persistence, message ordering, retry policies, and dead-letter handling.**

**Two Real-World Middleware Systems (0.5 marks each):**
1. **Apache Kafka** — distributed event-streaming platform.
2. **RabbitMQ** — supports the Pub-Sub pattern via exchanges and queues.
*(Other valid answers: Amazon SNS, Google Cloud Pub/Sub, Redis Pub/Sub, MQTT brokers like Mosquitto.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set A]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the Publish-Subscribe architectural pattern in detail, describing its key components, the working mechanism, and the different variants (topic-based, content-based, and channel-based). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw a UML sequence diagram for a weather-monitoring system in which three IoT sensors publish temperature readings, and two display dashboards subscribe to receive them. Explain the flow. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Key Components (3 marks):**
- **Publisher:** Generates messages tagged with a topic.
- **Message Broker / Event Bus:** Receives messages, applies filter logic, routes to subscribers.
- **Subscriber:** Registers interest in topics and consumes matching messages.
- **Topic / Event Channel:** Logical category for filtering.
- **Subscription Registry:** Internal data structure in the broker mapping topics to subscriber lists.

**Working Mechanism (2 marks):**
Subscribers send a `subscribe(topic)` request to the broker, which stores the binding. Publishers invoke `publish(topic, payload)`, which the broker receives. The broker queries its registry for matching subscribers and **fans out** the message to each. Delivery is **asynchronous** — the publisher does not wait for consumers to finish.

**Variants (2 marks):**
1. **Topic-Based:** Filtering by a string topic label (e.g., `"NEWS/SPORTS"`).
2. **Content-Based:** Filtering by a predicate on message attributes (e.g., `temp > 50`).
3. **Channel-Based:** Each subscriber binds to a specific named channel.

---

**(b) UML Sequence Diagram & Flow (7 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant S1 as Sensor 1
    participant S2 as Sensor 2
    participant S3 as Sensor 3
    participant B as Weather Broker
    participant D1 as Dashboard North
    participant D2 as Dashboard South

    D1->>B: subscribe(topic: TEMP_READING)
    B-->>D1: subscription ACK
    D2->>B: subscribe(topic: TEMP_READING)
    B-->>D2: subscription ACK

    S1->>B: publish(TEMP_READING, 22.5C)
    S2->>B: publish(TEMP_READING, 30.1C)
    S3->>B: publish(TEMP_READING, 18.9C)

    par Fan-Out Delivery
        B-->>D1: deliver 22.5C
        B-->>D2: deliver 22.5C
    and
        B-->>D1: deliver 30.1C
        B-->>D2: deliver 30.1C
    and
        B-->>D1: deliver 18.9C
        B-->>D2: deliver 18.9C
    end
```

**Flow Explanation (3 marks):**
1. The two dashboards register their interest in the `TEMP_READING` topic at system start-up.
2. Each sensor publishes its temperature reading asynchronously to the broker, which immediately acknowledges receipt and returns control to the sensor.
3. The broker fans out every published reading to **both** dashboards concurrently (the `par` block in the sequence diagram denotes parallel delivery).
4. The sensors and dashboards **never communicate directly** — they remain fully decoupled, allowing additional sensors or dashboards to be added without code changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - Do **not** draw lines directly between the sensors and the dashboards. This is the most common error and costs full marks in the diagram section.
> - The `subscribe` call must occur *before* any `publish` call in the sequence, or the brokers' registry will be empty.
> - Forgetting to **label the topic name** (`TEMP_READING`) on the publish and subscribe arrows is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Set B]**
**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the Publish-Subscribe pattern with the Point-to-Point messaging pattern using a suitable tabular comparison. Highlight at least five distinguishing properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider an e-commerce application where `OrderService` publishes an `ORDER_PLACED` event and three subscribers — `InventoryService`, `EmailService`, and `AnalyticsService` — consume it. Draw a UML component diagram showing this architecture and explain the message-routing mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Tabular Comparison (7 Marks — 1 mark per row + 2 marks for any extra insights):**

| Property | **Publish-Subscribe** | **Point-to-Point** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Receiver Count** | One publisher to many subscribers (1:N) | One producer to one consumer (1:1) |
| **Coupling** | Anonymous / fully decoupled | Direct queue-name coupling |
| **Routing Mechanism** | Topic / content-based filtering | Direct queue assignment |
| **Message Persistence** | Usually transient; depends on broker | Usually persistent until consumed |
| **Delivery Semantics** | Broadcast / fan-out to all matches | Round-robin / load-balanced to one |
| **Failure Handling** | Other subscribers unaffected if one fails | Message stays in queue if consumer fails |
| **Typical Use Case** | Notifications, event streaming, telemetry | Job queues, task distribution |

---

**(b) UML Component Diagram & Mechanism (7 Marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    OS["OrderService Publisher"]
    subgraph "Message Broker Tier"
        MB["Event Bus"]
        REG["Subscription Registry: ORDER_PLACED - InventoryService, EmailService, AnalyticsService"]
    end
    IS["InventoryService Subscriber"]
    ES["EmailService Subscriber"]
    AS["AnalyticsService Subscriber"]

    OS -- "publish(ORDER_PLACED, orderData)" --> MB
    MB -- lookup --> REG
    MB -- deliver --> IS
    MB -- deliver --> ES
    MB -- deliver --> AS
```

**Routing Mechanism Explanation (3 marks):**
1. `InventoryService`, `EmailService`, and `AnalyticsService` all send `subscribe("ORDER_PLACED")` to the broker on startup, populating the subscription registry.
2. When a customer places an order, `OrderService` invokes `publish("ORDER_PLACED", orderData)`. Control returns immediately.
3. The broker queries its registry, finds three matching subscribers, and **fans out** the event to each one in parallel.
4. Each subscriber processes the event independently — `InventoryService` decrements stock, `EmailService` sends a confirmation email, and `AnalyticsService` updates the day's sales dashboard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - In part (a), students often confuse **topic-based filtering** with **content-based filtering**. Remember: topic-based uses *string labels*, while content-based uses *boolean predicates on payload attributes*. Mixing them up costs marks.
> - In part (b), failing to show the **subscription registry** as a distinct element inside the broker is a 1-mark deduction. The examiner wants to see that the broker *stores* the subscription list.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**
>
> - **Pub-Sub = 1-to-N, decoupled, asynchronous, topic-filtered messaging.**
> - **Three core roles:** Publisher, Message Broker, Subscriber. Never draw direct arrows between Publisher and Subscriber.
> - **Broker is the heart:** It holds the subscription registry, performs filtering, and performs fan-out.
> - **Four variants:** Topic-based, Content-based, Channel-based, Type-based. Know at least the first three.
> - **Pub-Sub vs Point-to-Point:** Pub-Sub = broadcast + ephemeral; P2P = load-balanced + persistent. This is a *favourite* 3-mark question.
> - **Coupling reduction:** Dependency graph goes from $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot n)$ edges to $\mathcal{O}(m + n)$.
> - **Fan-out load:** Broker load $= \lambda \times N$. This is *why* brokers use partitioned topics.
> - **Real-world examples to remember:** Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, Amazon SNS, MQTT, Google Pub/Sub.
> - **UML diagrams expected in exams:** Sequence Diagram (lifecycle), Component Diagram (architecture), Deployment Diagram (physical nodes). Practice drawing all three.
> - **Always label the topic name** on both the `subscribe` and `publish` arrows in sequence diagrams.
> - **Common 1-mark traps:** (i) Drawing a direct line from publisher to subscriber, (ii) forgetting that Pub-Sub is *asynchronous*, (iii) confusing `subscribe` with `publish`.
> - **Decoupling is the single biggest keyword** to mention whenever explaining the pattern's benefits.
> - **Use the formal tuple notation** $\mathcal{P} = \langle P, S, T, B, f, g \rangle$ in 14-mark answers to score the "academic depth" marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model-View-Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model-View-Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** is a foundational **architectural design pattern** introduced by Trygve Reenskaug in 1979 (Xerox PARC) that decomposes an interactive software application into **three mutually coupled but logically decoupled components** — the **Model**, the **View**, and the **Controller** — in order to separate the *internal representation of information* (data + business logic) from the *modes of presentation* and the *input processing pipeline*.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECGT411 – Software Modelling Using UML)**, MVC is treated as a **structural pattern classifiable under the "Behavioral / Architectural" family** in GoF terminology, and it is the **default architectural framework** for nearly all object-oriented web, mobile, and desktop frameworks (e.g., Java Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django MVC, ASP.NET MVC, AngularJS).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 4 – Architectural Patterns):**
> MVC is a compulsory sub-topic under *Architectural Patterns / Presentation Patterns* and is a **frequently repeated 14-mark question** in KTU End Semester Evaluations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Model

To a first-time learner, the three roles of MVC can be memorized instantly using a **restaurant analogy**:

| MVC Component | Restaurant Counterpart | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | The **Kitchen** + **Pantry** | Holds raw data (ingredients) and applies business rules (recipes). Does not know who is eating. |
