| **View** | The **Plated Dish** + **Table Setting** | Pure presentation. Shows the current state of food to the customer. Cannot cook or modify ingredients. |
| **Controller** | The **Waiter** | Receives the customer order (user input), passes it to the kitchen, and brings the resulting dish back to the customer. |

When you (the user) click a button on a website, the **Controller** receives that click, asks the **Model** to update itself, and then tells the **View** to refresh — exactly like a waiter taking an order, the chef cooking, and the dish being served.

### 1.3 The "Three Concerns" Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** is the philosophical pillar of MVC. It dictates that a system must be partitioned so that each concern (data, presentation, user-input handling) lives in exactly **one logical module**, with **no overlap**.

The three concerns isolated by MVC are:
1. **Data concern** → handled by *Model*.
2. **Presentation concern** → handled by *View*.
3. **Control / Dispatch concern** → handled by *Controller*.

---

### 1.4 Boundary Conditions and Standard Metrics

- **Coupling metric:** MVC aims for **low coupling** (ideally *Content Coupling = 0* between Model–View).
- **Cohesion metric:** Each component targets **high functional cohesion** (Model = high *informational cohesion*, View/Controller = high *logical cohesion*).
- **Communication paths:** Strict MVC mandates **2-way communication between Controller↔View and Controller↔Model**, but **NO direct communication between Model↔View** (this is achieved via the *Observer* or *Publish-Subscribe* mechanism, or via the Controller acting as a relay).
- **Latency of update:** When Model state changes, all subscribed Views must re-render within a **single event-loop tick** in modern reactive frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 The Three Components — Exhaustive Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 The **Model**

The Model is the **domain-specific representation of the data and business rules** that drive the application. It is **completely UI-agnostic**.

- **Encapsulates:** application state, data validation, persistence logic, business workflows.
- **Knows about:** *nothing* about the View or the Controller (in classical MVC).
- **Notifies:** interested parties (typically Views or Controllers) via the **Observer / Listener** pattern when its state mutates.
- **KTU-required examples:** `UserAccount`, `Order`, `Inventory`, `Course`, `Student` classes in a university management system.

#### 2.1.2 The **View**

The View is the **passive visual representation layer** of the Model's current state.

- **Encapsulates:** rendering, formatting, layout, UI widgets, HTML/JSP/XAML/HTML templates.
- **Reacts to:** Model state-change events (push) **OR** Controller-triggered refresh commands (pull).
- **Forwards:** raw user gestures (mouse-clicks, key-presses, gestures) to the Controller for interpretation.
- **Constraint:** A View should contain **zero business logic** — only formatting logic.

#### 2.1.3 The **Controller**

The Controller is the **mediator and translator** between the human user and the Model.

- **Encapsulates:** input parsing, command mapping, workflow sequencing, view-selection logic.
- **Receives:** user events routed from the View.
- **Invokes:** appropriate methods on the Model to mutate state.
- **Selects:** which View should render the next response.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Stepwise Request Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Almost every KTU question on MVC tests the student's ability to **trace the request lifecycle**. Memorize the following 6-step pipeline.

1. **User Action (Input Generation)**
   The user performs a UI gesture (e.g., clicks a "Submit" button). The event is captured at the browser/UI layer.
2. **View → Controller (Event Dispatch)**
   The View intercepts the raw DOM event and dispatches it to the **registered Controller** (e.g., via a callback / event-handler binding).
3. **Controller → Model (Business Invocation)**
   The Controller parses the input, applies any request-level validation, and invokes one or more methods on the **Model** to mutate or query the application state.
4. **Model State Update (Internal Mutation)**
   The Model updates its internal data structures and **fires a `change` / `propertyChange` event** to all registered observers (Views or Controllers in Passive/Active variants).
5. **Model → View (Notification / Refresh)**
   In **Active MVC**, the View *directly* observes the Model and re-renders itself. In **Passive MVC**, the Controller reads the new state and explicitly tells the View to update.
6. **View → User (Response Rendering)**
   The View re-renders the updated Model state and presents the new output to the user, closing the loop.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every **symbol, abbreviation, and relationship rule** examiners expect students to know for the MVC question.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Used In / Exam Tag** |
|---|---|---|
| **M** | Model — domain data + business logic | KTU 2022, 2023, 2024 ESE |
| **V** | View — UI presentation | KTU 2022, 2024 ESE |
| **C** | Controller — input handler + mediator | All years |
| **SoC** | Separation of Concerns | Frequently asked definition |
| **Observer** | Pattern used by Model to notify View | Part A 3-mark staple |
| **Push MVC** | Model pushes updates to View | Module-level distinction |
| **Pull MVC** | View pulls state from Model | Module-level distinction |
| **Passive MVC** | Controller is sole mediator (no M↔V link) | Diagram-drawing question |
| **Active MVC** | Model notifies View directly | Diagram-drawing question |
| **GoF Category** | Architectural / Presentation Pattern | 2023 ESE Q5(b) |
| **Inventor** | Trygve Reenskaug, 1979, Xerox PARC | 3-mark Part A frequent |
| **Coupling target** | Model ↔ View = 0 (zero) | Conceptual question |
| **Mapping to MVC frameworks** | Spring MVC, Rails, Django, ASP.NET | 14-mark application question |

### 2.4 The Three MVC Variants — KTU-Relevant Distinctions

| **Variant** | **M ↔ V Link?** | **Mediator Role** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classical / Active MVC** | ✅ Yes (via Observer) | Controller is dispatch-only | Original Smalltalk/Swing Java |
| **Passive MVC** | ❌ No (broken) | Controller is sole bridge | Web frameworks (Spring, ASP.NET) |
| **MVC Model 2** | ❌ No | Front Controller + Dispatcher Servlet | J2EE / Jakarta EE servlets |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Apps:** Java Spring MVC, ASP.NET Core MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django, Express + EJS.
- **Mobile Apps:** iOS UIKit (UIViewController), Android (Activity ≈ Controller, View, Repository ≈ Model).
- **Desktop Apps:** Java Swing, .NET WPF + MVVM (a derivative).
- **Frontend Frameworks:** Angular, React (with Redux), Backbone.js — all implement MVC or its derivatives (MVP, MVVM, Flux).

### 2.6 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages (Examiner's favourite 6-bullet list):**
- Parallel development by 3 teams.
- High reusability of Model across multiple Views.
- Plug-and-play replacement of Views (e.g., web → mobile).
- Easier unit testing (Model testable without UI).
- Clear role-assignment for developers.
- Supports multiple synchronized Views of the same Model.

**Limitations:**
- Increases file/class count → complex for small apps.
- Steep learning curve for beginners.
- Indirection can reduce performance slightly.
- In Active MVC, multiple-observer management adds complexity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Worked Example — University Course Registration System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Hint:** Application-based questions (e.g., "Apply MVC to a Hospital Management System" or "Library System") appear every December/May paper. We will exhaustively derive the design for a *Course Registration* use-case so you can re-apply the reasoning to any system.

**Scenario:** A student logs in, views available courses, and clicks "Enroll" on a chosen course. The system must (i) verify seat availability, (ii) decrement seat count, (iii) refresh the course list shown to the user.

#### Step 1 — Identify the **Model** classes (domain entities + business logic)

$$
\text{Model} = \{ \texttt{Student}, \texttt{Course}, \texttt{Enrollment}, \texttt{RegistrationService} \}
$$

The `RegistrationService` enforces the business rule:

$$
\texttt{enroll}(s, c) \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \texttt{decrement}(c.\text{seats}) & \text{if } c.\text{seats} > 0 \\ \texttt{throw CourseFullException} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The Model fires a notification:

$$
\texttt{courseChanged} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{notifyObservers()}
$$

#### Step 2 — Identify the **View** components (presentation)

$$
\text{View} = \{ \texttt{LoginView}, \texttt{CourseListView}, \texttt{EnrollmentConfirmView}, \texttt{ErrorView} \}
$$

A `CourseListView` is bound to a `Course` Model and re-renders whenever `courseChanged` fires.

#### Step 3 — Identify the **Controller** components (input handlers)

$$
\text{Controller} = \{ \texttt{LoginController}, \texttt{CourseController}, \texttt{EnrollmentController} \}
$$

Mapping the request to method invocations:

$$
\texttt{CourseController}.\texttt{handleEnroll}(\text{request}) \rightarrow \texttt{RegistrationService}.\texttt{enroll}(\text{student}, \text{course})
$$

#### Step 4 — Trace the full lifecycle of one "Enroll" click

We model the lifecycle as a sequence of message-passes. Let:
- $U$ = User
- $V$ = `CourseListView`
- $C$ = `CourseController`
- $M$ = `RegistrationService` (Model)
- $M_c$ = `Course` (sub-Model)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&U \xrightarrow{\text{click}('Enroll')} V \quad &\text{[User clicks Enroll on a course card]} \\
&V \xrightarrow{\text{onEnroll}(c)} C \quad &\text{[View forwards raw event to Controller]} \\
&C \xrightarrow{\text{validate}(s, c)} M \quad &\text{[Controller delegates to Model]} \\
&M \xrightarrow{\text{checkSeats}(c)} M_c \quad &\text{[Model queries sub-Model]} \\
&M_c \xrightarrow{\text{seats}=5} M \quad &\text{[Sub-Model returns state]} \\
&M \xrightarrow{\text{decrement}(c), \text{fire}('courseChanged')} M_c \quad &\text{[Model mutates, fires event]} \\
&M \xrightarrow{\text{success}()} C \quad &\text{[Returns success to Controller]} \\
&C \xrightarrow{\text{selectView}(\text{Confirmation})} V \quad &\text{[Controller picks next View]} \\
&V \xrightarrow{\text{render}} U \quad &\text[View updates user] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This nine-step trace is the **canonical answer** the KTU examiner expects for a "trace the MVC flow" sub-part.

#### Step 5 — Verify the **Separation of Concerns** invariant

We assert the following invariants for full marks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Model} \cap \text{View} = \emptyset \\
&\text{Model} \cap \text{Controller} = \emptyset \\
&\text{View} \cap \text{Controller} = \emptyset \\
\end{aligned}
$$

and the directional communication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V \to C \;\;(\text{event dispatch}) \\
&C \to M \;\;(\text{command invocation}) \\
&M \to C \;\;(\text{result / notification}) \\
&C \to V \;\;(\text{render command}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

with the **Active-MVC edge** $M \to V$ (notification via Observer) optional.

#### Step 6 — Compute the **interaction complexity** (informal metric)

For $n$ Model classes and $m$ View classes with $k$ Controllers:

$$
\text{Edge count in classical MVC} = V \!\to\! C \;+\; C \!\to\! M \;+\; M \!\to\! C \;+\; C \!\to\! V
$$

which is at most $4k$ plus any Active-MVC observation edges $n \times m$ — a key reason why **small apps are often not worth implementing in pure MVC**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classical MVC — Class / Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph V_Cluster ["VIEW CLUSTER"]
        view1["View A"]
        view2["View B"]
        view3["View C"]
    end

    subgraph C_Cluster ["CONTROLLER CLUSTER"]
        ctrl1["Controller"]
    end

    subgraph M_Cluster ["MODEL CLUSTER"]
        model1["Model Class X"]
        model2["Model Class Y"]
    end

    view1 -- "1 raw user event" --> ctrl1
    view2 -- "1 raw user event" --> ctrl1
    view3 -- "1 raw user event" --> ctrl1

    ctrl1 -- "2 invoke / update" --> model1
    ctrl1 -- "2 invoke / update" --> model2

    model1 -- "3 notify state change" --> view1
    model2 -- "3 notify state change" --> view2

    ctrl1 -- "4 select / refresh" --> view1
    ctrl1 -- "4 select / refresh" --> view2
    ctrl1 -- "4 select / refresh" --> view3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram visually enforces the rule: **no direct Model ↔ Controller in the other direction** beyond the labelled arrows, and **no View ↔ View** edges.

### 4.2 MVC — UML Class Diagram (Formal Notation)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Model {
        -data : Object
        -observers : List~Observer~
        +addObserver(o : Observer) void
        +removeObserver(o : Observer) void
        +notifyObservers() void
        +getState() Object
        +setState(s : Object) void
        +businessLogic() void
    }

    class View {
        -model : Model
        +render() void
        +update() void
        +display() void
    }

    class Controller {
        -view : View
        -model : Model
        +handleRequest(req : Request) void
        +dispatch() void
    }

    class Observer {
        <<interface>>
        +update() void
    }

    Model ..> Observer : notifies
    View ..|> Observer : implements
    Controller --> Model : invokes
    Controller --> View : selects / refreshes
    View --> Model : observes
```

### 4.3 MVC — UML Sequence Diagram (Request Lifecycle)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant V as View
    participant C as Controller
    participant M as Model

    U->>V: Click "Enroll"
    V->>C: dispatchEvent("onEnroll")
    C->>M: invoke enroll(student, course)
    M->>M: validateBusinessRule()
    M-->>C: return success / failure
    M-->>V: fire "courseChanged" (Active MVC)
    C->>V: selectView(nextView)
    V->>U: render updated screen
```

### 4.4 Passive MVC vs Active MVC — Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ActiveMVC ["ACTIVE MVC"]
        direction LR
        A1[User] --> A2[View]
        A2 --> A3[Controller]
        A3 --> A4[Model]
        A4 -. "Observer notify" .-> A2
    end

    subgraph PassiveMVC ["PASSIVE MVC"]
        direction LR
        P1[User] --> P2[View]
        P2 --> P3[Controller]
        P3 --> P4[Model]
        P3 --> P5[View Update]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students draw a *direct* Model → View arrow in the *Passive* MVC variant. This is **wrong** and costs a full mark in the diagram-drawing sub-question. The dashed `Observer notify` line exists **only** in Active MVC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. List its three core components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **MVC** is an **architectural design pattern** that separates an interactive application into three logically independent components: the **Model**, the **View**, and the **Controller**.
> - The **Model** encapsulates the application data and business rules.
> - The **View** renders the Model's current state to the user.
> - The **Controller** receives user input, invokes the Model, and selects the appropriate View.
>
> This separation enforces the **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** principle, enabling parallel development, reusability, and easier maintenance.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for components list + 1 mark for naming the SoC principle)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the role of the **Observer pattern** in the classical (Active) MVC architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> In **Active MVC**, the Model is **decoupled** from the View, and the **Observer pattern** is used as the notification bridge. The Model maintains a list of registered observers (typically Views). Whenever the Model's state changes, it calls `notifyObservers()`, which automatically invokes `update()` on every registered View, causing it to re-render. This enforces **automatic, multi-View synchronization** without the Model needing to know how the View is implemented.

*(1 mark for naming pattern + 1 mark for explain + 1 mark for automatic synchronization benefit)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the direction of arrows** in the MVC diagram. Marks are awarded for **directed, labelled edges** (e.g., "1. user input", "2. invoke").
> 2. **Confusing MVC with its derivatives** (MVP, MVVM) costs full marks. Always state the variant you are drawing.
> 3. Forgetting the **Observer / Publish-Subscribe** connection in Active MVC is the single most common deduction in the 7-mark diagram part.
> 4. The "trace the request" sub-question must list **at least 6 numbered steps** to secure full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, ESE Q5(a)]**

**(a) Explain the Model-View-Controller architectural pattern with a neat diagram. List any four advantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

MVC is an architectural pattern that **decouples data (Model), presentation (View), and input-handling (Controller)** to enforce Separation of Concerns. The **diagram** is as shown in **Section 4.1** above (use the Mermaid figure; in a written exam, reproduce as three boxes — M, V, C — with directed labelled arrows showing V→C→M and the Observer-dashed M→V line).

**Four advantages (½ mark each):**
1. Promotes **parallel development** of UI, logic, and data layers.
2. **Multiple Views** of the same Model can be displayed simultaneously and stay synchronized.
3. **High reusability** — the Model can be reused across Views and platforms.
4. **Easier unit testing** — the Model is testable in isolation from the UI.
5. Clear **separation of roles** improves team productivity.
6. Supports **plug-and-play** replacement of Views (e.g., web → mobile).

*(2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for component definitions + 3 marks for advantages = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Apply MVC to a Library Management System. Identify the Model, View, and Controller classes and trace the request flow when a user issues a "Borrow Book" request. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Classes (1 mark) |
|---|---|
| **Model** | `Book`, `Member`, `BorrowRecord`, `LibraryService` (encapsulates borrow/return rules) |
| **View** | `LoginView`, `BookSearchView`, `BookDetailsView`, `BorrowConfirmView` |
| **Controller** | `LoginController`, `BookController`, `BorrowController` |

**Request Flow — "Borrow Book" (6 marks distributed across steps):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1.\;\;U \xrightarrow{\text{click "Borrow"}} \texttt{BookDetailsView} \\
&2.\;\;\texttt{BookDetailsView} \xrightarrow{\text{onBorrow()}} \texttt{BorrowController} \\
&3.\;\;\texttt{BorrowController} \xrightarrow{\texttt{borrowBook}(m, b)} \texttt{LibraryService} \\
&4.\;\;\texttt{LibraryService} \xrightarrow{\text{checkAvailability}(b)} \texttt{Book} \\
&5.\;\;\texttt{Book} \xrightarrow{\text{available}=\text{false}} \texttt{LibraryService} \\
&6.\;\;\texttt{LibraryService} \rightarrow \texttt{decrementCopies}(b) \\
&7.\;\;\texttt{Book} \xrightarrow{\text{fire "bookChanged"}} \texttt{BookDetailsView} \;\;(\text{Active MVC}) \\
&8.\;\;\texttt{BorrowController} \xrightarrow{\text{selectView}(\text{Confirmation})} \texttt{BorrowConfirmView} \\
&9.\;\;\texttt{BorrowConfirmView} \xrightarrow{\text{render}} U \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification of SoC (½ mark):**
The `Book` class contains **no UI code**, the `BookDetailsView` contains **no business logic**, and the `BorrowController` contains **no SQL or data-access code** — satisfying the SoC invariant.

*(1 mark class identification + 5 marks stepwise flow + 1 mark SoC justification = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, ESE Q8(b)]**

**(a) Compare and contrast Active MVC and Passive MVC. Draw labelled diagrams for both. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Axis (½ mark each × 6 = 3 marks) | **Active MVC** | **Passive MVC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Model ↔ View link** | Yes — via Observer pattern | No — broken link |
| **Controller role** | Pure event dispatcher | Sole mediator and updater |
| **View update trigger** | Model fires `change` event | Controller calls `view.update()` |
| **Multiple synchronized Views** | Native support | Manual coordination required |
| **Framework examples** | Smalltalk, Java Swing, .NET | Spring MVC, ASP.NET, Django |
| **Use case** | Desktop GUI with rich event model | Stateless web / request-response |

**Diagrams (4 marks — 2 marks each):**

- **Active MVC** — V→C→M with dashed `Observer notify` line from M back to V (see Section 4.4).
- **Passive MVC** — V→C, C→M, C→V only. **No direct M→V edge.**

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
> Active MVC is preferred for stateful, event-driven desktop UIs; Passive MVC dominates stateless web applications.

---

**(b) Discuss any four limitations of MVC and explain how MVVM and MVP address them. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Four limitations of MVC (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **High indirection** — three layers for even trivial tasks inflate code volume.
2. **Tight coupling to Observer** in Active MVC complicates the Model.
3. **Controller bloat** — Controllers accumulate workflow logic ("Massive View-Controller" problem).
4. **Testability of Views** is still hard because Views are often tightly coupled to UI toolkits.

**How MVP addresses them (2½ marks):**
- The **Presenter** is a *dedicated* middle layer, replacing the Controller's dispatch + render duties with a testable, UI-toolkit-independent class.
- The **Passive View** is a dumb interface; Presenter does everything. Easy to unit-test without UI.

**How MVVM addresses them (2½ marks):**
- The **ViewModel** exposes data and commands as observable properties (e.g., `INotifyPropertyChanged` in WPF).
- The **View** *binds declaratively* to the ViewModel — no manual `update()` call.
- Solves Controller bloat by giving the ViewModel the role of converting Model state into a view-friendly observable form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ MVC was invented by **Trygve Reenskaug, 1979, Xerox PARC** (Smalltalk).
- ✅ MVC is an **architectural / presentation pattern**, NOT a GoF "behavioral" pattern strictly, though it is *classified* in many KTU texts as behavioral.
- ✅ **Three components:** Model (data + logic), View (UI), Controller (input mediator).
- ✅ **Core principle:** Separation of Concerns (SoC).
- ✅ **Active MVC** = Model ↔ View direct via **Observer**; **Passive MVC** = Controller is sole mediator.
- ✅ **Standard request flow:** User → View → Controller → Model → (notify or return) → View → User.
- ✅ **Coupling target:** Model ↔ View = 0; Controller is the only allowed bridge.
- ✅ **Advantages to memorize (at least 4):** parallel dev, multiple views, reusability, testability, plug-and-play.
- ✅ **Frameworks to name:** Spring MVC, ASP.NET MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django, Angular, Swing.
- ✅ **Common derivatives:** MVP (Model-View-Presenter), MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel).
- ✅ **Diagrams must be:** directed, labelled, and variant-specified (Active vs Passive).
- ✅ **14-mark question must include:** (i) diagram (2–4 marks), (ii) component list (2 marks), (iii) stepwise flow (4–6 marks), (iv) advantages / limitations / variant comparison (2–4 marks).
- ✅ **Never draw a direct Model ↔ View arrow** unless you explicitly label it as the Observer-dashed Active-MVC edge.
- ✅ **Always mention SoC** in the conclusion of any MVC answer — it is the "polish" word that fetches the last half-mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Gang of Four / KTU Terminology)

The **Factory Method** is a *Creational Design Pattern* that defines an **interface for creating an object, but defers the actual instantiation to subclasses**. It allows a class to delegate the responsibility of object creation to its child classes, thereby promoting loose coupling between the *Creator* and the *Concrete Products*.

In the canonical KTU/GoF (Gang of Four – Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides) terminology:

> **Definition:** *"The Factory Method pattern defines an interface for creating an object, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Factory Method lets a class defer instantiation to subclasses."*

The pattern is also historically known as the **Virtual Constructor**.

## 1.2 Intent of the Pattern (The "Why")

The intent of the Factory Method is to:

1. **Encapsulate object creation logic** so that the client code does not depend on concrete classes.
2. **Eliminate the need to bind application-specific classes** into the main framework code.
3. Provide a **"hook" for subclasses** to extend or vary the products being created — this is a direct application of the **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**.
4. Decouple the *abstraction* (an interface or abstract class) from the *implementation* (a concrete class).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The Factory Method is the *most frequently asked* Creational Pattern in KTU ESE questions on Design Patterns. Always associate it with: **"Inversion of Control (IoC)"**, **"Hollywood Principle"** ("Don't call us, we'll call you"), and **"Dependency Inversion"**.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Logistics Company

Imagine a logistics company that ships goods either by **road** (Truck) or **by sea** (Ship). The central office (the *Client*) just says *"plan a transport for this cargo"* — it does not know or care whether a Truck or a Ship will actually be dispatched.

- The **Logistics App** is the *Creator* (abstract class).
- The **RoadLogisticsApp** and **SeaLogisticsApp** are the *Concrete Creators*.
- The `createTransport()` method is the *Factory Method*.
- The `Truck` and `Ship` are the *Concrete Products* that implement a common `Transport` interface.

When the client requests a transport, the appropriate subclass (Road or Sea) overrides the factory method and decides **which concrete product to instantiate** at runtime. This is exactly the philosophy of the Factory Method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy in One Line:** A restaurant franchise issues a franchise license (Factory Method contract) — each local branch (subclass) decides whether the menu item will be a Burger or a Pizza (concrete product), but the cooking process (interface) remains identical.

## 1.4 Classification Position

| Dimension | Classification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Pattern Category | **Creational** |
| GoF Book Chapter | Chapter 3 (Creational Patterns) |
| Scope | **Object** (operates on object instantiation) |
| Wraps | The "new" operator (via inheritance-based polymorphism) |
| Variant Relationship | Foundation for *Abstract Factory*, *Prototype*, and *Builder* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Participants

The Factory Method pattern formally defines exactly **four participants**. KTU questions often test whether the student can enumerate these correctly.

1. **Product (Abstract Product)** — Declares the interface for the objects that the factory method creates. It is typically an **abstract class or interface** (e.g., `Transport`).
2. **ConcreteProduct (Concrete Product)** — Implements the `Product` interface. Each `ConcreteCreator` returns the appropriate `ConcreteProduct` (e.g., `Truck`, `Ship`).
3. **Creator (Abstract Creator)** — Declares the **factory method** (abstract by default) that returns an object of type `Product`. It may also define a default implementation that uses the factory method. **Crucially, the Creator is not coupled to any ConcreteProduct.**
4. **ConcreteCreator (Concrete Creator)** — Overrides the factory method to instantiate and return a specific `ConcreteProduct`. Each `ConcreteCreator` is paired with exactly one `ConcreteProduct`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"C, C, P, P"** — Creator, ConcreteCreator, Product, ConcreteProduct. Always draw them in this order in UML class diagrams (left-to-right: Creator → ConcreteCreator, Product ← ConcreteProduct).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

Let us break down the *runtime* flow of a Factory Method:

1. **Client** invokes a service method on the *Creator* (e.g., `logisticsApp.planDelivery()`).
2. The *Creator*'s service method internally calls the **factory method** `createTransport()`.
3. The factory method, being **abstract in the base Creator**, is **resolved polymorphically** at runtime to the implementation in a *ConcreteCreator* subclass.
4. The *ConcreteCreator*'s factory method instantiates a **ConcreteProduct** (e.g., `new Truck()`) and returns it typed as `Product`.
5. The Creator's service method then uses the returned `Product` polymorphically, never knowing the concrete class.

This is a classic case of **late binding** via runtime polymorphism.

## 2.3 Applicability — When to Use Factory Method

Apply Factory Method when:

- A class **cannot anticipate the class of objects** it must create (e.g., a document editor that opens `.doc`, `.pdf`, `.xls`).
- A class wants its **subclasses to specify** the objects it creates.
- Classes **delegate responsibility** to one of several helper subclasses, and you want to **localise the knowledge of which helper subclass is the delegate** (the "helper" is the concrete product).
- You need to **decouple the client code** from concrete classes and gain the **Open/Closed benefit** — new products are added by introducing new concrete creators, without modifying existing creator code.

## 2.4 Consequences — The Trade-off Matrix

| Consequence | Benefit | Cost / Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eliminates binding to specific classes** | Client works with Product interface | More classes (parallel hierarchy) |
| **Provides hooks for subclasses** | Direct support for OCP | Indirect coupling via inheritance |
| **Connects parallel class hierarchies** | E.g., UI Widgets ↔ UI Layouts | None significant |
| **Defers instantiation to subclasses** | Runtime flexibility | Slightly higher complexity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse **Factory Method** with **Abstract Factory**. Factory Method uses **inheritance** and creates **one product per creator**. Abstract Factory uses **composition** and creates **families of related products** via an object composition. KTU questions frequently include a "Compare Factory Method vs Abstract Factory" sub-part.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Substitute for "Formula Sheet")

Since this is a Design Pattern topic (not numeric), the equivalent of a formula sheet is the **Pattern Fingerprint Table**:

| Field | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pattern Name** | Factory Method |
| **Category** | Creational |
| **GoF Alias** | Virtual Constructor |
| **Number of Participants** | 4 (Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator) |
| **Key Mechanism** | Polymorphism via inheritance (subclass overrides `factoryMethod()`) |
| **Coupling Style** | Decouples Creator from ConcreteProduct |
| **OCP Compliance** | High (extend by adding new ConcreteCreators) |
| **LSP Risk** | Low (subclasses only add behaviour, do not change contract) |
| **Implementation Language Default** | Java (GoF) — uses `abstract` and `interface` |
| **SOLID Principle Mapped** | Open/Closed, Dependency Inversion |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Factory Method is extensively used in:

- **Java Swing / AWT** — `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().createButton()` is a factory method pattern. The JVM on Windows returns a Win32 button; on macOS, a Cocoa button.
- **Spring Framework** — `BeanFactory` is a generic factory; specific implementations (`XmlBeanFactory`, `DefaultListableBeanFactory`) are concrete creators.
- **JDBC** — `DriverManager.getConnection(url)` returns a database-specific `Connection` without binding the client to Oracle/MySQL/PostgreSQL.
- **Logging Frameworks** — SLF4J's `LoggerFactory.getLogger()` defers logger creation to the underlying logging backend (Log4j, Logback).
- **Android `BitmapFactory`** — decodes bitmaps of various formats without binding the client to a specific decoder.

This is the pattern that **most production-grade frameworks** rely on internally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because Factory Method is a structural design concept, the equivalent of a "derivation" is the **exhaustive build-up of its class skeleton** and the **logical reasoning for each design decision**. We will derive the solution for the **Logistics Problem** step-by-step and then represent it in Java and UML.

## 3.1 The Problem Statement

> *"Design a logistics application that can plan deliveries via **Road Transport (Truck)** or **Sea Transport (Ship)**. The client should not be coupled to either specific transport type."*

### 3.2 Step 1 — Identify the Product Abstraction

We first identify a **common interface** that all variants must obey. Both `Truck` and `Ship` have a `deliver()` operation. Hence:

```java
// Product interface — declares operations all concrete products must implement
public interface Transport {
    void deliver();
}
```

> **Reasoning Step:** The Product interface is the **Type** the client will hold a reference to. By typing to this interface, the client is decoupled from any concrete transport.

### 3.3 Step 2 — Create Concrete Products

Each variant of the product becomes a *ConcreteProduct*. They implement the `Transport` interface:

```java
// ConcreteProduct 1
public class Truck implements Transport {
    @Override
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Deliver by land in a box.");
    }
}

// ConcreteProduct 2
public class Ship implements Transport {
    @Override
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Deliver by sea in a container.");
    }
}
```

> **Reasoning Step:** Each ConcreteProduct is a leaf in the Product hierarchy. The number of ConcreteProducts can grow without affecting the Creator.

### 3.4 Step 3 — Define the Abstract Creator

The Creator declares the **factory method** as abstract and provides a default service method (`planDelivery`) that uses it:

```java
// Creator — abstract class
public abstract class LogisticsApp {

    // The Factory Method — abstract; subclasses must override
    public abstract Transport createTransport();

    // Default service method that uses the factory method
    public void planDelivery() {
        Transport t = createTransport();  // calls the subclass
        t.deliver();
    }
}
```

> **Reasoning Step:** The Creator **does not use `new`** for any ConcreteProduct. The `createTransport()` call is a polymorphic call resolved at runtime. This is the **Hollywood Principle** in action.

### 3.5 Step 4 — Create Concrete Creators

Each ConcreteCreator overrides the factory method to return a specific ConcreteProduct:

```java
// ConcreteCreator 1
public class RoadLogisticsApp extends LogisticsApp {
    @Override
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Truck();   // decision made here
    }
}

// ConcreteCreator 2
public class SeaLogisticsApp extends LogisticsApp {
    @Override
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Ship();    // decision made here
    }
}
```

> **Reasoning Step:** Each ConcreteCreator is **paired one-to-one with a ConcreteProduct**. The pairing is the *essence* of the Factory Method pattern.

### 3.6 Step 5 — The Client (Demonstration)

The client code is now decoupled and uses the Creator's abstract reference:

```java
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LogisticsApp app;

        // Decision: which creator to instantiate is made by config / input
        String mode = "sea";
        if (mode.equals("road")) {
            app = new RoadLogisticsApp();
        } else {
            app = new SeaLogisticsApp();
        }

        app.planDelivery();  // output: "Deliver by sea in a container."
    }
}
```

> **Reasoning Step:** The client never uses `new Truck()` or `new Ship()`. It only knows `LogisticsApp` and `Transport`. This is **Dependency Inversion in its purest form**.

## 3.7 Mathematical/Logical Summary — "Pattern Formula"

The runtime behaviour can be expressed as the following logical equation:

$$
\text{concreteProductReturned} = \text{concreteCreatorInstance}.\text{factoryMethod}()
$$

Or, in extended form:

$$
\forall \, c \in \text{ConcreteCreators}, \quad c.\text{factoryMethod}() \mapsto p_c \in \text{ConcreteProducts}
$$

where the function $f$ from ConcreteCreators to ConcreteProducts is a **bijection** (one-to-one correspondence) in the canonical GoF form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ClientRequest} &\rightarrow \text{ConcreteCreator} \\
\text{ConcreteCreator}.\text{factoryMethod}() &\rightarrow \text{ConcreteProduct} \\
\text{Creator}.\text{serviceMethod}() &\xrightarrow{\text{uses}} \text{Product (via interface)} \\
\text{Client} &\xleftarrow{\text{decoupled from}} \text{ConcreteProduct}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The "bijection" property (one creator per concrete product) is what distinguishes Factory Method from Abstract Factory. If a single creator can return multiple types of products from related families, you are looking at **Abstract Factory** — a frequent KTU trick question.

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Factory Method | Simple Factory (Non-GoF) | Abstract Factory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GoF Recognised | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (idiom) | ✅ Yes |
| Uses Inheritance | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No (uses composition) |
| Uses Composition | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (holds factory) | ✅ Yes |
| Subclassing Required | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Products Created | One type per creator | One type per factory | Families of related types |
| Open/Closed Principle | ✅ High | ❌ Violated (modifying factory) | ✅ High |
| Typical Use Case | Frameworks, plugins | Small-scale refactoring | UI Themes, DB vendors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Factory Method Structure (GoF Canonical)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Product {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteProductA {
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteProductB {
        +operation()
    }
    class Creator {
        <<abstract>>
        +factoryMethod() Product
        +anOperation()
    }
    class ConcreteCreatorA {
        +factoryMethod() Product
    }
    class ConcreteCreatorB {
        +factoryMethod() Product
    }

    Product <|.. ConcreteProductA : implements
    Product <|.. ConcreteProductB : implements
    Creator <|-- ConcreteCreatorA : extends
    Creator <|-- ConcreteCreatorB : extends
    Creator ..> Product : creates
    ConcreteCreatorA ..> ConcreteProductA : instantiates
    ConcreteCreatorB ..> ConcreteProductB : instantiates
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU interpretation):**
- The **hollow triangle arrow** (`<|..`) denotes *implements* / *realises*.
- The **solid hollow triangle** (`<|--`) denotes *extends* / *inherits*.
- The **dashed arrow** (`..>`) denotes a *dependency* (Creator uses Product, ConcreteCreator instantiates ConcreteProduct).

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Runtime Object Creation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client
    participant CR as Creator (abstract)
    participant CCR as ConcreteCreator
    participant P as ConcreteProduct

    C->>CR: call anOperation()
    CR->>CCR: factoryMethod() (polymorphic)
    CCR->>P: new ConcreteProduct()
    P-->>CCR: return instance
    CCR-->>CR: return as Product
    CR->>P: call operation() polymorphically
    P-->>C: final result
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Step 1: Client invokes a high-level operation on the abstract Creator.
- Step 2: The Creator's method calls the **factory method** — but the *actual* method executed is in the subclass (ConcreteCreator).
- Step 3: The ConcreteCreator **instantiates** the ConcreteProduct using `new`.
- Step 4: The instance is returned, **typed up** as the Product interface, fulfilling the contract.
- Step 5: The Creator invokes an operation on the Product — *the Creator does not know the concrete type*.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Logistics Example Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Code] -->|uses| B[LogisticsApp Abstract Creator]
    B -->|inherited by| C1[RoadLogisticsApp]
    B -->|inherited by| C2[SeaLogisticsApp]
    C1 -->|factoryMethod returns| D1[Truck]
    C2 -->|factoryMethod returns| D2[Ship]
    D1 -.->|implements| E[Transport Interface]
    D2 -.->|implements| E
    B -.->|depends on| E
```

This flowchart visually maps the **Logistics analogy** from Section 1 to the **canonical GoF structure**, helping KTU students draw the same mapping in their exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define the Factory Method design pattern. State its GoF category and intent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1+1+1):**
- **Category:** Factory Method is a **Creational** design pattern, listed in the Gang-of-Four (GoF) catalogue (1 mark).
- **Definition:** It defines an interface for creating an object but **defers instantiation to subclasses** (1 mark).
- **Intent:** The Creator class delegates object creation to its subclasses, decoupling the client code from concrete product classes (1 mark).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for "Creational", 1 mark for "interface for creation", and 1 mark for "deferring to subclasses". Any 2 of the 3 key phrases will secure 2 marks.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: List the four participants of the Factory Method pattern. State the role of the ConcreteCreator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 2+1):**
The four participants are:
1. **Product** (abstract interface for created objects).
2. **ConcreteProduct** (specific implementation of the Product).
3. **Creator** (declares the abstract Factory Method).
4. **ConcreteCreator** (overrides the Factory Method to return a specific ConcreteProduct) (2 marks).

**Role of ConcreteCreator:** It is the subclass that **implements the factory method** and is responsible for instantiating and returning a single specific ConcreteProduct. Each ConcreteCreator is paired with exactly one ConcreteProduct (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Full ESE Pattern*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Full Question]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Factory Method design pattern with a suitable UML class diagram. Describe the roles of all four participants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Factory Method pattern with the Simple Factory idiom. Demonstrate Factory Method using a real-world example in Java. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Answer (7 Marks — Allocation: 3+2+2)

**Explanation of Pattern (3 marks):**
The Factory Method is a creational pattern that **defines an interface for creating an object** but allows **subclasses to decide** which concrete class to instantiate. The pattern promotes **loose coupling** between the client and product families. The standard "new" operator call is replaced with a call to a **factory method** that subclasses override.

**UML Class Diagram (2 marks):**
(Examiner expects a hand-drawn equivalent of the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 with proper UML notation: hollow triangle for inheritance, dashed arrow for dependency.)

**Roles of the Four Participants (2 marks):**
- **Product:** Declares the interface of objects the factory method creates.
- **ConcreteProduct:** Implements the Product interface.
- **Creator:** Declares the factory method returning a Product; may call it inside a service method.
- **ConcreteCreator:** Overrides the factory method to instantiate and return one specific ConcreteProduct.

---

#### (b) Model Answer (7 Marks — Allocation: 3+4)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Factory Method | Simple Factory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pattern Recognition | GoF-recognised | Idiomatic (not in GoF) |
| Mechanism | Inheritance + Polymorphism | Composition (single class) |
| Extension | Add new ConcreteCreator | Modify factory's switch-case |
| OCP Compliance | High | Violated |
| Class Count | More (parallel hierarchy) | Fewer |

**Java Real-World Example (4 marks) — Document Editor:**

```java
// Product
interface Document {
    void open();
    void save();
}

// ConcreteProducts
class PdfDocument implements Document {
    public void open() { System.out.println("Opening PDF"); }
    public void save() { System.out.println("Saving PDF"); }
}

class WordDocument implements Document {
    public void open() { System.out.println("Opening Word doc"); }
    public void save() { System.out.println("Saving Word doc"); }
}

// Creator
abstract class Application {
    public abstract Document createDocument();   // factory method

    public void newDocument() {
        Document doc = createDocument();
        doc.open();
    }
}

// ConcreteCreators
class PdfApplication extends Application {
    public Document createDocument() { return new PdfDocument(); }
}

class WordApplication extends Application {
    public Document createDocument() { return new WordDocument(); }
}
```

The client uses `Application` and `Document` interfaces — never `new PdfDocument()` directly. This is the canonical use of Factory Method in **document-editing frameworks** (1 mark for interface, 1 mark for each concrete class, 1 mark for Creator, 1 mark for ConcreteCreators).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Alternative]`**

**Q: (a)** List the applicability scenarios of the Factory Method pattern. Explain any three in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company needs to deliver goods either by **Truck** or **Ship**. The decision depends on the delivery mode chosen at runtime. Design a Factory Method solution with a complete Java implementation and explain how the **Open/Closed Principle** is satisfied. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Answer (7 Marks — Allocation: 1+2+2+2)

**List of Applicability (1 mark — full list required):**
The pattern applies when:
1. A class cannot anticipate the class of objects it must create.
2. A class wants its subclasses to specify the objects it creates.
3. Classes delegate responsibility to one of several helper subclasses, and you want to localise the knowledge of which helper subclass is the delegate.
4. You need to provide a library of classes but want to reveal only their interfaces, not their implementations.

**Detailed Explanation of Any Three (2 marks each):**

*Scenario 1 (Class cannot anticipate the class):* A document editor framework supports multiple file formats (`.doc`, `.pdf`, `.xls`). The framework cannot hard-code the document type at compile time. The Factory Method allows it to delegate document creation to format-specific subclasses (e.g., `WordApplication`, `PdfApplication`).

*Scenario 2 (Subclasses specify objects):* A `LogisticsApp` abstract class declares `createTransport()`. The `RoadLogisticsApp` subclass returns a `Truck`, while `SeaLogisticsApp` returns a `Ship`. The framework's high-level code remains unchanged, and the subclass makes the concrete choice.

*Scenario 3 (Localising knowledge of the delegate):* Consider parallel hierarchies — UI widgets (`Button`, `Checkbox`) and their platform-specific factories (`WindowsButton`, `MacButton`). The Factory Method ensures that adding a new widget (e.g., `RadioButton`) requires only a matching new creator, with no changes to the existing system.

---

#### (b) Model Answer (7 Marks — Allocation: 1+5+1)

**Design Intent (1 mark):**
The logistics company requires transport planning that is decoupled from specific transport types. New transport modes (Air, Rail) must be addable without modifying the existing application logic.

**Complete Java Implementation (5 marks):**

```java
// Product
interface Transport {
    void deliver();
}

// ConcreteProducts
class Truck implements Transport {
    public void deliver() { System.out.println("Truck delivery on road."); }
}
class Ship implements Transport {
    public void deliver() { System.out.println("Ship delivery on sea."); }
}

// Creator
abstract class LogisticsApp {
    public abstract Transport createTransport();
    public void planDelivery() {
        Transport t = createTransport();
        t.deliver();
    }
}

// ConcreteCreators
class RoadLogistics extends LogisticsApp {
    public Transport createTransport() { return new Truck(); }
}
class SeaLogistics extends LogisticsApp {
    public Transport createTransport() { return new Ship(); }
}

// Client
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LogisticsApp app = chooseApp("road");
        app.planDelivery();
    }
    static LogisticsApp chooseApp(String mode) {
        if (mode.equals("sea")) return new SeaLogistics();
        return new RoadLogistics();
    }
}
```

**Open/Closed Principle Justification (1 mark):**
To add a new `AirLogistics` (with `Plane` as ConcreteProduct), the developer only adds a new ConcreteCreator and a new ConcreteProduct. **No existing Creator, ConcreteCreator, Product, or ConcreteProduct class is modified.** The system is *open for extension* (new classes) and *closed for modification* (existing code untouched), satisfying OCP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare the factory method as `abstract`** in the Creator — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Using `new ConcreteProduct()` inside the Creator** instead of the factory method — this violates the pattern entirely; KTU examiners will deduct 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Factory Method with Simple Factory** — if the question asks for Factory Method, do not return a single `Factory` class with a `switch-case`. The GoF version **must use inheritance** and a polymorphic factory method.
> 4. **In UML, using the wrong arrow** — solid line for inheritance, dashed line for dependency, hollow triangle for *realisation* (interface implementation). Wrong arrows cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the `Creator`'s `anOperation()` (service method)** — the service method that uses the factory method is essential; without it, the pattern is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Category:** Creational GoF pattern; also called *Virtual Constructor*.
- **Core Idea:** Define an interface for object creation, but let **subclasses decide** which class to instantiate.
- **Participants (4):** `Product`, `ConcreteProduct`, `Creator` (with abstract `factoryMethod()`), `ConcreteCreator` (overrides `factoryMethod()`).
- **Key Mechanism:** **Polymorphism via inheritance** — the abstract factory method is overridden in subclasses to return a specific ConcreteProduct.
- **SOLID Mapping:** Primarily the **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** and **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**.
- **Distinguishing from Abstract Factory:** Factory Method creates **one** product per creator using inheritance; Abstract Factory creates **families** of related products using composition.
- **Distinguishing from Simple Factory:** Simple Factory is a single class with a `switch-case` (not GoF); Factory Method is inheritance-based and GoF-recognised.
- **Hollywood Principle:** "Don't call us, we'll call you" — the Creator calls the subclass's factory method; the client does not call subclasses directly.
- **Common Real-World Use Cases:** `Document` editors (Word/PDF), `LoggerFactory` (SLF4J), `DriverManager.getConnection()` (JDBC), `Toolkit.createButton()` (Java AWT), `BeanFactory` (Spring).
- **UML Notation Essentials:** Hollow triangle → inheritance/realisation; dashed arrow → dependency; solid line → association.
- **Exam Favourite Sub-Questions:** List participants, draw UML diagram, compare with Abstract Factory, justify OCP/DIP, write Java code, real-world example.
- **One-line Signature for Exams:** *"A creational pattern that encapsulates object creation behind an abstract method overridden by subclasses, decoupling the client from concrete product classes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Abstract Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstract Factory Pattern** is a *creational design pattern* that provides an **interface for creating families of related or dependent objects** without specifying their **concrete classes**. It is also known as **"Kit"** in the original GoF (Gang of Four) classification, and is frequently termed the *Factory of Factories*.

The pattern falls under **Creational Patterns** in the GoF catalog, alongside Factory Method, Builder, Prototype, and Singleton. It encapsulates a group of individual concrete factories under a common super-factory abstraction. The client code interacts **only with the abstract interfaces** of both the factory and the products — never with the concrete implementations.

| KTU 2024 Terminology | Definition |
|---|---|
| **AbstractFactory** | Declares the interface for operations that create abstract product objects. |
| **ConcreteFactory** | Implements the AbstractFactory interface to produce concrete product families. |
| **AbstractProduct** | Declares an interface for a type of product object (e.g., `Button`, `Checkbox`). |
| **ConcreteProduct** | Defines a product object to be created by the corresponding ConcreteFactory. |
| **Client** | Uses only the interfaces declared by AbstractFactory and AbstractProduct. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Themed Furniture Showroom":**
> Imagine you walk into a furniture showroom. You want a complete living-room set — a **Sofa**, a **Coffee Table**, and a **TV Unit**. You don't care *how* they're built; you just want all pieces to match a single style.

- If you say *"I want the **Modern** style"*, the showroom gives you a Modern Sofa, a Modern Coffee Table, and a Modern TV Unit (all designed by a single designer team).
- If you say *"I want the **Classic Victorian** style"*, you get the Victorian set, with the same three product types but a different look.

Here, the **showroom** is the *Abstract Factory*, the **two showrooms (Modern & Victorian)** are *Concrete Factories*, and the **Sofa/Table/TVUnit** are *Abstract Products*, with their specific styles being *Concrete Products*. The customer (Client) never has to know which wood, which fabric, or which carpenter is used — the family is **guaranteed to be stylistically consistent**.

> [!TIP]
> **Another easy analogy:** A GUI toolkit that must work on both **Windows** and **macOS**. The "Windows Factory" creates Windows-style buttons, checkboxes, and scrollbars. The "Mac Factory" creates Mac-style equivalents. You swap one factory, and the entire UI family swaps — automatically consistent.

---

## 1.3 Core Intent and Applicability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Intent:** Isolate the construction of a *family* of related objects so that a client can work with abstract interfaces and remain independent of the actual product implementations.

**Use the Abstract Factory Pattern when:**

1. The system must be **independent of how its products are created, composed, and represented**.
2. The system must be configured with **one of multiple families of products**.
3. A family of related product objects is designed to be used together, and this constraint must be enforced.
4. You want to provide a **class library of products**, exposing only their interfaces (not implementations).

The pattern is **NOT recommended** when:

- You only have a **single product family** to create → use Simple Factory or Factory Method instead.
- The product hierarchy is **unstable and changes frequently** → because every new variant requires extending both product classes and factory classes (a known scalability trade-off).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Pattern

The Abstract Factory pattern can be decomposed into **five primary participants** that work in cooperation:

### 2.1.1 The Five Participants Explained

> [!NOTE]
> Each participant has a well-defined role. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can correctly identify these roles from a class diagram.

1. **AbstractFactory (The Master Contract)**
   - Declares an interface containing one **factory method per product family member**.
   - In UML, this is an **abstract class** or **interface** with `<<abstract>>` or `<<interface>>` stereotype.
   - Does *not* contain any object construction logic itself — it only declares the signatures.

2. **ConcreteFactory (The Specialist Builder)**
   - Implements the AbstractFactory interface.
   - Each ConcreteFactory corresponds to **one specific product family** (e.g., `WinFactory`, `MacFactory`).
   - Responsible for instantiating the matching set of ConcreteProducts.

3. **AbstractProduct (The Product Contract)**
   - Declares an interface for each distinct *kind* of product.
   - In the furniture analogy: `AbstractSofa`, `AbstractCoffeeTable`, `AbstractTVUnit`.

4. **ConcreteProduct (The Real Object)**
   - A specific implementation of an AbstractProduct, created by one specific ConcreteFactory.
   - Example: `VictorianSofa implements AbstractSofa`.

5. **Client (The User)**
   - Holds a reference to an **AbstractFactory** and an array of **AbstractProduct** references.
   - The Client **never** knows which ConcreteFactory or ConcreteProduct it is using → the famous "depend only on abstractions" benefit of Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP).

### 2.1.2 Logical Flow Steps (How the Pattern Operates)

The execution lifecycle of an Abstract Factory can be summarized in **five operational steps**:

- **Step 1 → Client instantiation:** A Client object is created. It only has a reference of type `AbstractFactory`.
- **Step 2 → Concrete Factory selection:** At runtime (or configuration time), a specific `ConcreteFactory` instance is injected into the Client.
- **Step 3 → Product creation requests:** The Client calls factory methods on its `AbstractFactory` reference. Because of polymorphism, the ConcreteFactory's overrides execute.
- **Step 4 → Product family consistency:** Each method on the ConcreteFactory produces a ConcreteProduct that belongs to the *same family*. The family is automatically consistent.
- **Step 5 → Client uses products:** The Client treats the returned objects as `AbstractProduct` instances and operates on them through the abstract interface.

### 2.1.3 Why and How It Works (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Step 2 matters:** The selection of ConcreteFactory is the *single point of decision* for the entire product family. Changing it changes the whole UI / product set at once.
>
> **Why Step 4 matters:** This is the unique selling point of Abstract Factory over a plain Factory Method. **Cross-family consistency is enforced by construction** — the ConcreteFactory is the *only* object with permission to create its family's products.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Abstract Factory pattern is *qualitative* (design pattern, not mathematical), so the "equations" below are the **definitional relationships and constraints** between participants. These are the high-yield items KTU examiners look for.

| Concept / Symbol | Definition / Relationship | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of product families (e.g., Modern, Victorian) | $N \geq 1$ |
| $M$ | Number of distinct product types in a family (e.g., Sofa, Table) | $M \geq 1$ |
| $T_c$ | Total ConcreteProduct classes | $T_c = N \times M$ |
| $T_f$ | Total ConcreteFactory classes | $T_f = N$ |
| $I_{af}$ | AbstractFactory interface methods (one per product) | $I_{af} = M$ |
| $I_{ap}$ | AbstractProduct interfaces | $I_{ap} = M$ |
| Cohesion rule | All products from one ConcreteFactory belong to the same family | **Mandatory** |
| DIP rule | Client depends on abstractions, not concretions | **Mandatory** |
| OCP rule | Add new families by adding a new ConcreteFactory | **Preserved** |
| Liskov rule | ConcreteProduct must honor all AbstractProduct contracts | **Preserved** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Do not confuse Abstract Factory with **Factory Method**. Factory Method creates *one product*; Abstract Factory creates a *family of products*. The relationship is that Abstract Factory uses *multiple Factory Methods internally*.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

The pattern is heavily used in:

- **GUI Toolkits:** Java AWT/Swing, Windows Presentation Foundation (WPF) Themes.
- **Cross-Platform Frameworks:** Qt, GTK, Flutter (Material vs Cupertino widgets).
- **Database Access Layers:** Abstracting SQL Server vs Oracle vs PostgreSQL dialects.
- **Game Development:** Generating Orc, Elf, and Human factions with matching buildings/units (classic "Race" factory).
- **Cloud SDKs:** AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage as a "Cloud Storage" family.
- **Document Generation:** PDF, DOCX, HTML output strategies with consistent styling.

The engineering value lies in **enforcing consistency**, **supporting product portability**, and **decoupling high-level policy from low-level mechanism** — a direct application of the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** from SOLID.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — "Cross-Platform UI Toolkit"

To make the pattern fully concrete, this section walks through a complete, exam-ready example. Imagine we are building a cross-platform UI toolkit that must render `Button`, `Checkbox`, and `ScrollBar` consistently on **Windows** and **macOS**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the AbstractProduct Interfaces

The *three* abstract product interfaces must be declared first. Each one captures a single product type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is Step 1:** The product contracts are the *vocabulary* of the family. The factory's signatures must match these contracts exactly, so we define products first.

Interface `Button` declares `paint()`. Interface `Checkbox` declares `paint()`. Interface `ScrollBar` declares `paint()`. Each interface uses an abstract operation whose implementation will differ between families.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define the AbstractFactory Interface

The AbstractFactory must declare exactly **one factory method per AbstractProduct type**.

$$I_{af} = M = 3$$

In other words, for $M=3$ product types, the AbstractFactory exposes exactly $3$ creation methods. For our example:

- `createButton() : Button`
- `createCheckbox() : Checkbox`
- `createScrollBar() : ScrollBar`

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Implement the ConcreteProducts (Per Family)

For the **Windows family**, we instantiate:

- `WinButton implements Button` → `paint()` renders a Win32-style button.
- `WinCheckbox implements Checkbox` → `paint()` renders a Win32-style checkbox.
- `WinScrollBar implements ScrollBar` → `paint()` renders a Win32-style scrollbar.

For the **macOS family**, we instantiate:

- `MacButton implements Button` → `paint()` renders a Cocoa-style button.
- `MacCheckbox implements Checkbox` → `paint()` renders a Cocoa-style checkbox.
- `MacScrollBar implements ScrollBar` → `paint()` renders a Cocoa-style scrollbar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application of the formula:** With $N=2$ families and $M=3$ product types, the total number of ConcreteProduct classes is computed as:

$$T_c = N \times M = 2 \times 3 = 6$$

This matches our list above — six concrete classes, perfectly aligned with the formula.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Implement the ConcreteFactory Classes

Each ConcreteFactory **must guarantee that all three of its products belong to the same family**. The WinFactory only ever creates `Win*` products; the MacFactory only ever creates `Mac*` products. This is the **consistency invariant** of the pattern.

For each ConcreteFactory $f_i$ where $i \in \lbrace 1, 2 \rbrace$:

$$f_i.\text{createButton}() \rightarrow \text{ProductFamily}_i.\text{Button}$$

$$f_i.\text{createCheckbox}() \rightarrow \text{ProductFamily}_i.\text{Checkbox}$$

$$f_i.\text{createScrollBar}() \rightarrow \text{ProductFamily}_i.\text{ScrollBar}$$

Where $\text{ProductFamily}_i$ is a **closed set** of products. No method on $f_1$ (WinFactory) is permitted to return a product from $f_2$ (MacFactory). This is what the KTU examiner means by *"family consistency is enforced by construction."*

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — The Client Class

The Client class depends **only** on `AbstractFactory`, `Button`, `Checkbox`, and `ScrollBar`. It has no `import` or `reference` to any `Win*` or `Mac*` class.

The Client:

1. Receives an `AbstractFactory` reference (typically via constructor injection).
2. Calls the three factory methods.
3. Invokes `paint()` on each returned product.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Runtime Selection (Configuration)

> [!NOTE]
> The Client never decides *which* factory to use. A higher-level configuration or factory-of-factories creator (sometimes called the **Factory Selector** or **Configuration Module**) instantiates the right ConcreteFactory and injects it.

A typical selector is written as:

$$\text{AbstractFactory} = \text{Configuration.getFactory}(\text{platform})$$

Where `platform` is read from an external source (environment variable, config file, OS detection, etc.). This single line is the *only* place where a concrete class name appears in the application code.

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Adding a New Family (Demonstrating OCP)

Suppose we now need to support **Linux GTK**. Per the **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**, the existing code should **not** be modified.

- Add three new ConcreteProducts: `LinuxButton`, `LinuxCheckbox`, `LinuxScrollBar`.
- Add one new ConcreteFactory: `LinuxFactory implements AbstractFactory`.
- Update the configuration selector.

The total becomes:

$$T_c = N \times M = 3 \times 3 = 9 \quad \text{(ConcreteProducts)}$$

$$T_f = N = 3 \quad \text{(ConcreteFactories)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for Examiners:** Notice that adding a new product *type* (e.g., a new `Slider` widget) requires modifying the AbstractFactory interface and *all* ConcreteFactories. This is the **principal trade-off** of Abstract Factory and is the classic KTU "analyze the consequences" question.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Factory Method vs Abstract Factory)

This matrix is one of the most frequently asked comparison topics in KTU ESE.

| Criterion | Factory Method | Abstract Factory |
|---|---|---|
| Product scope | Single product type | Family of related products |
| Pattern nature | Single factory method | Multiple factory methods, grouped under one interface |
| Inheritance dependency | Subclass decides the product | Composition of factories selects the family |
| Number of creator classes | One per product variant | One per product *family* |
| Adding new variants | Subclass the Creator | Add a new ConcreteFactory |
| Adding new product types | Modify the Creator hierarchy | Modify AbstractFactory + all ConcreteFactories |
| Pattern relationship | Building block of Abstract Factory | Composes multiple Factory Methods |
| Open/Closed support | Good for new product variants | Good for new families, weak for new product types |
| DIP compliance | Client depends on Creator | Client depends on AbstractFactory + AbstractProducts |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for exams:** *Factory Method = "one product, multiple variants." Abstract Factory = "many products, multiple variant-sets."*
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Abstract Factory Structure

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid class diagram mirrors the canonical GoF Abstract Factory structure. Node IDs follow the Alpha Rule (purely alphanumeric with letter prefix). Labels are raw, uppercase alphanumeric text to ensure clean Mermaid rendering.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class AbsFactory {
        <<interface>>
        +createButton() AbsButton
        +createCheckbox() AbsCheckbox
        +createScrollBar() AbsScrollBar
    }

    class WinFactory {
        +createButton() AbsButton
        +createCheckbox() AbsCheckbox
        +createScrollBar() AbsScrollBar
    }

    class MacFactory {
        +createButton() AbsButton
        +createCheckbox() AbsCheckbox
        +createScrollBar() AbsScrollBar
    }

    class LinuxFactory {
        +createButton() AbsButton
        +createCheckbox() AbsCheckbox
        +createScrollBar() AbsScrollBar
    }

    class AbsButton {
        <<interface>>
        +paint() void
    }

    class AbsCheckbox {
        <<interface>>
        +paint() void
    }

    class AbsScrollBar {
        <<interface>>
        +paint() void
    }

    class WinButton {
        +paint() void
    }

    class WinCheckbox {
        +paint() void
    }

    class WinScrollBar {
        +paint() void
    }

    class MacButton {
        +paint() void
    }

    class MacCheckbox {
        +paint() void
    }

    class MacScrollBar {
        +paint() void
    }

    class LinuxButton {
        +paint() void
    }

    class LinuxCheckbox {
        +paint() void
    }

    class LinuxScrollBar {
        +paint() void
    }

    class ClientGUI {
        -factory AbsFactory
        -button AbsButton
        -checkbox AbsCheckbox
        -scroll AbsScrollBar
        +render() void
    }

    AbsFactory <|.. WinFactory
    AbsFactory <|.. MacFactory
    AbsFactory <|.. LinuxFactory

    AbsButton <|.. WinButton
    AbsButton <|.. MacButton
    AbsButton <|.. LinuxButton

    AbsCheckbox <|.. WinCheckbox
    AbsCheckbox <|.. MacCheckbox
    AbsCheckbox <|.. LinuxCheckbox

    AbsScrollBar <|.. WinScrollBar
    AbsScrollBar <|.. MacScrollBar
    AbsScrollBar <|.. LinuxScrollBar

    WinFactory ..> WinButton : creates
    WinFactory ..> WinCheckbox : creates
    WinFactory ..> WinScrollBar : creates

    MacFactory ..> MacButton : creates
    MacFactory ..> MacCheckbox : creates
    MacFactory ..> MacScrollBar : creates

    LinuxFactory ..> LinuxButton : creates
    LinuxFactory ..> LinuxCheckbox : creates
    LinuxFactory ..> LinuxScrollBar : creates

    ClientGUI --> AbsFactory : uses
    ClientGUI --> AbsButton : uses
    ClientGUI --> AbsCheckbox : uses
    ClientGUI --> AbsScrollBar : uses
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Runtime Behavior of Abstract Factory

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CFG as Configuration
    participant CLI as ClientGUI
    participant AFX as AbsFactory
    participant WFX as WinFactory
    participant WBT as WinButton
    participant WCB as WinCheckbox
    participant WSB as WinScrollBar

    CFG->>CLI: inject WinFactory (as AbsFactory)
    CLI->>AFX: createButton()
    AFX->>WFX: createButton()
    WFX->>WBT: new WinButton()
    WBT-->>WFX: return WinButton
    WFX-->>AFX: return as AbsButton
    AFX-->>CLI: return AbsButton

    CLI->>AFX: createCheckbox()
    AFX->>WFX: createCheckbox()
    WFX->>WCB: new WinCheckbox()
    WCB-->>WFX: return WinCheckbox
    WFX-->>AFX: return as AbsCheckbox
    AFX-->>CLI: return AbsCheckbox

    CLI->>AFX: createScrollBar()
    AFX->>WFX: createScrollBar()
    WFX->>WSB: new WinScrollBar()
    WSB-->>WFX: return WinScrollBar
    WFX-->>AFX: return as AbsScrollBar
    AFX-->>CLI: return AbsScrollBar

    CLI->>WBT: paint()
    CLI->>WCB: paint()
    CLI->>WSB: paint()
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are KTU Part A (2-mark conceptual) and Part B (3-mark short) style questions. Each is followed by a precise model answer.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define the Abstract Factory Pattern. List its primary participants.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Abstract Factory Pattern is a *creational design pattern* that provides an *interface for creating families of related or dependent objects* without specifying their concrete classes. It is sometimes called a *Kit* or *Factory of Factories*.
>
> **Participants (1 mark):** The five primary participants are:
> 1. **AbstractFactory** — declares the creation interface.
> 2. **ConcreteFactory** — implements the AbstractFactory to produce a specific family.
> 3. **AbstractProduct** — declares the interface for a product type.
> 4. **ConcreteProduct** — the actual product implementation.
> 5. **Client** — uses only the abstract interfaces.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**State two situations in which the Abstract Factory Pattern is most suitable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **(1.5 Marks)** When the system needs to be **independent of how its products are created, composed, and represented** — clients should not depend on concrete product classes.
2. **(1.5 Marks)** When the system must be **configured with one of multiple families of products**, and the constraint of using related products together must be enforced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer a 30% internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Style]

**(a)** With a suitable UML class diagram, explain the structure of the **Abstract Factory Design Pattern**. Label all participants clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **consequences and trade-offs** of using the Abstract Factory Pattern. Mention at least four consequences. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ⤷ Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Abstract Factory Pattern provides an interface for creating *families* of related or dependent objects. The class diagram consists of *one* AbstractFactory, *N* ConcreteFactories, *M* AbstractProducts, and *N × M* ConcreteProducts.

**Step 2 — Participants Description (3 Marks):**
- **AbstractFactory:** declares `createProductA()` and `createProductB()` methods.
- **ConcreteFactory1, ConcreteFactory2:** implement the AbstractFactory; each returns a specific family.
- **AbstractProductA, AbstractProductB:** declare the product interfaces.
- **ProductA1, ProductA2, ProductB1, ProductB2:** concrete product implementations.
- **Client:** uses only AbstractFactory and AbstractProduct interfaces.

**Step 3 — UML Class Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class AbsFactory {
        <<interface>>
        +createProductA() AbsProductA
        +createProductB() AbsProductB
    }
    class ConcreteFactory1 {
        +createProductA() AbsProductA
        +createProductB() AbsProductB
    }
    class ConcreteFactory2 {
        +createProductA() AbsProductA
        +createProductB() AbsProductB
    }
    class AbsProductA {
        <<interface>>
        +operationA() void
    }
    class AbsProductB {
        <<interface>>
        +operationB() void
    }
    class ProductA1 {
        +operationA() void
    }
    class ProductA2 {
        +operationA() void
    }
    class ProductB1 {
        +operationB() void
    }
    class ProductB2 {
        +operationB() void
    }
    AbsFactory <|.. ConcreteFactory1
    AbsFactory <|.. ConcreteFactory2
    AbsProductA <|.. ProductA1
    AbsProductA <|.. ProductA2
    AbsProductB <|.. ProductB1
    AbsProductB <|.. ProductB2
    ConcreteFactory1 ..> ProductA1
    ConcreteFactory1 ..> ProductB1
    ConcreteFactory2 ..> ProductA2
    ConcreteFactory2 ..> ProductB2
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of Relationships (1 Mark):**
The dashed arrow indicates *realization* (interface implementation), and the dotted arrow indicates *dependency/creation*. The Client (not shown) holds an `AbsFactory` reference and uses only `AbsProductA` and `AbsProductB` interfaces.

---

#### ⤷ Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Consequences of the Abstract Factory Pattern (7 Marks — at least four expected):**

1. **(1.5 Marks) It isolates concrete classes.** The Client is decoupled from the concrete product families, satisfying the **Dependency Inversion Principle**.

2. **(1.5 Marks) It makes exchanging product families easy.** A single line that changes the ConcreteFactory instance swaps the entire family of products, which is excellent for portability across platforms.

3. **(1.5 Marks) It promotes consistency among products.** Products from one factory are designed to work together; the pattern enforces this at the class level.

4. **(1.5 Marks) It supports the Open/Closed Principle for new families.** Adding a new product family requires adding a new ConcreteFactory and its products, with **no modification to existing client code**.

5. **(1 Mark) Trade-off: Difficulty in adding new product types.** Adding a new product requires modifying the AbstractFactory interface and *every* ConcreteFactory, which violates OCP in the *product-type* dimension.

6. **(Optional — 0.5 Mark bonus)** Increased class-count overhead; the pattern introduces many small classes and may be excessive for simple scenarios.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **difference between Factory Method and Abstract Factory Pattern** with a suitable example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle manufacturing company wants to build a system that produces **electric vehicles** and **petrol vehicles**, each having `Car` and `Bike` as two product types. Design an **Abstract Factory** solution with a complete UML class diagram and explain the role of the Client. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ⤷ Model Solution for Q.B (a)

| Aspect | Factory Method (1.5) | Abstract Factory (1.5) |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern goal | Define an interface for creating **one** product, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate. | Provide an interface for creating **families of related products** without specifying concrete classes. |
| Number of products | One product type per Creator | Multiple product types per factory |
