- **Throwaway** prototype → Final system is built **from scratch**.
- **Evolutionary** prototype → Final system **is** the refined prototype.

### Convergence Criterion
- Iteration stops when: $\lVert P_{n+1} - P_n \rVert \leq \varepsilon$
- Or simply: when the user is **satisfied** and the prototype accurately represents the final system.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If a 14-mark KTU question asks to "explain the prototype model with a diagram," always structure your answer as: **Definition → Diagram → 7–8 Phase Explanation → 4–5 Advantages → 4–5 Disadvantages → Comparison with one other model → Conclusion.** This structure is exactly what KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Builder Method
Structural Design Pattern and Its Types - Adapter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Design Pattern and Its Types - Adapter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Gang of Four - GoF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adapter Pattern (Gang of Four Definition):**
> *"Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect. Adapter lets classes work together that couldn't otherwise because of incompatible interfaces."*
> — Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides (1994)

The **Adapter Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** that acts as a **bridge between two incompatible interfaces**. It wraps an existing class (the *Adaptee*) with a new interface (the *Target*) so that a client can use it without modifying its original source code.

In the context of **UML modelling (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the Adapter is represented as a structural solution where:
- A **Target** interface is what the **Client** expects.
- An **Adaptee** class already exists with useful behavior but a mismatched interface.
- The **Adapter** class implements the Target interface and internally delegates calls to the Adaptee.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🔌 The Power Adapter Analogy (Universally understood by every engineering student):**

Imagine you bought a laptop in the **USA** (Type-A flat-pin plug). When you travel to **India** or **Europe**, the wall socket expects a **round-pin** plug. The laptop and the socket are both functional — but their **physical interface is incompatible**.

You do NOT:
- ❌ Rewire the laptop's internal circuitry
- ❌ Replace the Indian wall socket

You simply use a **travel power adapter** — a small device that:
- Implements the **Target interface** (Indian plug shape, fitting the Indian wall socket).
- Internally holds a reference to the **Adaptee** (the USA plug coming from the laptop).
- **Translates** the request (passes electricity through safely).

**That travel adapter is the Adapter Pattern in real life.**

| Real-World Object | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Laptop (US Plug) | **Adaptee** (existing class with useful behavior) |
| Indian Wall Socket | **Target** (interface the client expects) |
| Travel Adapter | **Adapter** (the bridging class) |
| You plugging in the laptop | **Client** (calling code) |

---

## 1.3 Classification within Design Patterns

The Adapter Pattern belongs to the **Structural Patterns** category of the GoF (Gang of Four) classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Structural Design Patterns (GoF Catalog):**
> Structural patterns are concerned with **how classes and objects are composed** to form larger structures. They simplify the structure by identifying relationships between entities.
>
> The seven Structural Patterns (in KTU 2024 syllabus scope) are:
> 1. **Adapter** (this module)
> 2. **Bridge**
> 3. **Composite**
> 4. **Decorator**
> 5. **Facade**
> 6. **Proxy**
> 7. **Flyweight**

The word **Structural** itself emphasizes the focus on **composition and relationships** between classes — not on object creation (Creational) or communication (Behavioral).

---

## 1.4 Why Adapter is Needed — The Core Problem Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Incompatibility Problem:**
> In large software systems, you often need to integrate **legacy code, third-party libraries, or external APIs** that have:
> - Different method names
> - Different parameter types
> - Different return types
> - Different data formats
>
> **without** modifying the existing (often closed-source) class. Adapter solves this by providing a **compatibility layer**.

Without an Adapter, the developer would be forced to either:
- Modify the Adaptee's source code (often impossible for libraries/APIs).
- Write wrapper code scattered throughout the client (code duplication, tight coupling).
- Refactor the client to match the new interface (high risk, high cost).

The Adapter pattern **decouples** the client from the adaptee, allowing both to evolve independently.

---

## 1.5 Two Architectural Variants of Adapter

The GoF catalog officially defines **two structural variants** of the Adapter pattern. This is a **high-yield KTU question area** — examiners frequently test the difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant 1 — Object Adapter (Composition-based):**
> The Adapter **holds a reference** (object pointer) to the Adaptee. This uses **object composition** (HAS-A relationship). **Preferred in modern object-oriented design.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant 2 — Class Adapter (Inheritance-based):**
> The Adapter **inherits from both** the Target (interface) and the Adaptee (class) using **multiple inheritance**. This is only possible in languages that support multiple inheritance (e.g., C++). **Not directly possible in Java/C# (single inheritance).**

---

## 1.6 KTU Syllabus Context

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| Module | **Module 4 — Structural Design Patterns** |
| Pattern Type | **Object Structural** (Object Adapter) / **Class Structural** (Class Adapter) |
| UML Diagrams Used | **Class Diagram**, **Sequence Diagram**, **Object Diagram** |
| Design Principles Applied | **Composition over Inheritance**, **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** |
| Typical Exam Weightage | 8–14 marks (frequently combined with Bridge or Decorator for 14-mark questions) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Participants of the Adapter Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GoF specification identifies **five** key participants (four mandatory + one optional). Every KTU answer must enumerate these.

| # | Participant | Role | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Target** | The domain-specific interface that the **Client** expects to use. Usually a Java `interface` or C++ abstract class. | ✅ Mandatory |
| 2 | **Client** | The class/object that wants to use the Adaptee's functionality but only knows the Target interface. | ✅ Mandatory |
| 3 | **Adaptee** | The **existing class** with a useful behavior but an **incompatible interface**. Cannot be modified (legacy/3rd party). | ✅ Mandatory |
| 4 | **Adapter** | The **bridge class** that implements Target and wraps the Adaptee. Translates Target calls into Adaptee calls. | ✅ Mandatory |
| 5 | **(Optional) ConcreteAdapter** | A specialized adapter for a particular Adaptee instance (used in multiple-adaptee scenarios). | ⚪ Optional |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **How the Adapter Pattern Operates Internally (Why and How):**

**Step 1 — Identification of Incompatibility**
- The Client is coded against an interface called `Target`.
- The legacy class `Adaptee` has the required behavior but exposes method `specificRequest()`.
- `Client` expects method `request()` instead.
- **Why:** The system architect identifies a reuse opportunity, not a redesign opportunity.

**Step 2 — Definition of Target Interface**
- An abstract `Target` interface is declared with the method(s) the Client calls.
- **Why:** Establishes the contract the Adapter must honor.

**Step 3 — Adapter Implements Target**
- A new class `Adapter` is created that `implements Target`.
- **Why:** The Adapter now becomes a valid Target from the Client's perspective.

**Step 4 — Adapter References Adaptee (Composition)**
- The `Adapter` class holds a private field `adaptee: Adaptee`.
- **Why:** Composition over Inheritance — keeps coupling loose and allows runtime swapping of adaptees.

**Step 5 — Translation of Calls**
- Inside `Adapter.request()`, the call is **translated** and **delegated** to `adaptee.specificRequest()`.
- Parameter and return-type conversions happen here.
- **Why:** The Adapter is the only place that knows the impedance mismatch exists. Client is blissfully unaware.

**Step 6 — Client Uses Adapter Unaware**
- The Client is given an `Adapter` instance (polymorphically as a `Target`).
- The Client calls `target.request()` and gets the expected behavior.
- **Why:** Client code never changes. **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** is honored.

---

## 2.3 Object Adapter vs. Class Adapter — Definitive Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is the most frequently tested 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE for this topic.** Memorize the structural difference (one uses a **diamond** in UML, the other uses simple inheritance arrows).

| Property | **Object Adapter** | **Class Adapter** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Composition (HAS-A) | Inheritance (IS-A) × 2 (multiple inheritance) |
| **UML Notation** | Adapter ──▷ Adaptee (association) | Adapter ──▷ Target, Adapter ──▷ Adaptee (both as **generalization** arrows) |
| **Language Support** | All OO languages (Java, C#, C++, Python) | Only C++, Python (multiple inheritance) |
| **Flexibility** | Can wrap **any** Adaptee subclass at runtime | Locked to a **specific** Adaptee class at compile time |
| **Adaptee Subclass Access** | Indirect (must go through Adaptee's public API) | Direct (protected/public members of Adaptee accessible) |
| **Number of Adaptees Supported** | Can adapt **multiple** Adaptees in one Adapter | Adapts **exactly one** Adaptee hierarchy |
| **Design Principle Followed** | Composition over Inheritance (favored by modern OOP) | Relies on inheritance (less flexible) |
| **Reusability of Adaptee** | High — Adaptee is just a referenced object | Lower — Adapter is tightly bound to Adaptee's class |
| **When to Use** | Default choice. When Adaptee is a concrete class or interface, when there are many Adaptees. | When you need to **override Adaptee's behavior** and language supports multiple inheritance. |
| **Preferred by KTU Examiners** | ✅ **Yes** (modern, GoF-recommended) | ⚠️ Mentioned for completeness only |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula" for an Adapter pattern in UML is essentially the **multiplicity and role constraints** of the four relationships in the structure diagram.

| # | Relationship | UML Notation | Multiplicity | Visibility | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Target | Dependency (dashed arrow) | 1 | private | `target` |
| 2 | Adapter ⬢ Target | Realization (dashed triangle) | 1 | public | — |
| 3 | Adapter → Adaptee | Association (solid line) | 1 (Object Adapter only) | private | `adaptee` |
| 4 | Adapter ──▷ Adaptee | Generalization (solid triangle) | — (Class Adapter only) | public | — |
| 5 | Adapter ⇢ Client | Inheritance/Association | — | — | creates Adapter |

**Design-Principle Mapping (high-yield for KTU):**

| Design Principle | How Adapter Honors It |
|---|---|
| **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** | Adaptee and Client are closed for modification but open for extension. |
| **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** | Adapter has ONE job — translate calls. |
| **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** | Client depends on Target abstraction, not concrete Adaptee. |
| **Composition over Inheritance** | Object Adapter uses HAS-A, not IS-A. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Adapter is used in real software engineering:**

1. **JDBC Drivers in Java** — `java.sql.DriverManager` uses adapter-like wrappers so applications can talk to MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL with the same `Connection` interface.
2. **Wrapper Classes in Java** — `Integer`, `Double`, `Boolean` adapt primitive `int`, `double`, `boolean` to be used where `Object` is required (Object adapters).
3. **Legacy System Integration** — Mainframe systems wrapped with Adapter classes to expose modern REST/JSON interfaces.
4. **Third-party SDK Integration** — Vendor libraries (e.g., payment gateways like Stripe, PayPal) are integrated via Adapters to match the application's internal `PaymentProcessor` interface.
5. **Testing & Mocking** — `Mockito` and similar frameworks use Adapter-like proxies to substitute real services in unit tests.
6. **Android Development** — `RecyclerView.Adapter` adapts heterogeneous data lists to displayable UI views.

**Industrial Use Case (Embedded Systems + UML modelling for KTU):**
Consider a sensor module that produces readings in `Celsius` but the monitoring system expects `Fahrenheit`. The Adapter converts the temperature unit — the sensor is the Adaptee, the monitoring system is the Client, the conversion logic is the Adapter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building an Adapter Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Scenario (typical KTU 14-mark design question):**
> A legacy payroll system has a class `LegacyPayroll` with method `computeOldSalary(int empId)` that returns a salary as a `float`. The new HR system expects a `PayrollTarget` interface with method `getSalary(String empCode)` that returns a `double`. Design an **Object Adapter** that allows the new system to use the legacy class **without modifying it**.

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify the Incompatibility

The mismatch is structural:

$$
\text{LegacyPayroll.computeOldSalary}(\text{int empId}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{float}
$$

$$
\text{PayrollTarget.getSalary}(\text{String empCode}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{double}
$$

Two incompatibilities exist:
1. **Parameter type:** `int` (legacy) vs. `String` (new) — must parse the String to extract int.
2. **Return type:** `float` (legacy) vs. `double` (new) — must widen the primitive.

**Conversion Logic (textual derivation):**

$$
\text{empId} = \text{Integer.parseInt(empCode)}
$$

$$
\text{double salary} = (\text{double}) \; \text{legacy.computeOldSalary(empId)}
$$

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Declare the Target Interface

The Target is what the **Client** sees. It captures the contract the Client expects.

```text
<<interface>>
PayrollTarget
─────────────────────
+ getSalary(empCode: String): double
```

**Reasoning:**
- `<<interface>>` stereotype is mandatory in UML to mark a non-instantiable contract.
- Method signature uses `String` parameter and `double` return — the **public** contract.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Declare the Adaptee (Legacy Class)

The Adaptee is **given** — it cannot be modified.

```text
LegacyPayroll
─────────────────────────────
- employeeDB: Map<int, float>
─────────────────────────────
+ computeOldSalary(empId: int): float
+ printOldReport(): void
```

**Reasoning:**
- The Adaptee has a useful method (`computeOldSalary`) but with the wrong signature.
- `printOldReport()` is irrelevant to our adapter — it shows Adaptees often have more functionality than needed.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Design the Adapter Class (Object Adapter)

This is the heart of the design.

```text
PayrollAdapter
──────────────────────────────────
- legacy: LegacyPayroll   // Composition: HAS-A
──────────────────────────────────
+ PayrollAdapter(legacy: LegacyPayroll)
+ getSalary(empCode: String): double
```

**Internal method body (algorithm of translation):**

$$
\text{getSalary}(empCode) \;\Rightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{step a:} & \text{empId} = \text{Integer.parseInt(empCode)} \\
\text{step b:} & \text{oldSalary} = \text{legacy.computeOldSalary}(\text{empId}) \\
\text{step c:} & \text{return } (\text{double})\;\text{oldSalary}
\end{cases}
$$

**Each step's role:**

| Step | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| a | `Integer.parseInt(empCode)` | Converts `String` parameter to `int`. Handles `NumberFormatException` (impedance mismatch #1 resolved). |
| b | `legacy.computeOldSalary(empId)` | Delegates the actual computation to the Adaptee. Adapter does **not** re-implement logic. |
| c | `(double) oldSalary` | Widens the `float` return to `double` (impedance mismatch #2 resolved). |

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Wire the Client

The Client is given a `PayrollTarget` reference, **not** a `LegacyPayroll` reference. This decouples the client from the legacy class.

$$
\text{Client} \;\dashrightarrow\; \text{PayrollTarget}
$$

The concrete object injected is the `PayrollAdapter` wrapping a `LegacyPayroll`.

```text
HRSystem (Client)
────────────────────────────
- payroll: PayrollTarget
────────────────────────────
+ generatePaySlip(empCode: String)
```

**Internal call:**

$$
\text{generatePaySlip}(empCode) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{payroll.getSalary}(empCode)
$$

The Client **never imports, references, or knows** the `LegacyPayroll` class. The Adapter is the only translation point.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Selection Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision matrix used by KTU examiners to award full marks:**

| Situation | Recommended Pattern | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Incompatible interface, legacy class cannot change | **Adapter** | Translates interface, no modification needed |
| Need to add new behavior dynamically | **Decorator** | Wraps + extends behavior at runtime |
| Need to simplify a complex subsystem | **Facade** | Provides unified high-level interface |
| Need to control access to an object | **Proxy** | Adds protection, lazy loading, or remote access |
| Need to share state across many objects | **Flyweight** | Reduces memory by sharing intrinsic state |
| Need to compose objects into tree structures | **Composite** | Treats individual and composite objects uniformly |
| Need to decouple abstraction from implementation | **Bridge** | Both can vary independently |

---

## 3.3 Class Adapter Derivation (C++-style walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **For C++ multiple-inheritance context only. In Java/C#, this is replaced with interface + composition (Object Adapter).**

```text
ClassAdapter  ──▷  PayrollTarget       (public inheritance)
ClassAdapter  ──▷  LegacyPayroll       (public inheritance)
```

**UML Notation Specifics:**

$$
\text{ClassAdapter} \;\xRightarrow{\text{realizes}}\; \text{PayrollTarget} \;\;\text{AND}\;\; \text{ClassAdapter} \;\xRightarrow{\text{is-a}}\; \text{LegacyPayroll}
$$

**Translation method (Class Adapter variant):**

$$
\text{getSalary}(empCode) \;\Rightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{empId} = \text{Integer.parseInt(empCode)} \\
\text{return } (\text{double}) \;\text{this.computeOldSalary}(\text{empId})
\end{cases}
$$

Note the direct use of `this.computeOldSalary` — possible because Class Adapter **inherits** from `LegacyPayroll`, so it can call Adaptee methods as if they were its own. Object Adapter, by contrast, **must** go through `legacy.computeOldSalary(...)`.

---

## 3.4 Pluggable Adapter Derivation (Two-Way Adapter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Way (Pluggable) Adapter** — an advanced extension sometimes asked in KTU 14-mark questions as a "modification" to a base Adapter.

A Two-Way Adapter makes the Adapter usable by **both** the new and old systems:

$$
\text{Adapter} \;\xrightarrow{\text{acts as}}\; \text{Target} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Adapter} \;\xrightarrow{\text{acts as}}\; \text{Adaptee}
$$

This is achieved by making the Adapter implement **both** the Target interface and expose the Adaptee's methods. Used when both new and legacy code must coexist (common in **incremental migration** projects).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Object Adapter — Full Class Diagram (UML)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +useTarget() void
    }

    class Target {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }

    class Adapter {
        -adaptee: Adaptee
        +Adapter(a: Adaptee)
        +request() void
    }

    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }

    class ConcreteAdaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
        +extraBehavior() void
    }

    Client ..> Target : depends on
    Adapter ..|> Target : realizes
    Adapter o-- Adaptee : has-a (composition)
    Adaptee <|-- ConcreteAdaptee : is-a
```

**Diagram Walkthrough (for valuation purposes):**

| Element in Diagram | UML Meaning | KTU Mark Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `Client ..> Target` | **Dependency** — Client uses Target but does not own it. | +1 mark for correct dependency arrow |
| `Adapter ..|> Target` | **Realization** — Adapter implements Target interface. | +1 mark for hollow triangle (realization) |
| `Adapter o-- Adaptee` | **Composition** — Adapter owns the Adaptee (filled diamond). | +2 marks — most common error is using hollow diamond (aggregation) or simple line |
| `Adaptee <|-- ConcreteAdaptee` | **Generalization** — inheritance. | +1 mark for solid hollow triangle |

---

## 4.2 Class Adapter — Full Class Diagram (UML)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +useTarget() void
    }

    class Target {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }

    class Adapter {
        +request() void
        +specificRequest() void
    }

    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }

    Client ..> Target : depends on
    Adapter ..|> Target : realizes
    Adapter --|> Adaptee : inherits (multiple inheritance)
```

**Key Visual Difference:**

- Object Adapter: `Adapter o-- Adaptee` (**filled diamond** + line = composition).
- Class Adapter: `Adapter --|> Adaptee` (**hollow triangle** = generalization/inheritance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If the question says "**Object Adapter**", you MUST show a **filled diamond** (composition) between Adapter and Adaptee. If the question says "**Class Adapter**", you MUST show a **hollow triangle** (generalization) between Adapter and Adaptee, plus note that the language must support multiple inheritance.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Runtime Collaboration

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Client
    participant AD as Adapter
    participant AE as Adaptee

    CL->>AD: request()
    Note over AD: translate parameters,<br/>convert types
    AD->>AE: specificRequest()
    Note over AE: executes original logic
    AE-->>AD: result (legacy format)
    Note over AD: convert return value,<br/>reformat to Target
    AD-->>CL: result (Target format)
```

**Sequence Diagram Interpretation (for 14-mark answers):**

| Step | Message | Direction | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `request()` | Client → Adapter | Client thinks it is calling Target |
| 2 | (internal translation) | Adapter | Adapter converts parameters |
| 3 | `specificRequest()` | Adapter → Adaptee | Delegation to legacy class |
| 4 | (legacy result) | Adaptee → Adapter | Result in Adaptee's format |
| 5 | (reformatting) | Adapter | Adapter converts return type |
| 6 | (Target result) | Adapter → Client | Client receives expected result |

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application<br/>Calls request] --> B{Target Interface<br/>Contract Exists?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Adapter Class<br/>Translates Call]
    B -->|No| D[Direct Coupling<br/>Not Recommended]
    C --> E[Parse Parameters<br/>e.g., String to int]
    E --> F[Delegate to Adaptee<br/>specificRequest]
    F --> G[Adaptee Executes<br/>Original Logic]
    G --> H[Return Raw Result<br/>e.g., float]
    H --> I[Adapter Reformats<br/>e.g., float to double]
    I --> J[Return to Client<br/>in Expected Format]
    D --> K[Maintenance Nightmare<br/>Tight Coupling]
```

**Reading the Flowchart (valuing full marks):**

- **Boxes A, C, E, F, G, H, I, J** depict the **happy path** of an Adapter.
- **Box D → K** represents what happens **without** the Adapter — the anti-pattern.
- **Decision diamond B** shows the precondition: the Target interface must exist.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Part A — Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Q1.** Define the **Adapter Pattern**. List its four primary participants.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The Adapter Pattern is a **Structural Design Pattern** that converts the interface of an existing class (Adaptee) into another interface (Target) that the client expects, enabling incompatible classes to work together. It is also known as a **Wrapper**.

**Four Participants (1.5 marks):**

| # | Participant | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Target** | Interface the client expects |
| 2 | **Client** | Collaborates with objects conforming to Target |
| 3 | **Adaptee** | Existing class with incompatible interface |
| 4 | **Adapter** | Bridges Target and Adaptee by translation |

---

### Part A — Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Object Adapter** and **Class Adapter** in one sentence each. State which language supports Class Adapter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Object Adapter (1 mark):** Uses **composition** (HAS-A relationship) to wrap the Adaptee, holding it as a field reference.
- **Class Adapter (1 mark):** Uses **multiple inheritance** to inherit from both the Target interface and the Adaptee class simultaneously.
- **Language Support (1 mark):** Class Adapter is supported in **C++** (and Python). It is **not directly possible in Java or C#** due to single-inheritance restriction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **forget the `<<interface>>` stereotype** on the Target — **−1 mark**.
> - Students use a **hollow diamond (aggregation)** instead of **filled diamond (composition)** for Object Adapter — **−1 mark**.
> - Students write **code snippets instead of UML class diagrams** for design-pattern questions — **−2 marks** (UML is the primary deliverable in this course).
> - Students fail to list **all four participants** explicitly — **−1 mark**.
> - Students confuse **Adapter with Decorator or Facade** — write the wrong intent in the answer — **−2 to −3 marks**.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q(A). (a)** Explain the **Adapter Design Pattern** with a suitable real-world example. Draw the **UML class diagram** for an **Object Adapter** and identify the participants. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition & Real-World Example (3 marks):**

The Adapter Pattern is a Structural pattern that allows two incompatible interfaces to collaborate by introducing a bridge (Adapter) class. It does this without modifying either the client or the adaptee.

> **Example:** Consider a **third-party payment gateway** library (`OldPaymentGateway`) that exposes a method `makePayment(int amountInCents)`. Our e-commerce application expects a `PaymentProcessor` interface with method `pay(double amountInRupees)`. An `PaymentAdapter` class bridges the gap by converting rupees to cents and delegating the call.

**UML Class Diagram (Object Adapter) — 3 marks:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class EcommerceApp {
        +checkout() void
    }

    class PaymentProcessor {
        <<interface>>
        +pay(amount: double) void
    }

    class PaymentAdapter {
        -oldGateway: OldPaymentGateway
        +PaymentAdapter(g: OldPaymentGateway)
        +pay(amount: double) void
    }

    class OldPaymentGateway {
        +makePayment(cents: int) void
    }

    EcommerceApp ..> PaymentProcessor : uses
    PaymentAdapter ..|> PaymentProcessor : realizes
    PaymentAdapter o-- OldPaymentGateway : composition
```

**Participant Identification (1 mark):**

| Participant | In this Example |
|---|---|
| **Client** | `EcommerceApp` |
| **Target** | `PaymentProcessor` (interface) |
| **Adaptee** | `OldPaymentGateway` (third-party class) |
| **Adapter** | `PaymentAdapter` (translation class) |

---

> **Q(A). (b)** With a **sequence diagram**, explain how the Adapter Pattern operates at runtime. Mention **two consequences** of using this pattern. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sequence Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant EC as EcommerceApp
    participant PA as PaymentAdapter
    participant OG as OldPaymentGateway

    EC->>PA: pay(amount: 1500.00)
    Note over PA: Convert double to int<br/>1500.00 * 100 = 150000 cents
    PA->>OG: makePayment(150000)
    Note over OG: Process legacy payment
    OG-->>PA: success status (int code)
    Note over PA: Translate return code to boolean
    PA-->>EC: payment successful (true/false)
```

**Operational Explanation (1.5 marks):**

At runtime, the **Client** invokes the Target method on the **Adapter**. The Adapter performs three operations:
1. **Parameter conversion** (e.g., `double 1500.00` → `int 150000` cents).
2. **Delegation** to the Adaptee's actual method.
3. **Return-type translation** (e.g., `int status code` → `boolean success`).

**Consequences (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Benefit — Decoupling:** The client is decoupled from the Adaptee's interface. Both can vary independently, satisfying the **Open/Closed Principle**.
2. **Drawback — Increased indirection:** There is one extra hop in method calls, which can slightly affect performance and increase the number of classes in the system.
3. *(Optional 3rd point)* **Benefit — Reusability:** A single Adapter can be reused by multiple clients needing the same translation.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Q(B). (a)** Compare **Object Adapter** and **Class Adapter** in terms of mechanism, UML notation, language support, and flexibility. Provide the UML class diagram for **Class Adapter**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Object Adapter** | **Class Adapter** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Composition (HAS-A) | Multiple Inheritance (IS-A × 2) |
| UML Notation | `Adapter o-- Adaptee` (filled diamond) | `Adapter --|> Adaptee` (hollow triangle) |
| Language Support | All OO languages | C++, Python only (not Java/C#) |
| Flexibility | Adapts any Adaptee subclass; can wrap multiple Adaptees | Locked to one Adaptee hierarchy; cannot wrap multiple Adaptees |
| Reusability | High | Lower |

**UML Class Diagram (Class Adapter) — 3 marks:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +operation() void
    }

    class Target {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }

    class Adapter {
        +request() void
    }

    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }

    Client ..> Target : uses
    Adapter ..|> Target : realizes
    Adapter --|> Adaptee : inherits
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The **Object Adapter** is preferred in modern OOP because of the **"Composition over Inheritance"** design principle, and because it works in all major OO languages including Java.

---

> **Q(B). (b)** A legacy **CSV file reader** has method `readCSV(filename: String): List<String[]>`. The new analytics module expects a `DataReader` interface with method `loadData(source: String): String`. Design an **Object Adapter** for this scenario. Provide the **UML class diagram**, **sequence diagram**, and explain the **translation logic**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class AnalyticsModule {
        +analyze() void
    }

    class DataReader {
        <<interface>>
        +loadData(source: String) String
    }

    class CSVAdapter {
        -csvReader: CSVFileReader
        +CSVAdapter(reader: CSVFileReader)
        +loadData(source: String) String
    }

    class CSVFileReader {
        +readCSV(filename: String) List~String[]~
    }

    AnalyticsModule ..> DataReader : uses
    CSVAdapter ..|> DataReader : realizes
    CSVAdapter o-- CSVFileReader : composition
```

**Sequence Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AM as AnalyticsModule
    participant CA as CSVAdapter
    participant CR as CSVFileReader

    AM->>CA: loadData("sales.csv")
    Note over CA: read CSV file path
    CA->>CR: readCSV("sales.csv")
    Note over CR: Read file, return List of String arrays
    CR-->>CA: List~String[]~ (rows of CSV)
    Note over CA: Concatenate rows into single String<br/>Add newline separators
    CA-->>AM: "Name,Age\nJohn,30\n..."
```

**Translation Logic (2 marks):**

Inside `CSVAdapter.loadData(source)`:

$$
\text{loadData}(source) \;\Rightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{step 1:} & \text{rows} = \text{csvReader.readCSV}(\text{source}) \\
\text{step 2:} & \text{result} = "" \\
\text{step 3:} & \text{for each row in rows:} \\
& \quad\text{result} \mathrel{+}= \text{String.join(","},\; \text{row}) \mathrel{+}= "\text{n}" \\
\text{step 4:} & \text{return result}
\end{cases}
$$

**Why this works:** The Client (`AnalyticsModule`) receives a single `String` (CSV-converted-to-text-block), while internally the Adaptee (`CSVFileReader`) returns a list of row arrays. The Adapter is the **sole translation point** — neither side needs modification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Adapter Pattern (print this before the exam):**

- ⭐ **Category:** Structural Design Pattern (Gang of Four).
- ⭐ **Intent:** Convert the interface of a class into another interface the client expects.
- ⭐ **Also Known As:** Wrapper.
- ⭐ **Four Participants (must list all):** Client, Target, Adapter, Adaptee.
- ⭐ **Object Adapter** uses **composition** (filled diamond, `o--`).
- ⭐ **Class Adapter** uses **multiple inheritance** (hollow triangle, `--|>`) — C++/Python only.
- ⭐ **Modern preference:** Object Adapter — honors "Composition over Inheritance."
- ⭐ **Design Principles Honored:** Open/Closed, Single Responsibility, Dependency Inversion.
- ⭐ **UML Diagrams Required in Answers:** Class Diagram (mandatory) + Sequence Diagram (for 14-mark).
- ⭐ **Real-World Analogy:** Power plug travel adapter (between incompatible sockets).
- ⭐ **Industrial Examples:** JDBC drivers, Java wrapper classes, third-party API integrations, Android RecyclerView.Adapter.
- ⭐ **Keyword for Spotting in Questions:** "Incompatible interface", "legacy code", "third-party class", "wrap", "translate".
- ⭐ **Common Confusion:** Adapter ≠ Decorator. Adapter **translates** interface, Decorator **adds** behavior.
- ⭐ **Common Confusion 2:** Adapter ≠ Facade. Facade **simplifies** a complex subsystem, Adapter **bridges** two specific interfaces.
- ⭐ **When NOT to use:** When you can directly modify the Adaptee, or when the interfaces are already compatible.
- ⭐ **Two-Way Adapter:** Advanced variant that makes the Adapter usable by both new and legacy clients.
- ⭐ **Pluggable Adapter:** Variant that allows runtime swapping of Adaptee via parameterized adapter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning:** In a 14-mark KTU answer, if you draw a UML class diagram **without** the `<<interface>>` stereotype on Target, **without** the filled diamond for Object Adapter, or **without** clearly labeling all four participants — expect to lose **2–3 marks** even if the textual explanation is correct. **Practice drawing the diagram from memory at least 5 times** before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bridge Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** (one of the 23 GoF — Gang of Four patterns) that **decouples an abstraction from its implementation** so that the two can vary **independently**. It achieves this by composing (bridging) the abstraction object with an implementation object at runtime through aggregation/association, rather than using inheritance to bind them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "The Bridge pattern separates the interface (abstraction) of a class from its implementation, allowing both to evolve independently. It uses composition over inheritance to reduce the proliferation of subclasses."

In UML terms, the Bridge pattern is represented by:
- An **Abstraction** class (the high-level control layer)
- A **RefinedAbstraction** class (specialized abstraction)
- An **Implementor** interface/abstract class (the implementation interface)
- A **ConcreteImplementor** class (concrete implementation)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Universal Remote Control**

Imagine a **Universal Remote Control** (the Abstraction) and different **Devices** it can control — TV, Radio, DVD Player (the Implementors).

- The **Remote** is your abstraction: it has buttons like `power()`, `volumeUp()`, `channelDown()`.
- The **Device** is the implementation: each device has its own way of executing these actions (a TV handles channel differently than a DVD player).
- You can pair **any remote** (basic remote, advanced remote with LCD) with **any device** (Sony TV, Samsung TV) without creating a class explosion.

Without the Bridge pattern, you would need classes like:
`SonyBasicRemote`, `SamsungBasicRemote`, `SonyAdvancedRemote`, `SamsungAdvancedRemote`...
This is the **class explosion problem** the Bridge pattern solves.

### 1.3 Intent of the Bridge Pattern

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pattern Category** | Structural (GoF) |
| **Also Known As** | Handle/Body |
| **Primary Intent** | Decouple abstraction from implementation |
| **Key Mechanism** | Composition / Aggregation |
| **Problem Solved** | Class explosion due to two independent dimensions of variation |
| **Applicability** | When both abstraction and implementation should be extensible via subclasses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Bridge pattern is **NOT** about bridging two unrelated classes — it is about **separating two orthogonal (independent) axes of variation** so each can grow on its own.

### 1.4 Participants in the Bridge Pattern

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Abstraction** | Defines the abstraction's interface; maintains a reference to an Implementor object. |
| **RefinedAbstraction** | Extends the interface defined by Abstraction with specialized behavior. |
| **Implementor** | Defines the interface for implementation classes (does NOT have to match Abstraction's interface exactly). |
| **ConcreteImplementorA / B** | Implements the Implementor interface with concrete platform-specific behavior. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Problem Bridge Solves — Class Explosion

Consider a system with **2 abstractions** × **3 implementations** using inheritance:

$$\text{Number of Classes} = N_{abstractions} \times N_{implementations}$$

For **5 abstractions** × **5 implementations**, you get **25 classes**!
With the Bridge pattern, you only need **5 + 5 = 10 classes**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Identify the two independent dimensions** of variation in your system.
2. **Create the Implementor interface** that captures one dimension (e.g., device-specific operations).
3. **Create ConcreteImplementor classes** for each variation in that dimension.
4. **Create the Abstraction class** that holds a reference (bridge) to an Implementor.
5. **Create RefinedAbstraction classes** for variations in the other dimension.
6. **Inject the Implementor** into the Abstraction via constructor or setter (composition).
7. **Client code** creates a RefinedAbstraction and pairs it with a ConcreteImplementor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bridge"?** The reference held by Abstraction to the Implementor acts as a **bridge** that connects the two hierarchies. This is the structural metaphor behind the name.

### 2.3 Bridge vs Adapter — Critical Distinction (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | Bridge Pattern | Adapter Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Decouple abstraction from implementation upfront | Make incompatible interfaces work together **after the fact** |
| **Design Time** | Designed **before** the system is built | Applied **after** to fix existing code |
| **Relationship** | Abstraction holds reference to Implementor (composition) | Adapter wraps the Adaptee |
| **GoF Category** | Structural | Structural |
| **Usage** | Anticipates independent variation | Retrofits compatibility |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element | Symbol/Representation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | `Abstraction` class | High-level control logic |
| RefinedAbstraction | Inherits from `Abstraction` | Extends abstraction behavior |
| Implementor | Interface/Abstract class | Low-level implementation contract |
| ConcreteImplementor | Implements `Implementor` | Concrete platform-specific code |
| Bridge Link | Aggregation arrow (empty diamond) | "has-a" relationship between Abstraction and Implementor |
| UML Stereotype | `<<interface>>` or `«interface»` | Used for Implementor in UML 2.x |

### 2.5 Key UML Notation Rules

- **Realization (Implementation)**: Abstraction → Implementor is shown as a **dashed line with a hollow triangle** (realizes/implements).
- **Aggregation/Bridge Link**: Abstraction ◇——> Implementor (empty diamond = aggregation).
- **Inheritance**: RefinedAbstraction ▷—> Abstraction (solid line with hollow triangle).
- **Realization (Implementor side)**: ConcreteImplementor - -▷ Implementor (dashed line with hollow triangle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the Bridge pattern's UML diagram with the Adapter pattern. Remember: **Bridge is designed from the start; Adapter is a retrofit.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Toolkits**: Separating window abstraction from the underlying OS (Windows/Linux/Mac).
- **JDBC Drivers**: Java's JDBC API is the abstraction; the database driver (MySQL, Oracle) is the implementor.
- **Device Drivers**: One generic driver interface, multiple device-specific implementations.
- **Persistence Layers**: Repository pattern variants bridging business logic to SQL, NoSQL, or file storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Justification of Class Reduction

Let:
- $A$ = number of abstractions
- $I$ = number of implementations

**Without Bridge (Inheritance):**

$$\text{Total Classes}_{inheritance} = A \times I$$

**With Bridge (Composition):**

$$\text{Total Classes}_{bridge} = A + I$$

**Class Reduction Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= A \cdot I - (A + I) \\
&= A \cdot I - A - I \\
&= A(I - 1) - I \\
&= (A - 1)(I - 1) - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If $A = 3$ and $I = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inheritance} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ classes} \\
\text{Bridge} &= 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ classes} \\
\text{Savings} &= 12 - 7 = 5 \text{ classes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Client Code Uses the Bridge

Consider a `Shape` (Abstraction) bridged with `Renderer` (Implementor).

**Step 1: Define the Implementor interface**
```
Interface Renderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double): void
    + renderSquare(side: double): void
}
```

**Step 2: Create ConcreteImplementors**
```
Class VectorRenderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double): void
    + renderSquare(side: double): void
}

Class RasterRenderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double): void
    + renderSquare(side: double): void
}
```

**Step 3: Define Abstraction**
```
Class Shape {
    - renderer: Renderer
    + Shape(r: Renderer)
    + draw(): void
}
```

**Step 4: Define RefinedAbstractions**
```
Class Circle extends Shape {
    - radius: double
    + Circle(r: Renderer, radius: double)
    + draw(): void
    + resize(factor: double): void
}

Class Square extends Shape {
    - side: double
    + Square(r: Renderer, side: double)
    + draw(): void
}
```

**Step 5: Client Configuration**
```
Client → new Circle(new VectorRenderer(), 5.0)
Client → new Circle(new RasterRenderer(), 5.0)
```

### 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough of `draw()` Execution

When the client calls `circle.draw()`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Client invokes } circle.draw() \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Circle.draw() is executed (RefinedAbstraction)} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Circle delegates to } renderer.renderCircle(radius) \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Specific renderer (Vector or Raster) executes logic} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Result returned to client}
\end{aligned}
$$

This delegation chain is the **essence of the Bridge pattern**: the abstraction never knows *how* the implementation works — it only knows *what* it can do.

### 3.4 When to Apply the Bridge Pattern — Decision Matrix

| Condition | Apply Bridge? |
|---|---|
| You want to avoid a permanent binding between abstraction and implementation | ✅ Yes |
| Both abstractions and implementations should be extensible via subclasses | ✅ Yes |
| Changes in implementation should NOT impact clients | ✅ Yes |
| You have a single fixed hierarchy with no orthogonal dimensions | ❌ No |
| You are retrofitting an existing interface to a legacy class | ❌ Use Adapter instead |
| You need to share implementation among multiple objects | ⚠️ Consider shared bridge instance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Bridge Pattern UML Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Abstraction {
        - implementor : Implementor
        + operation() : void
    }
    class RefinedAbstraction {
        + refinedOperation() : void
    }
    class Implementor {
        <<interface>>
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorA {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorB {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }

    Abstraction <|-- RefinedAbstraction
    Abstraction o-- Implementor : bridge link
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorA
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorB
```

### 4.2 Practical Example — Shape and Renderer Bridge

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Shape {
        - renderer : Renderer
        + Shape(r : Renderer)
        + draw() : void
    }
    class Circle {
        - radius : double
        + Circle(r : Renderer, radius : double)
        + draw() : void
        + resize(f : double) : void
    }
    class Square {
        - side : double
        + Square(r : Renderer, side : double)
        + draw() : void
    }
    class Renderer {
        <<interface>>
        + renderCircle(r : double) : void
        + renderSquare(s : double) : void
    }
    class VectorRenderer {
        + renderCircle(r : double) : void
        + renderSquare(s : double) : void
    }
    class RasterRenderer {
        + renderCircle(r : double) : void
        + renderSquare(s : double) : void
    }

    Shape <|-- Circle
    Shape <|-- Square
    Shape o-- Renderer
    Renderer <|.. VectorRenderer
    Renderer <|.. RasterRenderer
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Code] --> B[Create ConcreteImplementor]
    B --> C[Create RefinedAbstraction with Implementor]
    C --> D[Call Operation on Abstraction]
    D --> E[Abstraction delegates to Implementor]
    E --> F[ConcreteImplementor executes logic]
    F --> G[Result returned to Client]
```

### 4.4 Bridge vs Inheritance — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inheritance_Explosion [Without Bridge - Class Explosion]
        A1[BasicTVRemote] --> B1[BasicTVRemote_Sony]
        A1 --> C1[BasicTVRemote_Samsung]
        A1 --> D1[BasicTVRemote_LG]
        A2[AdvancedTVRemote] --> B2[AdvancedTVRemote_Sony]
        A2 --> C2[AdvancedTVRemote_Samsung]
        A2 --> D2[AdvancedTVRemote_LG]
    end

    subgraph Bridge_Solution [With Bridge - Decoupled]
        E[Remote Abstraction] --> F[Advanced Remote]
        E --> G[Basic Remote]
        I[Device Implementor] --> J[Sony TV]
        I --> K[Samsung TV]
        I --> L[LG TV]
    end
```

### 4.5 Sequence Diagram — How the Bridge Works at Runtime

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Circle as RefinedAbstraction
    participant Shape as Abstraction
    participant Vector as ConcreteImplementorA

    Client->>Circle: new Circle(vectorRenderer, 5.0)
    Client->>Circle: draw()
    Circle->>Shape: operation()
    Shape->>Vector: operationImpl()
    Vector-->>Shape: render result
    Shape-->>Circle: result
    Circle-->>Client: drawn circle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a Bridge pattern, always include the **aggregation (empty diamond) link** between Abstraction and Implementor. This is the visual signature of the Bridge pattern.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define the Bridge design pattern. State any two situations where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Bridge pattern is a **structural design pattern** that decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that both can vary independently. It uses **composition (aggregation)** rather than inheritance to bind abstraction and implementation at runtime.

**Two situations where it is applicable:**
1. When you want to avoid a permanent binding between an abstraction and its implementation (e.g., the implementation must be selected or switched at runtime).
2. When both the abstractions and implementations should be extensible through subclassing, and you want to avoid a combinatorial explosion of classes.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for situations — 1 each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between Bridge pattern and Adapter pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Bridge Pattern | Adapter Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Decouples abstraction from implementation at **design time** | Makes incompatible interfaces compatible **after design** |
| **When Applied** | Anticipated during system design | Applied to existing/legacy code |
| **Relationship** | Abstraction holds reference to Implementor | Adapter wraps the Adaptee |
| **Goal** | Independent variation of two dimensions | Reusability of an existing class with mismatched interface |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct difference. Minimum 3 differences needed for full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Draw the UML class diagram for the Bridge design pattern. Identify all participants and their roles.

**b) (7 Marks)** A drawing application supports two shapes — `Circle` and `Rectangle` — and two renderers — `VectorRenderer` and `RasterRenderer`. Design a Bridge-pattern based solution. Provide the Java/C++ pseudocode and explain how it resolves the class explosion problem.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — UML Class Diagram (7 Marks)

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Abstraction {
        - implementor : Implementor
        + operation() : void
    }
    class RefinedAbstraction {
        + refinedOperation() : void
    }
    class Implementor {
        <<interface>>
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorA {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorB {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }

    Abstraction <|-- RefinedAbstraction
    Abstraction o-- Implementor
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorA
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorB
```

**Participants and Roles (Award 2 marks):**

1. **Abstraction** — Defines the high-level abstraction interface; maintains a reference to the Implementor.
2. **RefinedAbstraction** — Extends Abstraction with more specialized behavior.
3. **Implementor** — Declares the interface for implementation classes (independent from Abstraction's interface).
4. **ConcreteImplementorA / B** — Implements the Implementor interface with platform-specific code.

**UML Notation Used (Award 1 mark):**
- Solid line with hollow triangle → inheritance
- Dashed line with hollow triangle → realization
- Empty diamond → aggregation (the Bridge link)

*(Award remaining marks for diagram correctness.)*

---

### Part (b) — Drawing Application Design (7 Marks)

**Without Bridge (Class Explosion):**

`VectorCircle`, `RasterCircle`, `VectorRectangle`, `RasterRectangle`, and any future combinations → 4 classes (and growing).

**With Bridge Solution — Code Design:**

**Step 1: Implementor Interface**
```
Interface Renderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double) : void
    + renderRectangle(width: double, height: double) : void
}
```

**Step 2: Concrete Implementors**
```
Class VectorRenderer implements Renderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double) : void {
        // Draws circle using vector graphics primitives
    }
    + renderRectangle(w: double, h: double) : void {
        // Draws rectangle using vector graphics primitives
    }
}

Class RasterRenderer implements Renderer {
    + renderCircle(radius: double) : void {
        // Draws circle using pixel manipulation
    }
    + renderRectangle(w: double, h: double) : void {
        // Draws rectangle using pixel manipulation
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Abstraction**
```
Class Shape {
    - renderer : Renderer
    + Shape(renderer : Renderer)
    + draw() : void
}
```

**Step 4: Refined Abstractions**
```
Class Circle extends Shape {
    - radius : double
    + Circle(renderer : Renderer, radius : double)
    + draw() : void {
        renderer.renderCircle(radius)
    }
}

Class Rectangle extends Shape {
    - width : double
    - height : double
    + Rectangle(renderer : Renderer, w: double, h: double)
    + draw() : void {
        renderer.renderRectangle(width, height)
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Client Usage**
```
Circle c1 = new Circle(new VectorRenderer(), 5.0)
Circle c2 = new Circle(new RasterRenderer(), 5.0)
Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle(new VectorRenderer(), 4.0, 6.0)
```

**How Bridge Resolves Class Explosion (Award 3 marks):**
- With Bridge: **2 Shapes + 2 Renderers = 4 classes**
- Adding a new shape (`Triangle`) requires only **1 new class** — it can pair with *any* existing renderer.
- Adding a new renderer (`SVGRenderer`) requires only **1 new class** — it works with *all* existing shapes.
- This is the **Open/Closed Principle** in action — open for extension, closed for modification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Explain the structure of the Bridge pattern with a suitable UML diagram. Discuss the role of aggregation in this pattern.

**b) (7 Marks)** A remote control system needs to support multiple remote types (BasicRemote, AdvancedRemote) and multiple devices (TV, Radio). Show how the Bridge pattern can be applied. Provide the structural diagram and explain the benefits.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Structure and Role of Aggregation (7 Marks)

**Structure of Bridge Pattern:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Abstraction {
        - implementor : Implementor
        + operation() : void
    }
    class RefinedAbstraction {
        + refinedOperation() : void
    }
    class Implementor {
        <<interface>>
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorA {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorB {
        + operationImpl() : void
    }

    Abstraction <|-- RefinedAbstraction
    Abstraction o-- Implementor
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorA
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorB
```

**Role of Aggregation in Bridge Pattern (Award 3 marks):**

Aggregation in the Bridge pattern represents a **"has-a" relationship** between the Abstraction and the Implementor. The key roles are:

1. **Runtime Binding**: The Abstraction is composed with an Implementor object at runtime, allowing dynamic switching of implementations.

2. **Decoupling**: The Abstraction does not need to know which ConcreteImplementor it is using — it only refers to the Implementor interface.

3. **Independent Variation**: New Implementors can be added without modifying the Abstraction hierarchy, and vice versa.

4. **Preferring Composition Over Inheritance**: The Bridge pattern embodies this core OO design principle — composition provides more flexibility than rigid inheritance.

---

### Part (b) — Remote Control System (7 Marks)

**Structural Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class RemoteControl {
        - device : Device
        + RemoteControl(d : Device)
        + togglePower() : void
        + volumeUp() : void
        + volumeDown() : void
    }
    class BasicRemote {
        + togglePower() : void
    }
    class AdvancedRemote {
        + mute() : void
    }
    class Device {
        <<interface>>
        + enable() : void
        + disable() : void
        + setVolume(percent : int) : void
    }
    class TV {
        + enable() : void
        + disable() : void
        + setVolume(percent : int) : void
    }
    class Radio {
        + enable() : void
        + disable() : void
        + setVolume(percent : int) : void
    }

    RemoteControl <|-- BasicRemote
    RemoteControl <|-- AdvancedRemote
    RemoteControl o-- Device
    Device <|.. TV
    Device <|.. Radio
```

**Design Explanation (Award 4 marks):**

1. `RemoteControl` is the **Abstraction** with a reference (bridge) to `Device`.
2. `BasicRemote` and `AdvancedRemote` are **RefinedAbstractions** — different remote types.
3. `Device` is the **Implementor** interface.
4. `TV` and `Radio` are **ConcreteImplementors** — different device types.

**Client Configuration:**
```
RemoteControl basicTV = new BasicRemote(new TV())
RemoteControl advancedRadio = new AdvancedRemote(new Radio())
```

**Benefits of Using Bridge Pattern:**

| Benefit | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Any remote can control any device at runtime |
| **Extensibility** | New remotes or devices can be added independently |
| **Reduced Class Count** | Only $N + M$ classes instead of $N \times M$ |
| **Clean Separation** | UI/control logic is separated from hardware/device logic |
| **Testability** | Mock device implementations can be injected for unit testing |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Bridge with Adapter**: Bridge is a proactive design choice; Adapter is a reactive fix. Examiners will deduct marks if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Aggregation Arrow**: The empty diamond between Abstraction and Implementor is the **defining visual feature** of the Bridge pattern. Omitting it = lose 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Using `<<abstract>>` Instead of `<<interface>>`**: For the Implementor, use `<<interface>>` (UML 2.x notation) or simply italicize the class name. Don't use `<<abstract>>` unless implementing an abstract class.
> 4. **Incorrect Class Count Reasoning**: When asked "how does Bridge reduce class explosion?", always show the math: $A \times I$ vs $A + I$.
> 5. **Conflating Inheritance and Composition**: Bridge is fundamentally about **composition** (has-a), not inheritance (is-a).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Pattern Type**: Bridge is a **Structural** GoF pattern.
- ✅ **Intent**: Decouple abstraction from implementation so both vary independently.
- ✅ **Key Participants**: Abstraction, RefinedAbstraction, Implementor, ConcreteImplementor.
- ✅ **Crucial UML Element**: **Aggregation (empty diamond)** from Abstraction → Implementor.
- ✅ **Class Count Formula**: Bridge = $A + I$ classes; Inheritance = $A \times I$ classes.
- ✅ **Bridge vs Adapter**: Bridge is designed upfront; Adapter is a retrofit.
- ✅ **Design Principle**: Demonstrates **"Prefer Composition Over Inheritance"**.
- ✅ **SOLID Principle**: Supports **Open/Closed Principle** — extensible without modification.
- ✅ **Common Use Cases**: JDBC drivers, GUI toolkits, device drivers, persistence layers.
- ✅ **Alternative Name**: "Handle/Body" pattern.
- ✅ **When NOT to Use**: Single fixed hierarchy, no orthogonal dimensions, retrofitting existing classes (use Adapter).
- ✅ **Bridge Link Direction**: Abstraction **has-a** Implementor (composition), NOT the reverse.
- ✅ **Java Equivalent**: Use an interface for Implementor, abstract class for Abstraction, constructor injection.
- ✅ **Examination Trick**: If a question says "two independent dimensions of variation" → it's a Bridge pattern question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proxy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proxy Pattern (Gang-of-Four Structural Pattern)**
> *"Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it."* — GoF Catalogue

In the context of **UML-based software modelling**, the **Proxy Design Pattern** is a structural pattern in which a class (called the **Proxy**) represents the functionality of another class (called the **RealSubject**). The Proxy acts as an intermediary, controlling, managing, or augmenting access to the RealSubject. From the client's perspective, the Proxy and RealSubject share an identical interface, making them substitutable.

In UML class diagrams, the Proxy pattern is expressed through:
- An **abstract Subject** interface
- A **RealSubject** concrete class
- A **Proxy** class that implements the **same** interface and holds a reference to the RealSubject
- A **dependency / association** from Proxy → RealSubject

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Debit Card is a Proxy for your Bank Account**

Consider three real-world scenarios:

| Scenario | Real-World Analogy | Proxy Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtual Proxy** | ATM displaying *"Account balance loading..."* placeholder | Defers loading of heavy resource until needed |
| **Protection Proxy** | Bouncer at a club checking ID before entry | Filters requests based on access rights |
| **Remote Proxy** | Travel agent booking on your behalf at a distant branch | Encapsulates network communication |
| **Smart Reference** | Shared Google Doc counting viewers | Adds housekeeping when object is accessed |

In **all four cases**, you (the **Client**) interact with a stand-in (the **Proxy**) — not the actual heavy/sensitive/distant object (the **RealSubject**). The Proxy forwards, filters, or enhances your request, then optionally contacts the real object.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Whenever a question asks *"control access to an object"* or *"stand-in for another object"* — the answer is **Proxy**.

### 1.3 Why Proxy? — The Motivation

- **Lazy Initialization:** Avoid loading huge images / database connections until they are actually needed.
- **Access Control:** Permit only authorized clients to invoke sensitive operations.
- **Remote Communication:** Mask the complexity of inter-process / network calls.
- **Logging / Auditing:** Wrap calls with metadata collection without modifying the RealSubject.
- **Caching:** Store recent results to avoid recomputation.

---

### 1.4 Standard Classification of Proxies (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks: "List the types/kinds of Proxies." Memorize the 5 below.**

| # | Proxy Type | Purpose | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Remote Proxy** | Represents an object in a different address space | RMI, SOAP, REST stubs |
| 2 | **Virtual Proxy** | Creates expensive objects on demand | Lazy-loading large images |
| 3 | **Protection Proxy** | Controls access based on caller identity | Role-based authorization |
| 4 | **Smart Reference** | Performs housekeeping on access | Reference counting, locking |
| 5 | **Cache Proxy** | Stores results of previous invocations | Web response caching |

### 1.5 Standard UML Vocabulary (KTU Board Term)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Subject** | The common interface shared by Proxy and RealSubject |
| **RealSubject** | The actual object that does the real work |
| **Proxy** | The surrogate that holds a `realSubject` reference |
| **Client** | The caller; only knows about `Subject` |
| **request()** | The canonical operation name used in GoF diagrams |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Participants — Exhaustive Breakdown

The GoF Proxy pattern formally defines **4 participants**. Each has a strict role:

#### (a) Subject
- An **abstract interface** (in Java: `interface`; in UML: `<<interface>>` stereotype).
- Declares the common operations exposed to clients.
- Both `RealSubject` and `Proxy` honour this contract → **Liskov Substitution Principle** is satisfied.

#### (b) RealSubject
- The **concrete class** containing the genuine business logic.
- Represents the resource that is *expensive*, *remote*, *protected*, or *cacheable*.
- It is unaware that a Proxy exists.

#### (c) Proxy
- Holds a **reference** to the RealSubject (`realSubject : RealSubject`).
- Implements the **same interface** as RealSubject (so it is type-compatible).
- **Controls** access by intercepting requests before/after delegating them.
- May take responsibility for **object creation**, **lifecycle**, **security check**, or **caching**.

#### (d) Client
- Collaborates with objects through the Subject interface.
- Treats Proxy and RealSubject **uniformly** — it cannot differentiate them.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow (How a Proxy Executes a Request)

> [!NOTE]
> **This 7-step flow is the canonical answer for "Explain how the Proxy pattern works."**

1. **Client** invokes a method on the **Subject** reference, which actually points to a **Proxy** object.
2. The **Proxy** receives the call before the RealSubject does.
3. The **Proxy performs pre-processing** (e.g., access check, lazy initialization, cache lookup).
4. If pre-processing passes, the **Proxy invokes the corresponding method on the RealSubject** (forwarding the request).
5. The **RealSubject executes the real business logic** and returns a result.
6. The **Proxy performs post-processing** on the result (e.g., caching the result, logging, locking release).
7. The **Proxy returns the final result** to the Client.

---

### 2.3 Applicability — When to USE the Proxy Pattern (KTU 2-Mark Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> Use Proxy whenever **one of these conditions holds:**

- Whenever there is a need for a **more versatile or sophisticated reference** to an object than a simple pointer.
- When the object is **remote** (different process / address space).
- When the object is **expensive to create** (lazy initialization).
- When the object needs **protection** based on access rights.
- When **additional actions** must be taken when the object is accessed (e.g., reference counting, persistent storage, transactions).

---

### 2.4 Consequences — Trade-Offs (High-Yield Table)

| Benefit | Drawback |
|---|---|
| Adds a protection layer without modifying RealSubject | Introduces an extra indirection (latency) |
| Hides complexity of remote/distributed objects | May complicate debugging |
| Supports lazy / on-demand initialization | Can lead to inconsistent state if misused |
| Enables caching, logging, auditing transparently | Subject and RealSubject must share interface |
| Reduces memory footprint for heavy resources | May be over-engineered for simple scenarios |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Conceptual Notation

> [!NOTE]
> Proxy is a *pattern*, not a math formula. However, KTU expects precise notation in class diagrams.

| UML Notation Element | Representation |
|---|---|
| Abstract Subject | `<<interface>> Subject` with `+request() : void` |
| Realization | RealSubject `- -▷` Subject (dashed hollow triangle) |
| Proxy-Subject relation | Proxy `- -▷` Subject (dashed hollow triangle) |
| Proxy → RealSubject | Association with role `realSubject` (multiplicity `1`) |
| Client → Subject | Dependency arrow (`- -▷`) |
| Generic operation | `request()` |
| State field in Proxy | `- realSubject : RealSubject` |

---

### 2.6 Related Patterns (KTU Comparative Question Favourite)

| Pattern | Relation to Proxy |
|---|---|
| **Adapter** | Adapter changes the interface; Proxy keeps the **same interface** |
| **Decorator** | Decorator adds behaviour; Proxy **controls** access |
| **Facade** | Facade simplifies a subsystem; Proxy represents **one** object |
| **State** | State changes behaviour internally; Proxy intercepts externally |

> [!WARNING]
> A very common KTU pitfall: students write *"Proxy changes the interface like Adapter"* — **WRONG**. Proxy and RealSubject share **identical** interfaces.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Java RMI:** `Stub` objects are Remote Proxies.
- **Spring AOP:** Dynamic Proxies wrap beans to add cross-cutting concerns.
- **Hibernate:** Lazy-loading proxies for entity associations.
- **Web servers:** Reverse proxies (NGINX) sit between clients and backend servers.
- **Operating Systems:** Virtual memory uses proxy paging — page tables map virtual to physical addresses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical UML Class Diagram — Element-by-Element Derivation

We will derive the GoF Proxy structure step-by-step. Each element is justified.

#### Step 1: Define the Subject Interface
The Subject must expose all operations that clients might invoke on a RealSubject.

$$\text{Subject} = \{\, \text{operations callable by Client} \,\}$$

In UML notation, this is rendered as an interface box.

#### Step 2: Define the RealSubject
The RealSubject implements every operation declared in Subject.

$$\forall\, \text{op} \in \text{Subject} \;:\; \text{RealSubject.realizes(op)}$$

#### Step 3: Define the Proxy
The Proxy must also realize the same Subject — this is the **substitutability guarantee**.

$$\text{Proxy} \;\text{realizes}\; \text{Subject}$$

#### Step 4: Establish Proxy → RealSubject Reference
The Proxy holds a *handle* to the RealSubject so it can forward requests.

$$\text{Proxy.containsReference}(\text{RealSubject})$$

The multiplicity is exactly **1** in the simple form, or **0..1** in a Virtual Proxy (because the RealSubject may not yet exist).

#### Step 5: Add Pre/Post Hooks
For pre-processing:

$$\text{Proxy.request}() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{preHook}() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{realSubject.request}()$$

For post-processing:

$$\text{Proxy.request}() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{preHook}() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{realSubject.request}() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{postHook}()$$

#### Step 6: Complete Class Diagram
Putting it all together yields the standard GoF Proxy class diagram (see Section 4).

---

### 3.2 Canonical UML Sequence Diagram — Interaction Trace

The sequence diagram proves *temporal correctness*. Below is the canonical interaction flow when a Client invokes `request()` on a Proxy.

```
Client          Proxy            RealSubject
  |                |                  |
  |-- request() -->|                  |
  |                |-- preHook()      |  (internal)
  |                |-- request() ---->|
  |                |                  |-- [real work]
  |                |<--- result -----|
  |                |-- postHook()     |  (internal)
  |<-- result -----|                  |
  |                |                  |
```

**Explanation of the derived trace:**

1. `Client → Proxy : request()` — Client invokes Proxy via Subject interface.
2. `Proxy : preHook()` — Proxy performs access control / cache check / lazy init.
3. `Proxy → RealSubject : request()` — Proxy delegates the call.
4. `RealSubject : [real work]` — RealSubject performs the core logic.
5. `RealSubject → Proxy : return value` — Result returned to Proxy.
6. `Proxy : postHook()` — Proxy performs caching / logging.
7. `Proxy → Client : return value` — Final result delivered to Client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Virtual Proxy**, if the RealSubject has not yet been created, step 3 is preceded by:
> `Proxy : realSubject = new RealSubject()` — the lazy-instantiation step.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Proxy Variants and Their Decision Logic

| Trigger Condition | Chosen Proxy Variant | Where Logic Resides |
|---|---|---|
| Object is in another JVM / process | **Remote Proxy** | Marshalling & network code inside Proxy |
| Object creation is expensive | **Virtual Proxy** | `realSubject == null` check inside Proxy |
| Caller identity matters | **Protection Proxy** | `checkAccess(caller)` inside Proxy |
| Same input → expensive recomputation | **Cache Proxy** | `Map<key, value>` field inside Proxy |
| Need to track usage | **Smart Reference Proxy** | Reference counter field inside Proxy |

The decision can be modelled as:

$$
\text{ProxyVariant} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Remote} & \text{if } \text{location}(\text{RealSubject}) \neq \text{location}(\text{Client}) \\
\text{Virtual} & \text{if } \text{createCost}(\text{RealSubject}) \gg \text{normal} \\
\text{Protection} & \text{if } \text{authorizationRequired}() = \text{true} \\
\text{Cache} & \text{if } \text{isDeterministic}(\text{request}) = \text{true} \\
\text{SmartReference} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Document Editor with Lazy Image Proxy

**Problem Context:** A Document Editor opens large image files. We must avoid loading images that are off-screen.

**Step 1 — Identify Roles:**
- `Subject` = `Image`
- `RealSubject` = `HighResolutionImage`
- `Proxy` = `ImageProxy`
- `Client` = `DocumentWindow`

**Step 2 — Pseudocode Logic:**

```java
class ImageProxy implements Image {
    private HighResolutionImage realImage;
    private String filename;

    public void display() {
        if (realImage == null) {           // preHook: lazy init
            realImage = new HighResolutionImage(filename);
        }
        realImage.display();                // delegate
        logAccess();                        // postHook: logging
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — UML Mapping:**

| Java Element | UML Element |
|---|---|
| `interface Image` | `<<interface>> Image` |
| `class HighResolutionImage implements Image` | `HighResolutionImage` realizing `Image` |
| `class ImageProxy implements Image` | `ImageProxy` realizing `Image` |
| `private HighResolutionImage realImage` | Association `ImageProxy` → `HighResolutionImage`, role `realImage` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always write `<<interface>>` stereotype above the Subject class name — this is worth 1 mark in board exams.

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Implementation Plan (Checklist for 14-Mark Answers)

1. Identify the **business requirement** (e.g., lazy loading, security).
2. Define the **Subject interface** with public operations.
3. Implement the **RealSubject** with genuine logic.
4. Implement the **Proxy** class realizing the same interface.
5. Add a **field of type RealSubject** (or pointer/reference) inside Proxy.
6. Insert **pre-hooks and post-hooks** around delegation.
7. Construct the **UML Class Diagram** with stereotypes.
8. Draw the **Sequence Diagram** showing message exchange.
9. Discuss **consequences** and a **real-world example**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical GoF Proxy Class Diagram (UML)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ISubject {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }
    class RealSubject {
        -state : String
        +request() void
        +realOperation() void
    }
    class ProxyPatternProxy {
        -realSubject : RealSubject
        -preCheck : boolean
        +request() void
        +preProcess() void
        +postProcess() void
        -delegateRequest() void
    }
    class ClientRole {
        +main() void
        +invoke() void
    }

    ISubject <|.. RealSubject : realizes
    ISubject <|.. ProxyPatternProxy : realizes
    ProxyPatternProxy --> RealSubject : holds reference
    ClientRole ..> ISubject : depends on
```

**Diagram Reading Order (left → right):**
1. `ISubject` (top-left) — the abstract contract.
2. `RealSubject` (middle-left) — the genuine implementation.
3. `ProxyPatternProxy` (middle-right) — the surrogate with pre/post hooks.
4. `ClientRole` (top-right) — the consumer.

---

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Virtual Proxy Pattern (Lazy Initialization)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ClientActor as Client
    participant ProxyActor as ImageProxy
    participant RealImageActor as HighResolutionImage

    ClientActor->>ProxyActor: display()
    activate ProxyActor

    Note over ProxyActor: preProcess() - check if realImage is null
    alt realImage is null (lazy init path)
        ProxyActor->>RealImageActor: new HighResolutionImage(filename)
        activate RealImageActor
        RealImageActor-->>ProxyActor: instance created
        deactivate RealImageActor
    end

    ProxyActor->>RealImageActor: display()
    activate RealImageActor
    RealImageActor-->>ProxyActor: image rendered
    deactivate RealImageActor

    Note over ProxyActor: postProcess() - log access, cache result
    ProxyActor-->>ClientActor: display() complete
    deactivate ProxyActor
```

**Reading the Sequence:**

- **Step 1:** Client calls `display()` on the Proxy.
- **Step 3–6:** Proxy executes `preProcess()` — if RealSubject is null, instantiate it (lazy loading).
- **Step 7–10:** Proxy delegates to RealSubject, which renders the image.
- **Step 12–13:** Proxy executes `postProcess()` (logging, caching).
- **Step 14:** Final return to Client.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Cache Proxy Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Sends Request with Key K] --> B{Proxy Receives Request}
    B --> C[preProcess: extract key K]
    C --> D{Cache Lookup for K}
    D -->|Cache Hit| E[Return Cached Value]
    D -->|Cache Miss| F[Delegate to RealSubject]
    F --> G[RealSubject Executes Real Logic]
    G --> H[Return Result to Proxy]
    H --> I[postProcess: Store Result in Cache]
    I --> J[Return Result to Client]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Client Receives Final Response]

    style A fill:#e1f5fe
    style K fill:#c8e6c9
    style B fill:#fff9c4
    style D fill:#ffe0b2
    style F fill:#f8bbd0
```

**Reading the Flowchart:**

- The Client never touches RealSubject directly.
- All requests pass through Proxy's `preProcess` (cache lookup) and `postProcess` (cache store).
- A **cache hit** short-circuits the RealSubject entirely.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Protection Proxy Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Identity: User] --> B[ProtectionProxy]
    B --> C{checkAccess: User Role}
    C -->|Admin| D[Allow: delegate to RealSubject]
    C -->|Guest| E[Reject: throw SecurityException]
    D --> F[RealSubject executes operation]
    F --> G[Return success]
    E --> H[Return access denied]

    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style B fill:#fff59d
    style C fill:#ffcc80
    style D fill:#a5d6a7
    style E fill:#ef9a9a
    style F fill:#a5d6a7
```
