```mermaid
    style G fill:#c8e6c9
    style H fill:#ffcdd2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**Define the Proxy design pattern. List any four common types of proxies with their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Proxy design pattern provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it. The Proxy implements the same interface as the real object and forwards requests to it, optionally adding pre- or post-processing.

**Four types of proxies (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Remote Proxy** | Represents an object located in a different address space; handles network communication. |
| 2 | **Virtual Proxy** | Defers creation of an expensive object until it is actually needed (lazy initialization). |
| 3 | **Protection Proxy** | Controls access to the real object based on caller permissions or credentials. |
| 4 | **Smart Reference / Cache Proxy** | Performs additional housekeeping (counting references, locking, caching results). |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**
**Differentiate between Adapter and Proxy design patterns.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Adapter Pattern | Proxy Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect | Provides a surrogate to control access to an object |
| **Interface** | Changes the interface (incompatible → compatible) | **Keeps the same interface** as the RealSubject |
| **Purpose** | Makes existing classes work with others without modifying them | Controls, defers, or secures access to an object |
| **When used** | Legacy code integration | Lazy loading, remote access, security, caching |
| **Relation** | Wraps existing class | Wraps a reference to a real object |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Both patterns wrap an object, but only Adapter *changes* the interface. Proxy preserves the interface — this distinction is **mandatory** in the answer.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat UML class diagram, explain the structure of the Proxy design pattern. List its participants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw a sequence diagram showing how a Virtual Proxy handles a lazy-loaded image object. Explain each step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Introduction (2 marks):**
The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it. The Proxy has the same interface as the RealSubject and forwards client requests, optionally performing pre-processing (security, lazy init) and post-processing (caching, logging).

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ISubject {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }
    class RealSubject {
        +request() void
        +realOperation() void
    }
    class ProxyImpl {
        -realSubject : RealSubject
        +request() void
        -preProcess() void
        -postProcess() void
    }
    class ClientApp {
        +main() void
    }

    ISubject <|.. RealSubject
    ISubject <|.. ProxyImpl
    ProxyImpl --> RealSubject : realSubject
    ClientApp ..> ISubject
```

**Step 3 — Participants (2 marks):**

| Participant | Role |
|---|---|
| **Subject** | Abstract interface exposing operations to clients |
| **RealSubject** | The genuine object that performs the real work |
| **Proxy** | Holds a reference to RealSubject, controls access, may perform pre/post hooks |
| **Client** | Interacts with Subject; remains unaware of Proxy vs RealSubject |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Sequence Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ClientActor as Client
    participant VirtualProxyActor as ImageProxy
    participant RealImageActor as HighResolutionImage

    ClientActor->>VirtualProxyActor: display()
    activate VirtualProxyActor
    VirtualProxyActor->>VirtualProxyActor: preProcess() check realImage == null
    alt realImage is null
        VirtualProxyActor->>RealImageActor: instantiate(filename)
        RealImageActor-->>VirtualProxyActor: new instance
    end
    VirtualProxyActor->>RealImageActor: display()
    RealImageActor-->>VirtualProxyActor: rendered image
    VirtualProxyActor->>VirtualProxyActor: postProcess() log access
    VirtualProxyActor-->>ClientActor: return success
    deactivate VirtualProxyActor
```

**Step 2 — Explanation (3 marks):**

1. The Client calls `display()` on the Proxy through the Image interface.
2. The Proxy invokes `preProcess()` to check whether `realImage` has been initialized.
3. If `realImage == null`, the Proxy instantiates `HighResolutionImage` (lazy loading).
4. The Proxy delegates `display()` to the RealSubject.
5. The RealSubject renders the high-resolution image.
6. The Proxy invokes `postProcess()` to log the access (housekeeping).
7. The final result is returned to the Client.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the Proxy design pattern with a real-world analogy. List the consequences of using the Proxy pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Protection Proxy for a banking application where only users with role "Manager" can approve loans. Provide the class diagram and explain the access-check logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Real-world Analogy (3 marks):**
Consider an ATM (Automated Teller Machine). The ATM acts as a **Proxy** for your bank account (the **RealSubject**). Both implement the same interface — *"perform transaction"*. The ATM intercepts the request to perform authentication (pre-hook), contacts the central bank server to apply the transaction (delegation), and prints a receipt (post-hook). The user never interacts with the actual bank database directly.

**Step 2 — Pattern Definition (2 marks):**
The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate object that controls access to a real object. Both share an interface (`Subject`), enabling client transparency. The Proxy may add lazy initialization, security, caching, or remote communication.

**Step 3 — Consequences (2 marks):**

| Benefit | Trade-off |
|---|---|
| Adds protection without modifying RealSubject | Extra indirection → slight latency |
| Supports remote/distributed access | Debugging is harder |
| Enables lazy initialization | Subject must define common interface |
| Supports caching transparently | Can be over-engineered for simple systems |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Identified Participants (2 marks):**

| Role | Class |
|---|---|
| Subject | `IBankService` |
| RealSubject | `BankServiceImpl` |
| Proxy | `ProtectionProxy` |
| Client | `BankClient` |

**Step 2 — Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class IBankService {
        <<interface>>
        +approveLoan() void
        +viewAccount() void
    }
    class BankServiceImpl {
        +approveLoan() void
        +viewAccount() void
    }
    class ProtectionProxy {
        -realService : BankServiceImpl
        -caller : User
        +approveLoan() void
        +viewAccount() void
        -checkAccess() boolean
    }
    class User {
        +username : String
        +role : String
    }
    class BankClient {
        +main() void
    }

    IBankService <|.. BankServiceImpl
    IBankService <|.. ProtectionProxy
    ProtectionProxy --> BankServiceImpl : realService
    ProtectionProxy --> User : caller
    BankClient ..> IBankService
```

**Step 3 — Access-Check Logic (2 marks):**

The `checkAccess()` method inside the `ProtectionProxy` enforces:

$$
\text{allowApprove} =
\begin{cases}
\text{true} & \text{if } \text{caller.role} = \text{"Manager"} \\
\text{false} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

If `allowApprove == false`, the Proxy throws a `SecurityException`. Otherwise, it delegates to `BankServiceImpl.approveLoan()`. Other operations like `viewAccount()` may allow broader roles (e.g., "Teller", "Customer").

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**

1. **Drawing Proxy with a *different* interface than RealSubject.** The Proxy **must** realize the **same** `Subject` interface — this is the defining feature. Marks deducted if interfaces differ.
2. **Confusing Proxy with Adapter or Decorator.** State clearly: Proxy keeps the interface; Adapter changes it; Decorator augments behaviour.
3. **Forgetting stereotypes in UML diagrams.** Use `<<interface>>` above the Subject name — omitting it loses 1 mark.
4. **Missing multiplicity on Proxy → RealSubject.** Use `1` or `0..1` (especially for Virtual Proxy).
5. **Omitting pre/post hooks.** Always show pre-processing and post-processing in the sequence diagram to demonstrate "control".
6. **Wrong arrow type for realization.** Use **dashed hollow triangle** (`- -▷`) — NOT a solid line. Solid lines are for inheritance (a relationship Proxy rarely has with RealSubject).
7. **Listing only 2 proxy types** when 4 are asked. Always aim for at least 4 to be safe.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Proxy Pattern**

- **Pattern Type:** Structural (Gang-of-Four, 1994)
- **Intent:** Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access.
- **Also Known As:** Surrogate
- **Key Participants (4):** Subject, RealSubject, Proxy, Client
- **Subject is `<<interface>>`** — both Proxy and RealSubject realize it.
- **Proxy holds a reference** to RealSubject (association, not inheritance).
- **5 Standard Types of Proxies:**
  1. Remote Proxy
  2. Virtual Proxy
  3. Protection Proxy
  4. Smart Reference
  5. Cache Proxy
- **Pre-hook / Post-hook logic** is what distinguishes a Proxy from a plain wrapper.
- **UML Realization arrow:** dashed hollow triangle.
- **Multiplicity on Proxy→RealSubject:** `1` (or `0..1` for Virtual Proxy).
- **Difference from Adapter:** Proxy = same interface; Adapter = different interface.
- **Difference from Decorator:** Proxy = controls access; Decorator = adds behaviour.
- **Real-World Examples:** Java RMI Stub, Spring AOP, Hibernate Lazy Loading, ATM, Debit Card.
- **Mandatory in 14-mark answers:** Class diagram + Sequence diagram + Participants + Consequences + Real-world example.
- **2-Mark Quick Answer:** *"Proxy provides a controlled surrogate for an object, sharing the same interface as the real object."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Composite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5)**, the term **"Composite"** encompasses two closely related concepts evaluated in Module 4 of PECGT411:

1. **Composite Structure Diagram** — A UML **structure diagram** (one of the 14 official UML 2.5 diagrams) that depicts the **internal structure of a classifier (Class, Component, or Use Case)** by showing its parts, ports, and the connectors through which they interact at runtime.
2. **Composite Pattern (Gang-of-Four)** — A structural design pattern that allows clients to treat **individual objects (Leaf)** and **compositions of objects (Composite)** uniformly through a common **Component** abstraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The "Composite" topic specifically evaluates your ability to **(a)** draw a Composite Structure Diagram with parts, ports and connectors, **(b)** distinguish between *composition* (the structural relationship) and the *Composite Pattern* (a behavioral design solution), and **(c)** apply the Composite Pattern in a real-world modelling scenario.

**Boundary Vocabulary You MUST Know (Bold = High-Frequency Examiner Keywords):**

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Classifier** | A UML model element that describes behavioral and structural features (e.g., Class, Component). |
| **Internal Structure** | The network of parts and connectors that realizes a classifier's behavior. |
| **Part** | A role instance situated inside the internal structure of an owning classifier. |
| **Port** | An interaction point on a classifier's boundary through which it exchanges signals. |
| **Connector** | A link (association role) connecting two parts or a part and a port. |
| **Collaboration** | A set of cooperating roles used collectively to fulfill a purpose. |
| **Component (Pattern)** | The abstract superclass declaring the common interface for both Leaf and Composite. |
| **Leaf (Pattern)** | A primitive object with no children in the Composite tree. |
| **Composite (Pattern)** | A container node that holds child Component objects. |
| **Part-Whole Relationship** | A "has-a" semantic where a whole is made of parts. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Mnemonic: "The Office Building")**
>
> Imagine a **Software System** as a large corporate office building.
> - The **Building (Classifier)** is one black-box to outsiders — they only see the front door (**Port**).
> - Inside the building there are **Departments (Parts)** — HR, Finance, Engineering.
> - **Hallways and elevators (Connectors)** allow the departments to talk to each other.
> - The **Composite Structure Diagram** is the architect's *floor plan* showing this internal layout.
> - Now think of the **Composite Design Pattern** as a *departmental hierarchy*: the **CEO (Component)** gives orders, each **Manager (Composite)** can forward orders to **Sub-Managers or Employees (Leaves)**. The CEO doesn't care whether the recipient is a single Employee or a whole Department — *the call is uniform*.

This dual nature — **structural diagram** (what UML *draws*) and **structural pattern** (how designers *organize code*) — is exactly what KTU examiners test in Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Why "Composite" Matters in Software Modelling

- **Encapsulation at the architecture level:** Composite Structure Diagrams expose *only* the ports externally; internal parts are private implementation.
- **Reuse and uniformity:** The Composite Pattern lets a single client code handle trees of arbitrary depth (e.g., file systems, GUI widgets, organizational charts).
- **NEP 2020 Outcome Alignment:** Maps directly to **CO4** of PECGT411 — *"Apply advanced UML diagrams and design patterns to model complex software architectures."*

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Notation Legend

| UML Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Rectangle with class name (e.g., `Car`) | **Classifier with internal structure** |
| Rectangle inside classifier (e.g., `engine: Engine`) | **Part** (typed by a classifier) |
| Small square on boundary | **Port** |
| Solid line between parts | **Connector (assembly/simplified)** |
| Dashed line | **Dependency** |
| Hollow diamond (in class diagram) | **Aggregation** (weak part-whole) |
| Filled diamond (in class diagram) | **Composition** (strong part-whole) |
| Triangle-headed line | **Generalization** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** If a question says *"explain internal structure"* → it's about **Composite Structure Diagram**. If it says *"treat individual and group objects uniformly"* → it's about the **Composite Design Pattern**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Faces of "Composite" in UML

### Face A — Composite Structure Diagram (CSD)

A **Composite Structure Diagram** is a **structure diagram** in the UML 2.x taxonomy. It exists to solve a recurring modelling problem: *class diagrams show static relationships between classes, but they don't show how instances of those classes collaborate inside a single enclosing instance.*

#### 2.1.1 Core Building Blocks

1. **Classifier with Internal Structure**
   The outer rectangle represents the enclosing classifier. Inside it, a second rectangle (or several) is drawn — each is a **part**.

2. **Part**
   A part is a *property* of the classifier that is typed by another classifier. The notation is:
   $$ \texttt{partName : TypeName [multiplicity]} $$
   Example: `frontWheel : Wheel [2]` inside class `Bicycle`.

3. **Port**
   - **Provided Port (lollipop):** The enclosing classifier *offers* an interface to the outside.
   - **Required Port (socket):** The enclosing classifier *needs* an interface from the outside.
   - **Full Port (small square on boundary):** Encapsulated, has its own behavior.

4. **Connector**
   A line that ties two parts, or a part and a port. Connects roles, not instances.

5. **Collaboration**
   A *use* relationship — an occurrence of a collaboration inside a classifier is drawn with a dashed ellipse labelled `«role»` and roles attached.

#### 2.1.2 Why a CSD Beats a Class Diagram Here

| Aspect | Class Diagram | Composite Structure Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Shows *types* | ✔ | ✔ |
| Shows *instances inside a containing instance* | ✘ | ✔ |
| Shows *connectors between parts* | ✘ (only associations) | ✔ |
| Shows *ports on a boundary* | ✘ | ✔ |
| Supports *collaboration roles* | ✘ | ✔ |

### Face B — Composite Design Pattern (GoF)

**Intent:** *"Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Composite lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly."* — GoF, 1994.

#### 2.1.3 Pattern Participants

| Role | Responsibility | Knows Children? |
|---|---|---|
| **Component** (abstract) | Declares the common interface; default implementations for child-management. | Optionally |
| **Leaf** | Represents end objects; no children. | No |
| **Composite** | Stores child Components; implements child-related operations. | Yes |
| **Client** | Manipulates objects through the Component interface. | — |

#### 2.1.4 The Four Canonical Operations

1. `operation()` — defined in Component, executed in Leaf & Composite.
2. `add(Component)` — sensible default in Component (often throws or no-op), overridden in Composite.
3. `remove(Component)` — symmetric to add.
4. `getChild(int)` — Composite-only accessor.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table verbatim — it is the **exam-day mnemonic anchor** for any "Composite" question.

| # | Concept | Key Property | UML Notation | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Part** | Property of an enclosing classifier | `p : T [n..m]` inside outer rect | Don't draw a *class* with an attribute named `T`; use a separate rectangle for the part. |
| 2 | **Port** | Interaction point on boundary | Small square on the enclosing rectangle's border | Ports are *on the boundary line*, not floating in space. |
| 3 | **Connector** | Link between two parts | Solid line | Don't confuse with *association*; connectors are inside one enclosing instance. |
| 4 | **Composition (structure)** | Strong ownership, lifetimes tied | Filled diamond on owner class | Diamond sits on the *whole* side, not the part side. |
| 5 | **Aggregation (structure)** | Weak ownership, shared lifetimes | Hollow diamond | Same placement rule as composition. |
| 6 | **Component (Pattern)** | Abstract supertype | Abstract class with italic name | Don't make it concrete; the whole point is polymorphism. |
| 7 | **Leaf (Pattern)** | No children | Concrete class | Leaf must *not* override `add/remove` meaningfully. |
| 8 | **Composite (Pattern)** | Holds children | Concrete class with `children` collection | Store `Component` references, not `Leaf` references. |
| 9 | **Operation Traversal Rule** | Composite forwards `op()` to all children | Pseudocode loop | Use recursion, not iteration across types. |
| 10 | **Client Transparency** | `client.operation(root)` works for any node | — | Don't downcast to `Composite` in client code. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** Composite Structure Diagrams model ECU (Electronic Control Unit) internal sub-modules and their wiring — *directly used in automotive AUTOSAR architectures.*
- **GUI Frameworks (Swing, JavaFX, AWT):** Every container (e.g., `JPanel`) is a Composite; widgets (e.g., `JButton`) are Leaves. The `add(Component)` API is a textbook Composite.
- **File Systems:** Folder = Composite, File = Leaf, both extend `FileSystemNode`.
- **Organizational Charts:** Manager = Composite, Employee = Leaf, both extend `Person`.
- **XML/JSON DOM Trees:** Element nodes are Composites, text nodes are Leaves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whenever you encounter a *recursive tree-like structure* in your domain (directory, document, hardware assembly, organizational hierarchy), the Composite Pattern is the canonical GoF solution — and a Composite Structure Diagram is the canonical UML visualization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How an Examiner Builds a "Composite" Question

The matrix below is the **step-by-step cognitive scaffold** examiners use. Master it and you can reverse-engineer *any* question.

| Step | Examiner's Hidden Intent | Student's Required Action | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Test if you can **define** Composite. | Write a one-line definition (≤ 30 words). | 2 |
| 2 | Test if you can **distinguish** the two faces. | Tabulate CSD vs Composite Pattern. | 3 |
| 3 | Test if you can **draw** a CSD with parts, ports, connectors. | Produce a neat Mermaid/PlantUML diagram. | 4–7 |
| 4 | Test if you can **apply** the Composite Pattern. | Draw the class diagram (Component, Leaf, Composite) and trace `op()`. | 5–7 |
| 5 | Test if you can **evaluate trade-offs** (transparency vs safety). | Discuss 1 pro and 1 con. | 2–3 |

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Exercise — Library Catalogue

**Problem (Modelled from KTU pattern):** *Model a library catalogue where Books and Boxes-of-Books must be uniformly searchable by title. Apply the Composite Pattern and draw the corresponding UML Class Diagram and a Composite Structure Diagram showing how a "ReferenceSection" is internally composed.*

### Step 1 — Identify the Hierarchy

A `ReferenceSection` contains:
- Books (Leaves — no children).
- Sub-Sections (Composites — may contain Books or other Sub-Sections).

Both must respond to `searchByTitle(String)`. ⇒ **Composite Pattern applies.**

### Step 2 — Build the Class Diagram (Pattern Application)

The four participants in the Library context:

- **Component (abstract):** `LibraryItem` — declares `searchByTitle(String) : List<LibraryItem>` and default `add/remove`.
- **Leaf:** `Book` — implements `searchByTitle` by checking its own title.
- **Composite:** `BoxOfBooks` (or `Section`) — stores `List<LibraryItem> children`; recursively calls `searchByTitle` on each child.
- **Client:** `Librarian` — calls `searchByTitle` on a root `LibraryItem` reference.

### Step 3 — Derive the `searchByTitle` Recursion

Let $T(v)$ be the result of calling `searchByTitle(s)` on a node $v$ in the tree, where $s$ is the query string.

For a **Leaf** node $v$:

$$
T(v) = \begin{cases} \{v\} & \text{if } \text{title}(v) = s \\ \varnothing & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For a **Composite** node $v$ with children $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$:

$$
T(v) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} T(c_i)
$$

The full traversal of a tree rooted at $r$:

$$
T(r) = \bigcup_{v \in \text{descendants}(r) \cup \{r\}} \text{leafMatch}(v)
$$

where

$$
\text{leafMatch}(v) = \begin{cases} \{v\} & \text{if } v \text{ is a Leaf and } \text{title}(v) = s \\ \varnothing & \text{if } v \text{ is a Leaf and } \text{title}(v) \neq s \\ \bigcup_{c \in \text{children}(v)} T(c) & \text{if } v \text{ is a Composite} \end{cases}
$$

This piecewise definition is the **mathematical essence** of the Composite Pattern's `operation()`.

### Step 4 — Trace an Example

Suppose we have the following tree:

$$
\text{ReferenceSection} \rightarrow \{\text{Book: "C++ Primer"},\; \text{AlgorithmsBox} \rightarrow \{\text{Book: "CLRS"},\; \text{Book: "C++ Primer"}\}\}
$$

Query $s = $ `"C++ Primer"`.

- Leaf `C++ Primer` (top level) ⇒ matches ⇒ contributes itself.
- Composite `AlgorithmsBox`:
   - Leaf `CLRS` ⇒ no match.
   - Leaf `C++ Primer` ⇒ matches ⇒ contributes itself.
- Composite `ReferenceSection` ⇒ union of children results = `{Book1, Book3}`.

Final answer: **2 books** found. The trace confirms recursive uniformity.

### Step 5 — Composite Structure Diagram for `ReferenceSection`

The enclosing classifier is `ReferenceSection`. Inside it, three parts are drawn: `b1: Book`, `algorithmsBox: AlgorithmsBox`, and a port `searchPort: ISearch`. Connectors link `searchPort` to each part.

> [!TIP]
> **How to Earn Full Marks in a CSD Drawing:**
> 1. Draw the outer rectangle with classifier name in bold.
> 2. Place each part as an inner rectangle labelled `name : Type`.
> 3. Place ports **on the boundary line** as small squares.
> 4. Draw connectors as **solid lines** between parts or between a part and a port.
> 5. Add `«role»` labels on connector ends if asked.

### Step 6 — Transparency vs Safety Trade-off (2-Mark Favourite)

| Aspect | Transparent (Component has `add/remove`) | Safe (Composite has `add/remove` only) |
|---|---|---|
| Client convenience | High — single API. | Lower — downcast required. |
| Type safety | Low — `add` on Leaf is meaningless. | High — compile-time enforced. |
| Common usage | GoF original, Java AWT, Swing. | Modern variants (e.g., Composite transparency *with* exception in Leaf). |
| KTU Answer Hint | "Maximises uniformity." | "Maximises type safety." |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of a CSD (Algorithm in Plain Steps)

1. **Identify the enclosing classifier** (the whole).
2. **Identify the parts** (its internal collaborating instances).
3. **Determine multiplicities** of each part (e.g., 4 wheels on a car).
4. **Identify external interactions** → these become **ports**.
5. **Determine internal communication paths** → these become **connectors**.
6. **Add role labels** to connector ends if the question demands "with roles."
7. **Validate** that every external interaction is mediated by a port (not by a raw part).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall 1:** Drawing parts *outside* the enclosing rectangle — *parts must be drawn inside.*
> **Examiner Pitfall 2:** Forgetting multiplicities on parts — *always annotate `[n..m]`.*
> **Examiner Pitfall 3:** Putting a port in the middle of the diagram — *ports belong on the boundary line only.*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Composite Structure Diagram — "SmartCar" Example

The Mermaid block below draws a Composite Structure Diagram for a `SmartCar` enclosing classifier with four parts (`engine`, `battery`, `display`, `brakeSystem`), two ports (`drivePort`, `diagnosticPort`), and connectors.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A ["SmartCar : CarClassifier"]
        direction TB
        engine["engine : Engine\n[1]"]
        battery["battery : Battery\n[1]"]
        display["display : Display\n[1]"]
        brake["brakeSystem : BrakeSystem\n[2]"]
    end

    drivePort(["drivePort : IDrive"])
    diagPort(["diagnosticPort : IDiagnostic"])

    drivePort -.->|signal| engine
    drivePort -.->|power| battery
    diagPort -.->|query| display
    diagPort -.->|status| brake
    engine ---|fuelFlow| brake
    battery ---|powerLine| display

    classDef port fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class drivePort,diagPort port
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Outer rectangle = `SmartCar` (the enclosing classifier).
- Inner rectangles = **parts** with `name : Type` and multiplicity.
- Rounded squares on the boundary = **ports** (`drivePort` is a provided port; `diagPort` is a full port).
- Solid lines = **connectors**.
- Dotted lines = **signal connectors** (semantic distinction often tested).

## 4.2 Composite Design Pattern — Class Diagram (Library Catalogue)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class LibraryItem {
        <<abstract>>
        +String title
        +searchByTitle(String s)* List~LibraryItem~
        +add(LibraryItem) void
        +remove(LibraryItem) void
    }
    class Book {
        +String isbn
        +searchByTitle(String s) List~LibraryItem~
    }
    class Section {
        -List~LibraryItem~ children
        +searchByTitle(String s) List~LibraryItem~
        +add(LibraryItem) void
        +remove(LibraryItem) void
    }
    class Librarian {
        +runQuery(LibraryItem, String) void
    }

    LibraryItem <|-- Book
    LibraryItem <|-- Section
    Section "0..*" o-- "1" LibraryItem : children
    Librarian ..> LibraryItem : uses

    classDef abstract fill:#FFE599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef concrete fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class LibraryItem abstract
    class Book,Section,Librarian concrete
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `LibraryItem` is the abstract **Component** (italic font or `<<abstract>>` stereotype).
- `Book` is the **Leaf**.
- `Section` is the **Composite**; note the aggregation/composition diamond to `LibraryItem` collection.
- `Librarian` is the **Client** (dashed dependency arrow).
- Inheritance arrows from `LibraryItem` to `Book` and `Section` show the generalization hierarchy.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — `searchByTitle` Recursive Call

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant L as Librarian
    participant S as Section : root
    participant B1 as Book A
    participant Sub as Section : child
    participant B2 as Book B

    L->>S: searchByTitle("C++")
    activate S
    S->>B1: searchByTitle("C++")
    B1-->>S: []
    S->>Sub: searchByTitle("C++")
    activate Sub
    Sub->>B2: searchByTitle("C++")
    B2-->>Sub: [Book B]
    Sub-->>S: [Book B]
    deactivate Sub
    S-->>L: [Book B]
    deactivate S
```

**Reading the sequence:**
- The client (Librarian) only ever calls `searchByTitle` on the *root* `LibraryItem` reference — that's the **uniformity** the Composite Pattern promises.
- The Composite (`Section`) recursively forwards the call to its children.
- The Leaf (`Book`) executes the local check and returns.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Fallback Schematic)

For examiners who need a quick "box-and-arrow" mental model of the Composite Pattern's runtime behavior, here is a block architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Client Request\n(e.g., searchByTitle)"] --> B["Component Interface\n(LibraryItem)"]
    B --> C1["Leaf Handler\n(Book)"]
    B --> C2["Composite Handler\n(Section)"]
    C2 --> D1["Iterate children"]
    D1 --> E1["Forward request to child 1"]
    D1 --> E2["Forward request to child 2"]
    D1 --> E3["Forward request to child n"]
    E1 --> B
    E2 --> B
    E3 --> B
    C1 --> F["Aggregate Results"]
    C2 --> F
    F --> A
```

**Reading the flow:**
- All requests enter through the **Component** interface.
- The dispatcher decides at runtime whether the target is a Leaf (executes locally) or a Composite (iterates and forwards).
- Results are aggregated bottom-up and returned to the Client.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Composite Structure Diagram. List its key components.**

> **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
> A **Composite Structure Diagram** is a UML structure diagram that models the *internal structure* of a classifier by showing its parts, ports, and the connectors through which they communicate. It is used when the *collaborations inside a single instance* are important and a class diagram's external associations are insufficient.
> **Key components:**
> 1. **Enclosing classifier** (outer rectangle).
> 2. **Parts** (inner rectangles labelled `name : Type [mult]`).
> 3. **Ports** (small squares on the boundary).
> 4. **Connectors** (lines tying parts/ports).
> 5. **Collaboration use** (dashed ellipse for role-based reuse).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Composite Design Pattern? Mention any two real-world examples.**

> **Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**
> The **Composite Design Pattern** is a GoF structural pattern that composes objects into **tree structures** to represent part-whole hierarchies, allowing clients to treat individual objects (Leaf) and compositions (Composite) **uniformly** through a common **Component** interface.
> **Examples:**
> 1. **File systems:** `File` (Leaf) and `Folder` (Composite) both extend `FileSystemNode`.
> 2. **GUI toolkits (Swing/AWT):** `JButton` (Leaf) and `JPanel` (Composite) both extend `Component`; `add(Component)` is the canonical Composite API.
> (Other valid: organizational chart, XML DOM, menu-bar hierarchy.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. Drawing a CSD with **parts placed outside** the enclosing rectangle → **−2 marks**.
> 2. Forgetting **port placement on the boundary** (drawing ports inside the rectangle) → **−1 mark**.
> 3. In Composite Pattern, **storing `Leaf` references in the Composite's collection** instead of `Component` references → **−2 marks** (breaks uniformity).
> 4. Writing a **recursive `searchByTitle` as a loop with `instanceof`** checks → **−1 mark** (anti-pattern; defeats polymorphism).
> 5. Omitting the **transparent vs safe trade-off** discussion in 14-mark answers → **−2 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Composite Structure Diagram Focus

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a **Composite Structure Diagram**. Use an example of an **OnlineShoppingSystem** that has internal parts like `ProductCatalog`, `PaymentGateway`, `OrderProcessor`, and external ports `placeOrderPort` and `trackOrderPort`. Show all connectors. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss **two advantages** and **one limitation** of using Composite Structure Diagrams over Class Diagrams when modelling complex software systems. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the enclosing classifier:** `OnlineShoppingSystem`.

**Step 2 — Identify parts with multiplicity:**

| Part Name | Type | Multiplicity |
|---|---|---|
| `catalog` | `ProductCatalog` | 1 |
| `payment` | `PaymentGateway` | 1 |
| `orders` | `OrderProcessor` | 1..\* |
| `logger` | `Logger` | 1 |

**Step 3 — Identify ports:**
- `placeOrderPort : IPlaceOrder` (provided, full port) — on left boundary.
- `trackOrderPort : ITrackOrder` (provided, full port) — on right boundary.

**Step 4 — Identify connectors:**

| From | To | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `placeOrderPort` | `orders` | Incoming order request. |
| `orders` | `catalog` | Validate items. |
| `orders` | `payment` | Charge customer. |
| `orders` | `logger` | Audit log. |
| `trackOrderPort` | `orders` | Status query. |

**Step 5 — Render the diagram (described in words, since the rendered Mermaid follows the same logic as Section 4.1):**

> The outer rectangle is labelled `OnlineShoppingSystem`. Inside, four inner rectangles are placed: `catalog : ProductCatalog [1]`, `payment : PaymentGateway [1]`, `orders : OrderProcessor [1..*]`, `logger : Logger [1]`. On the **left boundary** a small square labelled `placeOrderPort` is drawn. On the **right boundary** a small square labelled `trackOrderPort` is drawn. Solid connector lines join `placeOrderPort → orders`, `trackOrderPort → orders`, `orders ↔ catalog`, `orders ↔ payment`, and `orders ↔ logger`. All connector ends carry optional role labels such as `«placeOrder»`, `«trackOrder»`, `«validate»`, `«charge»`, `«audit»`.

**Step 6 — Justify the design:** Encapsulation is preserved because external actors (Customer, Tracking Service) interact **only** through the ports, never directly with internal parts. This enables safe refactoring of internal parts without breaking external contracts. **(7 marks distributed: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for parts/ports, 2.0 for connectors, 1.0 for multiplicity, 1.0 for justification.)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

| S.No | Advantage / Limitation | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Advantage — Internal Visibility:** CSDs expose *parts* and *connectors* that class diagrams cannot show, making architectural intent clear. | A class diagram only shows `OnlineShoppingSystem` as a black box with attributes and methods. The CSD shows the *runtime* mesh of cooperating instances. |
| 2 | **Advantage — Encapsulation via Ports:** External interactions are routed through ports, hiding internal structure. | This aligns with the *Interface Segregation Principle* and makes substitutability (Liskov) testable. |
| 3 | **Limitation — Scalability:** CSDs become cluttered for systems with > ~10 parts. | Mitigation: Use **Collaboration** stereotypes to group parts, or split into nested CSDs. |

**(7 marks: 2 per advantage with full explanation, 3 for the limitation with mitigation.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Composite Design Pattern Focus

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the **Composite Design Pattern** with a UML Class Diagram. Use a **University Organizational Hierarchy** example where `Department` (Composite) contains `Employee` (Leaf) and other `Department` instances. Clearly identify Component, Leaf, Composite, and Client roles. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Write the algorithmic trace of how a `printAllNames()` operation is dispatched recursively from the top-level `Department` (say, the Vice-Chancellor's office) down to every Employee. State the **uniformity property** mathematically. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the participants in the University context:**

| GoF Role | University Class | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Component** (abstract) | `UniversityMember` | Declares `printAllNames()`, default `add/remove`. |
| **Leaf** | `Employee` | No children; overrides `printAllNames` to print own name. |
| **Composite** | `Department` | Holds `List<UniversityMember> members`. |
| **Client** | `AdminPortal` | Treats the root as a `UniversityMember`. |

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram (textual representation; Mermaid equivalent in Section 4.2):**

```
                <<abstract>>
              UniversityMember
              ----------------
              +name : String
              +printAllNames()* : void
              +add(UniversityMember) : void
              +remove(UniversityMember) : void
                       ▲
                       │
        ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
        │                             │
     Employee                     Department
   -----------                 -----------------
   +empId : int               -members : List<UniversityMember>
   +printAllNames()           +printAllNames()
                              +add(UniversityMember)
                              +remove(UniversityMember)
                              +getMember(int)
        △                              △
        │                              │
   AdminPortal ──uses──▶ UniversityMember (polymorphic reference)
```

**Step 3 — Attribute allocations for marks (7 marks):**
- 1.5 marks for identifying the four roles with University analogues.
- 2.0 marks for the class diagram (correct abstract class, inheritance, aggregation with multiplicity `0..*`).
- 1.5 marks for the methods (polymorphic `printAllNames`, child-management on Composite).
- 1.0 mark for the Client linkage (dependency on the **Component**, not on Concrete classes).
- 1.0 mark for multiplicity and naming consistency.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Mathematical Trace of `printAllNames()`**

Let $N(v)$ denote the set of names printed when `printAllNames()` is invoked on a node $v$ in the hierarchy.

**Leaf case (Employee $v$):**

$$
N(v) = \{\text{name}(v)\}
$$

**Composite case (Department $v$ with members $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$):**

$$
N(v) = \{\text{name}(v)\} \cup \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} N(c_i)
$$

**Recursive expansion at the top-level Department $r$:**

$$
N(r) = \{\text{name}(r)\} \cup N(c_1) \cup N(c_2) \cup \ldots \cup N(c_k)
$$

Substituting the Leaf case at the leaves gives a closed form:

$$
N(r) = \{\text{name}(r)\} \;\cup\; \bigcup_{v \in \text{descendants}(r)} \{\text{name}(v)\}
$$

That is, `printAllNames` outputs the name of every node in the subtree rooted at $r$, **regardless of whether the node is a Leaf or a Composite**.

**Uniformity Property (mathematically stated):**

Let $f : \text{Component} \to \text{void}$ be the polymorphic operation `printAllNames`. Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the set of all Composite instances, $\mathcal{L}$ the set of all Leaf instances, and $\mathcal{T} = \mathcal{C} \cup \mathcal{L}$ the set of all Component instances. The **uniformity property** is:

$$
\forall \, x \in \mathcal{T} : f(x) \text{ is well-defined and terminates}
$$

Equivalently, **a single client call `f(root)` correctly produces output for both single employees and whole departments** without any `instanceof` check, type-test, or downcast on the client side.

**Algorithmic Pseudocode (mentally verified):**

```
procedure printAllNames(node : UniversityMember) is
    output(node.name)
    if node is Department then
        for each child in node.members loop
            printAllNames(child)        -- recursive call
        end loop
    end if
end procedure
```

*Note:* The `if node is Department then` is the **only** place where the type is tested, and it is hidden *inside* the Composite. The **Client** (AdminPortal) never needs to perform this check — that is the essence of uniformity.

**Mark distribution for (b):**
- 2.0 marks for the mathematical trace (Leaf case, Composite case).
- 1.5 marks for the closed-form union expression.
- 1.5 marks for the formal uniformity property (∀ quantifier, well-definedness).
- 2.0 marks for the pseudocode and the explanation of *why* uniformity holds (single API, polymorphism).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section**

- [ ] **Composite Structure Diagram (CSD)** = structure diagram in UML 2.x; shows *internal parts*, *ports*, *connectors* of a single enclosing classifier.
- [ ] **Parts are drawn INSIDE** the enclosing rectangle; **ports are drawn ON the boundary line**; **connectors are solid lines**.
- [ ] **CSD ≠ Class Diagram** — CSD shows *instance-level collaborations inside a containing instance*; Class Diagram shows *type-level associations between classes*.
- [ ] **Composite Design Pattern** is from GoF (1994); it composes objects into **tree structures** for **uniform treatment** of individual and composite objects.
- [ ] **Four participants of Composite Pattern:** **Component** (abstract), **Leaf**, **Composite**, **Client**.
- [ ] **Composite's children collection MUST be typed as `Component`** (not `Leaf` or `ConcreteComposite`) — this is what makes uniformity possible.
- [ ] **Operation traversal is recursive** — Composite calls `operation()` on every child, then aggregates results.
- [ ] **Two flavours of Composite Pattern:**
   1. **Transparent** — `add/remove` declared in Component (uniform, but unsafe on Leaf).
   2. **Safe** — `add/remove` declared only in Composite (safe, but requires downcast in client).
- [ ] **Real-world analogues:** file systems, GUI toolkits (Swing/AWT/Javafx), XML DOM, org charts, menu bars, hardware assemblies, AUTOSAR ECUs.
- [ ] **UML notation of composition (relationship) ≠ Composite Pattern** — composition is a *filled-diamond* structural relationship; Composite Pattern is a GoF behavioural-structural pattern. Examiners love this confusion.
- [ ] **Ports can be:** *Provided* (lollipop), *Required* (socket), or *Full* (small square on boundary with internal behavior).
- [ ] **Connectors** are between *parts*, or between a *part* and a *port* — never between two ports directly (use a part as a relay, or a delegation connector).
- [ ] **Multiplicity on parts** is shown as `[n..m]` inside the part rectangle.
- [ ] **Mathematical essence:** the operation on a tree is a **union over descendants**; uniformity means the same API works for any node.
- [ ] **Always include a trade-off discussion** in 14-mark answers (transparency vs safety; CSD vs Class Diagram).
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite 2-mark question:** *"Distinguish between aggregation, composition, and the Composite Pattern."* — answer all three in tabular form.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite 7-mark question:** *"Draw a Composite Structure Diagram for [domain] with at least 3 parts and 2 ports."* — use the SmartCar or OnlineShoppingSystem template.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite 14-mark question:** *"Apply the Composite Pattern to [domain] and trace the recursive operation."* — use the Library or University Org template.

> **End of Module 4 — Composite Notes. All the best for your KTU ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decorator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decorator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Decorator Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** from the *Gang of Four (GoF)* catalogue that allows behavior to be added to individual objects dynamically (at run-time), without affecting the behavior of other objects from the same class. It provides a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality by wrapping an original object inside a special "wrapper" object that shares the same interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim GoF phrasing, frequently quoted):**
> *"Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality."*
> — *Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (1994)*

**Classification (GoF Taxonomy):**
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Pattern Category | **Structural** (Object-Scoped) |
| Pattern Family | GoF — *Gang of Four* |
| Aliases | **Wrapper**, *Smart Proxy* (light variant) |
| Coupling Type | **Object Composition** (HAS-A over IS-A) |
| Purpose | **Open/Closed Principle** compliance |
| Intent Rank (KTU Frequency) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (High-yield) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza-Add-On Model" 🍕

Imagine you walk into a pizza shop and order a **Margherita base pizza**. The base pizza is your core object. Now, as the cashier builds your order, they dynamically *layer*:
1. 🧀 Extra Cheese → wraps the base, adds cost/flavor
2. 🍄 Mushrooms → wraps the (cheese-decorated) pizza, adds cost/flavor
3. 🌶️ Jalapeños → wraps the previous, adds cost/flavor

At every step, the **cost and description keep growing**, but the pizza itself is never "re-baked" from a different class. Each add-on is a *decorator* wrapping the previous state, and the cashier treats the final object **exactly like any pizza** (uniform interface).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Instead of creating 64 subclasses (`MargheritaWithCheeseAndMushroomWithJalapeño...`), the Decorator lets you mix-and-match responsibilities at *run time* by stacking lightweight wrapper objects.

---

### 1.3 Why Decorator Exists — The Problem It Solves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inheritance Explosion Problem:**
> Suppose `Window` has 4 optional features (scrollbar, border, menu, status-bar). Subclassing yields $2^4 = \mathbf{16}$ classes. With 8 features, this balloons to $2^8 = \mathbf{256}$ classes — an unmaintainable combinatorial explosion.

| Approach | Drawback |
|---|---|
| Compile-time **Inheritance** | Static; combinatorial class explosion; recompile on change |
| **Decorator** (Composition) | Dynamic; unlimited combinations; obeys OCP & SRP |

**Core Tenets Satisfied:**
- **Open/Closed Principle (OCP):** Classes open for extension, closed for modification.
- **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP):** Each decorator handles one concern.
- **Favor Composition over Inheritance** — GoF's recurring mantra.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Intent, Motivation, and Applicability

#### 2.1.1 Intent (The "What")
To attach **additional responsibilities** to an object **dynamically**, providing a flexible alternative to subclassing.

#### 2.1.2 Motivation (The "Why")
Without Decorator, you would need to anticipate *every* feature combination at design time. Decorator defers this decision to *run time*, where the actual requirements are known.

#### 2.1.3 Applicability — When to Use
Use the Decorator pattern when:
1. You need to add responsibilities to individual objects *dynamically and transparently*, without affecting other objects.
2. You need to add responsibilities that can be *withdrawn* later.
3. Extension by subclassing is **impractical** (class explosion).
4. A class definition is *hidden* or otherwise unavailable for subclassing.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trigger Words** in exam questions: *"add responsibilities dynamically"*, *"flexible alternative to subclassing"*, *"attach features at run time"*, *"open-closed principle"*.

---

### 2.2 Structure — The 4 Canonical Participants

The GoF structure defines **four** participants. Memorizing these is critical for KTU class-diagram questions.

| # | Participant | Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Component** (`IComponent`) | Abstract base | Declares the common interface for both wrappers and wrapped objects |
| 2 | **ConcreteComponent** | Core object | The original object to which extra duties can be added |
| 3 | **Decorator** (often `abstract`) | Base wrapper | Holds a reference to a `Component`; delegates all ops to it |
| 4 | **ConcreteDecorator** (A, B, C, …) | Specific add-on | Adds state/behavior before/after delegation |

---

### 2.3 High-Yield Structural Rules (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These rules are tested repeatedly in KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions.

| Rule # | Rule | Why It Matters |
|:---:|---|---|
| R1 | `Component` is **abstract**; the **client** always programs to this interface | Guarantees transparency |
| R2 | `Decorator` **IS-A** `Component` (inheritance) | Enables transparent substitution |
| R3 | `Decorator` **HAS-A** `Component` (composition) | The wrapping mechanism |
| R4 | `ConcreteDecorator` calls `component.Operation()` (or its override) and **adds behavior before/after** | The "decoration" |
| R5 | `ConcreteComponent` may be the **only** concrete base; multiple decorators may **stack** | Runtime flexibility |
| R6 | Decorators should be **lightweight**; otherwise consider `Strategy` | Anti-bloat |
| R7 | A `Decorator` is **transparent** to the client — it does not know it is decorated | Encapsulation |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Count Sheet

Since design patterns don't have traditional "formulas," here is the **quantitative** cheat sheet that KTU examiners love:

$$
N_{\text{classes}}^{\text{(inheritance)}} = 2^n \quad \text{where } n = \text{number of optional features}
$$

$$
N_{\text{classes}}^{\text{(decorator)}} = 1 + 1 + n = n + 2 \quad \text{(Component + ConcreteComponent + }n\text{ ConcreteDecorators)}
$$

$$
\text{Combinatorial reduction factor} = \frac{2^n}{n+2} \;\xrightarrow{n \to \infty}\; \infty
$$

$$
\text{Num. of run-time configurations} = 2^n - 1 \quad (\text{excluding the bare base})
$$

| Metric | Inheritance | Decorator |
|---|---|---|
| Class count growth | Exponential $2^n$ | Linear $n+2$ |
| Runtime configurability | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Compile-time cost | High | Low |
| Memory overhead | Low (compile-time) | Slight (one object per wrap) |
| OCP compliance | Partial | Full |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Decorator Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Java I/O** | `FileInputStream` → `BufferedInputStream` → `DataInputStream` (canonical example) |
| **GUI Toolkits** (Swing/JavaFX) | Adding borders, scrollbars, shadows to widgets |
| **Web Middleware** | Wrapping HTTP request handlers (logging → auth → caching) |
| **Middleware Stacks** | Express.js, ASP.NET Core request pipeline |
| **Graphics APIs** | Wrapping drawable objects with fills, strokes, transforms |
| **Enterprise Java** | `EJB` interceptors, CDI decorators |
| **Network Stacks** | SSL/TLS wrapping of plain TCP sockets |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Decorator Class Diagram (Step-by-Step)

We derive the canonical Decorator class diagram from first principles, mimicking how a KTU topper would build it in an exam.

**Step 1 — Identify the Common Interface**

A client must treat wrapped and unwrapped objects **uniformly**. Therefore, define an abstract `Component`:

```text
Component
─────────
+ Operation() : void
```

**Step 2 — Identify the Core Concrete Object**

The `ConcreteComponent` is the *default* object that may receive additional responsibilities:

```text
ConcreteComponent
─────────────────
+ Operation() : void   { default behavior }
```

**Step 3 — Identify the Base Wrapper**

The `Decorator` class is itself a `Component` (for transparency) but **holds a reference** to a `Component` it wraps:

```text
Decorator
─────────
- component : Component
+ Operation() : void   { delegate to component }
```

**Step 4 — Identify the Specific Add-Ons**

Each `ConcreteDecorator` extends `Decorator` and injects state + behavior. The general form is:

$$
\text{ConcreteDecorator.Operation}() \;\equiv\; \text{addedBehaviorBefore()} \;+\; \text{super.Operation}() \;+\; \text{addedBehaviorAfter()}
$$

**Step 5 — Stitch the 4-Tier Hierarchy**

The structural alignment is captured in this equation of relationships:

$$
\text{ConcreteDecorator} \;\xrightarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{Decorator} \;\xrightarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{Component} \;\xleftarrow{\text{extends}}\; \text{ConcreteComponent}
$$

$$
\text{Decorator} \;\xrightarrow{\text{aggregates}}\; \text{Component} \quad \text{(recursive composition)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mathematical Class-Count Savings

Let $n$ denote the number of optional features.

**Inheritance (naïve subclassing):**
For each of the $n$ features we have two states: present or absent. Each feature yields a binary choice, producing:

$$
N_{\text{inherit}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} 2 \;=\; 2^{n}
$$

**Decorator (composition):**
We need exactly $1$ abstract `Component`, $1$ `ConcreteComponent`, and $n$ `ConcreteDecorator` classes:

$$
N_{\text{decor}} \;=\; 1 + 1 + n \;=\; n + 2
$$

**Reduction Ratio:**

$$
\rho(n) \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{inherit}}}{N_{\text{decor}}} \;=\; \frac{2^{n}}{n + 2}
$$

**Sample Evaluation Table (often asked in 3-mark questions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(2) &= \frac{2^2}{2+2} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.00 \\
\rho(4) &= \frac{2^4}{4+2} = \frac{16}{6} \approx 2.67 \\
\rho(6) &= \frac{2^6}{6+2} = \frac{64}{8} = 8.00 \\
\rho(8) &= \frac{2^8}{8+2} = \frac{256}{10} = 25.6 \\
\rho(10) &= \frac{2^{10}}{10+2} = \frac{1024}{12} \approx 85.33
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For $n=8$ features, inheritance demands **256** classes while Decorator requires only **10** — a 25.6× reduction. This single table is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Collaborations (Object-Level Trace)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part-B questions on Decorator often demand a **sequence diagram** *and* a text-trace of the collaboration. This is the matrix to memorize.

| Step | Object | Action | Resulting State |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client | `Component* c = new ConcreteComponent();` | Bare object |
| 2 | Client | `c = new ConcreteDecoratorA(c);` | A-wrapped |
| 3 | Client | `c = new ConcreteDecoratorB(c);` | B(A-wrapped) |
| 4 | Client | `c.Operation();` | Call enters **outermost** decorator |
| 5 | `ConcreteDecoratorB` | Executes pre-behavior | `addedStateB` engaged |
| 6 | `ConcreteDecoratorB` | Delegates to inner `Operation()` | Forwards to `Decorator` |
| 7 | `ConcreteDecoratorA` | Executes pre-behavior | `addedStateA` engaged |
| 8 | `ConcreteDecoratorA` | Delegates to inner `Operation()` | Forwards to `ConcreteComponent` |
| 9 | `ConcreteComponent` | Performs **core** operation | Base behavior done |
| 10 | Control returns | Bubbles up | Post-behaviors fire in reverse stack order (LIFO) |

**Key Insight (LIFO "Onion-Peel" Effect):**

$$
\text{Execution order} \;=\; \text{outermost pre} \;\to\; \cdots \;\to\; \text{inner pre} \;\to\; \text{core} \;\to\; \text{inner post} \;\to\; \cdots \;\to\; \text{outermost post}
$$

---

### 3.4 Pseudocode Model (UML-Compatible, for Theory Questions)

```text
abstract class Component {
    public abstract void Operation();
}

class ConcreteComponent : Component {
    public override void Operation() { /* core behavior */ }
}

abstract class Decorator : Component {
    private Component component;
    public Decorator(Component c) { this.component = c; }
    public override void Operation() { component.Operation(); }
}

class ConcreteDecoratorA : Decorator {
    public ConcreteDecoratorA(Component c) : base(c) {}
    private string addedState = "A-feature";
    public override void Operation() {
        AddedBehaviorBefore();
        base.Operation();
        AddedBehaviorAfter();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU theory questions rarely ask for *working code*; they ask for the **class structure** and the **sequence of delegations**. Practice writing the `Operation()` body in plain prose: *"pre-behavior, delegate via `base.Operation()`, post-behavior."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Class Diagram (GoF Structure)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class IComponent {
        <<interface>>
        +Operation() void
    }
    class ConcreteComponent {
        +Operation() void
    }
    class Decorator {
        <<abstract>>
        -component : IComponent
        +Decorator(c : IComponent)
        +Operation() void
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorA {
        -addedState : string
        +Operation() void
        +AddedBehavior() void
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorB {
        -addedState : string
        +Operation() void
        +AddedBehavior() void
    }
    IComponent <|.. ConcreteComponent : implements
    IComponent <|.. Decorator : implements
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorA : extends
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorB : extends
    Decorator o-- IComponent : wraps (aggregation)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU:** Always label the *two* key relationships explicitly — (1) **inheritance** from `Component`, and (2) **aggregation/has-a** from `Decorator` back to `Component`. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for these.

---

### 4.2 Object Diagram — "Stacking Three Decorators" (Run-Time Snapshot)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorB_obj {
        +addedState = "Border"
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorA_obj {
        +addedState = "Scrollbar"
    }
    class ConcreteComponent_obj {
        +baseData = "Window"
    }
    Client --> ConcreteDecoratorB_obj : delegates Operation()
    ConcreteDecoratorB_obj --> ConcreteDecoratorA_obj : component ref
    ConcreteDecoratorA_obj --> ConcreteComponent_obj : component ref
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Diagram Insight:** At *run time*, a chain like `ConcreteDecoratorB → ConcreteDecoratorA → ConcreteComponent` exists. Each `Operation()` walks **outward-in** (pre), executes the **core**, then **inward-out** (post).

---

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Execution Trace of `Operation()`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant DecB as ConcreteDecoratorB
    participant DecA as ConcreteDecoratorA
    participant Core as ConcreteComponent

    Client->>DecB: Operation()
    activate DecB
    Note over DecB: addedBehaviorBefore() (Border drawn)
    DecB->>DecA: Operation()
    activate DecA
    Note over DecA: addedBehaviorBefore() (Scrollbar drawn)
    DecA->>Core: Operation()
    activate Core
    Core-->>DecA: core draw done
    deactivate Core
    Note over DecA: addedBehaviorAfter() (Scrollbar finalize)
    DecA-->>DecB: return
    deactivate DecA
    Note over DecB: addedBehaviorAfter() (Border finalize)
    DecB-->>Client: return
    deactivate DecB
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Stacking Metaphor)

> [!TIP]
> The following flowchart distils the Decorator runtime into 6 steps. Use it in your answer sheet for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client invokes Operation on outermost decorator] --> B[ConcreteDecoratorB pre-behavior]
    B --> C[ConcreteDecoratorB forwards to component ref]
    C --> D[ConcreteDecoratorA pre-behavior]
    D --> E[ConcreteDecoratorA forwards to component ref]
    E --> F[ConcreteComponent executes core Operation]
    F --> G[Return to ConcreteDecoratorA post-behavior]
    G --> H[Return to ConcreteDecoratorB post-behavior]
    H --> I[Control returns to Client]
```

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| `A` | Client entry point |
| `B` | Outermost decorator pre-hook |
| `C` | Delegation step (composition arrow) |
| `D` | Intermediate decorator pre-hook |
| `E` | Inner delegation step |
| `F` | Core execution in `ConcreteComponent` |
| `G`, `H` | Post-hooks fire in LIFO order |
| `I` | Final return |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct, Short-Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Decorator pattern. List any two situations in which it is applicable.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Decorator pattern is a **structural design pattern** that dynamically attaches additional responsibilities to an object without modifying its class. It is a flexible alternative to subclassing, achieved by wrapping the original object in a `Decorator` that shares the same `Component` interface.

**Applicable situations:**
1. When responsibilities need to be **added or withdrawn dynamically** at run time.
2. When subclassing leads to an **exponential explosion** of classes (e.g., $2^n$ combinations).
3. *(Bonus)* When a class definition is *hidden* and cannot be subclassed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Identify the four participants in the Decorator pattern and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Participant | Role |
|---|---|
| **Component** | Abstract class/interface defining operations common to both wrapped and wrapper objects |
| **ConcreteComponent** | The original object to which extra responsibilities may be attached |
| **Decorator** | Abstract class holding a reference to a `Component`; implements the `Component` interface and delegates operations to it |
| **ConcreteDecorator** | Specific subclass of `Decorator` that adds state/behavior before/after delegating to the wrapped object |

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU Pattern 2024 Scheme]**
>
> *(a)* **[7 Marks]** A text-viewer application displays the contents of a plain `TextView`. The team wants to add the following responsibilities **dynamically and independently**: (i) a **scrollbar**, (ii) a **black border**, and (iii) a **horizontal/vertical scroll option**. Apply the **Decorator pattern** to design the system. Draw the **UML class diagram** and the **UML object diagram** showing the configuration where a `TextView` is decorated with **border + scrollbar + vertical-scroll**.

> *(b)* **[7 Marks]** Draw the **UML sequence diagram** for the `Draw()` operation, and explain the **collaboration** between the four participants when the outermost decorator is invoked.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the Component Hierarchy**
- `VisualComponent` (abstract) → declares `Draw()`, `Resize()`
- `TextView` (ConcreteComponent) → implements core `Draw()` of plain text

**Step 2 — Identify the Decorators**
- `Decorator` (abstract) → extends `VisualComponent`, holds a `VisualComponent component` field
- `ScrollDecorator` (ConcreteDecorator A)
- `BorderDecorator` (ConcreteDecorator B)
- `VerticalScrollDecorator` (ConcreteDecorator C)

**Step 3 — Class Diagram**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class VisualComponent {
        <<abstract>>
        +Draw() void
        +Resize() void
    }
    class TextView {
        +Draw() void
        +Resize() void
    }
    class Decorator {
        <<abstract>>
        -component : VisualComponent
        +Decorator(vc : VisualComponent)
        +Draw() void
        +Resize() void
    }
    class ScrollDecorator {
        -widthToFill : int
        +Draw() void
    }
    class BorderDecorator {
        -borderWidth : int
        +Draw() void
        +DrawBorder() void
    }
    class VerticalScrollDecorator {
        +Draw() void
    }
    VisualComponent <|-- TextView
    VisualComponent <|-- Decorator
    Decorator <|-- ScrollDecorator
    Decorator <|-- BorderDecorator
    Decorator <|-- VerticalScrollDecorator
    Decorator o-- VisualComponent : wraps
```

**Step 4 — Object Diagram for `Border + Scrollbar + VerticalScroll` Configuration**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client
    class BorderObj {
        -borderWidth = 2
    }
    class ScrollObj {
        -widthToFill = 80
    }
    class VScrollObj {
    }
    class TV {
        +plainText
    }
    Client --> BorderObj : Draw()
    BorderObj --> ScrollObj : component
    ScrollObj --> VScrollObj : component
    VScrollObj --> TV : component
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration equation:**
> $$\text{Client} \to \text{BorderDecorator} \to \text{ScrollDecorator} \to \text{VerticalScrollDecorator} \to \text{TextView}$$
> This is a runtime snapshot — the class diagram remains unchanged.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Sequence Diagram for `Draw()`**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cli as Client
    participant Bd as BorderDecorator
    participant Sc as ScrollDecorator
    participant Vs as VerticalScrollDecorator
    participant Tv as TextView

    Cli->>Bd: Draw()
    activate Bd
    Bd->>Bd: DrawBorder(width=2)
    Bd->>Sc: Draw()
    activate Sc
    Sc->>Sc: scrollTo()
    Sc->>Vs: Draw()
    activate Vs
    Vs->>Tv: Draw()
    activate Tv
    Tv-->>Vs: plain text rendered
    deactivate Tv
    Vs-->>Sc: vertical scrollbars drawn
    deactivate Vs
    Sc-->>Bd: horizontal scrollbars drawn
    deactivate Sc
    Bd-->>Cli: full window with border
    deactivate Bd
```

**Collaboration Explanation (5 points, ~1.4 marks each):**

1. The `Client` invokes `Draw()` on the outermost wrapper, `BorderDecorator`.
2. `BorderDecorator` executes its **pre-behavior** — `DrawBorder(2)` — and then delegates the call to its contained `VisualComponent` (a `ScrollDecorator`).
3. `ScrollDecorator` adds its **own pre-behavior** (compute scroll width) and forwards to its contained `VerticalScrollDecorator`.
4. `VerticalScrollDecorator` adds its **pre-behavior** and finally calls the **core** `TextView.Draw()`, producing the base text.
5. Control **bubbles back** in LIFO order, executing post-behaviors: vertical-scroll finalize → scroll finalize → border finalize. The client receives a fully decorated result.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> - Drawing the *class diagram* when asked for the *object diagram* (or vice-versa) → **-2 marks**.
> - Forgetting the **aggregation arrow** from `Decorator` back to `Component` → **-1 mark**.
> - In the sequence diagram, drawing arrows the **wrong way** (e.g., `TextView → Border` instead of `Border → TextView`) → **-1.5 marks**.
> - Missing the **LIFO post-behavior** step in the trace → **-1 mark**.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU Pattern 2024 Scheme]**
>
> *(a)* **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Intent**, **Motivation**, and **Structure** of the Decorator pattern. Use a real-world example (e.g., Java I/O streams) to justify your answer.
>
> *(b)* **[7 Marks]** Discuss any **four consequences** of using the Decorator pattern, and show — with a table — how Decorator reduces the number of classes for $n = 5$ features compared to naïve inheritance.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Intent (2 marks):**
The Decorator pattern's intent is to *attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically*. It is positioned as a flexible, **run-time** alternative to static subclassing. The change is **transparent** to the client because both the wrapper and the wrapped object share a common interface.

**2. Motivation (2 marks):**
Static inheritance locks responsibilities at *compile time*. If a UI toolkit must support optional features (scrollbars, borders, shadows) that vary per user-session, the combinatorial space of subclasses grows as $2^n$. Decorator resolves this by composing responsibilities at *run time*, allowing any mix without class explosion.

**Java I/O Example (1 mark):**
```text
InputStream in = new DataInputStream(
                    new BufferedInputStream(
                      new FileInputStream("data.bin")));
```
Each wrapper (`Buffered`, `Data`) decorates the one below it, transparently adding responsibilities (buffering, type conversion) while the client still sees an `InputStream`.

**3. Structure (2 marks):**
The GoF class structure is a 4-tier hierarchy:
- `Component` (abstract) — common interface
- `ConcreteComponent` — the core
- `Decorator` (abstract) — wraps a `Component`
- `ConcreteDecorator(s)` — add specific duties

> The defining relationships are: **inheritance** from `Component` (transparency) and **aggregation** of a `Component` inside `Decorator` (the wrap).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Four Consequences (~1.5 marks each):**

1. **More flexible than static inheritance** — Responsibilities can be added/removed at *run time* by stacking or unwrapping decorators.
2. **Avoids feature-heavy classes at the top of the hierarchy** — Instead of one bloated superclass, each responsibility lives in its own small decorator.
3. **Many small objects can result** — A single heavily-decorated object becomes a *chain* of wrappers; debugging/understanding takes effort.
4. **Identity-related issues** — A decorated object is *not identical* to the wrapped object (its `typeid` differs). Code that uses `==` for identity breaks.
5. *(Bonus)* **Hard to instantiate many different decorators** — A `Factory` or `Builder` may be needed to assemble complex stacks.

**Class-Count Table for $n = 5$ Features:**

| Approach | Class-Count Formula | Value at $n=5$ |
|---|---|---|
| Naïve Inheritance | $2^n$ | $2^5 = 32$ |
| Decorator Pattern | $n + 2$ | $5 + 2 = 7$ |
| **Reduction Factor** | $\dfrac{2^n}{n+2}$ | $\dfrac{32}{7} \approx 4.57$ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{inherit}} &= 2^5 = 32 \\
N_{\text{decor}} &= 5 + 2 = 7 \\
\rho(5) &= \frac{32}{7} \approx 4.57
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - Forgetting the **identity-related consequence** → loses 1.5 marks.
> - Confusing **Decorator** with **Strategy** (Strategy changes *algorithm*, Decorator changes *responsibilities*) → **-2 marks**.
> - Writing only **3 consequences** when 4 were asked → **-1.5 marks** (proportional deduction).
> - Showing $2^5 = 32$ but not computing $n+2 = 7$ for the Decorator count → **-1 mark**.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this last, 2 minutes before exam):**

- ✅ **Category:** Structural pattern (GoF).
- ✅ **Intent:** Add responsibilities to an object **dynamically** — flexible alternative to subclassing.
- ✅ **4 Participants:** `Component`, `ConcreteComponent`, `Decorator`, `ConcreteDecorator`.
- ✅ **Two Key Relationships:** (1) **Inheritance** `Decorator IS-A Component`; (2) **Aggregation** `Decorator HAS-A Component`.
- ✅ **Formula:** Inheritance $= 2^n$ classes; Decorator $= n + 2$ classes.
- ✅ **Core Principle:** Open/Closed Principle (OCP) + Favor Composition over Inheritance.
- ✅ **Execution Order:** LIFO onion-peel — outermost pre → … → core → … → outermost post.
- ✅ **Identity Caveat:** A decorated object is **not** identical (`==`) to the original.
- ✅ **Real-World Anchor:** Java I/O streams — `DataInputStream(BufferedInputStream(FileInputStream))`.
- ✅ **Killer Diagram:** Class diagram MUST show the cyclic-looking aggregation `Decorator → Component`.
- ✅ **Distinguish From:** *Strategy* (algorithm swap, not responsibility addition), *Proxy* (controls access, not adds behavior), *Adapter* (changes interface, not adds behavior).
- ✅ **High-Yield 14-Marker Trifecta:** Class Diagram + Object Diagram + Sequence Diagram — always include all three for full marks.
- ✅ **Trigger Words to Recognize:** "dynamically add," "wrap," "alternative to subclassing," "OCP," "composition over inheritance," "LIFO execution."

> *"A Decorator is to a class what accessories are to an outfit — added when needed, removed when not, never modifying the original garment."* — *Anonymous KTU Topper, 2024*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Façade
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT411 - SOFTWARE MODELLING USING UML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Façade

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Façade Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** (from the Gang of Four (GoF) catalogue) that provides a **unified, high-level interface** to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. It defines a higher-level object that makes the subsystem easier to use by wrapping a set of complex classes, libraries, or frameworks behind a single, simplified entry point.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the Façade is classified under **GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** principles as well as the GoF **Structural Patterns** category, specifically engineered to:
- **Promote Loose Coupling** between clients and subsystem classes.
- **Reduce Coupling** by hiding the internal complexity of cooperating classes.
- **Apply the Principle of Least Knowledge** (a.k.a. **Law of Demeter**), which states that an object should interact only with its immediate friends, not with strangers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU's Module 4 (Design Patterns / Advanced Structural Modeling), Façade is discussed alongside Decorator, Composite, and Proxy. The examiner typically tests: (1) intent, (2) UML class diagram, (3) applicability scenarios, and (4) consequences/trade-offs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Concierge" Metaphor

Imagine you walk into a five-star hotel in Kerala. You do **not** want to:
1. Call the chef directly to order food.
2. Talk to the housekeeping staff to clean the room.
3. Coordinate with the valet to park your car.
4. Ring the laundry supervisor separately.

Instead, you speak to **one person** — the **Concierge** (the Façade). You say: *"I need food, my room cleaned, and my car parked."* The concierge coordinates with all the internal subsystems and gets the work done.

**The Concierge = Façade Object**
**The kitchen, housekeeping, valet = Subsystem Classes**
**You (the guest) = Client Object**

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner Memory Hook:** *"Façade = One door, many rooms behind it."* A client uses **one** simple interface, while many subsystem operations happen silently behind it.

---

## 1.3 Standard UML Notations & Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| UML Element | Symbol / Notation | KTU Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Class** | Rectangle with 3 compartments | ✅ Used |
| **Interface (Lollipop)** | Circle on a stick | ✅ Used for subsystem contracts |
| **Realization (implements)** | Dashed hollow triangle arrow | ✅ Façade → Subsystem Interfaces |
| **Dependency (uses)** | Dashed arrow | ✅ Client → Façade |
| **Aggregation / Composition** | Hollow / filled diamond | ✅ Façade ◇ Subsystem (aggregation) |

**Mandatory Bold Constants/Metrics to Remember:**
- **GoF Pattern Count:** **23** (Creational: 5, Structural: 7, Behavioral: 11).
- **Façade falls under:** Structural Patterns = **7** total.
- **Law of Demeter (LoD):** Often called the *"Principle of Least Knowledge"*.

---

## 1.4 Position in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4

Module 4 in PECGT411 typically groups design patterns into structural families. Façade sits with:

- **Adapter** → Converts an interface into another the client expects.
- **Bridge** → Decouples abstraction from implementation.
- **Composite** → Treats groups of objects uniformly.
- **Decorator** → Adds responsibilities dynamically.
- **Façade** → Simplifies a complex subsystem.
- **Flyweight** → Shares fine-grained objects efficiently.
- **Proxy** → Provides a surrogate for another object.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Façade Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> The Façade pattern does **not** prevent advanced clients from accessing subsystem classes directly. It only provides a **convenient, simplified default API**.

### 🔧 Step-by-Step Operational Flow

- **Step 1 — Client Request Initiation**
  - The client object (e.g., a UI controller, a REST endpoint) needs to perform a complex business operation. Without Façade, it would need to know about **N** different subsystem classes, their methods, and their invocation order.

- **Step 2 — Façade Object as Entry Point**
  - The client calls **one method** on the Façade object. The Façade exposes a small, focused set of high-level methods (e.g., `placeOrder()`, `generateReport()`).

- **Step 3 — Façade Coordinates Subsystems**
  - The Façade method internally creates/instantiates or delegates to existing subsystem objects. It knows *which* subsystems to call, *in what order*, and *with what parameters*.

- **Step 4 — Subsystem Execution**
  - Each subsystem class performs its specialized work (e.g., `InventoryService.checkStock()`, `PaymentService.charge()`, `ShippingService.dispatch()`).

- **Step 5 — Result Aggregation & Return**
  - The Façade collects results from subsystems, performs any necessary orchestration logic (e.g., rollback on payment failure), and returns a single, unified result to the client.

- **Step 6 — Optional "Power User" Access**
  - Subsystem classes remain publicly accessible for advanced users who need granular control. Façade only hides them by default — it does **not** wrap them in a black box.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Façade — Architectural Motivations

| Motivation | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Hide Complexity** | Client code becomes shorter, cleaner, and more maintainable. |
| **Decouple Client from Subsystem** | Subsystems can be refactored or replaced without touching client code. |
| **Layered Architecture** | Façades naturally form **architectural boundaries** between layers (UI ↔ Business ↔ Data). |
| **Encapsulation of Workflow** | Multi-step business processes (e.g., checkout) are centralized in one place. |
| **Easier Testing** | Mocking one Façade is easier than mocking 10 subsystem classes. |
| **Reduced Learning Curve** | New developers only learn the Façade API, not the entire subsystem. |

---

## 2.3 Applicability — When to Use Façade (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!TIP]
